```
On Sat, 01 Apr 2000 22:28:37 GMT, Foodman <foodman123@aol.com> wrote:

>This prompts me to ask: do people still design systems using
>sorts? And, if so, why? Or, is this just some old legacy stuff?
>

April Fools right?

In the old days of RM COBOL before they had "SORT" we simulated it
using indexed files.  Maybe your experience is that sort is slow?
Older MicroFocus implementations were such.  Realia on the other hand
was LIGHTNING fast.  Fujitsu today has a good performing sort, and a
better one if you use their POWERBSORT.  There's LOTS of reasons to
use sort, but I won't get into it.  I'm convinced this was an April
fools joke.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: FASTSRT, COBOL II, SyncSort, VSE

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-04-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.vse-l,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38E6F2CE.175B24B4@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<38e66554.25614639@nntp.sprynet.com> <8c5t66$g3t$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Foodman wrote:
> 
> In article <38e66554.25614639@nntp.sprynet.com>,
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> sorts? And, if so, why? Or, is this just some old legacy stuff?

For consistently stored internal stuff, I agree.  For data from
foreign sources then sorting into account number order makes
processing more efficient.  FOr other stuff sequential is the best way
to process it.  Typically you add 15% just creating the index, so be
sure you need it first.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: FASTSRT, COBOL II, SyncSort, VSE - small digression

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 2000-04-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.vse-l,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<WFIF4.16145$0o4.104259@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<38e66554.25614639@nntp.sprynet.com> <8c5t66$g3t$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38E6F2CE.175B24B4@zip.com.au>`

```
In article <38E6F2CE.175B24B4@zip.com.au>,
Ken Foskey  <waratah@zip.com.au> wrote:
>Foodman wrote:
>> 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>sure you need it first.

Curious that you should mention this, Mr Foskey... please permit me a bit
of a digression.

On my present pig-of-a-contract She Who Signs My Timesheets demands that
*all* development (except for online, which is CICS) be done in
EasyTrieve... pfui!  I've taught myself the language and can manage most
tasks in it now but the other day I noticed something... curious.

The Task:  400K input recs (in three concatenated files), each 600 bytes,
each containing a 10-byte Transaction Num.  Take the Transaction Num, use
it for a primary key VSAM read, if rec exists use some data from the VSAM
file, write a report.

I trust this description is sufficient... anyhow, this thing ran for
thirty-eight minutes of wall time and swallowed 1.5 min of CPU.  I was
bored and had a few extra minutes so, just for laffs, I re-wrote the code,
*still* in EasyTrieve (pfui!), to sort the input files on Transaction Num,
sort the VSAM file to a flatfile on Transaction Num (all sorts were done
in EasyTrieve (pfui!) as well, it supports a kind of SORT INFILE TO
OUTFILE USING INREC-KEY statement) and then processing, in Easytrieve, in
a standard (oh... did I mention Easytrieve?  PFUI!) COBOL 101 'match the
update tape to the Master File and create a new Master' match/merge
fashion... *with* a loop controlled by GO TOs *but* adhering to modified
Yourdon rules in that the only GO TOs were to TOP-OF-LOOP and
BREAK-OUT-OF-LOOP.

The result ran in a bit less than half the wall time (18 vs. 38 min) and a
third of the CPU (.5 vs. 1.5 min).

DD
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: FASTSRT, COBOL II, SyncSort, VSE

- **From:** infocat@sprynet.com (Frank Swarbrick)
- **Date:** 2000-04-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.vse-l,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38e6c186.49155185@nntp.sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<38e66554.25614639@nntp.sprynet.com> <8c5t66$g3t$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
On Sat, 01 Apr 2000 22:28:37 GMT, Foodman <foodman123@aol.com> wrote:

>In article <38e66554.25614639@nntp.sprynet.com>,
>infocat@sprynet.com (Frank Swarbrick) wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>sorts? And, if so, why? Or, is this just some old legacy stuff?

It's a long story in this case, but...  Basically, I am reading input
from two DL/I databases, one for demand (checking) and one for
savings.  These are in order by bank, branch, and account number.  The
output VSAM KSDS is one file for all of the records (demand and
savings), in the same order with one exception:  I replace the actual
bank number with a generic bank number (000000001).

Now I will grant you that, since it is a KSDS, there seems to be
little reason for the sort.  But it's felt by my team leader that it
is more efficient this way, since it avoids CA/CI splits.  How much
better this is I really couldn't say...  Now that I think about it, we
do recreate the file daily, so having the sort is even less necessary.
Hmm.


Frank Swarbrick
home: infocat@sprynet.com / work: frank.swarbrick@efirstbank.com
"Ignorance and prejudice - and fear walk hand in hand" --Rush "Witch Hunt"
```

However, is it possible that you have the same file named for both your USING
and GIVING files. (or in your case for in the INPUT procedure and the GIVING
phrase).  If so, then I think this might be your problem.

#### ↳ Re: FASTSRT, COBOL II, SyncSort, VSE

- **From:** "Searcher" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.vse-l,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<teyF4.528$ps.368@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<38e66554.25614639@nntp.sprynet.com>`

```
Was your 'GIVING' file a Keyed file?

(I presume so due to 'DUPE KEY'.
What was the access mode on the file?

FASTSRT ONLy changes who does the I/O.
W/O FASTSRT, even with USING/GIVING,  COBOL does the I/O
With FASTSRT, SORT does not return control to COBOl.

You see, INPUT / OUTPUT Procedure just specifies that
SORT is to use an INPUT (E15) or OUTPUT (E35) Exit Routine --
Your code.  W/O FASTSRT, the COBOL Object Program STILL
DOES THE PHYSICAL I/O for USING/GIVING!!!

Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com> wrote in message
news:38e66554.25614639@nntp.sprynet.com...
> Generally when we do internal sorts in our shop we use both input
> procedure and output procedure.  Yesterday I had reason to use an
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: FASTSRT, COBOL II, SyncSort, VSE

- **From:** infocat@sprynet.com (Frank Swarbrick)
- **Date:** 2000-04-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.vse-l,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38e6c405.49790400@nntp.sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<38e66554.25614639@nntp.sprynet.com> <teyF4.528$ps.368@news4.mia>`

```
On Sat, 1 Apr 2000 21:24:45 -0800, "Searcher" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
wrote:

>Was your 'GIVING' file a Keyed file?
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>DOES THE PHYSICAL I/O for USING/GIVING!!!

It is a keyed file, yes.  (My reasoning for the sort given in a
previous message.)  The access is actually DYNAMIC.  I'll explain:

As I said previously, my input is two DL/I databases; savings and
demand.  Previously the program was run twice; once for each system.
There were two output VSAM KSDS files; again one for each system.  No
sort was involved because everything was in the same order.  After the
file was created it was closed, and then reopened as I-O.  Another
file (a sequential file, with the accounts not in any particular
order)  was read in.  We would then take the account number etc. and
use it as the key for a random read into the KSDS.  We would change
some information and then rewrite the record to the KSDS.

The key, BTW, is as follows:
    05  DDR-KEY.
        10  DDR-BANK    PIC s9(5) comp-3.
        10  DDR-BRANCH  PIC S9(3) comp-3.
        10  DDR-ACCOUNT  PIC S9(7) comp-3.

My goal is twofold:  1) To use a generic bank number of 00001 rather
than the one from the DL/I database, and 2) to process both the
savings followed by the demand and combine them in to one output file.

Anyway, this does seem to work when I don't use fast sort.  I'm sure
it would also work if I used no sort at all (because the output is a
KSDS).  I just would like to know, for my own edification, why it's
not working *with* fast sort.

Thanks for your input.

Frank Swarbrick
home: infocat@sprynet.com / work: frank.swarbrick@efirstbank.com
"Ignorance and prejudice - and fear walk hand in hand" --Rush "Witch Hunt"
```

```

Charles Hammond <ceh1@ritz.cec.wustl.edu> wrote in message
news:Pine.SOL.3.96.1000331142224.10088A-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu...
> I guess I am just used to this small 32 megs of memory and limited
by a 4
> disk stroage system on this old legacy VSE/ESA System 370.  Maybe I
need a
> job where they have better equipment.  Or maybe I don't know what it
> really can do.
>
> I am used to watching a program run for 15 minutes...
>
 The latest table handling thread (Destroying a COBOL Array) does not
represent a normal application design. I would try to avoid the need
to store all my input in a table.

Most of the time tables are limited to reasonable sizes.
It is better to break a complex operation into simple steps, and
endure the additional I/O overhead.
The added program complexity involved in trying to do it all in one
step will generally increase the program development and maintenance
costs.

The methods you would use, are the methods I would use in OS/390.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Tables In COBOL

- **From:** Joseph Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2000-04-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38E73F07.7D30E560@iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000331142224.10088A-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu> <uribpO6m$GA.271@cpmsnbbsa04>`

```

DennisHarley wrote:
> 
>  The latest table handling thread (Destroying a COBOL Array) does not
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> The methods you would use, are the methods I would use in OS/390.
Actually, this make an important distinction between 'mainframe' and Unix/Windows
style programming.

On a mainframe the cost of I/O to a program is relatively cheap. Odds are that
the Cache controller already has your data in cache, also depending upon the
block size of the data, I/O will ensure that the next record to read is almost always
in the computers memory. What this translates to, is that the 'mainframe' reads
data in HUGE gulps at a time. So the typical technique is to read the data a single
record at a time and attempt to process it directly.

These days, you would not even attempt to handle the problem this way. The users data
would have been loaded into a DB2 or Oracle database, and SQL calls would be made to
retrieve the data. If you used DB2, and you set the appropriate controls on the table,
you could have DB2 read the ENTIRE table into memory. In which case you would be
accessing data in memory - just like an array.
```

<a id="t-3def41cb28"></a>
## Thread: I am serious. — 6 this month / 8 total

_Thread started [2000-03](2000-03.md#t-3def41cb28)._

##### ↳ ↳ Re: I am serious.

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<S4cF4.18634$QJ3.2904834@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<8bvqrl$nav$114@info.sta.net.cn> <155F4.784$lT3.36321@news.swbell.net>`

```
Jerry P wrote in message <155F4.784$lT3.36321@news.swbell.net>...
>"We're sorry, the page has been removed from our service."
>
>Boy, it didn't take long to knock this spammer down.
>


I may have found a better way.

A couple of days ago, I got a piece of unsolicited marketing material via
e-mail (I do not use the "j" word). It had no name, no phone number, no
contact address and no "remove" link; it asked me to send a form with my
credit card info to an e-mail address at another domain, and referred to a
web site (at yet another domain)  I should click on to get rich or lose
weight or have my way with Tyra Banks or something equally unlikely.

I thought this very naughty to request money without identification; so I
wrote to abuse@domainname.com for the e-mail address, the original mail
address on the message and the web site. I got a 'bot response from all
three. Two of the three followed up with personal notes (very specific, not
form letters). One said they checked and found the sender id was not at
their domain, and sorry they couldn't help. The web site operator
(homestead.com) wrote back that looked over the web site, decided that the
user was in violation of the terms of service, and they shut it down.

So, perhaps a real good way to discourage irresponsible use of these free
ISP' (e.g., yahoo, hotmail, homestead, etc) is to write to the
"abuse@domainname" address when you get unsolicited propositions not
complying with your idea of "reasonable marketing promotions."

My $0.02.

MCM
PS: anyone got Tyra Banks' phone number?
```

Found this thread very interesting.

I deal with spammers by sending something like the following (assuming it is
"replyable". If it isn't I just hit delete):

"Please remove me from your mailing list.

There, I asked you nicely.

The next time I receive unsolicited mail from you, You will receive 20,000
replies an hour, from a number of different sources, 24 hours a day, for a
full week. This will degrade your server and prevent you doing business. See
how you like it...."

I could actually do this, although I wouldn't do so lightly. So far I've
never had to...

Liked the look of www.spamcop.org

Pete.

PS If you're reading this, my ISP has solved his NG problems and I'm back...




Michael Mattias wrote in message ...
>Jerry P wrote in message ...
>>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: I am serious.

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<FaOF4.3747$_D3.67939@news.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<8bvqrl$nav$114@info.sta.net.cn> <155F4.784$lT3.36321@news.swbell.net> <S4cF4.18634$QJ3.2904834@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <kwoF4.1037$lT3.47145@news.swbell.net> <iCtF4.19760$QJ3.3170889@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <8c62l8$ia1$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`

```
Bad move. Here's a test.

Get a HOTMAIL (or YAHOO or EXCITE) free mail account. Do nothing with
it for a month. You will discover it has NO mail sent to it.

Then, on your next piece of SPAM, where you are given the opportunity
to 'remove' yourself, do so with the HOTMAIL account.

Your HOTMAIL cup will runneth over almost immediately as the spammer
now has a known, good, e-mail address.

A trick we use is to send e-mail to spammers with a return address of
'abuse@(whatever)' We have NEVER gotten mail addressed to
'abuse@.....'.

### text centering in COBOL

- **From:** <jacasey@bajor.its.deakin.edu.au>
- **Date:** 2000-04-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jAfF4.9$ie.2127@news.deakin.edu.au>`

```
Hi,

I am doing a COBOL course and just wondering are there any functions to
center text etc?  my target platform will be Acu cobol on Solaris.
```

#### ↳ Re: text centering in COBOL

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 2000-04-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8c6u9i$161$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<jAfF4.9$ie.2127@news.deakin.edu.au>`

```
<jacasey@bajor.its.deakin.edu.au> wrote in message
news:jAfF4.9$ie.2127@news.deakin.edu.au...
> Hi,
>
> I am doing a COBOL course and just wondering are there any functions to
> center text etc?  my target platform will be Acu cobol on Solaris.

With Acucobol you got a function C$JUSTIFY, check it out.

Syntax:
    CALL "C$JUSTIFY" USING DATA-ITEM, JUSTIFY-TYPE

Cheesle
```

## Thread: COBOL Lives — 1 msgs

### COBOL Lives

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-04-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38e61692.79576975@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
If you have seen the latest IEEE software you have seen the COBOL
Lives artwork.  I really like it and I have contacted the artist about
buying a larger version as a print of the original artwork.  He is
working to get a price for such a print, and has had at least one
other inquiry.  If YOU are interested in a print (or two!) of this
very interesting artwork - please let me know via email and I will put
you in touch with the artist as well.  For obvious reasons I don't
want to post his email address here.

You can see a small version of the artwork on a picture of the cover
of IEEE softwware at http://www.cobolreport.com or
http://www.flexus.com.  The tiny pic hardly does it justice, however.
```

<a id="t-19c8818191"></a>
## Thread: 3-tier — 1 msgs

### Re: 3-tier

- **From:** "Westley Lodge Pty Ltd" <wlodge@hotkey.net.au>
- **Date:** 2000-04-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38e679f1@news.iprimus.com.au>`
- **References:** `<v1t5esssoi5cim6v4a7290hgmee69i118j@4ax.com>`

```
We developed an IBM mainframe CICS system in the three tier architecture a
few years ago. This meant the following.

Each transaction is really divided into...
1). The main (screen?) program.
2). A business validation / execution layer
3). All of the data modules.

All of the data was used via linkages set up specifically for each module.

So a customer maintenance transaction would...

LAYER 1.
1a. Receive all of the screen data. This data was captured in a "view"
linkage.
1b. use an "Evaluate" statement to check the actual function to be performed
(ie add, change etc), and call a central control module (a scheduler?) with
the "view" linkage and the function to be performed.
1c. Evaluate the return code. If any errors occured display error.
That  is all the top layer would do.


LAYER 2.
2a. The control module (scheduler?) would then just call the appropriate
validation module. The secret here is to have a validation routine for
"each" item to be validated. If a module is coded that does more than one
validation, then you are really asking for trouble (believe me I know. We've
done this and it cost us undue hardship).
2B. Each of the validation modules performs some kind of validation, and
returns some kind of status whether it was successful or not. If it wasn't,
a status is returned from the validation module to the Control module and
from there to LAYER1.
2C. If all was fine the control module does the commit, or returns any data
collected via another linkage (ie the "collected" linkage back up to LAYER
one.


LAYER 3,
For each of the files used, a module is written to perform all of the
relevant actions.

To fully understand this,  the linkage of the CONTROL system ( in our case
anyway), is a constant redefinition of a 4k buffer into the various linkages
we use. The control module (the scheduler) is called many times throughout
the transaction life.

Generally this approach is OK, but one must stick to it like glue. any
deviation from this could have catastrophic results. We have a particular
validation module which is function specific (ie we validate all of the data
required for RECEIPTS). This module consists of code which is is as complex
as you get. The simpler approach would have been to have validation modules
for each item to be validated. Yes that would mean more programs but hey,
simpler to manage, and maintain.


As a final note, if we need to turn some of our VSAM files into DB2 tables,
all we need to do is rewrite the module in LAYER3. etc etc....

I hope this makes sense.

Regards

Theo

news:v1t5esssoi5cim6v4a7290hgmee69i118j@4ax.com...
> someone said in cobol they seperate thw tiers into file handling,
> screen and business logic. i guess my question is, how do you abstract
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…

#### ↳ Re: Reentrant Cobol from TSO Call - Is it possible?

- **From:** postingid@dissensoftware.com (Binyamin Dissen)
- **Date:** 2000-04-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<u04desosjlnft6170dmapl7skgu74knm6q@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<38E0E14E.9E4377A0@bt.com> <38E203FA.A5C13342@zip.com.au> <38E32770.45454CBD@bt.com> <38E352AA.8E0E78F3@zip.com.au> <38E49C3E.DAE22154@bt.com>`

```
On Fri, 31 Mar 2000 13:38:22 +0100 Jeremy Coast <Jeremy.Coast@bt.com> wrote:

:>I was being lazy. I was asked to write a generic subroutine that could
:>be called by any "COBOL" program. And it can. However, when I originally
:>came to test it I decided I wanted to play with the test data (two
:>records) and some standing lookup data and call the program (via clist)
:>all instream in my JCL, and without having to write another test module
:>to do the call.

:>That's when I found that the subroutine was doing the initialisation
:>every time. So I thought (dangerous that sometimes - thinking I mean) it
:>was obviously something to do with the environment: "What I need is a
:>cobol stub to setup a cobol environment". I then trawled through the
:>TSO/E Programming Services manual to learn how to call a clist from a
:>cobol program. Hit upon the TSO Services Facility and lifted the sample
:>program which calls IKJTSOEV to initialise a TSO/E environment (hang on,
:>didn't I want to create a cobol environment) and then calls TSOLNK
:>(alias for IKJEFTSR) passing %MYCLIST as a parameter to invoke the clist
:>which in turn calls my cobol subroutine. Hmmmn!

:>Well it all worked up to a point. I got my cobol stub to run a test
:>clist, and I was able to interogate the return-code from that. Myclist
:>was able to call the cobol subroutine, and I could tell from the journal
:>that the program ran once ok. However, an abend resulted from the return
:>of control back to the clist, and the whole house of cards collapsed.

:>The abend was S30A ERROR DURING FREEMAIN and
:>Reason-code - 14 - subpool supplied is not for private area storage
:>or private area storage not in indicated subpool.

   [ snipped ]

The issue in this case is if your clist is using the TSO CALL command, the
target program will be invoked under a subtask of the original program and not
part of the current task. Any storage allocated by the subtask in a non-shared
subpool will be automatically freed when the subtask ends. 

The 30A would make sense if the calling program is trying to do some cleanup
of the subroutine.

There is no way to automatically share the subpools - it would have to be at
the point of the attach.

You might be able to avoid the subtask problem by using PLATPGM but that would
require the assistance of your friendly systems programmer.

Another approach is to write an IKJCT44B (refer to TSO/E Customization) and
have is issue a LINK to your subprogram. I don't recall if IKJCT44B has to be
in STEPLIB or if you could put it in your COBOL load library.

Go for it.
```

```
Please Tony and all Newbies,

I care very much for helping, and I do not think that I ever have been
offensive to anyone, but....

I just returned after 5 days in England, and the NG had 573 new entries. I
spent 25 minutes to read all the entries to this subject carefully and I
remember the many of the views in this discussion from a similar thread I
started some months ago.

Bear with me and other old-timers. We are spending hours in the newsgroup
and we do help the newbies. I
just cannot find the time to help all. That is why someone seems to be
ignored.

Another reason could be, that when a simple question has been answered in
the newsgroup, we often see exactly the same question from 2, 3, 4 other
good students within 3 days. I agree that we should help and respect
everyone, but couldn't the students respect us by reading the newsgroup
before asking?

A few questions gives me a feeling that the students didn't even think the
problem over before asking. I might be wrong, but I haven't got the time to
find out. If the questions are specific, we can reply with help and links.

I am sorry for the times I need to say "I cannot enter this thread - it will
take too much time", and I am happy when I see that others had the time.

Take this as a suppementary entry in the discussion. I agree with most of
the other entries - especially the positive ones.

Regards
Ib

in my small, parochial set of experiences, happened.  'You were waiting
for the X Project?  Heh, the Corner Office was all hot on that *last* week
but they cooled, now they're hot on Y... nobody told you?  Hey, that's the
way business goes, y'know.')

professional as a cab-driver.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mainframe Programming Opportunities - Not!

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-04-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3905d422.8861098@news.cox-internet.com>`
- **References:** `<38e2d709$0$63695@news.execpc.com> <38fa610b.6095012@news.gw.total-web.net> <38fb3c1f.12540373@news.cox-internet.com> <3903a5b4.116767762@news.gw.total-web.net>`

```
On Mon, 24 Apr 2000 01:45:12 GMT, pknudsen@gw.total-web.net (Paul
Knudsen) wrote:

>What makes you think Java, etc will "fail to deliver"?  If it wasn't
>any good wouldn't someone have noticed by now?

I think what we are seeing is a splitering of diciplines.  Few have
any idea what the big picture is.  I hear all the time that I should
rewrite such and such an application in JAVA.  Why I ask?  So it can
run slower?  

We use JAVA where I work.  We actually use it as stored procedures in
an Oracle database.  For what it is good at, JAVA is fine.  It's when
you are so myopic that you try to force JAVA onto a process that just
plain isn't suited for it.

I see the same thing here with Databases.  I know SQL, I work with
Oracle databases.  We were doing some queries yesterday that just
would not come together in a timely fashion.  The multiple joins to
different tables, inner and outer were just too slow.  I extracted the
data, did some minor file manipulation to eliminate some of the joins
and we very quickly had our results.  What did I use to manipulate the
data?  A JAVA program?  No -- COBOL.  COBOL excells at data
manipulation.

So I'm not saying that JAVA is bad or that in itself it will fail to
deliver.  (Or XML or any other emerging technology).  But what I do
see is people falling so head over heels in love with the "new" stuff
that they try to use it for EVERYTHING, when it's just not suited for
the task.
```

## Thread: Future of COBOL.... — 79 this month / 81 total

_Topics: [`COBOL's future, legacy, and obsolescence`](topics.md#future)_

### Future of COBOL....

- **From:** firdaus <callfirdaus@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<seetcnsl9178@corp.supernews.com>`

```
hi
I am thinking of studying COBOL(main frame)..can anyone please advise the 
future of COBOL now a days jobwise...I live in uk..is there any job 
prospects particularly in uk (or anywhere)?..Which are the sub-languages I 
have to learn after the COBOL please advise..Which OS to choose..CICS 
etc..please let me know..thankyou.
```

#### ↳ Re: Future of COBOL....

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 2000-04-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8c88mu$7qi$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<seetcnsl9178@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Last week I discussed the subject with some friends in UK (Newbury). Many
larger businesses will have CICS/Cobol/DB2 as a company platform. This will
be combined with a LAN (TCP/IP) with PC's running WinNT. Everywhere is a
gost (JAVA).

My advice is: Be good at Cobol and understand how CICS fits in. Be god at
DB2 (or Oracle) and then take a close look at JAVA. If you can do all that,
I beleive you can afford a house. I saw ordinary houses for sale - imagine £
450,000, tuff.

Good luck
Ib

#### ↳ Re: Future of COBOL....

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-04-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8c8lvp$mho$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<seetcnsl9178@corp.supernews.com>`

```
(The following is controversial and arguable but is based on informed
opinion and a large amount of knowledge and experience. I'm not going to
preface everything with IMHO because what I say is OBVIOUSLY my opinion, and
I see no reason to apologise for it or be humble about it. I can support
every one of the statements below with reasoned argument and will gladly do
so if you want more information...)

Advice to the young....(remembering what happened to Polonius...)

COBOL has as bright a future as any other procedural language, although the
"glory days" (70s and early 80s until PCs became established...) are gone.

It will take you about 2 weeks to learn COBOL (although Thane, another
regular contributor to this NG, has written a book which lets you "fast
Track" it in 24 hours) and about 3 months before you become proficient. The
language is simple and elegant and ideally suited for the purpose for which
it was intended. (Regrettably it is being "enhanced" to the point where much
of the original simplicity is being lost, but don't let that put you off.)

For an investment of 3 months, you can then use it to make a living for the
next 10 to 15 years. After that, procedural languages (not just COBOL) will
be as relevant as Latin is today.

I would strongly recommend that you learn COBOL, enjoy using it, get a
background in computer programming as a basis for the skill set of tomorrow,
and then learn JAVA and HTML/XML as Web Enablement is the direction for the
future and OO (object orientation) is the methodology which will take us
there. (Some people would argue that knowing COBOL is a hindrance to
understanding OO, but I don't ascribe to that. Learning COBOL is a great
introduction to computers in general and besides, its real good fun...)

Fortunately, by the time Commerce stops using COBOL, you will have obtained
the necessary background and skills to continue to make a living whether it
involves actual programming or not.

There are still many companies in the UK who need mainframe COBOL
programmers. If you are prepared to work in London you need never go hungry.
I would recommend getting a couple of years under your belt then going
freelance.

 (I have worked as a freelance  (if you count running a small software
house/consultancy as also being "freelance"...??) in the UK and Europe for
the last 25 years and was unemployed (when I didn't want to be...) for a
total of 6 weeks in that time. This kind of life requires mobility and a
high tolerance on unexpectedness, but it is great fun and, looking back, I
wouldn't change it.)

I notice that elsewhere in this thread you were advised to learn DB2 (SQL,
actually, or "Structured Query Language" for Relational Databases. This is
excellent and VERY SOUND advice. Take it.

Good luck, and remember you can always call on the COBOL community if you
need help.

Pete.

OK Pete don't just tantalize us. So COBOL has a 10-15 year life span
left. In your predictions what replaces it - one of the existing OO
languages or something new ?

I do believe that if we don't get our act together, (make our concerns
known NOW to our COBOL Standards, as Bill keeps plugging) then Pete's
prediction will come about. You can debate here to your heart's content
as to whether internal/external sorts are the way to go, DBs v flat
files, or how you structure a perform loop for a sequential file read,
iteration/recursion (Towers of Hanoi, Ackermann etc......) - these are
all irrelevant to the greater picture of where programming is heading -
to accommodate business needs.

I need hardly add I don't need to worry about Pete's prediction - I'll
predict I will be well and truly a space cadet in 15 years time :-).

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of COBOL....

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-04-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38E8E424.6AB6E33@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<seetcnsl9178@corp.supernews.com> <8c9utc$llc$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8ca4rj$s29$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8cagus$pqh$2@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <2d4G4.16208$0o4.105369@iad-read.news.verio.net> <38E8D443.D5CEA977@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" wrote:
> 
> OK Pete don't just tantalize us. So COBOL has a 10-15 year life span
> left. In your predictions what replaces it - one of the existing OO
> languages or something new ?

COBOL will probably fade away, not being replaced by anything in
particular.  Various tools will nibble at jobs once performed by COBOL
until there is no longer a body of skilled COBOL programmers.  There
will be data managers and experts in a wide variety of tools. 

I remember a COBOL program I wrote which would give a manager a cross
table of data he supplied.  He would write up his input table on a piece
of paper, which was transcribed into coding sheets to be punched up in
cards.  Then at night, it would run and he would get a greenbar
listing.  If he wanted a different view, he changed the parm cards and
reran it the next day. 

Now he would type his figures into a spreadsheet and look at many more
views than I had allowed, and get the result immediately.  Did
Lotus/Excel/Quattro Pro replace COBOL?  I suppose in this case.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of COBOL....

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "ChrisOsborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ekhV38en$GA.304@cpmsnbbsa04>`
- **References:** `<seetcnsl9178@corp.supernews.com> <8c9utc$llc$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8ca4rj$s29$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8cagus$pqh$2@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <2d4G4.16208$0o4.105369@iad-read.news.verio.net> <38E8D443.D5CEA977@home.com> <ulqgv3Zn$GA.361@cpmsnbbsa04>`

```
Anyone should be wary of BIG BANG projects.  There were big project failures
(truly enormous) long before SAP came along.

I liked an article I read in Enterprise Systems Journal which tried to
figure out what was to blame in big project failures.  The final conclusion
on many projects (but not the only one, of course)?  People just have a
tough time handling the bigness behind it.  Although it may seem like other
problems are at hand, the lack of an ability to wrap the mind around such a
huge number of tasks hurts the ability of such projects to succeed.  Of
course, there are many disciplines that can help offset this to a large
degree (and there are occasional heroic managers who can handle it all), but
they aren't used very often in the software industry.

Maybe.  I just finished a SAP project that is running fine.  It took several
temper tantrums and a bit of screaming/jumping up and down, but that is
about standard when faced with amateurs.
Did it on budget and almost on time too.  I would say "on time", because we
were ready on time, but SAP was not ready for us, even though we only got
the contract 10 weeks before due date. We were running as a sub-system
within 12 hours of SAP. Both are now running reasonably well, though the
customer is still using most of our "redundant" reports because the SAP ones
are not ready.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of COBOL....COBOL was a Quick and Dirty

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 2000-04-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38EA51FC.F0302CC8@melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<seetcnsl9178@corp.supernews.com> <8c9utc$llc$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8ca4rj$s29$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8cagus$pqh$2@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <2d4G4.16208$0o4.105369@iad-read.news.verio.net> <38E8D443.D5CEA977@home.com> <ulqgv3Zn$GA.361@cpmsnbbsa04> <38E8F07D.6D554D2@home.com>`

```
According to an article in the latest IEEE Software, the Cobol
language was never meant to last. It was a quick and dirty effort
meant to last a year or two, by the 'short term committee'.
Another group was meant to come with a longer term solution, but
Cobol became entrenched and that never happened.

This is in the article clarifying Grace Hopper's (limited) role
in the creation of the Cobol language. She did have a significant
role in setting the scene for Cobol via her role in Flow-matic
and popularising he idea of the 'compiler' a term she apparently
invented.

What this means, if anything, I am not sure.

Tim Josling
```

In article <8ccp6v$p5d$1@nnrp1.deja.com>, Foodman  <foodman123@aol.com> wrote:
>In article <WhlG4.16244$0o4.106197@iad-read.news.verio.net>,
>docdwarf@clark.net () wrote:

Oh well... this is why I try to keep in mind Wittgenstein's statement of
'I cannot know what you mean, only what you say.'

>What I meant was I hadn't learned
>any new things like java or horrible stuff like that. I was talking
>about programming rather than languages. . .

Eh?  Back to the Realm of Confusion for me (good thing I'm so comfortable
there!); how does one separate 'programming' from 'language(s)'?  This
seems, to me, to be similar to saying 'I was talking about composition
rather than English'... if nothing else (for most Americans) discussion
about composition *happens* in English.

Have puzzlement!

DD

Hi:

Quite an amusing fellow, aren't you?

I do think that you can separate programming from coding.
Remember those plastic things with funny shapes cut-out from
the olden days? You could use those to make diagrams to solve
a problem. That was programming. Then you coded it, that was coding.

Still confused? Feel free to further your enlightenment this way<g>

Thanks

TOny Dilworth

the former then I'd like to know where one might find a
flowchart-compiler.

argument?  Naaaahhhh, that's the easy way out.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of COBOL....

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-04-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8cf185$1at$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<seetcnsl9178@corp.supernews.com> <8c8lvp$mho$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <8c9utc$llc$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8cagur$pqh$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <8ccjfg$iv6$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

Foodman wrote in message <8ccjfg$iv6$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>In article <8cagur$pqh$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>,
>"Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>achieved in 3 months.


Then we'll agree to differ on that one. Anyone working in a COBOL shop who
has had a formal COBOL course, has the availability of experienced
programmers to call on, and  is not producing working programs after 3
months, needs help or should be fired.

"Proficient " in this context does NOT mean "expert programmer". It means
what it says, look up the word.

>As long as those 'other experienced programmers' know what day it is.


Jeez! And I thought I was cynical!!!

If they don't know what day it is they shouldn't be working there.

(Fortunately, in my experience, most of them DO know not only what day it
is, but the exact minute and second   (function CURRENT_DATE) ...especially
when the pubs are opening...)


>> Of course it also depends on your personal enthusiasm and how bright
>you are...
>
>Rather than 'brightness', I think it has more to do with aptitude.


So how many dim people do you know with an aptitude for computer
programming? Enthusiasm counts for nothing? I noticed you didn't comment on
it.


>>
>> What's your point, Tony?

>My point is that knowing a language does not make you a programmer.


THAT's not a point! That's axiomatic and I never suggested otherwise. Are
you just here for an argument ?(That's a non-variant parameter <grin>)

Knowing a language DOES make you able to write code in that laguage. If that
code works then you are proficient in the language. When you are consistenly
proficient you may call yourself a programmer. It does not imply that you
know EVERYTHING about programming; just that you use a particular language
to make computers do things.

>>
>> (Did it take you 2 years...<grin>)
>>
>Actually, I am still learning after 30 years. Are you?


I was asking about "proficiency" not "programming". You took it out of
context.

Wounded to the quick, I respond......

Here's my resume...
2 years in Assembler then sent on the COBOL course...
after 2 weeks: Understood COBOL syntax.
after 2 months: Was writing simple commercial programs which tended to work
most of the time...
after 2 years: wrote programs which required little maintenance and worked
consistently in production.
after 10 years: Taught COBOL to others.
after 20 years: Taught programming to others.
after 25 years:  Learned C and VB. Wrote COBOL for fun (and profit).
after 30 years: Re-evaluated many of the things we are still doing and
decided it could be better. Still writing COBOL for fun (and profit).
after 35 years: Learned JAVA. Give advice to GOD on COBOL...(He's not doing
very well; all this OO vs Structured debate has confused him, so He's
winding down the Universe and working on a less ambitious project...in
JOVIAL....). Still writing COBOL for fun and profit. (Hey, make hay while
the sun shines...15 years and counting...)

Since 1965 I have managed to maintain my enthusiasm for and delight in
COBOL. Am I still learning programming? I guess it depends on how you define
"programming". I STILL try to make every program I write (in whatever
language) better than the last one I wrote.

Pete.
```

here's my resume....
assembler class
got a job
After one month learned CICS
after two months assigned Cobol work
after two 1/2 months understood cobol syntax.
after 4 months went to Cobol class 
after six months learned DB2
after fifteen months learned REXX
after two years still hate cobol, love assembler!

>Since 1965 I have managed to maintain my enthusiasm for and delight in

One final question.
What was the status of assembler 15 years ago? On the way out?
Well I am currently writing several NEW programs in assembler (In a
very large shop with over 4000 programmers around the world). If cobol
goes the way assembler was supossed to go all the current cobol
programers should have nothing to worry about.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of COBOL....

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-04-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8cn24b$jk8$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<seetcnsl9178@corp.supernews.com> <8c8lvp$mho$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <8c9utc$llc$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8cagur$pqh$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <8ccjfg$iv6$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8cf185$1at$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <38eebf0b.12785890@news.radiks.net>`

```

Ed Pluta wrote in message <38eebf0b.12785890@news.radiks.net>...
>On Wed, 5 Apr 2000 21:27:58 +1200, "Peter E. C. Dashwood"
><dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>>
That is a VERY good point you've raised, Ed.

See, although Assembler is another procedural language (and they're ALL
entering Gotterdammerung), it is the closest thing we have to machine code.

You could make an argument that Assembler is ALL we ever needed and
everything else was intended for people who don't really understand
computers...

Your question has made me think about where I think things are going and I
believe the ONLY procedural language which will still be around in 50 years
is Assembler (although it will be used for handling "nervous breakdowns" in
the intelligent software, not for developing systems (the users will be
doing that).

Despite the fact that I am predicting expert computers which will monitor
and "help" other computers, there will be occasions where human intervention
is required, and Assembler will be the only way to see what's actually going
on. (The machines will have overridden basic "classes" in firmware...the
only way to see what they did is to look at the machine code. Assembler maps
would facilitate this...).
Until there is a radical change in computer hardware, we will probably
always need Assembler. (But NOT for Application Development...)

Pete.



 I can see major changes in software but
```

Dear me,
Hopefully I can get my pension before that happens.

On the other hand - ordinary people are doing gardening, even I, when I
cannot avoid it. I am happy doing gardening, you know, dig a hole, put in
tree, if dry, then give water - end. I couldn't care less about latin names
and the origin of the tree. It grows or die where I put it. The most
beautiful trees are where I can enjoy them all summer when I sit in the
shadow with a cold beer. Sometimes they live and sometimes they don't, and I
don't care about why.

Is that what happens with the IT industry. I can see it coming. Excel with
macros (Remember to put the cursor in A1 before you start the macro),
Aunty's telephone number in Access (haven't yet found out how to sort a
table). Yes, you have the right to make your own solutions - to
auto-generate if you like. As long as I spend lots of money on pretty, but
dying trees, you can spend it on pretty, but dying tools - we all need a
hobby.

At some point, someone puts in quality to the discussion. At that time I
will do the gardeners programs, and he will plant pretty trees in my
garden - and his trees won't die.

Geee, I should try to find my roots in Nepal.

Regards
Ib

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **Date:** 2000-04-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8cs70k$kcn$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<seetcnsl9178@corp.supernews.com> <8c8lvp$mho$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <8c9utc$llc$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8cagur$pqh$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <8ccjfg$iv6$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8cf185$1at$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <38eebf0b.12785890@news.radiks.net> <8cn24b$jk8$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <8cndks$dva$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

Foodman wrote in message <8cndks$dva$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>In article <8cn24b$jk8$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>,
>  "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>
>I wonder if you would mind, if you have the time, elaborating
>on those 'nervous breakdowns' and 'intelligent software' and
>how assembler would be used to fix them?
>

Nope. I don't have the time, and I've already done it at length in this
forum last year. I sending you a transcript of that stuff but do not intend
to republish it here.

Assembler WON'T be used to fix them (that isn't what I said.) It COULD be
used as a diagnostic aid to help a human understand what exactly was wrong
and the nature of the class overrides taken by the concept engine.

(It will be impossible to manually debug the concept engine because of the
nature of its heuristic software which will have modified itself millions of
times a second to arrive at a result... However, an Assembler map would show
what the final result was. )

Repair would be effected NOT by using Assembler (or any other Procedural
language) but by a human and a "specialist" computer interacting with the
"sick" machine.


>
>>not for developing systems (the users will be
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I don't know what you'll be doing for a living.

I'll be retired or semi retired, writing short stories based on a full and
colourful life, writing articles on the history of computing and "the lost
art of computer programming", and probably writing the odd COBOL program
just for the fun of it... If not, I'll be providing advice and consultancy
to industry, more or less the way I do now. Believe it or not, there are
other things in life besides COBOL.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of COBOL....

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38EF7F37.DD4A5EFE@home.com>`
- **References:** `<seetcnsl9178@corp.supernews.com> <8c8lvp$mho$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <8c9utc$llc$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8cagur$pqh$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <8ccjfg$iv6$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8cf185$1at$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <38eebf0b.12785890@news.radiks.net> <8cn24b$jk8$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`

```


"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:
> 
> That is a VERY good point you've raised, Ed.
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

And when I asked you the direct question "What language replaces COBOL"
you said "I'm not going to rise to the bait" :-)

Now we are right back to square one. We could use machine code if you
want to go boggle eyed looking at ones and noughts - but then we got
shorthand 'Assembler'. So now take it a stage further, without any
intermediary jazz make COBOL translate directly to Assembler - wasn't
that the original intent when COBOL standards started. 

Where did COBOL fail - it didn't keep up (speed wise) with developer
requirements, hence the introduction of what I call 'the cryptic 
languages' to fulfill this need. An extremely clear and concise language 
I believe COBOL can fulfill this role - just so long as it keeps
abreast.

I see somebody has already picked up on your 'expert computers' -
harking back to 'Who cares ?" and Dulcinea. As you know I am with you
most of the way on this as regards expert systems. But that bit where
you suggest an 'intervention is/maybe required' in systems design - then
I think COBOL translated to Assembler neatly fits that role.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of COBOL....

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-04-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8cs9ce$kfk$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<seetcnsl9178@corp.supernews.com> <8c8lvp$mho$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <8c9utc$llc$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8cagur$pqh$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <8ccjfg$iv6$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8cf185$1at$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <38eebf0b.12785890@news.radiks.net> <8cn24b$jk8$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <38EF7F37.DD4A5EFE@home.com>`

```

James J. Gavan wrote in message <38EF7F37.DD4A5EFE@home.com>...
>
>
>"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:
>>

>And when I asked you the direct question "What language replaces COBOL"
>you said "I'm not going to rise to the bait" :-)
>
Jimmy it is really awesome how you manage to totally spin what I say, on the
basis of your own predilection.

I take great care with my use of words. Most of the time I mean exactly what
I say, no more, no less. There are NO hidden agendas, or subtle meanings, or
"reading between the lines".

I am NOT saying Assembler will replace COBOL.. Computers at the time we are
talking about will NOT need programming by means of ANY language other than
Natural ones (English, Spanish, etc.) If you could clear your mind long
enough to read what I actually wrote, you will NOT find ANY reference to
Assembler being used for programming. (I see it as a possible diagnostic
tool because it is less tedious than machine language; I do NOT see it being
used to program. It would be pointless in the environment I am talking
about.)

Like so many people in this forum you are totally blinded by the way things
are NOW and mistakenly (I hope) believe that they will ALWAYS be that way.
Typical misconceptions:

1. Users can't do anything without us professionals.

2. I've invested a heap of time and money in getting a skill like computer
programming so nobody better tell me it may have a limited life.

3. It's worked this way for 50 years, why change?

I'd love to go into detail blowing away each of the above myths, but I
really don't have the time. However I shall indulge myself by the following
quickies in response to each point:

1. Users are gathering strength every day. Computing is no longer in the
hands of Corporates who could afford to maintain expensive artisans to keep
the computers running. The man in the street wants a computer that can
understand his needs and that he can interact with painlessly in natural
language.

There will be 2 billion users by the year 2015. They are NOT going to become
"computer programmers" and they are not going to tolerate being reliant on
an arrogant elite technocracy to get the things they want done with
computers. They will demand smarter more responsive systems, and history has
shown that trying to buck the market is certain disaster. A 2 billion
marketplace will not go away or rollover until programmers and software are
available. The smart money is on the software developers who will give them
what they want; that will be the end of "computer programming" as we
understand it today. I intend to be part of this as I find the alternative
(NOT being partof it...) completely unacceptable.

2. This is the same argument as "I invested a heap of money in buying
Betamax. It was the best system, right?"

The market thought otherwise (I'm not going to argue whether they were
right) and today the Betamax standard is a distant memory.

Throughout history people have invested time and money in what they thought
was a secure future. (What about the Nottinghamshire apprentices who spent
10 years learning how to make lace, only to have the Industrial Revolution
provide a machine (after everyone said it would be impossible to build a
machine which could handle the complexity of lace-making) which could do it
faster and faultlessly.

There is no "secure future". Not in working for a Corporation, not in going
contract, not in ANYTHING. (Well, maybe out of deference to Ben Franklin,
there is a secure future for Morticians and Tax Collectors...)

For the rest of us the ONLY security we have is in the 2 pounds of grey
slush that sits inside our craniums. It took 3 million years to build it; it
is adequately suited to purpose and if you use it, you will certainly
survive. Stop bleating about bad luck, no jobs, the unfairness of
life...deal with it. Move on. Use your brain.

3. This is the typical "ostrichasaurus" approach. If you stick your head in
the sand you can't see the light in the sky which heralds the approaching
comet. Also, it avoids any need to use one's brain (see 2 above).

>
>Now we are right back to square one. We could use machine code if you
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>I think COBOL translated to Assembler neatly fits that role.

OK, I give up...<grin> Proof again (if proof were needed) that people WILL
believe what they WANT to believe...I guess it's easier for many people than
THINKING...


(BTW Jimmy, I'm waiting on some private mail from you in response to my
last, the OO stuff...)

Regards,

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of COBOL....

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-04-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38F1E384.62ED5479@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<seetcnsl9178@corp.supernews.com> <8c8lvp$mho$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <8c9utc$llc$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8cagur$pqh$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <8ccjfg$iv6$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8cf185$1at$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <38eebf0b.12785890@news.radiks.net> <8cn24b$jk8$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <38EF7F37.DD4A5EFE@home.com> <8cs9ce$kfk$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <8csff7$m9p$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Foodman wrote:
> 
> What do you think of the Microsoft products like Office, etc?
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> his system?

He doesn't have his system.

Which is the point.  If a system is to be "owned" by one person, he
needs to know that system.  He's responsible for maintenance, and when
he leaves the system can die.  If a system is owned by a department -
that department needs to decide how it is to be maintained and used.  If
it is owned by the company, the company needs to control it.  Find out
who your customer is (company, department, individual) - and make sure
the ownership/maintenance responsibilities are understood by all
parties.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of COBOL....

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ct1e8$9uh$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<seetcnsl9178@corp.supernews.com> <8c8lvp$mho$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <8c9utc$llc$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8cagur$pqh$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <8ccjfg$iv6$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8cf185$1at$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <38eebf0b.12785890@news.radiks.net> <8cn24b$jk8$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <38EF7F37.DD4A5EFE@home.com> <8cs9ce$kfk$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <8csff7$m9p$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38F1E384.62ED5479@cusys.edu>`

```

> >
> > What happens when the user quits in the middle of developing
> > his system?
>
> He doesn't have his system.

Hi:

Or, to be more precise, his successor doesn't have a system.

Some things need to be under corporate control. You can't
have the users going out and buying their own furniture,
etc. Individual systems have to be designed with the
entire business in mind and every system is going to
interface with some other system, somewhere, so, somebody has
to be in charge of the whole shebang - us - not the user.

Thanks

Tony Dilworth



Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

news:8ct1e8$9uh$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
>
> > >
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…

It depends on how you define system.
If a user wants to use THEIR COMPUTER to do processing within THEIR
DEPARTMENT, it is their business.
An example might be work-load distributition (project-management),
where who does the work has no impact on Corporate Application
systems.

Some of us use our computers to automate portions of our jobs, should
we refrain from doing so?
If not, why should we prevent users from doing the same?

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of COBOL....

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38F23C86.FC8211DC@home.com>`
- **References:** `<seetcnsl9178@corp.supernews.com> <8c8lvp$mho$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <8c9utc$llc$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8cagur$pqh$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <8ccjfg$iv6$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8cf185$1at$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <38eebf0b.12785890@news.radiks.net> <8cn24b$jk8$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <38EF7F37.DD4A5EFE@home.com> <8cs9ce$kfk$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <8csff7$m9p$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38F1E384.62ED5479@cusys.edu> <8ct1e8$9uh$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <OutHUJyo$GA.342@cpmsnbbsa04>`

```


DennisHarley wrote:
> 
> Foodman <foodman123@aol.com> wrote in message
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> If not, why should we prevent users from doing the same?

Dennis,

This is where I diverge from Pete Dashwood's concept. I agree with him
entirely about automating the whole process leaning towards an eventual
AI approach - but I caution against lack of 'Systems' intervention in a
large corporation as you imply. It isn't a question of us protecting our
turf. EDP (or the now fancy 'IT') that's our job with the intent of
providing data information support.

Let me give some examples, first two go back to 60's but put them in
perspective with users having access to modern EDP query tools :-

1. Marketing complained that with 3,600 products the sales analysis was
sub-divided into 2,000 product groups - much too much detail. Give them
a spreadsheet package or whatever, and they could sit down to get the
analysis they wanted. The real answer that we gave them -  for each
product we've given you a product group and a sub product group - reduce
those numbers and you get less paper. Took our advice - now they had 500
product groups.

2. Can we get a print-out which will give us this ? What are you after ?
Well we want this and this and this...... Answer : You do realize that
all that information is available from the following print-outs, loading
sheets from depots which we have here at head office .....?

3. DIY Retail chain in Calgary. Accountant designed system before I
arrived as DPM. Ran three months of the new G/L. After third month
queried with him why we had to come to a grinding halt - produce him a
report; he then did a journal entry which we entered and then finished
off the month. Guess what he was doing. We had something like 100
countrified retail outlets. The interim report we produced was their
total expenses for the month. From that he took 10% of Head Office
expenses and pro-rated per branch based on their expenses. He was truly
surprised when I told him a slight mod to the existing system could
automatically generate that journal entry.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of COBOL....

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "DennisHarley" <LegacyBlue@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ePn8M3zo$GA.207@cpmsnbbsa03>`
- **References:** `<seetcnsl9178@corp.supernews.com> <8c8lvp$mho$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <8c9utc$llc$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8cagur$pqh$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <8ccjfg$iv6$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8cf185$1at$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <38eebf0b.12785890@news.radiks.net> <8cn24b$jk8$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <38EF7F37.DD4A5EFE@home.com> <8cs9ce$kfk$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <8csff7$m9p$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38F1E384.62ED5479@cusys.edu> <8ct1e8$9uh$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <OutHUJyo$GA.342@cpmsnbbsa04> <38F23C86.FC8211DC@home.com>`

```
I have no problem with your concern about Corporate Systems, and our
responsibility to ensure that proper methods are used. I also agree
that we should present alternative solutions, when the user presents a
solution which is impractical.

I have my own war stories; a user who screwed up a financial
application because he had the ability to "correct" individual fields
in a system, or the Payroll group which prepared input to update the
system based on a report of update activity already applied through an
automated method.

My point is that users (managers) have a right, and responsibility to
administer their own departments however they see fit. This does not
extend to the point where they can comission changes to Application
Systems without approval and oversight from MIS.

If they use their computer to manage their department, and there is no
direct impact on Application Systems, we should help them where we
can.

I recently worked on a financial system where the user was given Excel
Spreadsheets, or Access Databases with customer billing information.
They worked on new billing rates. When they were done, we were
provided with new billing rate information. The updated billing rates
were not applied directly to the system. We applied existing business
rules to the data they provided, and rejected some of the proposed
updates. Only after the user and IT were in accord, were the updates
applied.

The user was happy. They did not have to work through a programmer who
might or might not give them what they wanted (which was the case in
the past).
They were computer literate. They used the facilities available to
them in an effective manner.

The programmer who gave them the business rules at the beginning of
the project did a poor job.
The rules given were either incomplete, or just plain wrong. The user
wasn't the one who screwed up.

I am not saying that these users were capable of designing, and
implementing an Application system. They are capable of using the
computer as a tool.

James J. Gavan <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
news:38F23C86.FC8211DC@home.com...

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of COBOL....

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-04-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8cvcgq$pgm$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<seetcnsl9178@corp.supernews.com> <8c8lvp$mho$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <8c9utc$llc$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8cagur$pqh$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <8ccjfg$iv6$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8cf185$1at$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <38eebf0b.12785890@news.radiks.net> <8cn24b$jk8$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <38EF7F37.DD4A5EFE@home.com> <8cs9ce$kfk$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <8csff7$m9p$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38F1E384.62ED5479@cusys.edu> <8ct1e8$9uh$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <OutHUJyo$GA.342@cpmsnbbsa04> <38F23C86.FC8211DC@home.com>`

```
Jimmy,

Responses below...
James J. Gavan wrote in message <38F23C86.FC8211DC@home.com>...
>
>
>DennisHarley wrote:
>>
>> Foodman <foodman123@aol.com> wrote in message

>
>This is where I diverge from Pete Dashwood's concept. I agree with him
>entirely about automating the whole process leaning towards an eventual
>AI approach - but I caution against lack of 'Systems' intervention in a
>large corporation as you imply.

I don't think Corporate users actually using their computers to help them do
their jobs (which is what Dennis pointed out) is at odds with the concept of
"systems" in a Corporation.

It isn't the 60's any more, Jimmy. Corporate data these days can and should
be shared via the LAN and the Company Intranet. There is no longer a
requirement for a centralised "big iron" processor, controlled by the
Priests of Programming, as the only way to handle corporate data and
corporate systems.

Distributed computing is showing more and more that it is not only feasible
but actually very successful to allow departments to maintain and control
their own data, sharing it with other departments as required. It is simply
a different model of corporate computing. Having already stirred up somewhat
of a hornet's nest here, I don't want to take it all off topic into the
world of "Distributed versus Centralized" processing (although this is
certainly a good topic for another thread...)

The fact is that the marketplace is already voting with its feet...try
getting people in corporations to give up their PCs and go back to 3270s
(even with colour screens [there's posh!]) and I don't think you'll get many
takers.

Dennis is right when he says that people in departments can use their PCs to
assist in their normal work (working offline using tools to present data in
an effective, often graphical, format). The data they are presenting can
just as well be on a local database as on a corporate mainframe (in fact it
is becoming more and more irrelevant WHERE the data is located (even if it
IS on a mainframe)) as long as it can be accessed by the people with the
appropriate authority. The only reason you need a "system" coordinator in
this scenario is to resolve disputes about who owns what and to prevent
unnecessary duplication of the corporate data resource. (A good database
administration group can carry out this function easily and effectively.)

Foodman says they'll be buying their own furniture next... I would have no
problem with that. My experience is that a happy and comfortable workforce
is a productive one. I certainly don't object to them using their own
computers...


It isn't a question of us protecting our
>turf. EDP (or the now fancy 'IT') that's our job with the intent of
>providing data information support.

I believe the point here is that users are becoming empowered to provide
their own data/ information support... What used to be "our domain" is
becoming less and less so. Why? Because it's cheaper and arguably more
effective to let users do it.
>
>Let me give some examples, first two go back to 60's but put them in
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>product groups.

OK. But why couldn't they simply call up the specific group/s they wanted to
see on a screen? Then you would've reduced the paper to zero. Too many
products in each group to allow manageable screen display? OK, let 'em drill
down to summary information and detail on just the specific products they
needed.

At the time you're speaking of, this would've been a "cutting edge" (dare I
say, "science fiction"...<grin>) solution. Today this could be achieved with
little or no programming using the corporate LAN or Intranet and ORACLE or
even ACCESS. (And more and more corporate users are becoming capable of
doing it... Just wait til the current crop of sixth formers hit the
streets... These are kids who are growing up with office software taken as
read and computer literacy as fundamental as being able to skateboard or
juggle...)
>

>2. Can we get a print-out which will give us this ? What are you after ?
>Well we want this and this and this...... Answer : You do realize that
>all that information is available from the following print-outs, loading
>sheets from depots which we have here at head office .....?
>
It's a good example from the time...I guess anyone who was around then
remembers stacks of lineflow being distributed to branches (Where it usually
sat in a corner until it was replaced by next month's lot... I remember we
used to sell all the old reports for scrap at the end of the year to a paper
re-cycling company (all the old punch cards also...) and the proceeds went
to the Xmas Party. Man! we had some parties....but I digress...))


>3. DIY Retail chain in Calgary. Accountant designed system before I
>arrived as DPM. Ran three months of the new G/L. After third month
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
But that's not an argument for centralised system support, Jimmy.  Today he
would do it himself with Excel (after deriving raw data from the G/L
package, IF the G/L package couldn't do it for him...)

Summing up, Your Honour, the Defense contends that Jimmy's case is not
proven...

(Although we can agree that centralised processing is still a valid model,
it is not the ONLY model, and time will tell how much we are prepared to let
user departments be responsible for their own data...)

Regards,

Pete.
```

Foodman wrote:
> 
> > It depends on how you define system.
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> Tony Dilworth

Let me go to an extreme.  The machine shop uses a specialized CAD/CAM
program which is attached to its equipment.  That is nothing to do with
IS.  From here, we can come up with a spectrum of applications, each
having a bit more IS applicability.  

HR is putting its Word document on the Web telling us their harassment
policy (their against it).  Is the intranet IS business?  How about if
they posted it on the extranet or even the internet?

We shouldn't be setting this policy, we should simply be defining the
consequences to our CUSTOMERS setting their policies.
```

```
I noticed that DennisHarley as LegacyBlue@email.msn.com said...
> 
> It depends on how you define system.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> If not, why should we prevent users from doing the same?

Agreed.  I have no objection to users developing their own applications 
for their own isolated systems.  I have returned to one large company 
where, when I left, there was the usual centralised IT department and, 
when I returned, each department had responsibility for its own systems.

Unfortunately, there is now no central IT area to take responsibility 
for the core business systems and the integrity of the entire operation 
was creaking at the seams with no coordination between IT sections other 
than an occasionally meeting in an exotic location.

Some areas still integrate with the old core systems, mainly because the 
old core systems work, while others have extracted "their" data into 
local systems and report back into the core systems.  It's almost like 
nobody is too bothered about the company that lets them go their own way 
like this.  That's one contract that I was happy to finish with.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of COBOL....

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38FA7DAA.F7945269@home.com>`
- **References:** `<seetcnsl9178@corp.supernews.com> <8c8lvp$mho$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <8c9utc$llc$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8cagur$pqh$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <8ccjfg$iv6$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8cf185$1at$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <38eebf0b.12785890@news.radiks.net> <8cn24b$jk8$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <38EF7F37.DD4A5EFE@home.com> <8cs9ce$kfk$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <8csff7$m9p$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38F1E384.62ED5479@cusys.edu> <8ct1e8$9uh$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <OutHUJyo$GA.342@cpmsnbbsa04> <MPG.13643897404024239896f3@news.freedombird.net>`

```


Charles F Hankel wrote:
> 
> Agreed.  I have no objection to users developing their own applications
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> like this.  That's one contract that I was happy to finish with.

While I don't disagree with individual users having tools to help them
do their own thing - the case that you cite illustrates all too well how
a corporation, whether small or very large can get completely off-track
if there isn't that co-ordination of IT access.

Consider - would the Purchasing/Office Services Department be happy if
every user did their own thing and went and bought their particular
heart's desire from a local office furniture store ?

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of COBOL....

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** Charles F Hankel <noos@hankel.freedombird.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.136abef8dc8376c6989710@news.freedombird.net>`
- **References:** `<seetcnsl9178@corp.supernews.com> <8c8lvp$mho$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <8c9utc$llc$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8cagur$pqh$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <8ccjfg$iv6$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8cf185$1at$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <38eebf0b.12785890@news.radiks.net> <8cn24b$jk8$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <38EF7F37.DD4A5EFE@home.com> <8cs9ce$kfk$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <8csff7$m9p$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38F1E384.62ED5479@cusys.edu> <8ct1e8$9uh$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <OutHUJyo$GA.342@cpmsnbbsa04> <MPG.13643897404024239896f3@news.freedombird.net> <38FA7DAA.F7945269@home.com>`

```
I noticed that James J. Gavan as jjgavan@home.com said...
> 
> Charles F Hankel wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> heart's desire from a local office furniture store ?

The root cause of the situation was that the user departments had always 
paid for their computer usage, systems development and maintenance work.  
At some time, the Department Of Terribly Bright Ideas suggested that, as 
the users were paying, maybe they should also have control over what 
they bought for computing services and equipment.

As a result, the IT department was split up into teams that were not 
only allocated to but were also paid by the user departments, so each 
user department got its own IT section.  It looked like a fairly mammoth 
task of politics and diplomacy to keep the core systems together as much 
as they had.  I still think it was one of the best shops that I had ever 
worked in before I left in the 1970s and it was mildly saddening to see 
what they had become.  Still, as they say in the Hitch Hikers Guide to 
the Galaxy, it's an SEP (Someone Else's Problem).
```

right now, the only thing keeping cobol afloat, as i see it, is its
built in database compatibility and the fact the web isn't very well
documented. however, cobol could catch up, *if* the compilers were
free for the lesser versions (i happen to know alot of other languages
offer free compilers, such as c, basic, or html)

```

"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:
>  
> 3. It's worked this way for 50 years, why change?
>
This is what really throws a spanner in the works.
50 YEARS!
If programming had evolved at the same rate as the hardware we use,
it would be all over red rover by now.

The fact that this hasn't happened suggests that Programming and system
design are in fact several orders of magnitude harder than other tasks.

In fact, I place it up there with Artificial Intelligence of the order of
Man. Not necessarily un-do-able, but as yet WE don't know how to do it.

Come back in another century. When we have a good working knowledge of AI,
then I think we will be starting down the road to self programming computers.

In the mean time, I'll just keep on programming and raking in the money.
Then I'll buy stocks in the AI companies that take over my job.
```

It has. Back then we had almost no memory for our programs, and we made

Now, we have large amount of memory, and our programs takes all that and
fill the swap too.
Back then, everyone learned to read letters.
Now we have to show pictures to make them understand.

And then you go away with Enterprise. AI is the solution to everything, I
beleive. Did you know that a professor was dismissed in 1971 for saying: 'If
we want to create AI, we need to understand the prototype, the human brain,
and still we have so much to learn'. Perhaps he was dismissed because other
professors from that university wouldn't give interviews about AI if he was
there. BTW the university was supported heavily by Sperry-Rand, and in 1970
they announced that AI would be there within 5 years.

In 1980 they told us that we would see this brand new AI within 5 years.

In 1990 they..... yes, you've guessed it.

But of course, buy Stocks. They will probably keep going up until the time
where you and your dreams will retire.
