I noticed that Peter E. C. Dashwood as dashwood@enternet.co.nz said...
> 
> Your question has made me think about where I think things are going and I
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> doing that).

As someone who at the crustier end of the age range here, I don't think 
that I'm alone in recalling the various products that have come to 
market proclaiming the end of the programmer.  While some of these 
claims can be partially substantiated to one degree or another, the 
question that remains in my mind is that software, even the software 
that does away with programmers, still needs programming by someone.

You opt for Assembler as the stayer, and Jimmy advocates a high-level 
language that acts as a man-machinecode interface.

I daresay that I will be pushing up daisies before this time comes, 
waiting for a fourth millennium archaeologist to reconstruct my personal 
history in context with the then knowledge of life at the beginning of 
the third millennium and trying to work out what my Psion 5 is for.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of COBOL....

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-04-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8csdt2$klr$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<seetcnsl9178@corp.supernews.com> <8c8lvp$mho$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <8c9utc$llc$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8cagur$pqh$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <8ccjfg$iv6$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8cf185$1at$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <38eebf0b.12785890@news.radiks.net> <8cn24b$jk8$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <MPG.135a8c25ff0d99a19896ce@news.freedombird.net>`

```
Hi Charles!

My responses interspersed below...

(BTW I agree 100% with your comments prior to the bit about "software still
needs programming by someone"...)

Charles F Hankel wrote in message ...
>I noticed that Peter E. C. Dashwood as dashwood@enternet.co.nz said...
>>
>> Your question has made me think about where I think things are going and
I
>> believe the ONLY procedural language which will still be around in 50
years
>> is Assembler (although it will be used for handling "nervous breakdowns"
in
>> the intelligent software, not for developing systems (the users will be
>> doing that).
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>market proclaiming the end of the programmer.
Yep. Who could forget "The Last One" (superseded 2 years later by "The Next
One")

And then there was the JSP phase with Dyjkstra as God and Michael Jackson
(NOT he of the curious complexion and squeaky voice) as his Prophet...

Many Prophets have come along, blown their horns and led us all into the
Wilderness... After a year or two we came back and did it in COBOL because
it was cheaper and relatively easy to get people who could write it.

Those days are over. The Business environment has moved on, the requirements
are different, and the skills need to be more diverse. The modern office is
dealing not just with batch processing, but the Internet /Extranet/
Intranet/, the Telecommunications, the Multimedia, Client/Server, integrated
data and communications and all the technology which the new age promises.
Many of these things are a far cry from what COBOL was originally intended
for.

The biggest single change has been the growth since 1983 in the use of PCs.
Many mainframe programmers wrote them off as "toy computers" in those days
(I wonder what those guys are doing now...). This growth gave us a market
which forced the development of "Office" software... Spreadsheets,
Databases, Word Processors, Presentation and DTP software...no mainframe or
mainframe COBOL required.

Why? Because "Users" (those people who (according to some in ths NG) can't
live without us professionals to tell them what they want...) demanded it,
and there was money to be made in giving it to them. Of course, once they
got a taste for it there was no stopping them...next thing you know,
Client/Server. Users share data across the Intranet and/or LAN.

A desperate rearguard action was fought by the beseiged mainframers so that
many mainframes remain as high powered, very secure, corporate servers, but
this will have a limited life. New generations of systems which can fit
inside an orange box and have maybe 100 times the power of the mainframes of
the 80s are just around the corner.

In the meantime, we still continue to manage computer software development
projects in the same tried and proven way we always have...the Waterfall.
And we still tolerate delivering systems late, over budget, and of little
real use to the business because they took so long to develop that by the
time we delivered them, the requirement had changed drastically.

For how much longer? There was a time when Users had no choice; if they
wanted a computer system the ONLY people who could give it to them were IT.
Those days are going...I give it 15 years at the outside. In the meantime,
more and more companies are embracing RAD type methodology for development.
This means User involvement throughout. Not as a nuisance and interference
which distracts us from programming, but as a real and valuable input to the
development process. There is far too much in this to go into here but
believe me, when it is implemented correctly, this technique gives results.
Having managed projects both ways over many years in different size
organizations, I can tell you I am sold on RAD.

The time is coming when computers REALLY won't need programming at all. (By
"programming" I mean in the sense of the sequential Von Neumann model we use
today, where the computer is given a detailed series of instructions as to
how to solve a problem.

Instead, there will be a completely different model which will involve
INTERACTION and ITERATION, in Natural Language, with a smart operating
system to achieve problem solution.

It will happen by the evolution of software in quantum leaps from where we
are today to where we MUST BE tomorrow. It isn't Science Fiction, it can be
extrapolated logically from today's cutting edge technology with a small
injection of vision and imagination.

I can see how it can be achieved, and if I can, then you can bet there are
many who can. History will show that Computer Programming was a phenomenon
of the latter part of the 20th Century.


>While some of these
>claims can be partially substantiated to one degree or another, the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>language that acts as a man-machinecode interface.


NO! I DON'T! That was the last thing I intended when I made that post. The
whole thing has been taken out of contect and placed back in TODAY.

At the time I was talking about, computers will not be programmed using
Assembler or any other Procedural language.

The thought occurred to me that Assembler would be the only remnant of
today's technology which still might have relevance, but NOT for writing
programs with. And that relevance will only last as long as the underlying
hardware architecture continues to be the way it is now (I give that 50
years...)

>
>I daresay that I will be pushing up daisies before this time comes,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I too have a PSION. It's a 2... (hangs head in shame...) I use it to keep me
amused on long flights where I program it in OPL. It is probably the most
function laden Psion 2 on the planet <grin> although I've been promising
myself to replace it. I was too slow replacing the battery a few months ago
and lost all the birthdays and address/phone numbers which weren't in
packs...

Regards,

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of COBOL....

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-04-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38F1E210.4B665BFD@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<seetcnsl9178@corp.supernews.com> <8c8lvp$mho$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <8c9utc$llc$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8cagur$pqh$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <8ccjfg$iv6$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8cf185$1at$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <38eebf0b.12785890@news.radiks.net> <8cn24b$jk8$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <MPG.135a8c25ff0d99a19896ce@news.freedombird.net> <8csdt2$klr$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`

```
I was thinking - if I want to see how software tools will effect the
programming industry and business in general, I might look at a more
mature industry.

Think of your lathe operator.  (I may have some details below wrong):

In the old days you had master lathe operators who designed the jigs,
the journeymen who could set the jigs, and the apprentices who could run
the lathes.   You didn't want to reset things very often, making as many
identical parts as possible.

Nowadays, the "jigs" are electronic.  They program the final shape to
tighter tolerance, maybe make one run on a wax dummy, and create the
part.  They make money with much smaller runs and can make parts which
couldn't have been made in the past.

So what does the master lathe operator do?  I imagine, for a one-of-a
kind product, everything.  But he uses CAD-CAM software and encyclopedic
knowledge (on line) of many more materials than he used before.  He
probably has interesting ways to test the strength of his products
before and after they have been made.  At any rate, what he learned 30
years ago is considerably different than what he does now.  The big shop
used to have lots of belts on the ceiling, while the home shop just had
one lathe.  Now the professional lathe is more expensive (including
cad-cam), but one can make a living with just one, out of his garage...

It would be educational to find out more about this - and useful to
think of a mature data processing industry in the same way as I was
thinking of the lathe operating shop.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of COBOL....

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-04-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8cve96$pj3$2@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<seetcnsl9178@corp.supernews.com> <8c8lvp$mho$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <8c9utc$llc$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8cagur$pqh$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <8ccjfg$iv6$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8cf185$1at$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <38eebf0b.12785890@news.radiks.net> <8cn24b$jk8$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <MPG.135a8c25ff0d99a19896ce@news.freedombird.net> <8csdt2$klr$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <38F1E210.4B665BFD@cusys.edu>`

```
This is an excellent analogy, Howard.

In previous posts to this NG I used the analogy of the Master Printer of 60
years ago. The only way people could produce printed material was by going
to a print shop.

Technology advanced in response to user demand. (Not necessarily demand in
the field of printing, but there was a spinoff from the demand in other
areas...)

Two major factors come into this:

1. Motivation. (The people today who don't know how to use a DTP package are
the ones who have little or no requirement and therefore little motivation.
If their motivation increases they will learn how to use the software. It
helps of course, if the software gets easier to use with each passing
release...),

2. Accessibility. (If you don't have access to a PC you are kind of stuck
with doing it the old (hard) way... If you DO have access but the user
interface is so unfriendly as to make the thing virtually unusable, you
might as well not have access...)

Now consider what happens when motivation increases but accessibility
doesn't. The situation becomes untenable. There is a Marketplace screaming
for access. History has shown that Marketplaces do not scream for very long
before they are satisfied...

SOMEONE will give the users what they want.

I liked your lathe shop very much and see many comparisons with modern
"production line" component based software devopment.

I believe this is the technology which will take us to tomorrow.

Pete.

(And yes, I am making a living with just one, out of my garage...<grin>)
```

Foodman wrote in message <8d1n13$7sg$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>In article <8csdt2$klr$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>,
>  "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Visit the DSDM Web Site. Get a copy of their HELP file. It's pretty close to
the methodology I use. I have modified it slightly (so I can't claim that
what I use is DSDM in the strictest sense of the word) in the light of
experience and finding what works. This is a METHODOLOGY for system design,
development, and implementation. I have used it successfully on three major
projects and a couple of minor ones. In every case we came in on time, on
budget, with delighted users.

Before that, I managed many  projects using the "Waterfall". On the rare
occasions when we got them in on time, they did not deliver what was
required, because what was required NOW was no longer what was in the signed
off requirement which the users signed THEN...

I am NOT endorsing so-called RAD development tools for program coding.

>Also, if possible, can you provide an operational
>system and the source code for it to demonstrate your RADical
>approach.
>
ROFL! yeah, right...

I would be much more inclined to take you seriously, Tony, if I believed you
were genuinely interested in doing something you haven't done before, or
even approaching something new with an open mind. As I am satisfied this is
NOT the case, why waste my time?

(Now, where did I put that gluepot...?)

Pete.

PS Anyone in the NG who IS genuinely interested, should mail me privately
and I'll forward material on RAD, DSDM, and my own brand of it. I am also
planning a book on the subject of RAD versus the Waterfall (though God knows
when I'll get round to writing it...)
```

> I would be much more inclined to take you seriously, Tony, if I
believed you
> were genuinely interested in doing something you haven't done before,

Hi:

My very survival depends on my software being usable. If I did
not have an open mind, I would be out of business. The software
I live off was originally written for DOS in 1986. Trying to
sell DOS software in a Windows world is hopeless. Therefore,
I HAD to embrace Windows (via SP2). Because I have to buy
everything I use, I have to be sure that it is going to provide
me with an advantage. Since I live off the internet, I HAD to
learn HTML to the extent necessary.

I have been curious about things like Java and OO, but based on
what I see, and WHAT I NEED, there is nothing for me there and
nothing I have read in CLC has convinced me otherwise, in fact,
the evident confusion about OO and its mysterious properties
supports my opinion that it is unecessary for day-to-day
business applications. I just can't imagine anyone using Java
over COBOL for these applications.

Thanks

TOny Dilworth





Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>agree you have no need of JAVA in this context.

Pete:

Your statement above seems to imply that you are under the impression
that Tony is "web enabling" his software in order for it to run as an
internet based application.

Although we do offer a new product which does support "web enabling,"
I believe that he was specifically referring to an internet thin
client application enabling tool.

Certainly in many cases it is beneficial to "web enable" your
application, we find that an ideal approach is to be able to run the
same source program as a non-web browser based internet thin client
for high speed business to business based applications and a web
browser client based application for mass marketing type internet
applications.

>As I don't have SP2 and JAVA is free, I DO use it. And find it very useful.

It is very useful for web-browser based internet thin client
applications, but for a non-web browser based internet application,
Java is one of many programming languages which can be used to develop
the application.

>Doesn't mean I'm throwing away my COBOL tools though...
>
>Pete.
>
>

Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of COBOL....

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 2000-04-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8cbadr$7h7$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<seetcnsl9178@corp.supernews.com> <8c8lvp$mho$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <8c9utc$llc$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Tony, you are not very polite.

I think that I know what you mean, and it could be turned into a very
friendly advice.

The fine art of making programs has almost nothing to do with learning a
language.
Many young people are trying to make a living in the DP-world. We need a
wide range of skills here, but none of us can cover everything. If
programming feels difficult to a person, it will never be a passion, and the
person will move to an area of the business where his/her skills can be used
in a better way.

Learning the first language is always the hardest language to learn, because
the knowledge of making programs comes on top of it. I think that Cobol is
very good as the first language. It's pretty easy to learn and you can
easily feel if programming is the future for you. If so, the next language
is easier. If not, don't waste time for other languages but try to find
another exciting line in th DP-world.

If everything fails, go for management. Many did that before you - trust me.

Regards
Ib

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Future of COBOL....

- **From:** david <dskirk@usa.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<g9jnes45smngi4s4m4t69n4em7584mmkcq@4ax.com>`

```
On Wed, 5 Apr 2000 08:42:27 -0400, "Alan H Russell"
<RUSSELAH@apci.com> wrote:

>The current month issue of IEEE Software magazine is devoted to COBOL,
>including the question you raise.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>>http://www.help.com/

i'm not sure the question is being answered...   and i'm sure the IEEE
authors had an interest -- yes, i was approached...    i've been in
cobol since 1965, using, teaching, writing, etc.   yes, there is a
future, although it will be with certain employers; not everyone.
right now, it's living on momentum. a person could learn cobol, work
for a large employer, get promoted into management while procedural
cobol code was still in use. yes, i'm still receiving royalty payments
on my CICS book, but i know it's going to become a narrow focused
language and not for the masses. to develop a career 20 years ago, a
knowledge of CICS was valuable -- today it's limited. sorta' like
wanting to sell a pristine 1947 plymouth...  there may be a few buyers
willing to pay top $$$, but most of us will just see it as out-moded
transportation.   just my thoughts...
david

>

__________________________________________________
david                               dskirk@usa.net
```

…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>DD
This almost certainly WON'T work across all platforms. (I'd be surprised if
it worked on ANY platform, and it is certainly NOT a solution to the
underlying problem).

It is predicated on the (I believe false) hope that moving an invalid packed
field to a non-packed target will NOT cause a data exception. In other
words, an 0C7 can only be raised during a PACK operation. (Maybe someone in
the NG who has access to a mainframe can confirm or deny whether this is the
current situation...?)

It is now some years since I worked with mainframes, but I seem to recall
that the UNPACK macro could also raise this exception due to an invalid sign
being detected. Even if I'm wrong, and the above works OK, it is a very
dodgy "solution".

The best solution to this problem is NOT to move data to the group level
above DATO-NUM.
(Use INITIALIZE or simply move zero to DATO-NUM when you start whatever
processing cycle it is used in, if it needs to be initialized).

Do you really need to use a PACKED field at all? This format is rooted in
the days when external media cost a fortune and it was necessary to
"compress" everything possible.

(In fact, the original IBM 360s had the decimal instruction set (support for
PACKED) as an option. The idea was that if you were going to use this
beautiful new technology (in 1965) for commercial data processing, you'd
probably need to process money and therefore require a decimal arithmetic
capability that was simpler and took up less space than the native floating
point provided. Later, after finding how surprisingly good for commercial
applications the 360 range was, IBM made the decimal instruction set a
standard part of the system.)

There is a real question as to whether we need this format in today's
processing. There probably isn't an IBM programmer alive who has never had
an 0C7 caused by "packing a blank" or some other invalid character. Yes, you
can argue that if you are processing invalid data, it is better NOT to
continue (that was IBM's philosophy) but other vendors who never implemented
PACKED and the associated data exception, still made a living...

I also question the point of style where a NUMERIC (Class) test is applied
to a field defined as Packed. Although some vendors allow it, Class tests
should only be applied to fields which COULD be something other than they
are defined as. Asking whether a field defined as Pic xxxx is NUMERIC  or
ALPHABETIC makes sense because it could be either. Asking whether a field
defined as numeric is numeric is nonsense. It implies the programmer doesn't
know what is going on with the data in his program. The corollary from this
is that raw data should always be read as Pic x (string) data, then
validated and moved to numeric fields once we are sure that it is numeric.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol - Data Exception (Conversion Data).

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-04-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8c8mo4$mic$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<8bng6o$11p$1@news.flashnet.it> <lHKD4.9069$0o4.66330@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8c65ph$id4$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <38E6EE78.FE1C148E@zip.com.au>`

```
Thanks Ken, for setting that straight.

I could've sworn that using UNPACK did it too, but I am struggling to recall
Assembler programming in 1968 <grin>.

OK, I was wrong on that one, but I stand by the rest of what I said. It is
an ugly solution that is being proposed....

While we're on it, I can't see the post that Bill Klein did to this thread
where he commented on what I wrote. But I DID receive his post by
mail...weird things going on with my ISP...

Bill, I agree that there are existing extensions to allow Class tests
(NUMERIC and
ALPHABETIC) to be applied to numeric data fields. I just don't condone it. I
really can't see the point. To me it seems logically idiotic to ask whether
a numeric field is numeric. If there was any chance of it NOT being, why
define it as numeric? Yes, I know accidents happen and we clobber fields
inadvertently, but the solution has to be NOT to set things up so that can
happen, rather than closing the door after the horse has gone.

I believe these "extensions", which Bill advises are about to become
standard, just encourage sloppy programming and are being implemented
because people don't understand the proper use of Class tests.

In the early days of COBOL (COBOL 59), BEFORE the PICTURE clause was
invented, fields were described using 3 clauses: SIZE, CLASS, and USAGE.

So a field which today we would describe as "pic x(10)." needed "SIZE 10,
CLASS ALPHANUMERIC, USAGE DISPLAY".

Obviously, this was a bit tiresome, even for COBOL programmers, so PICTURE
was a blessing. But we all understood what CLASS meant. It meant there were
three possibilities: NUMERIC, ALPHABETIC, and ALPHANUMERIC. A field defined
as pic X(10) could hold anything and there were times when it would be
advantageous to know what the CLASS of the data in it was...

Enough said.

Pete.

Ken Foskey wrote in message <38E6EE78.FE1C148E@zip.com.au>...
>"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:
>>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol - Data Exception (Conversion Data).

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-04-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38E888A2.E333FE89@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<8bng6o$11p$1@news.flashnet.it> <lHKD4.9069$0o4.66330@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8c65ph$id4$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <38E6EE78.FE1C148E@zip.com.au> <8c8mo4$mic$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`

```
"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:
> 
> I could've sworn that using UNPACK did it too, but I am struggling to
> recall Assembler programming in 1968 <grin>.
> 

Rewind.

If you move a packed field to a packed field of the same definition
the compiler will make this a simple byte move*.  If you pack or
unpack (move to or from a display type number) then you had better
have your numbers right.

Sorry for the confusion.

*  Depending upon compiler options, it seems to work this way
optimized and crash in no optimization but do not quote me on this.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol - Data Exception (Conversion Data).

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** postingid@dissensoftware.com (Binyamin Dissen)
- **Date:** 2000-04-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<53nhes0u02d35fgf94q53mdfgf5tv7g41v@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<8bng6o$11p$1@news.flashnet.it> <lHKD4.9069$0o4.66330@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8c65ph$id4$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <38E6EE78.FE1C148E@zip.com.au> <8c8mo4$mic$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <38E888A2.E333FE89@zip.com.au>`

```
On Mon, 03 Apr 2000 22:03:46 +1000 Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au> wrote:

:>"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:
 
:>> I could've sworn that using UNPACK did it too, but I am struggling to
:>> recall Assembler programming in 1968 <grin>.

:>Rewind.

:>If you move a packed field to a packed field of the same definition
:>the compiler will make this a simple byte move*.  If you pack or
:>unpack (move to or from a display type number) then you had better
:>have your numbers right.

Actually PACK and UNPACK will not fail because the data isn't decimal.

PACK combines the low nibbles of two adjacent bytes into one byte, and flips
the nibbles of the last byte.

UNPK splits a byte into two nibbles, place x'f' in the high nibbles, and flips
the nibbles of the last byte.

The packed arithmetic instructions such as AP, SP, CVB, etc. will fail if the
data is not packed with the last nibble being a sign.

:>Sorry for the confusion.

:>*  Depending upon compiler options, it seems to work this way
:>optimized and crash in no optimization but do not quote me on this.

I would expect if the packed fields are identical lengths it would generate
MVC, if unequal length it would generate ZAP.
```

Thanks Ben,

your elucidation was faultless and brought back some distant memories...of
course, it isn't the macro instructions which fail it is the decimal
instruction set; I seem to recall CVB being a persistent offender...

So what about this move in COBOL? Invalid data in a packed field, moving it
to  a display numeric field. Are you saying that the UNPACK causes no
problem, and the subsequent filling of the target will be achieved by MVC or
ZAP? Sounds reasonable and it looks as though I was wrong about the move
causing an 0c7 (but I already knew that from Ken...)

I still think the whole approach is decidedly dodgy.

These days PACKED fields are best avoided.

Pete.

Which one?  Tanith Lee wrote the short story "Red as Blood", which is
the Snow White story with a twist.  The victor always writes the
history, and in this case the Queen won.  Here's what we know about Snow
White:

Her skin as white as snow.
Her lips as red as blood.
When a woodsman under direct orders from his Queen tries to kill her,
she looked at him and charmed him to kill a stag instead.
She went off into the woods to live with a bunch of misshapen servants.
Animals follow her bidding, and possibly even storms.
She appeared to be dead in her coffin, but was revived by the kiss of
life.

It is easy to see what crowd 'Snow White' belongs to.

Well, Mr Dashwood, I'm glad that someone else managed to set you straight
before I 'graced' the newsgroup with a compile-listing (*with*
PMAP!)... oh, heck, why not...

WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.                                
01  NUMFLD PIC S9(9) COMP-3 VALUE +1.                   
01  STUFF.                                              
    05  DATA-AREA.                                      
        10  NUM-AREA PIC S9(5) COMP-3.                  
    05  TEST-AREA PIC X(5).                             
    05  TEST-AREA-NUM REDEFINES TEST-AREA PIC 9(5).     
PROCEDURE DIVISION.                                     
                                                        
    MOVE ALL 'A' TO DATA-AREA.                          
    MOVE NUM-AREA TO TEST-AREA-NUM.                     
    IF TEST-AREA NOT NUMERIC                            
        DISPLAY ' TUTTI BUONO - ', TEST-AREA-NUM.       
    GOBACK.                                             

... generates...

MOVE       000320                     START    EQU   *                
           000320  05 EC                       BALR  14,12            
           000322  0001                        DC    X'0001'          
           000324  92 C1 6 008                 MVI   008(6),X'C1'     
           000328  D2 01 6 009 6 008           MVC   009(2,6),008(6)  
MOVE       00032E  F3 42 6 00B 6 008           UNPK  00B(5,6),008(3,6)
           000334  96 F0 6 00F                 OI    00F(6),X'F0'     
IF         000338  58 20 C 020                 L     2,020(0,12)      
           00033C  DD 04 6 00B 2 000           TRT   00B(5,6),000(2)  
           000342  58 10 C 030                 L     1,030(0,12)      
           000346  07 81                       BCR   8,1              
DISPLAY    000348  05 EC                       BALR  14,12            
           00034A  0002                        DC    X'0002'          
           00034C  58 F0 C 024                 L     15,024(0,12)     
           000350  05 EF                       BALR  14,15            
           000352  58 F0 C 028                 L     15,028(0,12)     
           000356  05 1F                       BALR  1,15             
           000358  0001                        DC    X'0001'          
           000358  0001                        DC    X'0001'      
           00035A  10                          DC    X'10'        
           00035B  00000F                      DC    X'00000F'    
           00035E  0C000038                    DC    X'0C000038'  
           000362  0000                        DC    X'0000'      
           000364  0F                          DC    X'0F'        
           000365  000005                      DC    X'000005'    
           000368  0D000200                    DC    X'0D000200'  
           00036C  000B                        DC    X'000B'      
           00036E  FFFF                        DC    X'FFFF'      
GOBACK     000370                     GN=01    EQU   *            
           000370  05 EC                       BALR  14,12        
           000372  0003                        DC    X'0003'      
           000374  58 F0 C 024                 L     15,024(0,12) 
           000378  05 EF                       BALR  14,15        
           00037A                     GN=02    EQU   *            

... etc.

Running the code produces the display:

IEF376I  JOB/FRDS38LF/STOP  2000094.1124 CPU    0MIN 00.02SEC SRB    0MIN00.00
 TUTTI BUONO -  1 1                                                            

... so yes, it *does* work... and yes, it *is* ugly.

DD
```

```
One part of what Pete says, I think I need to disagree with (regardless of
compiler).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol - Data Exception (Conversion Data).

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-04-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8c8nm5$mjp$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<8bng6o$11p$1@news.flashnet.it> <lHKD4.9069$0o4.66330@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8c65ph$id4$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <8c8g9r$drn$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Re-read your post Bill (now I can see it...)

Point 2 is a good one. You're quite right that our program may "inherit" bad
data records from a previous process. My position is predicated on the
assumption that data being passed from another program has been validated.

In fact, I think data should be validated at the time it is acquired (I like
the way OO handles this; the object owning the data is responsible for it).

I agree the world is not perfect and there are times when you may need to
apply a CLASS test to numeric fields. But you won't catch me doing
it...<grin>

Pete.



William M. Klein wrote in message <8c8g9r$drn$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>...
>One part of what Pete says, I think I need to disagree with (regardless of
>compiler).
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>2) The time to do a numeric test (IMHO regardles of USAGE) is when the
field
>might have been "filled" by some group move (and hence may have invalid
>data).  This certainly happens when you move another group to the group
>containing a numeric field (which is often SIGNIFICNANTLY more efficient
that
>moving the individual elementary fields).  More importantly, it ALWAYS
>happens when you read a record.  You get the entire record - and if it has
>any packed, binary, or other numeric fields, these may or MAY NOT be
"valid".
>I think such fields need to be tested somewhere in your application.  This
>may happen when you first get "user input" (and you do validation there) -
>but it also may occur when the records are first about to be used.  This
>depends on the "origin" of the data and the control your application has on
>such.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol - Data Exception (Conversion Data).

- **From:** "DennisHarley" <LegacyBlue@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uE8ThRIo$GA.229@cpmsnbbsa03>`
- **References:** `<8bng6o$11p$1@news.flashnet.it> <lHKD4.9069$0o4.66330@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8c65ph$id4$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`

```

Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:8c65ph$id4$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz...

I'm getting into the fray a little late, some of the posts have
clarified the processes involved.

1) PACK & UNPACK will not cause an 0C7. (Already in several posts.)

>
> Do you really need to use a PACKED field at all? This format is
rooted in
> the days when external media cost a fortune and it was necessary to
> "compress" everything possible.

We used packed whether we like it or not.
1) DB2 carries decimal numbers in packed format, thus the DCLGEN will
have computational-3 fields. (I beleive the MS-Jet engine also
supports decimal format for COBOL applications).

2) When you use a display numeric item, it is converted to COMP-3 for
processing.
There may be some situations where it is converted to binary, but it
will be converted to comp-3 first.
Using COMP or COMP-3 cuts your conversion overhead.
You not only save data space (WS, Linkage etc), you also save
Instruction space (Procedure Division). You would probably need a
high-volume computational situation to see a difference in CPU
utilization.

>
> There is a real question as to whether we need this format in
today's
> processing. There probably isn't an IBM programmer alive who has
never had
> an 0C7 caused by "packing a blank" or some other invalid character.
Yes, you
> can argue that if you are processing invalid data, it is better NOT
to
> continue (that was IBM's philosophy) but other vendors who never
implemented
> PACKED and the associated data exception, still made a living...
>

You can pack a blank or low-values and continue processing in some
situations.
If you move the field to an UNSIGNED field, an "Or Immediate" OI will
be generated to force the Sign to unsigned. (x"F0" or x"0F"). Check
out Doc's PMAP.

field-a PIC S9(5).
Field-b pic 9(5) comp-3.

assume FIELD-A = spaces X'4040404040"
MOVE FIELD-A to field-B.
after PACK Field-b = x'000004'
after OI Field-b = x'00000F'
```

if you ever change environments, especially if you work in an old,
unfamiliar OS.

the hottest NYSE stocks of 1980) manufactured computers that used EBCDIC.

there still using them (they get support from 3rd party support vendors
who've taken over care of these aging computers).

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38EEECFA.340E8C90@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<8bng6o$11p$1@news.flashnet.it> <8c65ph$id4$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <uE8ThRIo$GA.229@cpmsnbbsa03> <8cl3cc$7he$1@news.igs.net> <LIpH4.16508$0o4.109295@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8clogi$hr0$1@news.igs.net>`

```
donald tees wrote:
> 
> Strangely enough, though I have coded Cobol now for thirty-three years, I
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

An IBM Series/1?  Was that EDX or RPS (or perhaps even CPS or Unix)? 
The IBM Series/1 was an EBCDIC machine and I have fond memories of
coding ANSI-74 COBOL on it.  My recollection was that COBOL on the
Series/1 supported only DISPLAY USAGE and COMP numerics.  No COMP-3 or
packed-decimal of any kind.

If you used the RPS operating system, it was similar to OS/360 or MVS. 
EDX (Event Driven Executive) was quite a bit different.

I remember my very first COBOL program on the Series/1 (just about 21
years ago).  They needed a day-of-week subroutine.  I had no idea how
that was done, so they gave me an old S/360 assembler routine to copy
from.  I managed to convert it without understanding it completely, but
it did need a date with a four-digit year.

We got rid of our last S/1 in January.  IBM stopped making them around
1992.  They weren't Y2K compliant.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol - Data Exception (Conversion Data).

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** bbreynolds@aol.comskipthis (Bruce B. Reynolds)
- **Date:** 2000-04-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000410174535.18319.00000830@ng-fz1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<38EEECFA.340E8C90@worldnet.att.net>`

```
>An IBM Series/1?  Was that EDX or RPS (or perhaps even CPS or Unix)? 
>The IBM Series/1 was an EBCDIC machine and I have fond memories of
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Packed decimal available in the MFSL
(Mathematical and Functional Subroutine
Library), which could be used with COBOL,
PL/I, FORTRAN, or assembler on RPS and
CPS, plus EDL under EDX; essentially
a combination of the CSP (Commercial
Subroutine Package) and SSP (Scientific
Subroutine Package) from S/360 and
1130/1800 days.  The examples were
written as if cards were still in use.

The Series/1 is Y2K compliant, and
EDX is also compliant, except that the
date displays on the operator commands
do not zero fill, so that there is garbage
shown; tested and in use on Series/1's
and emulated Series/1's at my last
contracts.

PS: IBM sold an incrediably small number
of the "IX" for Series/1.
```

#### ↳ Re: Mainframe systems?

- **From:** Charles F Hankel <noos@hankel.freedombird.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.13567d4ba9d369fa9896a5@news.freedombird.net>`
- **References:** `<38E19325.DEF76E92@pacbell.net> <n3j4eso8avdar2ja50aq71322a4pk2981h@4ax.com> <38E35916.E61F4564@zip.com.au> <j4faes8f9dnaa7qcteod3kqu1jkt7rdite@4ax.com>`

```
I noticed that G Moore as gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com said...
> Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au> wrote:
> 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> and dumb (3270) terminals to client/server for clients.

None that I have ever seen, especially as the 3270 isn't as dumb as a lot 
of people try to make out.  If you want good CAD/CAM stuff, CADAM/Catia 
provides this in spades but I've forgotten the model numbers for the 
terminals used with that sort of application.

The only justification for PCs with terminal emulators is the 
availability of wordprocessing and the like, and consequently not wanting 
to take up deskspace with two keyboards and screens.

I honestly don't understand why the likes of IBM haven't come up with a 
terminal that also has a few, possibly hard-wired, local applications.  I 
did think that they might have done this when Java appeared on the scene, 
but no.  If they could be produced at a reasonable price, I'm sure a lot 
of IT managers would look seriously at them to reduce the on-cost that 
PCs incur, not only in hardware and software, but also in network and 
support costs, not to mention getting rid of the Toytown stuff that a lot 
of users crowd their PCs with.  I've never had a virus at home but I've 
seen loads of them infesting PC LANs in big offices.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Mainframe systems?

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-04-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38EC93A8.5D46D530@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<38E19325.DEF76E92@pacbell.net> <n3j4eso8avdar2ja50aq71322a4pk2981h@4ax.com> <38E35916.E61F4564@zip.com.au> <j4faes8f9dnaa7qcteod3kqu1jkt7rdite@4ax.com> <MPG.13567d4ba9d369fa9896a5@news.freedombird.net>`

```
Charles F Hankel wrote:
> 
> The only justification for PCs with terminal emulators is the
> availability of wordprocessing and the like, and consequently not wanting
> to take up deskspace with two keyboards and screens.

I love being able to cut and paste.  CTL-C & CTL-V are my good friends,
even when the only program I'm running is my 3270 emulator.

I just wish it were easier to switch between 80 and 132 column
emulation.
 
> I honestly don't understand why the likes of IBM haven't come up with a
> terminal that also has a few, possibly hard-wired, local applications.  I
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> seen loads of them infesting PC LANs in big offices.

It is cheaper to simply have a network computer.   The primary
applications we use at work are:
1.  3270 Emulator
2.  Microsoft Office on the LAN (primarily for documentation on the LAN)
3.  Netscape on the LAN (primarily for documentation on the LAN)
4.  E-mail

For the above, there certainly isn't a need for a floppy drive, and a
hard drive could be eliminated as well (making it easier to dial in from
home and access my saved e-mail).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mainframe systems?

- **From:** "ChrisOsborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-06T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<OpYAIGDo$GA.271@cpmsnbbsa04>`

```
There used to be a product called TPX (for MVS), or Terminal Productivity
Executive.  It's main purpose was to manage multiple sessions in differing
software at once so that you could be logged into CA-7, TSO, CICS, and
anything else made known to TPX.  It had a cut and paste feature usable in
any of these environments, although it wasn't as good as a PC's cut and
paste.  The ISPF/PDF editing facility had CUT and PASTE after a fashion, but
it was only inside ISPF.

You're not the only one wishing it was a snap to switch between 80 and 132
columns.  My favorite working screen was 43x80, but that was only because
the 132 column screens did weird things when you had ISPF split screens when
one screen supported 132 columns fully, and the other screen did not.  Oh,
and this was all back on an MVS/XA system.


"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:38EC93A8.5D46D530@cusys.edu...

True, our "Host Explorer" is just a dumb terminal really with some decent 
cut and paste features that are handy.  Why doesn't someone invent the
double cut and paste.  Cut 1  Cut  2   Paste 1  Paste 2 in the Edit menu.  2
clipboards, if you will.

I'm not disagreeing with anyone at all, but my job necessitates me to use
more than just the "Host Explorer."  I also use many other tools every day.

I find Windows NT pretty stable to be honest with you.  Our old "Dynacomm
Elite" terminal used to crash the whole desktop when the connection to the
host was lost.  WordPerfect is sometimes unstable, especially when doing
macros and scripts.  But it's working pretty good for me lately, it probably
only crashes about once a month and that's being used pretty much all day on
my 8-5 shifts.

Some FUEL for the FIRE baby!

Jeff (on call)

## Thread: Variables with Fujitsu 3.0 - New to Cobol — 10 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Variables with Fujitsu 3.0 - New to Cobol

- **From:** "John Wolanski" <brgr88@REMOVETHISyahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<REQF4.12966$_c3.99568@typhoon.columbus.rr.com>`

```
I got a copy of Fuj. Cobol 3.0 to learn Cobol and right now I'm trying to do
a lesson with alphanumeric variables.  But I'm having a bit of a problem.

When I compile and try to run a program that uses an alpha variable, it
doesn't get past the ACCEPT part until I enter enough characters to equal
the variable size; numeric variables don't seem to pose a problem.

For example, in a program that accepts input and then redisplays it, if the
variable size is set to 10, such as in:

01 THE-NAME PIC X(10).

if I enter 'John', I'm still left with the prompt awaiting more input until
there are 10 characters entered.  So if I enter 'John' two more times, it
displays:

JohnJohnJo


It seems like it won't accept anything less than the specified variable
size.  Is this some "feature" of the compiler that can be turned off, or
something that must be coded around?  Like I said, numerics (eg, PIC 9(2). )
don't seem to have a problem.

Thanks for any help.

- John Wolanski

Here is the code:

@OPTIONS MAIN
IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
PROGRAM-ID.  HELLO02.
ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.

DATA DIVISION.
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
01 THE-NAME PIC X(10).

PROCEDURE DIVISION.
PROGRAM-BEGIN.
DISPLAY "Enter someone's name.".
ACCEPT THE-NAME.
DISPLAY "Hello " THE-NAME.
PROGRAM-DONE.

STOP RUN.
```

#### ↳ Re: Variables with Fujitsu 3.0 - New to Cobol

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8c94o5$uu4$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<REQF4.12966$_c3.99568@typhoon.columbus.rr.com>`

```
One of the questions that we seem to get once every few months.

With Fujitsu (but not all COBOLs), you will get the desired behavior if you
change:

 ACCEPT THE-NAME.

     to

 ACCEPT THE-NAME from Console.
```

> there are 10 characters entered. So if I enter 'John' two more times,
it
> displays:
>
> JohnJohnJo
>
> It seems like it won't accept anything less than the specified
variable
> size. Is this some "feature" of the compiler that can be turned off,
or
> something that must be coded around? Like I said, numerics (eg, PIC 9
(2). )
> don't seem to have a problem.
>
> Thanks for any help.
>
> - John Wolanski


Hi:

The x(10) specifies the maximum number of characters the field can
contain. Just press enter after typing 'john'.

Thanks

TOny Dilworth


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Variables with Fujitsu 3.0 - New to Cobol

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ca48s$pu9$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<REQF4.12966$_c3.99568@typhoon.columbus.rr.com> <8ca07q$n3t$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Do not listen to "Foodman", he is wrong.

The Fujitsu console routines are *extremely* primitive, and only suitable
for very basic debugging.  If you really want to do screen I/O, then use of
the screen section is about the minimum that you can get away with.

Foodman wrote in message <8ca07q$n3t$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>In article <REQF4.12966$_c3.99568@typhoon.columbus.rr.com>,
>"John Wolanski" <brgr88@REMOVETHISyahoo.com> wrote:
…[49 more quoted lines elided]…
>Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Variables with Fujitsu 3.0 - New to Cobol

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ccqbn$c1n$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<REQF4.12966$_c3.99568@typhoon.columbus.rr.com> <8ca07q$n3t$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8ca48s$pu9$1@news.igs.net> <8ccncd$n6r$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Yes.  Keep in mind there is both a screen and a console.  The console is
intended as the console of a mainframe ... messages like "please mount
tape".  The screen is for programmed I/O.

I rather like having two separate devices.  It is handy for debugging, and I
use the console only for that.  However the console routines are as
primitive as hell.

Foodman wrote in message <8ccncd$n6r$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>In article <8ca48s$pu9$1@news.igs.net>,
>"donald tees" <donald@willmack.com> wrote:
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Variables with Fujitsu 3.0 - New to Cobol

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ccqem$mkr$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<REQF4.12966$_c3.99568@typhoon.columbus.rr.com> <8ca07q$n3t$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8ca48s$pu9$1@news.igs.net> <8ccncd$n6r$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Yes, that is exactly how the Fujitsu system works (which is "fully" ANSI
conforming - as is the other way of working).

Note: As stated elsewhere, if you do add "FROM CONSOLE" - then Fujitsu works
as you expect.
```

<a id="t-381f96f15a"></a>
## Thread: Animator very slow — 2 msgs

### Animator very slow

- **From:** "Johan den Boer" <jj.den.boer@hccnet.nl>
- **Date:** 2000-04-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8c727p$k1h$1@news.hccnet.nl>`

```
Hi,

I made a new rts32 in micro focus cobol and linked some sql librarys
 oracle ) and application
libraries with it. When I want to animate a program it is very slow, so you
actually
cannot use it.
Is there a way to speed up the animator.

I am using MF Cobol on HP platform and Oracle 7.3.4.
```

#### ↳ Re: Animator very slow

- **From:** "RUSSELL STYLES" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 2000-04-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8c9d55$eg7$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<8c727p$k1h$1@news.hccnet.nl>`

```
    I am not familar with the Unix version of  MF, but be aware that some
animator functions can reduce speed by many orders of magnitude.

    I am speaking of the ability to check a given condition at every
statement,
(with pc - Environment, break, until).  With this set, it performs an if
with EVERY
statement.  The ability to monitor a data name for change is much faster.

    Try to use the Brakpoint/If at a given spot rather than EVERWHERE.

    If this is not the problem, trying increasing available memory - doesn't
the unix MF limit
you to a set amount of memory by default?  Animator takes up a fair amount
of memory.





Johan den Boer <jj.den.boer@hccnet.nl> wrote in message
news:8c727p$k1h$1@news.hccnet.nl...
> Hi,
>
> I made a new rts32 in micro focus cobol and linked some sql librarys
>  oracle ) and application
> libraries with it. When I want to animate a program it is very slow, so
you
> actually
> cannot use it.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Free Cobol compiler

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ccqus$lpa$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<0xaG4.1079$X21.51498@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8cbcdu$3nn$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <38E9F111.9518F3B3@att.net>`

```
Valid question (about when the FAQ was last posted).

The answer is that I have "switched" to just having the pointer posted (every
15 days).  This was because of the EXTREME length of the FAQ - and the fact
that many places (particularly those outside the US) require users to pay for
both connect time and bytes transferred.  This means that (for a person in
the know) even if they try to put the FAQ in a "killfile" they may end up
paying for downloading it before they can ignore it.

At the time that I switched to the "pointer" post, it was suggested that I
*do* actually post the full thing every once and a while (to make it easier
to find in such things as deja.com).

Is there general agreement that it should be posted completely every quarter
or so? More often? Less often?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Free Cobol compiler

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** aflinsch <avflinsch@att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38EA2F89.FA430863@att.net>`
- **References:** `<0xaG4.1079$X21.51498@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8cbcdu$3nn$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <38E9F111.9518F3B3@att.net> <8ccqus$lpa$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> Valid question (about when the FAQ was last posted).
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> paying for downloading it before they can ignore it.

Fair enough reason. But it would depend on exactly which newsreader
one is using. 
Using the extra bandwidth, would be of concern for those who are
actually running 
a local news spool. I would venture that the majority of those folks
are not paying 
by the byte.

Here at work I tend to use Netscape, as it is what is available.
Netscape downloads 
headers first, then articles when requested. Retrieving the header of
a pointer to 
the faq takes up as much bandwidth as downloading the header of an
article which
contains the entire faq. At home i tend to use Agent, Pan or slrn,
depending on what
room/computer I happen to be in/using at the time. All of these
newsreaders only 
download headers, then download the article when requested. As for
following a 
pointer/link  to the faq in the news article, Netscape, Agent and slrn
can follow a 
link, Pan's current release (0.80beta2) can't do this yet. 

> 
> Is there general agreement that it should be posted completely every quarter
> or so? More often? Less often?
> 

Personally I wouldn't mind seeing the pointer posted every week or
every other week. 
While the entire faq should be posted monthly, possibly with a
crosspost to news.answers 
and comp.answers, which would be "traditional" other places to post
the faq.
```

Bill, I must say that you are doing a tremendous work in the FAQ. You are
very kind to offer the non-WEB version, I just feel that you should offer a
download-version for schools and universities as well.

If a person has E-mail and no WEB access, it is very inconvenient when nice
friends send a mail with an attachment for some MB. You are stucked until
the mail is received.

A FTP version could be down-loaded and copied to a couple of CD's, placed in
the library. The students could lend the CD's for homework.

Just an idea.

Regards
Ib

- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.135a888bf57714519896cd@news.freedombird.net>`
- **References:** `<0xaG4.1079$X21.51498@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8cbcdu$3nn$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <38E9F111.9518F3B3@att.net> <8ccqus$lpa$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <8ckvfd$h78$1@ins20.netins.net>`

```
I noticed that Floyd Johnson as floydj@netins.net said...
> 
> William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> who cannot access the web access to the FAQ

Veering off slightly, there are moves afoot in the UK between ISPs, the 
phone companies and Oftel (the telephone systems regulator) to provide 
unmetered Internet access.  The whole thing is basically agreed in 
principle, but there is a lot of nitty-gritty detail being hammered out 
with Oftel stamping on the less fair of the phone companies' proposals.

At Freedom Bird, we are anticipating that unmetered access will be 
available to our users within a few weeks from now, and I don't see us 
as being any different to other ISPs in this respect.  If so, the UK 
will be another chunk of users for whom online time will no longer be an 
issue and will, IMO, drive debate in the rest of the EU, and other 
countries where people occasionally identify with the UK.  Brace 
yourselves, the British are coming.  :-)
```

<a id="t-99975f13f7"></a>
## Thread: The Wrong People — 27 msgs

### The Wrong People

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8canls$i42$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
 Hi:

I have always felt that most software is written by the 'Wrong People'.
For example, a 'computer-scientist' is the last person who would be
qualified to design and program a payroll application; they would
approach the application from the wrong perspective, and, in all
likelihood, using the wrong tools like C++, Java, VB and their
odious ilk.

Pete Dashwood recently provided fuel for my fire by sending an
article titled 'Building user interfaces for object-oriented
systems, Part 1' by Allen Holub (AH). (You can read the
article at www.holub.com.) Mr. Dashwood describes AH as
a 'World Authority' and 'lecturer at UCBerkeley' on OO.

AH is an apparently distinguished programmer whose experience
is largely C++ and Java. COBOL is not mentioned in the article.
He appears to be a 'computer-scientist' but I seriously wonder if
he has ever designed, programmed, implemented and supported a
SIGNIFICANT business application. For sure, if he had, the
article would, I think, reflect that, but it doesn't.

AH makes some observations about system design and uses them to
argue for an OO approach. To me, the arguments lack validity, are
somewhat contrived, and therefore can not be used to support the
argument. Furthermore, anyone who would suggest using Java for
business applications must be suspect. . .

Since AH appears to be on the forefront of OO development (albeit
in Java), it is not unreasonable to assume that he and others
of his type, are establishing YOUR OO future. Yet, I think that
his background is not suitable to the task when the task is
business applications. If one's perceptions of the problem are
wrong then the solutions will be wrong. (If you need to be convinced
of the utter absurdity of Java vis-a-vis COBOL, read the second in the
series of articles by AH.)

After writing this stuff, I thought about the hostile reception
it would probably receive in the CLC and that AH might take a dim
view of his article being savaged. I decided to post it anyway
with my advance apologies to AH and those who do not share my
anti-OO views.

I have extracted bits of the article and made some comments about
them below.
                   *           *           *

"Bjarne Stroustrup, the inventor of C++, once characterized object-
oriented
programming as 'buzzword-oriented programming' and certainly one
of the most abused (or at least misunderstood) buzzwords in the pack is
object itself."

---Sorry, but I couldn't resist to point out that the article refers to
   a veritable cornucopia of buzzwords, viz: Unicode, SQL, Model View
   Controller, Visual Basic, MFC, Visual Cafe, Visual J++, C++, Visual
   Age, Jbuilder, JavaBeans, Abstract Windowing Toolkit, Remote
   Invocation Method (Pete threw in Active-X and Corba) as well as
   all the usual OO buzzwords and perhaps others I missed.

                   *           *           *

"here are some rules of thumb that you can apply to see if you're
really looking at an object-oriented system:

1. All data is private. Period. (This rule applies to all
implementation details, not just the data.)"

---Private to whom or what? And, if it is private how do you
   share data?

2.get and set functions are evil. (They're just elaborate ways to
make the data public.)

3. Never ask an object for the information you need to do something;
rather, ask the object that has the information to do the work for
you."

---Isn't this like a sub-routine?

4. It must be possible to make any change to the way an object is
implemented, no matter how significant that change may be, by modifying
the single class that defines that object.

5. All objects must provide their own UI."

---I assume that the UI is presented on the screen. How can the object
   know about the current state of the screen if all objects provide
   their own UI? Do they consult each other?

                   *           *           *

"Don't confuse ease of maintenance with lack of complexity, by
the way. Object-oriented systems are usually more complex than
procedural
systems but are easier to maintain."

---Sorry, but that doesn't sound good to me.

"The idea is to organize the inevitable complexity inherent in real
computer
programs, not to eliminate it. Object-oriented designers, as a class,
consider the elimination of complexity to be an impossible goal."

---There are two types of complexity. The first is the logic required,
   for example, to process a file sequentially with many levels of
   control. The second type is the application complexity such
   as calculating taxes, etc.

---The former can be eliminated by providing a universal model for
   that class of applications. The latter you are stuck with.

---Although the elimination of one type of complexity is
   possible, the propOOnents make no attempt to remove it because
   they do not recognize the possibility.

---(If the object-oriented designers can't eliminate complexity,
   perhaps we should eliminate the object-oriented designers.)

                   *           *           *

"If anything, good object-oriented systems are more complex than
procedural
ones, but in such systems the program is better organized and thus
easier
to maintain."

---How does 'better-organized' become 'more complex'? I would like to
   see a real-world example of this. Perhaps, AH could be persuaded
   to enter the Great Programming Contest?

                   *           *           *

"Consider a system designed to get names from users.  You might be
tempted to use a TextField from which you extract a String, but that
just won't work in a robust application. What if the system needs
to run in China?  (Unicode comes nowhere near representing all the
idiographs (sic) that comprise written Chinese.) What if a user wants
to enter a name using a pen (or speech recognition) rather
than a keyboard?"

---What if the user wants to use his A/R system for A/P?
   This is just another fatuous argument.

---A business (COBOL) programmer would never "use a textfield
   from which you extract a string" to get a name.

                   *           *           *

"In the simple example above, you're tasked with adding an employee ID
to
every name in every screen that displays employee names."

---Sorry, but this is an unrealistic example. Whoever designed the
   system which would ultimately require this modification would
   need a good talking-to.

                   *           *           *

"I'll use an ATM machine for this example (as do many books), not
because
any of us will be implementing ATMs, but because an ATM is a good
analog
for both object-oriented and client/server architectures.  Look at the
central bank computer as a server object and the ATM as a client object.

A procedural solution

Most procedural database programmers would look at the server as a
repository of data and the client as a requester of that data. Such a
programmer might approach the problem of an ATM transaction as
follows:

1. The user walks up to a machine, inserts the card, and punches in his
   or her PIN.

2. The ATM formulates a query of the form "give me the PIN associated
with
   this card," sends the query to the database, and verifies that the
   returned value matches the one provided by the user. The ATM sends
   the PIN to the server as a string -- part of the SQL query -- but
   the returned number is stored in a 16-bit int to make the
   comparison easier.

---A business programmer would never store the PIN as a '16-bit
   integer'.

3. The user requests a withdrawal.

4. The ATM formulates another query, this time: "give me the account
   balance." It stores the returned balance in a 32-bit float.

---A business programmer would never store the balance as a
   '32-bit float'. Just another example which proves Java is
   NG for business.

5. If the balance is large enough, the machine dispenses the cash, and
   then posts an "update the balance for this user" to the server."

---Later on, AH 'improves' this 5-step solution with a 9-step solution.
   (The 5-step solution does not meet with his approval. He did say
   that OO was 'more complex', though.)

---I get the impression that, when people talk about client-server,
   that they look upon the program on the client as being completely
   separate and different from the program on the server and that the
two
   just send messages back and forth. This is implicit in AH's example
here.

---Using COBOL and SP2 the SAME program can run on a stand-alone
   PC, be executed by stations on a LAN directly from the server,
   and, be executed as a thin client. The SAME physical program.

---So, my ATM solution has a SINGLE COBOL program.

   1. Accepts card number and PIN from customer.

   2. Looks in ISAM file to verify that PIN is correct for card number.

   3. Customer asks for money which is dispensed and ISAM record
      rewritten to reflect reduced balance appropriately.

---Apart from the mechanical considerations of dispensing the money,
   the ATM application is much simpler than the Time Clock example
   in the Great Programming Contest.  Perhaps, we should have a separate
   ATM contest for the OO fraternity to put their theories into
   practice?

                   *           *           *

"So what's wrong with this picture? Let's start with the returned
balance.
What happens when Bill Gates walks into our bank wanting to open a
non-interest-bearing checking account and put all his money in it?
We really don't want to send him away, but the last time we looked,
Bill was worth about 87.94 gigabucks. Unfortunately, the 32-bit float
we're using for the account balance can represent at most 20 megabucks
(4 gigabucks, divided by 2 for the sign bit, divided by 100 for the
cents)."

---It is silly to suggest that Gates would put all of his money into
   a single checking account. I would hope that people who design
   ATM systems for banks pay attention to the rules and regulations
   of the bank governing the amount of money that can be in an
   account and how much may be withdrawn with a single transaction.
   Surely, the bank would determine the maximum amount and the
   designer would have made the appropriate fields as big as
   required. Why not have some REALISTIC EXAMPLES to reinforce
   the OO theory?

"Similarly, the 16-bit int used for the PIN can hold at most four
decimal digits. What if Bill wants to use "GATES" (five digits)
for his PIN"

---"GATES" is not 'five digits' it is five characters. And,
   the COBOL programmer would not use a '16-bit int'.
   This is another silly example of an artificial requirement
   that wouldn't happen in the real world. A bank is not going
   to let people arbitrarily change the format of their PINs
   and why should they?

"The procedural solution to all these problems is to change the ROMs in
every ATM in the world (since there's no telling which one Bill will
use)
to use 64-bit doubles instead of 32-bit floats to hold account balances
and to 32-bit longs to hold five-digit PINs. That's an enormous
maintenance
problem, of course. "

---I don't know much about the innards of ATMs but would hope that
   they use PCs rather than ROMs. If people are designing ATMs using
   ROMs rather than PCs, they need a good talking-to.

                   *           *           *

"Stepping into the real world for a moment, the cost of software
deployment
is one of the largest line-items on an IT department's budget."

---When VB, C++, Java. etc., software takes up millions and millions of
   bytes and can only realistically be distributed by CD, then
   software deployment would be a large line-item.  However, I
   would guess that a COBOL ATM EXE would be less than 200K -
   NOT 200M as would probably be the case for Java.

---If ATMs used PCs and a thin-client COBOL program, they could easily
   be updated while the city sleeps at no cost because of the
   teeny (relatively speaking) size of the EXE.

---Of course, if the ATM was a node on a network, running a COBOL
   program, it wouldn't be necessary to update each ATM at all.

                   *           *           *

"One of the appeals of Java (and the NC architecture, which is still
alive and well) in an enterprise-level company is that, by eliminating
deployment costs, you can save hundreds of millions of dollars every
year. The client/server equivalent of "swapping all the ROMs" --
deploying new versions of the client-side applications -- is a big
deal.

---But it is only a big deal because of the horrific overhead inherent
   in languages other than COBOL (see above).

                   *           *           *

"Similar maintenance problems exist inside most procedural programs,
even
those that don't use databases. Change definitions of a few central
data
types or global variables (that is, the program's equivalent of the
data
dictionary), and virtually every subroutine in the program might have
to
be rewritten. "

---Rewritten?!?!? Recompiled, perhaps, and that can be avoided with
   foresight.

                   *           *           *

"It's exactly this sort of maintenance nightmare that object-oriented
design hopes to solve."

---But, most of those 'nightmares' can be avoided by design.
   Object-oriented design will create different nightmares.

                   *           *           *

Based on what AH wrote, I conclude that he is 'Wrong People' when
it comes to helping to improve design methods for business
applications. This is not to say that AH is not highly-qualified in
some areas other than business applications.

Based on what I have read about OO, I conclude that it is
totally unnecessary, and will only end up making things worse
than they already are.

The Great Programming Contest came into being in order to
let people prove their points. I dearly hope that someone
will enter using an OO approach to prove that it has merit.

Anyway, that's what I think.

Thanks

Tony Dilworth








Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: The Wrong People

- **From:** "Charles W. Cribbs II" <CHARLESCRIBBS@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8caoca$3d8c$1@newssvr03-int.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<8canls$i42$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

you can be sure that most computer scientist have never written business
applications, just look at the books they write.  most of the good books on
business applications are written by junior college instructors who come
from business backgrounds.

Foodman <foodman123@aol.com> wrote in message
news:8canls$i42$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> Hi:
>
…[355 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: The Wrong People

if you can't share data with the current system, you essentially *are*
re-inventing the wheel.... everytime.

>>                    *           *           *

>> procedural

that has yet to be seen. show me a single instance of an oo system
which has been maintained, and does comparable work to a procedural.
also, since *most* code is thrown out, this might not be an advantage.

>> "The idea is to organize the inevitable complexity inherent in real
>> computer

screw organizing it. just hide as much of it as you can.

>> ---There are two types of complexity. The first is the logic required,
>>    for example, to process a file sequentially with many levels of
>>    control. The second type is the application complexity such
>>    as calculating taxes, etc.

i see two types, but different types. the interface to the OS, and the
logic itself.

>> ---The former can be eliminated by providing a universal model for
>>    that class of applications. The latter you are stuck with.

universal model. hehe. oh, you mean like attachments, ftp-by-mail,

>> ---Although the elimination of one type of complexity is

or it is impossible to remove.

oo won't solve any maintenance nightmares. as far as java goes, it may

##### ↳ ↳ Re: The Wrong People

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8cbg92$jlc$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<8canls$i42$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8caoca$3d8c$1@newssvr03-int.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Yes, I agree.

However, it is a major jump from there to the idea that the entire
methodology of OO is invalid, or even that it is unsuitable for business
use.

If you look at PowerCobol, for example, you will see all the same problems
that you get with trying to write business apps in Basic. The reason is
simple.  They implemented PowerCobol using null terminated strings for data,
or single sized arguments that effectively disable all the data checking
that Oops uses. In fact, the code that PowerCobol creates is not OOP Cobol
(!!!). It attempts to write pseudo OOP code using standard subroutines, and
fails rather badly ... in my opinion because it attempts to write C++ in
standard Cobol rather than generating OOP Cobol.

I think that if you play around with a true OOP Cobol, and ignore all the
crap coming from the academic world, you will find that it not only has a
viable place in our methodology, but solves a rather large number of
maintenance problems.

Charles W. Cribbs II wrote in message
<8caoca$3d8c$1@newssvr03-int.news.prodigy.com>...
>
>you can be sure that most computer scientist have never written business
…[365 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

Hi,
This thread will take months.

Let me start to provoke a reaction from all kind OO'ers. This business is
the only one I know of, where the tool is chosen before you make the
product. Earlier in this NG we have seen our world compared to industries
for cars, boats and aeroplanes.

Some of us are software designers, others are programmers. Some people are
writing useful books about 'how to..', and they are doing more for our
business than the remaining group - The Academic Approach to Confuse
Readers.

We have seen them though the years. They travel all over the world to
promote themselves and their Bank Accounts, and by God, our managers gets 3
buzzwords and start a revolution.

Some day in the future we must realize, that making programs is something we
do for the benfit of our customer - NOT to feed our own ambitions to me
smart.

Just thought you liked to know.

Regards
Ib

#### ↳ Re: The Wrong People

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38E94DC3.698AA8C1@home.com>`
- **References:** `<8canls$i42$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```


Foodman wrote:
> 
>  Hi:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> odious ilk.

<snip>

No, I'm not going to rush to the defence of OO ;-) What's the point, you
and Mr. Moore would rather die at the stake than accept it 'might' just
be useful. Rather, I'll take it from a different angle - who is writing
about what.

I haven't absorbed AH's articles yet and want to re-read. Sometimes we
read into the written word what turns us on, (or turns us off in your
case). Peter has had me perplexed for over a year now because he was
very sceptical about OO from what he read. Now at long last the bell has
started to ring silently and he is looking at it. I'll stack his
experience up against yours - were you programming in the late
fifties/sixties ? 

The author WAS writing about Java, for a Java magazine, and how it is
applied - not a case for using OO, (Java and OO are like bread and jam -
you can't separate them) - that's my reading, but of course not yours. I
think I would disagree with some of AH's points just as Thane has
already done so. Agreed his Gates illustration was a little over the
top, but I think his general observation was valid. Still,
tongue-in-cheek, I'll comment as analyst rather than programmer :-

- BILL can't withdraw all his money in one bang - the ATM screen isn't
set up for him to enter in zillions
- Logistics, while Bill is busily grabbing handfuls of money, is he
backed-up with the complete N. American U-Haul fleet to carry away his
loot
- Logistics II - an army of monkeys are stood on the backside of the ATM
stuffing in greenbacks as quickly as Bill can take them out. Again a
second U-Haul fleet required to deliver freshly minted notes for the
monkeys to put into the ATM
- Do the banks really want ATMs the size of the Empire State Building
- Not knowing your federal regulations on banking, I did question the
sanity of any bank or bank manager who would allow for an ATM
transaction to be in excess of $Kx

Now as to who should write the books/articles. How many CURRENT good
books are there on COBOL which are not authored by academics ? The only
two that immediately come to mind are Thane's "TYC in 24 Hours", and
"COBOL Unleashed" with contributions by regulars in this News Group.
Nothing as yet from an 'OO Practitioner' other than the 'academics'
Arranga/Coyle and Wilson, and as I have written previously theirs are
two good starter books if you are interested in the topic. (There are
some others but chuck 'em !). 

Heads down and writing code we 'developers' just don't have the time,
and as Thane will tell you he stands a better chance of making his
fortune with Regis on "Do You Wanna Be a Millionaire" than the royalties
he picks up from his book. 

Faced with the void between authors and developers, we should be glad
for what we can get, sift the wheat from the chafe and make our own
judgement as to who is promoting a realistic technique/methodology. But
don't condemn a guy for promoting an idea that you disagree with.

'The Contest' - Had you been prepared to take on the systems analyst's
mantle and specified what the DB/flat-files should hold, what should be
printed out - then I could have entered your 'contest' (but I hate the
word 'contest' - substitute 'Comparison' ????). While presenting an
interesting comparison of the various tools people would have used, SP2,
AcuCOBOL 'dual screen', OO/GUIs, Win APIs, VB, Activex, DBs, flat-files
etc....... - it would prove nothing - only that there is more than one
solution to the same problem. 

Jimmy
```

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:

>No, I'm not going to rush to the defence of OO ;-) What's the point, you
>and Mr. Moore would rather die at the stake than accept it 'might' just
