```
i can accept it might be useful... but the first time a person makes
an application, they probably do it structured. my question is not can
it be done oo, but how easy is it to move from structured to oo.

for instance, take the following code:

000690 MAIN SECTION.
000700 FIRST-PARA.
000710     OPEN INPUT CUSTOMER.
000720     OPEN OUTPUT ZIPSORT.
000730     DISPLAY SPACE LINE 1 ERASE.
000740     DISPLAY "=== CUSTOMER LABELS ===" LINE 2
000750        POSITION 28.
000760     DISPLAY "1 - ALL CUSTOMERS BY ZIPCODE" LINE 4
000770        POSITION 28.
000780     DISPLAY "2 - CUSTOMERS BY SALESMAN" LINE 6
000790        POSITION 28.
000800 GET-SEL.
000810     ACCEPT SELNR LINE 8 POSITION 28 PROMPT CONVERT.
000820     DISPLAY SELNR LINE 8 POSITION 28.
000830     IF SELNR = 1 OR SELNR = 2
000840        NEXT SENTENCE
000850     ELSE
000860        GO TO GET-SEL.
000870*    IF SELNR = 1 GO TO SORT-PARA.
           IF SELNR = 1 GO TO SI-000.
000880     DISPLAY "ENTER SALESMAN #" LINE 12 POSITION 28.
000890 GET-SM.
000900     ACCEPT SM LINE 12 POSITION 45 PROMPT CONVERT.
000910     IF SM = 0 GO TO GET-SM.
000920     DISPLAY SM LINE 12 POSITION 45.
001010 SI-000.
001020     READ CUSTOMER NEXT AT END GO TO SI-999.
001030     IF FSTATUS = "99" GO TO SI-000.
001040     IF SELNR = 1 GO TO SI-001.
001050     IF SM NOT = CUST-SALESMAN GO TO SI-000.
001060     MOVE CUST-NO TO ZIPSORT-CUST ZIPSORT-CNO.
001070     WRITE ZIPSORT-RECORD.
001080     GO TO SI-000.
001090 SI-001.
001100     MOVE ZIPCODE TO ZIPSORT-KEY.
001110     MOVE CUST-NO TO ZIPSORT-CNO.
           IF ZIPSORT-RECORD > LOW-VALUES
001120       WRITE ZIPSORT-RECORD.
001130     GO TO SI-000.
001140 SI-999.
           CLOSE CUSTOMER ZIPSORT.
           DISPLAY SPACE LINE 1 ERASE.
           STOP RUN.

how would you make it into oo? you have to assume alot of the
production code is not commented.
```

```
It looks like JSP-cobol generated to OSVS.
No, it is not preferable to CONVERT anything from 10-15 years back to OO. An
OO application is made by someone who knows about OOA/OOD. The designsteps
are totally different from traditional analysis.

The closest you can get is a very usefull feature in MF. If you compile with
a compiler directive 'MFOO', the sections will be methods. That is not meant
as conversion, but a way of invoking the old applications from the newly
developed OO. A temp solution to be used until the old stuff is re-analyzed
and re-programmed.

Regards
Ib
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The Wrong People

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38EA93C6.F80342F6@home.com>`
- **References:** `<8canls$i42$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38E94DC3.698AA8C1@home.com> <v6ckes0clkv256neuuumc7ob0r85t5kr3c@4ax.com>`

```


G Moore wrote:
> 
> "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> for instance, take the following code:

OK for starters - and this is important - I would gauge my learning as
follows - OO - 20% - those #@#$#@ Windows GUIs - 80% ! Bear in mind
because of money constraints I do it all in NetExpress, OO, GUIs and
printing. I'm sure I would have preferred SP2 or ScreenIO for the GUI
bit - but wasn't aware of their existence when I got started with
Visoc/NetExpress.

Now what you show as code is a 'classical' structured - but - it isn't
split into three tiered; I mentioned that one before. Take all those
steps you include opening, reading, writing, closing, displaying,
getting menu choices etc.... Across an application or many applications
they are extremely repetitive steps. Now you can achieve the same in
structured but the key in OO has to be the buzz-word re-usability. Where
can I re-use the same class/methods for a different file(object) or
different display(object).

It requires some serious up-front analysis/planning - more so than
structured, I would suggest. If you can plan at the beginning, looking
for similarities then you can design with that thought in mind. All my
flat files follow the same routines, duplicating classes to access each
file, sequential, relative, indexed, prime or alternate keys etc... Same
goes for 'windowing' - rather than re-invent the wheel for each dialog I
now have myDialog.cbl; pass the IDs of controls to this generic class
and let it worry about setup and returning events to the BusinessLogic.

That other thread where people are saying how a file should be
processed, the common classes take care of all open/close/read/read
next/read previous/start/write/rewrite/delete etc.. and for every access
check the file-status, even for the good "0?'s".

Back to your example - bear in mind I'm thinking 3-tier and much of it
is going on in 'subsidiary' classes - it just isn't easy to illustrate
in brief. (If I could take your sample and re-produce it in 4 to 10
pages - it wouldn't be OO). Bear with me - intrigued by my approach, Ed
Arranga of cobolreport.com has asked me to write a series of articles on
OO. For want of a title you could call it 'How this Dummy Did It'. It
wont be picture perfect and no doubt I've goofed on OO theory - but it
works.

To get some of the answers to what you are asking, you'll patiently have
to await publication (probably April/May). It will show you what I was
thinking, some of the goofs, and how I arrived at where I am. We all
develop a particular style, whether or not mine is easily readable or
not, I don't know. Ken and Thane say it wasn't difficult to follow - and
for Howard's eyes - that means it is maintainable. 

Then you take it from there - you either arrive at the conclusion that
the whole thing is hogwash or perhaps, 'Hey maybe this guy's got a
point, but the jerk could have done it better or more efficiently'. To
the latter - Good - bring me up-to-date ! 

Jimmy
```

```
> production code is not commented.

> reply to email gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com remove the spam

I could simply rewrite this in OO.  This one program would not
necessarily be worth it, one page turns into four say.  First I would
make the program readable by restructuring it to separate the menu
functions and the business logic.  I would then create a generic menu
object and use that generic object adding options and methods to the
menu.  This one program would not be worth the effort of building the
logic of the menu, but I assume that there are many programs in the
system all with menus.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

#### ↳ Re: The Wrong People

- **From:** "David Olson" <dqolson@fastdial.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sen50nlmrfg118@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<8canls$i42$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Basically I tend to agree that the last place a "computer scientist" should
get anywhere near is a business application...and most especially a banking
application:)

The ATM example is something I *do* know about (and lots of
sleep-deprivation getting ATM systems up).  In real life, the ATM example is
both simpler and more complex than what the example shows.

Simpler because the card data and the pin are encrypted by the hardware in
the ATM. The ATM only recieves an ACK (account, bank and PIN OK) or a NAK.

More complex when the rest of the transactuion is processed: The trans has
to be routed to the right bank/system where the account is checked not only
for available balance, but holds, and daily limits.

If all is still well...the debit is still only temporarily held until the
ATM returns a code that the requested amount was sucessfully dispensed. ATMs
can and do run out of money, misfeed, or otherwise screw up. Sometimes they
can fix themselves (like dispense 3 tens instead of a ten and a twenty if it
runs out of twenties) Sometimes the ATM only knows something went wrong, and
returns a "best guess" at what really happened.

Only after everything is good does the ATM tell the main database to debit
the money from the account. And even then, it's still only a "memo post"
until the big batch run .... but thats a whole nother tale:)

But the point is that while a few newer ATMs have Client-like capabilities
(and Yup there are PCs inside them) for the most part the logic is still
really "dumb terminal" stuff for the most part. Most of the data only flows
*from* the ATM. What is returned is mostly just commands to tell the ATM
what to do. About all that gets sent back as data is transaction ID,
transaction amount, and, if used, account balances.
```

#### ↳ Re: The Wrong People

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-04-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38EA0061.9F3100F9@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<8canls$i42$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Foodman wrote:
> 
> "here are some rules of thumb that you can apply to see if you're
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>    share data?
 
> 2.get and set functions are evil. (They're just elaborate ways to
> make the data public.)
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> ---Isn't this like a sub-routine?

POints 1,2 and 3

If you cannot utilize information then you are reinventing the wheel. 
The point is that a lot of systems share data when they should share
something else.

For example a stock control system:

procedural:

	subtract qty-sold from qty-on-hand

OO:
	invoke "sell" from product using qty.

What is the difference.

I can replace the second with complex multiple update logic or even an
Remote procedure call to a central server without changing the
business logic.

Yes you could do the same thing in procedural, and you really should,
but you do not think that way.  Bad OO programmers do not always think
that way as well and expose there data items.

> 5. All objects must provide their own UI."
> 
> ---I assume that the UI is presented on the screen. How can the
>    object know about the current state of the screen if all objects
>    provide their own UI? Do they consult each other?

1.  The solution can 'draw through' the UI.  You implement the UI as a
set of primitives and draw it through your business application.

2.  This point is hotly contested in the OO group.  Model View
Controller (commonly referred to as MVC) is the common way to develop
a
UI and this is totally opposite to what Holub proposes (look at
www.oma.com for a MVC description)

>                    *           *           *
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> ---Sorry, but that doesn't sound good to me.

Complex systems are the norm now.  Isolation of change is the trick
that programmers must manage.

> "The idea is to organize the inevitable complexity inherent in real
> computer  programs, not to eliminate it. Object-oriented designers,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>    such as calculating taxes, etc.

These would be two objects an access method that is 'contained' in the
business object.  We can replace the access object with another
implementation totally.

> 
> ---The former can be eliminated by providing a universal model for
>    that class of applications. The latter you are stuck with.

Are you.  There are a surprising number of high level processes that
could be reused but procedural does not support reuse only code
copying.

> ---Although the elimination of one type of complexity is
>    possible, the propOOnents make no attempt to remove it because
>    they do not recognize the possibility.

Huh?

> ---(If the object-oriented designers can't eliminate complexity,
>    perhaps we should eliminate the object-oriented designers.)

Complexity is inherent in development today.  Should we remove
development?

>                    *           *           *
> 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>    to enter the Great Programming Contest?

I do not fully subscribe to this.  It is a perception of complexity,
taken out of context it can be misconstrued.

>                    *           *           *
> 
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>    from which you extract a string" to get a name.

What about if you have name scattered through 500 programs in your
suite.  The name must change from 40 bytes long to 50 bytes long.  How
do you do this reliably?  OO encapsulates the length in one place so
the update is one change in one place and a recompile.

>                    *           *           *
> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>    need a good talking-to.

This is reality, it happens every single day in this business.  The
system designed by someone familiar with all the employee numbers is
replaced by someone else and they need the names as confirmation.

>                    *           *           *
> 
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>    integer'.

A cobol programmer would not store the number as a 16 bit integer.
A business programmer may not be so restricted.

> 
> 3. The user requests a withdrawal.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>    NG for business.

OK replace this with number is PIC 9(13)V99 because that is all Cobol
can cope with today.  What happens tomorrow with 31 digit numbers?
Same problem different descriptions.

> 5. If the balance is large enough, the machine dispenses the cash, and
>    then posts an "update the balance for this user" to the server."
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> ---So, my ATM solution has a SINGLE COBOL program.

This is the way an ATM works to reduce the flow of data to and from
the
server.  Would you break a good hardware design to make it easier to
program?  The flow of information between ATM's is a huge business ask
any telco.
> 
>    1. Accepts card number and PIN from customer.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
   Pass!
>                    *           *           *
> 
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
This is called teaching.  None of the programs I wrote at Uni were
real.

> "Similarly, the 16-bit int used for the PIN can hold at most four
> decimal digits. What if Bill wants to use "GATES" (five digits)
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>    and why should they?

My pin is a word, it is keyed in on a number pad.  Have you not
used an ATM lately nor a mobile phone.  Yes they do, in Australia
some banks allow 6 digit pins, others only four.  This is called
market differentiation.

> 
> "The procedural solution to all these problems is to change the ROMs in
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>    program, it wouldn't be necessary to update each ATM at all.

A compiled Java byte code is tiny.  You are also changing a hardware
premise that was designed with data traffic in mind.  ATMs are a
good design for what they do.
> 
>                    *           *           *
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>    in languages other than COBOL (see above).

You have never given a compiled copy to be loaded on another machine?
Deployment is a very real business issue.

> 
>                    *           *           *
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>    foresight.

Yes it can, but should it.  If you work out every nuance in a design
you will build in so much complexity into your solution as to make it
unmaintainable.  OO helps this problem.

The most flexible system I ever wrote never had a parameter change and
was just decommissioned.  That flexibility was wasted!

>                    *           *           *
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>    Object-oriented design will create different nightmares.

Your experience here is....

>                    *           *           *
> 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Thanks for you opinions,  they are well thought out. I hope that
you will allow others to trade OO thoughts without interruption.
Any questions you have about OO, without baiting argument, please
feel free to jump in.

I know your opinions and I do actually understand them because
I had the same reservations for years. I have been converted by
keeping an open mind and like Pete one thing really did it for
me, your 'path to the truth' will no doubt be unique :-}
  Apologies to the JC fraternity.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: The Wrong People

- **From:** G Moore <gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-04T00:00:00

```
>> ---A business (COBOL) programmer would never "use a textfield
>>    from which you extract a string" to get a name.

>What about if you have name scattered through 500 programs in your
>suite.  The name must change from 40 bytes long to 50 bytes long.  How
>do you do this reliably?  OO encapsulates the length in one place so
>the update is one change in one place and a recompile.

this is false. first, the recompile spans all locations of the
previous variables locations. secondly, a normal editor would be able
to do the same thing as well. and maybe some of us want to go through
and see all the changes in the code, for instance a multiply with an
on size error might have a different meaning if you increase a single
variable.

>> ---A business programmer would never store the balance as a
>>    '32-bit float'. Just another example which proves Java is
>>    NG for business.

>OK replace this with number is PIC 9(13)V99 because that is all Cobol
>can cope with today.  What happens tomorrow with 31 digit numbers?
>Same problem different descriptions.

cobol today can cope with 31 digits, if i remember the new standard.
now maybe an older compiler can't, but you'd also have to upgrade your
OO compiler to go to more digits.

>> ---Using COBOL and SP2 the SAME program can run on a stand-alone
>>    PC, be executed by stations on a LAN directly from the server,
>>    and, be executed as a thin client. The SAME physical program.
 
>> ---So, my ATM solution has a SINGLE COBOL program.

>This is the way an ATM works to reduce the flow of data to and from
>the
>server.  Would you break a good hardware design to make it easier to
>program?  The flow of information between ATM's is a huge business ask
>any telco.

the hardware isn't half as expensive as the technician required to
replace it, or programmer (unless you are dealing with the server
end).

>> ---Of course, if the ATM was a node on a network, running a COBOL
>>    program, it wouldn't be necessary to update each ATM at all.

>A compiled Java byte code is tiny.  You are also changing a hardware
>premise that was designed with data traffic in mind.  ATMs are a
>good design for what they do.

the size of the compiler, to me, is irrelevant. the only machine which
needs one on it should be the mainframe. the point of c/s is the
client (ATM) is insecure. yes, it's possible to load up the machine
with *gobs* of memory and it's own compiler. why bother, if it takes a
few seconds to type in the pin?

reply to email gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com remove the spam
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The Wrong People

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-04-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38EB4D14.DC56900A@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<8canls$i42$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38EA0061.9F3100F9@zip.com.au> <1sckesk23g18jk06e9u3nuefhhiesschfv@4ax.com>`

```
G Moore wrote:
> 
> >> ---A business (COBOL) programmer would never "use a textfield
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> variable.

The point is that you need to change 500 plus programs.  With a
centralized object you would change it ONCE.  You ONLY recompile the
object that stores the data NOT the system, it does not know about a
customer it just uses the object customer.  This is the fundamental
difference, try to understand it and you have why it works.

> >> ---A business programmer would never store the balance as a
> >>    '32-bit float'. Just another example which proves Java is
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> your OO compiler to go to more digits.

Your point against the argument is....

If you need 31 digit accuracy due to a business need (and it happens)
then you upgrade your compiler.  Now you need to change code and the
real cost starts.

> >> ---Using COBOL and SP2 the SAME program can run on a stand-alone
> >>    PC, be executed by stations on a LAN directly from the server,
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> end).

your point is....

This is nothing to do with OO.  It is an efficient technical model
with trade-offs.

> >> ---Of course, if the ATM was a node on a network, running a COBOL
> >>    program, it wouldn't be necessary to update each ATM at all.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> it takes a few seconds to type in the pin?

The ATM does NOT have a compiler, you transmit the compiled version of
the program.  Transfer a small program once every three months of say
10K to stop many transmissions to the server of 1K for each
transaction.  The data traffic cost saving is phenomenal.  If you
service EVERY keystroke through the central server then you would have
to at least triple the size of your central server.  Your typical
microwave can perform more complex programs than the ATM performs.

This is nothing to do with OO.  It is an efficient technical model
with trade-offs.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The Wrong People

```
Ken Foskey wrote:
> 
> The point is that you need to change 500 plus programs.  With a
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> difference, try to understand it and you have why it works.

Then you need to test 500 programs, and when you migrate to production
all 500 programs will be migrated together.

That is if all 500 programs work correctly.  If one of them uses the
object differently than expected, you may need to make changes and then
retest all 500 programs again.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The Wrong People

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38EC11F0.A46FA309@home.com>`
- **References:** `<8canls$i42$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38EA0061.9F3100F9@zip.com.au> <1sckesk23g18jk06e9u3nuefhhiesschfv@4ax.com> <38EB4D14.DC56900A@zip.com.au> <38EB80E1.56CDE529@cusys.edu>`

```


Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> Ken Foskey wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> retest all 500 programs again.

Sheesh ! Maintenance, maintenance..... Now we know your daddy was in the
USAF - an engineer, airframe mechanic, electrical engineer ....? (Like
father, like son :-) )

It's not all doom and gloom Howard. There sure as hell aren't 500
different methods to access the customer DB ! Possibly some 10 - 15
spread between the 500 'callers'. Agreed the class containing the 15
methods might do 'nested' or 'private' calls to methods contained
therein, but not direct invokes from 'callers'. I'm guessing any good
editor would now allow you to do a search. Test method-x against, say
two programs.

For real - I'm doing it. Have a Description File, fixed format and
record-type tells me which type of description, Technicians' names,
Equipment names, Company names etc..... As I've developed, I've changed
my approach within the DBI class - oooh - which 'callers' have I
affected ? Assume method was "get-name-from-object". A quick global
search in the IDE gives me classes invoking that method - even where I'm
introducing a new method or changing an exisiting one - it is no big
deal. Test a couple of times against 1 or 2 classes invoking the method
- not 500 !

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The Wrong People

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** G Moore <gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<j1anesc3ms1sictm55qgodq0jhsodlctkd@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<8canls$i42$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38EA0061.9F3100F9@zip.com.au> <1sckesk23g18jk06e9u3nuefhhiesschfv@4ax.com> <38EB4D14.DC56900A@zip.com.au>`

```
>The point is that you need to change 500 plus programs.  With a
>centralized object you would change it ONCE.  You ONLY recompile the
>object that stores the data NOT the system, it does not know about a
>customer it just uses the object customer.  This is the fundamental
>difference, try to understand it and you have why it works.

it seems the same as one monolithic program with a single ws section
to me. 

there ain't no such thing as a free lunch.

>> cobol today can cope with 31 digits, if i remember the new standard.
>> now maybe an older compiler can't, but you'd also have to upgrade
>> your OO compiler to go to more digits.

>Your point against the argument is....

>If you need 31 digit accuracy due to a business need (and it happens)
>then you upgrade your compiler.  Now you need to change code and the
>real cost starts.

compiler change = $1000 

< 

finding all instances of pic 9(30) = 1 grep and replace command

>> >> ---Of course, if the ATM was a node on a network, running a COBOL
>> >>    program, it wouldn't be necessary to update each ATM at all.
 
>> >A compiled Java byte code is tiny.  You are also changing a hardware
>> >premise that was designed with data traffic in mind.  ATMs are a
>> >good design for what they do.
 
>> the size of the compiler, to me, is irrelevant. the only machine
>> which needs one on it should be the mainframe. the point of c/s is
>> the client (ATM) is insecure. yes, it's possible to load up the
>> machine with *gobs* of memory and it's own compiler. why bother, if
>> it takes a few seconds to type in the pin?

>The ATM does NOT have a compiler, you transmit the compiled version of
>the program.  Transfer a small program once every three months of say
>10K to stop many transmissions to the server of 1K for each
>transaction.  The data traffic cost saving is phenomenal.

i don't believe the major cost of traffic is the fact you have the
data stored non-locally, and the startup phone call.

maybe atms work differently from card swipers, but they seem to take
the same amount of time.

reply to email gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com remove the spam
```

## Thread: new errorfile found (great) — 6 msgs

### new errorfile found (great)

- **From:** Stefan Meyer <meyerst@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ca9q5$1ov$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hi,

I just found a new "error-file" after opening a corrupt MF-ISAM-File
using MF Cobol Version V4.1 revision 6 running on Unix. I got Error
9/199 and found the following information in the file
MFIOERROR.newnumber:

userid = polen
progname = FHREDIR
filename = FAPOS.IT
organization = 2
open mode = 128
operation = 0
last status = 0/0
errno = 25
date = 000403 time = 15584900

Every time I open this file I get a new MFIOERR.newnumber file.

I have never seen this before and I realy didn't change anything.

But it's great and I would like to get this file everytime a hard-error
occurs. But how can I enable/disable this feature?

Does anybody know where I can switch it on/off?

TIA - Stefan Meyer


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: new errorfile found (great)

- **From:** Rich Rohde <richNOriSPAM@richware.net.invalid>
- **Date:** 2000-04-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1ef90677.4b1325a4@usw-ex0101-005.remarq.com>`
- **References:** `<8ca9q5$1ov$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Stefan,

199 is a run-time system message. "Operating System error code
lies outside expected range (Fatal).

o A system call has returned an unexpected error number which is
not documented.

o Contact Technical Support who will help you to discover the
specific cause of this error.

This comes from the Micro Focus COBOL error message manual.

Regards,
Rich

* Sent from RemarQ http://www.remarq.com The Internet's Discussion Network *
The fastest and easiest way to search and participate in Usenet - Free!
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: new errorfile found (great)

- **From:** Stefan Meyer <meyerst@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8cc3ta$2dl$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8ca9q5$1ov$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <1ef90677.4b1325a4@usw-ex0101-005.remarq.com>`

```

thanks for the information but I was wondering about the MFIOERR-File
only. The errorcode is known and handled by the program. I just forget
that this file is corrupt when starting the program....

Bye - Stefan Meyer
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Get name of PC

- **From:** "Lucas, Todd" <todd.lucas@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38e90512_4@news1.prserv.net>`
- **References:** `<8cagp1$s0o$1@news.mclink.it> <8carpu$n1h$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I use the following when running under Micro Focus Cobol Workbench,
NetExpress, or Mainframe Express:

       environment division.
       special-names.
           call-convention 66 is win32nol.

       working-storage section.
       01  c3comreg-data-length            pic s9(9) comp-5.
       01  c3comreg-data                      pic x(8192).

Procedure Division code:
      * Get the currently logged-on UserName:
                   move spaces                 to c3comreg-data
                   move length of c3comreg-data to c3comreg-data-length
                   call win32nol 'GetUserNameA' using
                       by reference c3comreg-data
                       by reference c3comreg-data-length
                   end-call

      * Get the computer name:
                   move spaces                 to c3comreg-data
                   move length of c3comreg-data to c3comreg-data-length
                   call win32nol 'GetComputerNameA' using
                       by reference c3comreg-data
                       by reference c3comreg-data-length
                   end-call


Parameters
c3comreg-data = Points to a buffer to receive the null-terminated
character string containing the computer name.
c3comreg-data-length = Points to a variable that specifies the maximum
size, in characters, of the buffer. This value should be large enough to
contain MAX_COMPUTERNAME_LENGTH + 1 characters.

Return Values
If the function succeeds, the return value is nonzero and the variable
represented by the length parameter contains the number of characters
copied to the destination buffer, not including the terminating null
character.
If the function fails, the return value is zero. To get extended error
information, call GetLastError.
```

<a id="t-918f108dec"></a>
## Thread: DB2 Collection names with MF Cobol — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### DB2 Collection names with MF Cobol

- **From:** iboyes@ibcs.co.nz (Ian Boyes)
- **Date:** 2000-04-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.databases.ibm-db2,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38e8f16a.252728@news.clear.net.nz>`

```
Hi

I am running MFcobol with DB2 Connect 6.1 on HP Ux

When I am compiling MF Cobol programs with embedded DB2 SQL, a package
is created in the database with a collection name of the user
currently connected to the database. I am trying to get the compile to
generate a package with a different collection name.

If I was using a bind statement I can define the collection like
'bind program.bnd collection unix '
but since the bind statement is embedded into the cobol run time
system, I am not sure how to define the collection name

Thanks for any help
Ian Boyes
```

#### ↳ Re: DB2 Collection names with MF Cobol

- **From:** "Randy Zimmerman" <rzmerant@execpc.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.databases.ibm-db2,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38e9126c$0$87056@news.execpc.com>`
- **References:** `<38e8f16a.252728@news.clear.net.nz>`

```
If you are using the Server Express product, you can use the DB2 directive
to invoke the MF DB2 precompiler with COLLECTION option.  The Database
Access manual should have details.

If you are using an older MF product, try the SQL directive with COLLECTION
option.  There should be a DB2 readme that details syntax for new directives
in the mf install directory.

IBM also supplies a precompiler that is documented in the Command reference
under PRECOMPILE.

In general MF maps the IBM precompiler directives using the similar
syntax/options.


"Ian Boyes" <iboyes@ibcs.co.nz> wrote in message
news:38e8f16a.252728@news.clear.net.nz...
> Hi
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Ian Boyes
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38e90740.261244025@209.98.98.12>`

```
We currently have an accounting app written in MF COBOL (for UNIX).
We would like to use this app in an environment that uses a DBCS.  The
Oracle database has been created to use the required character set.
Does the MF COBOL source code require any modification to support a
DBCS?

I thank you in advance for your time!

Jason Martin
```

#### ↳ Re: Double Byte Character Set (DBCS) application

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8cb2i4$a1c$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<38e90740.261244025@209.98.98.12>`

```
Look at PICTURE "N" and USAGE NATIONAL for support of DBCS characters

I am not positive about UNIX, but there are restriction on how it works on
WinTel depending upon whether or not you have a "dbcs" enabled machine and
OS.
```

```
LEGAL CABLE TV DE-SCRAMBLER

Want to watch Sporting Events?--Movies?--Pay-Per-View??

*This is the Famous R-O Shack TV Descrambler 
You can assemble it from Radio Shack parts for about $12 to $15.

We Send You: 
1, E-Z To follow Assembly Instructions. 
2. E-Z To read Original Drawings. 
3. Total Parts List.

**** PLUS SOMETHING NEW YOU MUST HAVE! ****
Something you can't do without.

THE UP-TO-DATE 6 PAGE REPORT: 
USING A DESCRAMBLER LEGALLY

Warning: You should not build a TV Descrambler without 
reading this report first.

Frequently Asked Questions--CABLE TV DESCRAMBLER

Q: Will the descrambler work on Fiber, TCI, Jarrod 
and satellite systems? 
A: The answer is YES. In respect to satellite, 
you just get more stuff! There is one exception: 
The descrambler will not work with DSS satellite.

Q: Do I need a converter box? 
A: This plan works with or without a converter box. 
Specific instructions are included in the plans for each!
Q: Can the cable company detect that I have the descrambler? 

A: No, the signal descrambles right at the box and does 
not move back through the line!

Q: Do I have to alter my existing cable system, 
television or VCR? 
A: The answer is no!

Q: Does this work with my remote control? 
A: The answer is yes. The descrambler is 
manually controlled--but very easy to use!

Q: Can you email me the plans?
A: No the program comes with an easy to follow picture guide.

Q: Does this work everywhere across the country? 
A: Yes, everywhere in the USA plus England, 
Brazil, Canada and other countries!

Q: Is this deal guaranteed? 
A: Yes, if you are unhappy for any reason we will refund your money.

Q: When I order, when will I get my package? 
A: We mail out all orders within 24 hours of receiving them.
 
Q: How much does it cost to get the instruction 
plans, the easy to follow diagram, and most 
important of all the "Using a Descrambler LEGALLY Report".
A: You get the complete 6 page report and instruction package all for just--$10.00 

Q: How do I order? 
A: Fill out form below and send it, along
   with your $10.00 payment to:

CABLE FREE-TV
12187 S. Orange Blossom Trail #116
Orlando Fl 32837
 
(Cash, Check or Money Order.) 

(Florida residents include 7% Florida State Sales Tax) 

(All orders outside the U.S.A. add $5.00)


PRINT YOUR:

NAME______________________________________________________

ADDRESS___________________________________________________

CITY/STATE/ZIP____________________________________________

E-MAIl ADDRESS____________________________________________ 

We are NOT ASSOCIATED in any way with RADIO SHACK. 
Neither the design nor instructions were developed 
by, are sold by, or are endorsed by Radio Shack. 
Parts for this fine-tuning device are available 
at many electronics stores (including Radio Shack) 
This is not a Radio Shack product.
```

## Thread: Sql Server — 18 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Sql Server

- **From:** "Kurt Barlar" <kbarlar@larimore.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<CJoG4.3$jA6.206231@news1.i1.net>`

```
My company is looking to switch over to sql server and I am in need of
sample code accessing sql server through Cobol, if anyone can help it would
be greatly appreciated.

Thanx!
Kurt B
```

#### ↳ Re: Sql Server

- **From:** "ChrisOsborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uYETzKmn$GA.235@cpmsnbbsa03>`
- **References:** `<CJoG4.3$jA6.206231@news1.i1.net>`

```
I've never done COBOL CLI (call level interface) programming, but that's the
subject you'd want to look up.  That, and whether or not you have an ODBC
driver for your particular compiler in order to allow for communication
between SQL Server and COBOL.  Oh, and maybe also see if you need a COBOL
pre-compiler for the CLI statements (not sure, but you definitely need a
pre-compiler for embedded SQL).


"Kurt Barlar" <kbarlar@larimore.net> wrote in message
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Sql Server

- **From:** "jess.morgan" <jess.morgan@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6hPG4.903$4A1.42535@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<CJoG4.3$jA6.206231@news1.i1.net> <uYETzKmn$GA.235@cpmsnbbsa03>`

```
I'm still working on the development of this, but I'll give an overview.  I
am using COBOL CLI in a CICS transaction to call a transaction on MTS/COMTI
(running on SNA Server).  The COMTI component will (hopefully) kick off a VB
program.  In the VB program, I can connect to SQL Server.  My goal is to
transfer data from the mainframe to the PC so that it can be viewed and
edited via intranet/internet access.  I am have already developed the
transfer of the data back to the mainframe using COMTI.

I can post some sample COBOL CLI code if you're interested.

Jess
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sql Server

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "jess.morgan" <jess.morgan@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%3bH4.1055$8v5.63985@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<CJoG4.3$jA6.206231@news1.i1.net> <uYETzKmn$GA.235@cpmsnbbsa03> <6hPG4.903$4A1.42535@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <38ecac6a.36231331@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
I already responded personally to Thane, but figured I should post something
as well... just in case anyone else is reading.  It turns out that Microsoft
ComTI doesn't support host initiated transactions.  I was previously told
(by MS) that this feature would be available with it's latest and greatest
invention HIS 2000 (Host Integration Server).   Today I was told (by MS,
again) that this functionality is not available and they do not know when it
will be.  So, although COBOL/CICS CLI (call level interface) is an option
when connecting to programs on different platforms, it is not the solution
for connecting COBOL/CICS to SQL Server.  If anyone else has any helpful
hints, our ears and minds are open!
Jess
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Sql Server

- **From:** "DennisHarley" <LegacyBlue@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eR5A4YKo$GA.233@cpmsnbbsa03>`
- **References:** `<CJoG4.3$jA6.206231@news1.i1.net> <8chtff$gj7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

Foodman <foodman123@aol.com> wrote in message
news:8chtff$gj7$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> In article <CJoG4.3$jA6.206231@news1.i1.net>,
>   "Kurt Barlar" <kbarlar@larimore.net> wrote:
> > My company is looking to switch over to sql server and I am in
need of
> > sample code accessing sql server through Cobol, if anyone can help
it
> would
> > be greatly appreciated.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Tony Dilworth

Tony,

I'm getting psychic or psychotic, I knew what your post would say
before I opened it.

If you visit the IBM Web site.
http://www-4.ibm.com/software/data/db2/
You will find a free Relational Database system to play with.
Look for:
Download a free copy of DB2 Universal Database Personal Developer's
Edition V6.1 for Windows NT, Windows 95/98, OS/2, or Linux; or get a
free 60-day trial CD-ROM with DB2 Universal Database V6.1 for Windows
NT, Windows 95/98, Linux, OS/2, AIX, HP-UX, Sun Solaris, or SCO.

I would also recommend reading some books on SQL, and Relational
Databases in general.

If you spend some time, I am sure you will find it worthwhile.
You will either have a convincing argument for not using SQL, or you
will find that you like it, and wonder why everyone isn't using it.
```

```
I went to the site and began the download (after going through a
registration process).

The transfer rate immediately dropped to 3.0 KB/second, with an estimated
transfer time of over 11 hours and 20 minutes (yeah, I know how unreliable
the counter is).  This for the first "common" .zip file at 84 megs.  The
webpage notes that you should also download the "english" .zip file at about
31 megs, as well.

It's probably best to get ready to do the download right before you go to
bed.
```

```
The install went okay, but none of the applications would run because I'm on
Win2000.

Microsoft seems to be doing a good job at keeping other people out of its
systems.  SQL Server 7.0, which was not designed from the beginning to work
with Win2000 (only NT 4.0, which DB2 is also available for), installed and
worked.

Oh well, maybe IBM will come up with a compatible version soon.
```

```
other features that you won't want to download but would investigate if you
had. FWIW, I downloaded UDB 6.1 a couple of months ago. According to my logs
it took 3 minutes or so for all 6 files (base, language module, extensions,
etc.). Aren't cable modems wonderful?

Karl
```

```
2000-04-07 10:20:30 MESZ

Kurt Barlar wrote:
> 
> My company is looking to switch over to sql server and I
> am in need of sample code accessing sql server through
> Cobol, if anyone can help it would
> be greatly appreciated.

I do not like doing advertizing my employers products,
but in this case, I may hint at the compined abilities
of Cobol - ESQL - the Sesam Database, all on the BS2000
Plattform. Its a reliable workhorse, but, like many
mainframe solutions, its very expensive.

I have to mention this, although I do not like Cobol
very much.

You may find some info at
http://www.siemens.de/servers/bs2000/bs2_us.htm

And I may tell you a secret: The BS2000-Cobol
now can do objects. The ESQL-PreCompiler cannot (yet).
So, running ESQL in Object-Cobol, you may encounter the
most interesting situations, because ESQL-PreCompiler
Cobol-Compiler see quite different things in the source!
- Much more entertaining than solving crossword-puzzles.

Herwig
```

<a id="t-7d3ed5dc4a"></a>
## Thread: MF DialogSystem vs. Everyone else — 17 msgs

### MF DialogSystem vs. Everyone else

- **From:** "Bill Wood" <nospambeavis27@bigfoot.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<#cw8h1in$GA.232@cpmsnbbsa03>`

```
I recently ran into a problem that I thought was a bug, but MicroFocus tells
me it is expected behavior and I'm curious if everyone agrees that it is
expected behavior.  Here is the scenario:

Put a button with a mnemonic shortcut on a window
Hide the button
Use the mnemonic to fire the button

My question is should a hidden button fire when the mnemonic is used?  I
don't think so, but Dialog and Visual C both do.  I'm curious if other
products do also.  The workaround is simple enough, disable the button when
you hide it and enable it when you show it.  If we had known about this 2
years ago when we started the project that would be no problem, but our
application currently has over 500 screensets and that is a lot of
retro-fitting.  Anyway, I'm just curious how other products work and what
other people think.  Thanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: MF DialogSystem vs. Everyone else

- **From:** "Lucas, Todd" <todd.lucas@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38ea03aa_1@news1.prserv.net>`
- **References:** `<#cw8h1in$GA.232@cpmsnbbsa03>`

```
Or totally dump Dialog System altogether and use a Visual Basic
front-end that calls your Cobol business logic contained in a DLL
created by NetExpress.  I used to be a firm supporter of Dialog - the
only reason was that it worked on OS/2 and Windows.  Now that we have
dropped support for OS/2, VB is the front-end GUI of choice.

Just a thought...

TL
```

```
"Lucas, Todd" wrote:
>

Whoa there ! Don't go dumping anything. (BTW I don't use Dialog System).
From correspondence I see so many different options being used :-

- Fujitsu	 - Thane Hubbell	- SP2
- Fujitsu	 - Pete Dashwood	- Activex
- Fujitsu	 - Don Tees 	  	- Screen Section and  SP2 
- NetExpress	 - Ken Mullins 		- Win APIs
- NetExpress	 - Simon Hart 		- OO/GUIs, D/S and Activex
- NetExpress	 - Jim Smith 		- OO/GUIs
- NetExpress	 - Gene Webb		- OO Visual Basic
- NetExpress	 - Yours truly		- OO/GUIs

Each of the above will give you a sound argument for why they took their
particular route. Which one is right - none - they are all different
solutions. There is a time investment in learning a particular tool,
plus all that code you have written using the 'old' tool. It requires
careful thought before you make a switch. Each new tool means a new
learning curve.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
>

>Whoa there ! Don't go dumping anything. (BTW I don't use Dialog System).
>From correspondence I see so many different options being used :-
>
>- Fujitsu - Thane Hubbell - SP2
>- Fujitsu - Pete Dashwood - Activex

For MS WINDOWS GUI development I use Fujitsu PowerCOBOL with ActiveX
components where possible. For GUI platform independence and Web Enablement
(where required) I am moving to JAVA and CORBA, with Fujitsu OOCOBOL 97
routines in the background for data processing which is not what JAVA does
best. I am still firmly persuaded that component based development is the
way to go, and I believe OOP is the best way to build the components. (That
is NOT a criticism of structured COBOL, which has served us long and well
over many years.)

Horses for courses. No Evangelism, no bigotry, and totally respect everyone
else's right to do it differently. Getting systems developed is hard enough
without vacillating over what tool to use. I use whatever works, with the
shortest learning curve and the quickest return on investment.

>- Fujitsu - Don Tees   - Screen Section and  SP2
>- NetExpress - Ken Mullins - Win APIs
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>learning curve.

Yes it does, Jimmy. Fortunately each one you learn makes the next one a bit
easier. Or maybe we just get smarter with age <grin>??

Pete.
```

```
have

I would have agreed with you in the past, but I think that VB may not
be the language of choice in that it ties you to Windows.
The ability to work on a variety of platforms should still be a
concern.
While there is no current alternative contender for the desktop OS of
choice, it may not be that way forever.

Relational Database products have implemented extensions to SQL, which
effectively bound a user to a product. When competing products came
along which offered a price/performance advantage, those who used the
extensions would have to rewrite portions of their systems to migrate
to a better product.

I think we need to develop flexible solutions.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MF DialogSystem vs. Everyone else

- **From:** "Lucas, Todd" <todd.lucas@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38ea494c_2@news1.prserv.net>`
- **References:** `<#cw8h1in$GA.232@cpmsnbbsa03> <38ea03aa_1@news1.prserv.net> <#pgEfkln$GA.351@cpmsnbbsa04>`

```
You can develop flexible solutions, as long as you keep your business
loginc and front-end user-interface separate.  There is no one GUI
development tool (that I know of) that works on all platforms.  My take
is, if you are going to do something then do it right - use the best
products available for the most widely used platform.  Like I said, we
used Dialog system successfully for more than 4 years, but it outlived
its usefulness when we dumped OS/2.  Not saying that this is the right
solution for you, but it was definitely right for us.

Thanks - TL
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MF DialogSystem vs. Everyone else

- **From:** "Lucas, Todd" <todd.lucas@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
Rich,

We use all three of the 32-bit MF products (Workbench, NetExpress, and
MFE) and Visual Basic 5 (or 6 if you prefer), and that is all as far as
development goes.  We do alot of API programming to interface with
various communications packages as well (IBM Personal Communications,
TCP/IP, APPC, CPI-C, etc.), but these are probably not required for what
you want to do - but then again only you know what you want to do.
<grin>

We simply create all of our business logic in Cobol and create either
.DLL or .EXE files, and then create a Visual Basic .EXE that invokes the
Cobol .DLL's / .Exe's.  It's really easy and simple to do.  It used to
be a big hassle with the 16-bit MF products, but they have made some
great progress here in the last few years to make their stuff "play"
better with the other "kids" on the block - VB, VC, Win32 API,
Powerbuilder, etc...

If you need some examples of VB calling Cobol just let me know and I
will send some to you off-line...

Thanks - TL
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MF DialogSystem vs. Everyone else

```
Lucas, Todd wrote in message <38ea03aa_1@news1.prserv.net>...
>Or totally dump Dialog System altogether and use a Visual Basic
>front-end that calls your Cobol business logic contained in a DLL
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>TL

Hmmm....so why would anybody dump a system because it works the way it is
SUPPOSED to (rather than the way the programmer thought it ought to...)?

This behaviour of MF DIALOG is (forgive the pun...) right on the button.

I was intrigued by your reason for using and dropping DIALOG.

I also used it (found it very good and liked the separation from the
underlying complexity of PANELS). I dropped it because I dropped MF, not
because there was anything wrong with it.

Did you really only use it because of OS/2? If so, why not use Presentation
Manager?

As for using VB as the GUI development tool of choice, yes, many
corporations are doing this and I have managed several projects where we
used VB successfully as a front end to the mainframe backend. But check
again....VB also implements the properties of a push button in exactly the
same way as DIALOG (and, in fact, anything that uses WINDOWS; it is a
WINDOWS standard).

In this instance, the fault was not with the software but with the
programmer.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MF DialogSystem vs. Everyone else

- **From:** "Lucas, Todd" <todd.lucas@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38eb5e72_3@news1.prserv.net>`
- **References:** `<#cw8h1in$GA.232@cpmsnbbsa03> <38ea03aa_1@news1.prserv.net> <8ceemu$68$2@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`

```
Can you honestly say that creating / maintaining code in Dialog System
is as easy as doing it in VB?  I certainly did not think so, not to
mention that VB has so much more to offer - not only in the base
language itself, but in third-party custom controls, OCX, etc.

Creating a screenset is fairly easy - maintaining one is altogether
different.  The only reason we used it was because the same code worked
for both OS/2 and Windows platforms.  It was hard to add new
functionality, the passing of variables between the Cobol program and
Dialog was cheesy, not to mention the un-professional look of the
application - and no, it was not the programmers fault on how it looked
either.  Throw into account the extra code to support varying screen
resolutions, and you have a mess that is a maintenance nightmare!

And like you, I wasted alot of time developing native panels
applications before "discovering" Dialog.  At that time I thought it was
the best thing since sliced bread.  I saw how much easier it was to code
an application using it vs. Panels2.  About three years and alot of
frustration later, I discovered VB and it was euphoria all over again!
I guess that's the way of nature though - start with something and
always look for a better way.

I'm relieved that we got rid of it.  Just my take...

TL
```

#### ↳ Re: MF DialogSystem vs. Everyone else

- **From:** khansen@screenio.com (Kevin Hansen)
- **Date:** 2000-04-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38ea2f31.90704996@wingate>`
- **References:** `<#cw8h1in$GA.232@cpmsnbbsa03>`

```
Just when you think you've got it figured out, someone comes up with a
new wrinkle...

We would do the same thing today in GUI ScreenIO, but you've got a
very valid point.  If an accelerator or mnemonic key is assigned to a
pushbutton or a menu selection, you'd reasonably expect the
accelerator would be disabled if the menu selection or pushbutton was
grayed out (disabled). 

Otherwise, as you discovered, the user can circumvent your disabling
of the menu item.  It strikes me as clumsy to require you to handle
the accelerator key separately in your program.  The screen manager
should take care of it automatically because the items are clearly
related.

I think we'll incorporate this disabling of the accelerator in our
next update...  Appreciate the idea!

Kevin

<nospambeavis27@bigfoot.com> wrote:

>Hide the button
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>other people think.  Thanks.

http://www.ScreenIO.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MF DialogSystem vs. Everyone else

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-04-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38ea52c7.64909286@news.cox-internet.com>`
- **References:** `<#cw8h1in$GA.232@cpmsnbbsa03> <38ea2f31.90704996@wingate>`

```
On Tue, 04 Apr 2000 18:25:14 GMT, khansen@screenio.com (Kevin Hansen)
wrote:

>Just when you think you've got it figured out, someone comes up with a
>new wrinkle...
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

There is a difference between "Greyed out" and "hidden" yes?  With
Dialog System I am fairly sure that if you grey it out it won't
respond to the accelerator key.  But hidden - I would not expect it to
respond, but I can see others making a case for having it respond --
those who live in the event driven UI world.   Without the button it
may be difficult to detect the key being pressed, so they assign ALT+H
to a hidden (as opposed to greyed out) button.  

My preference - hidden or greyed out should disable the accelerator
key (as it does with sp2).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MF DialogSystem vs. Everyone else

- **From:** khansen@screenio.com (Kevin Hansen)
- **Date:** 2000-04-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38ea5697.100790779@wingate>`
- **References:** `<#cw8h1in$GA.232@cpmsnbbsa03> <38ea2f31.90704996@wingate> <38ea52c7.64909286@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
There is indeed a difference between grayed out and hidden.  We call
'em "blind menu items" instead of hidden, but the principle is the
same;  you might not want to show an item on the menu or on a button,
but you still want it to return an event to the application.

Blind menu items can, of course, also be enabled/disabled at runtime
if you choose to do so, just as a visible button or menu selection may
be grayed out at runtime.

Kevin

On Tue, 04 Apr 2000 20:41:51 GMT, thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane
Hubbell) wrote:
>
>There is a difference between "Greyed out" and "hidden" yes?  With
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>to a hidden (as opposed to greyed out) button.
```

#### ↳ Re: MF DialogSystem vs. Everyone else

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 2000-04-05T00:00:00

```
Hey,
I beleive you started a discussion about methods and tools, but you ask
about shortcut keys in Dialog System.

I would expect all disabled objects to disabled for mouse-clicks and
shortcut keys. If you hide something, it's still there - so, disable and
then hide; that might be the solution.

On the other hand - you could have some global dialog that unconditionally
executes a procedure on an event as F5. I wouldn't expect that dialog to be
stopped, just because you disable an object.

Regards
Ib

Bill Wood skrev i meddelelsen <#cw8h1in$GA.232@cpmsnbbsa03>...
>I recently ran into a problem that I thought was a bug, but MicroFocus
tells
>me it is expected behavior and I'm curious if everyone agrees that it is
>expected behavior.  Here is the scenario:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
```

#### ↳ Re: MF DialogSystem vs. Everyone else

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-04-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ceemt$68$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<#cw8h1in$GA.232@cpmsnbbsa03>`

```
This is exactly the behaviour I would expect programming in C, VB, JAVA, or
Fujitsu PowerCOBOL.

Just because you HIDE a control does NOT mean it is disabled.

It's no reflection on MF Dialog (other than a good one; they have
implemented it the way Windows intended)

This, to me, is a perfect example of what's going wrong with COBOL.

People don't bother to read how things actually work, implement in the way
in which they think it should work, then jump up and down to get the
Language changed because their way is "better".

Take the COBOL DIVIDE for example. The original language spec. specified:
DIVIDE ....INTO... (This was because BY had already been used for MULTIPLY).
Thousands of programmers wrote DIVIDE... BY (well, COBOL's an "English-like"
language, right?), grizzled because it didn't work and the vendors then
incorporated it into the language. A trivial example, granted, but there are
many more.

So now we see someone who obviously doesn't understand Windows controls,
writing a Windows program and then suggesting that what the Vendor has
provided is wrong. Why? Because it didn't do what one person expected.

How can we ever get a simple, stable, standard in anything if everyone
expects it to behave in a different manner? By the time the language is
extended to include all the "good ideas" we end up with a morass of options
and alternative constructs which suck all the simple elegance which was
there originally, out of it. (Just look at COBOL today...)

Here's my take on it: Use what is provided. If it doesn't work AS SPECIFIED,
then jump up and down at the vendor, or use some other tool. If you REALLY
don't like what is specified, then consider using a different tool.

The over-complication of COBOL (supposedly in response to user demands, but
actually in a frantic game of "catch-up", to enable commercial programmers
to put men in orbit, analyze chaos theory, and re-write the Operating
System) should be a warning to us all. If something is simple, elegant, and
ideally suited to the purpose for which it was intended, DON'T MESS WITH IT!

Fortunately, Bill Gates has too much on his mind at the moment to worry
about changing the behaviour of Windows controls, so get used to the fact
that hidden controls are NOT disabled, write it up to experience, and move
on....

Pete.

Bill Wood wrote in message <#cw8h1in$GA.232@cpmsnbbsa03>...
>I recently ran into a problem that I thought was a bug, but MicroFocus
tells
>me it is expected behavior and I'm curious if everyone agrees that it is
>expected behavior.  Here is the scenario:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>products do also.
```

## Thread: sort in microfocus cobol, divide by zero error — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### sort in microfocus cobol, divide by zero error

- **From:** <k_jinx@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sejv3stseop141@corp.supernews.com>`

```
I am in Microfocus COBOL in Windows '95 trying to do a SORT command.  I am
```

<a id="t-74a4c9203b"></a>
## Thread: The Great Programming Comparison — 1 msgs

### The Great Programming Comparison

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8cclp5$lar$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hi:

Someone suggested changing the name of the Great Programming Contest
to the Great Programming Comparison.

This is a good idea. It is a wonderful opportunity for everyone to
show everyone else their way of doing it.


---------


The Great Programming Comparison.

Initially, the idea was to develop an OO program and an old-fashioned
program at the same time so as the throng could learn what the heck OO
is all about and to compare different methods.

It grew from that to become a Free-For-All.

You are invited to enter the comparison. You may use any language or
method you want - anything at all but it must run on the PC under
Windows because everyone has access to Windows.

I think everyone would like to see a structured entrant and
at least one OO entrant - after all it was OO that started all
this.

The contest is to design, program and distribute a simple Time-Clock
application. The application must do the following:

a. Allow the creation and subsequent maintenance (change, delete)   of
employee records in a file.

b. For each employee, the initials (prime key), name, address, social-
   security number and telephone must be provided for. All fields are
required except the second line of address. State must be validated.

c. Employees must be able to punch-in and punch-out.

d. The application must provide the means for the user to make a
backup of the employee-file.

e. You must design the screen(s), files, etc. You are developing   a
complete, though tiny, application.


Of course, a real-world program would do more. Like print an
hours-worked report. The objective of the contest is to compare
programming methods so we need to keep it simple so additional
things like that should be left out. Points are not awarded for
additional features, in fact, there are no points to be won.

Here are the rules:

You can use anything you want and do it any way you want.
Since this is intended to be educational and to allow people
with different opinions to evaluate different methods, please
document all but the most blatantly obvious things.

People got vociferous when I suggested that the contest be
conducted in three parts which reflected the approach I would
take. Some people do things backwards, it seems, perhaps me?

So, all you have to do is email your executable and all source
code in ZIP format to Foodman123@aol.com. I will make entries
available via the Internet. Remember this is not a race.

Would someone like to come up with some criteria for
evaluating the entrants?

Thanks

Tony Dilworth






Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

---

<a id="t-8e6c27cc56"></a>
## Thread: OO - "schematic" of Data Division sections in a Class — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo) · [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### OO - "schematic" of Data Division sections in a Class

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ccqi4$4fn$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
After questions from Thane and Ken in comp.lang.cobol and my (possibly
erroneous) interpretation of the draft Standard, Thane and I have been having
a prolonged (and occasionally painful) discussion with J4 people on the
subject of working-storage and local-storage in objects (which also ended up
including the file section as well).

It appears that there is now "consensus" on what the draft is saying - which
I have summarized in the following schematic.

There are one or two potentially "controversial" items that I mention at the
end.  If you want a change (not clarification) to the draft related to these,
you should send your comments to J4 or (possibly better because it would be a
significant change to WG4 via your national body).

   ***

 Class-Id. Class1.
 *> no data division allowed here
  Factory.
   Data Division.
    File Section.
  *>  no restrictions on files defined here
  *> NOTE It is recommended, though not generally required, to use
  *> one of the those features (those required for files
  *> in object instances) in factory objects, because
  *> inheritance can lead to multiple factory objects with
  *> identical file descriptions.

    Working-Storage Section.
  *> items defined here are put in initial state the first time this
  *> factory is referenced in the run-unit and it lasts as long as
  *> run-unit exists.  Each invocation of "NEW" gets the same
  *> (last-used state) items.

  *> no local-storage section allowed here
        ...
   Procedure Division.
    Method-ID. Meth1.
     Data Division.
      File Section.
       *> There are no restrictions on files defined here.
       *> When a file section is specified in a method, one file
       *> connector is shared among all invocations of that method.

      Working-Storage Section.
       *> Items defined here are set to initial state the first time
       *> this method is referenced.  It remains in last-used state
       *> for all subsequent references to this method - regardless
       *> of whether or not the same instance - (recursion instance OR
       *> object instance)

      Local-Storage Section.
       *> Items defined here are automatic and, hence, are set to
       *> initial state every time this method is made active. The
       *> values "persist" (not last-used state) as long as the method
       *> is active - even if recursion to the same method occurs
       *> in the middle of its being active. The recursed instance
       *> will get a new copy of local-storage but the original
       *> local-storage will be retained and when execution returns to
       *> this instance such items will retain their previous values.
       *> The data is "destroyed" (no longer available) when an EXIT
       *> METHOD occurs from this instance of this method.
         ...
    End Method Meth1.
  End Factory.
  Object.
   Data Division.
    File Section.
    *> Each file defined in an instance definition
    *> shall be described with one of the following features:
       *> Dynamic file assignment
       *> Sharing mode
       *> EXTERNAL attribute

    Working-Storage Section.
       *> Items defined here are set to initial state the first time
       *> this Object is "instantiated" via the "NEW" method.  The
       *> data exists (persists) as long as this object instance
       *> exists. If another instance object is created (via another
       *> "NEW" method on this class) it gets a new copy of THIS
       *> working-storage which persists with that object instance.

 *> no local-storage section allowed here
        ...
   Procedure Division.
    Method-ID. Meth2.
     Data Division.
      File Section.
    *> There are no restrictions on files defined here.
    *> When a file section is specified in a method, one file
    *> connector is shared among all invocations of that method.

      Working-Storage Section.
       *> Items defined here are set to initial state the first time
       *> this method is referenced.  It remains in last-used state
       *> for all subsequent references to this method - regardless
       *> of whether or not the same instance (recursion instance OR
       *> object instance)

      Local-Storage Section.
       *> Items defined here are automatic and, hence, are set to
       *> initial state every time this method is made active. The
       *> values "persist" (not last-used state) as long as the method
       *> is active - even if recursion to the same method occurs
       *> in the middle of its being active. The recursed instance
       *> will get a new copy of local-storage but the original
       *> local-storage will be retained and when execution returns to
       *> this instance such items will retain their previous values.
       *> The data is "destroyed" (no longer available) when an EXIT
       *> METHOD occurs from this instance of this method.
         ...
    End Method Meth2.
   End Object.
  End Class Class1.

  ******

Notes/Question:

1) Working-storage in an object instance definition is "unique" to each
object instance.  HOWEVER, working-storage in the methods within an object
instance definition is static for the entire run-unit.  This means that WS
items defined within a method are SHARED across all instances of object
instances of that class. (This may be "counter-intuitive" if not just plain
distasteful to OO purists - as it certainly impacts data encapsulation.)

2) Files defined in an Object Instance definition are speechify to that
instance - sort-of.  Actually, they are required to use EXTERNAL, dynamic
file assignment, or file sharing to handle the fact that the file connector
in each object instance is distinct from the "similar looking" file connector
in another object instance.  ON THE OTHER HAND, files defined within a method
are shared among all object instances. Therefore, each object instance
("auto-magically") references the exact same file connector.

3) As far as I know, none (few?) of the existing OO implementations match
this definition completely and exactly.  (A Siemens person told me theirs
did, but he mentioned a few things that are "missing" from their current
implementation.)
```

#### ↳ Re: OO - "schematic" of Data Division sections in a Class

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-04-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38ea546f.65334016@news.cox-internet.com>`
- **References:** `<8ccqi4$4fn$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

What Bill has posted is correct.  Now let me re-post something I sent
around to J4 et all.  At first I was stunned by this, thinking it was
really really really bad.  But now I think it's not GOOD, but
understandable and OK.  The upshot of all this is that when
considering OO COBOL you have to realize that the "method" portion of
an object is always resident and the "data" portion is what is
allocated and released when objects are created and destroyed.  My
summary:


In OO COBOL - Methods are in memory and persist to the end of
the run unit.  Methods are not destroyed when an object reference
is set to null.  Creating a new object, simply creates a new area of
object Working-Storage and allows methods to work on this
storage -- the nature of which is an "object" instance.


Now, concerning method Working-Storage:  This is associated with
the METHOD, and is in memory with the method.  It persists to the
end of the run unit.  It is in initial state upon the first invocation
of
the method, but thereafter is in last used until the end of the run
unit.  Destroying objects, creating new ones, etc has no impact on
method WS.  If one wants "Fresh and distinct" storage for a
method invocation, one must use Local-Storage.

Why this is good - Constants associated with a method, used for
whatever purpose, have a place to go OUTSIDE the object data.
Meaning a million instances of the object don't create a million
unnecessary data values.

Why it is bad.  It's not well understood.  That's the only reason it's
bad.  I think that if it can be clarified somehow, it would be a good
thing.  I do not propose changing the draft standard "model" for
OO.  I can see some very good reasons for having it this way.  It
CAN be abused.  We may revisit this in the future.  However, I
think that if OO COBOL programmers understand the way methods
work, everything will be fine.


------
More - Fujitsu Specific.  Local-Storage is an extension brought on
mainly by the recursion requirement.  I have not been able to test
Fujitsu with Local Storage as they do not yet support recursion
(outside OO) and so do not support Local-Storage. 

I am going to write a test program to see if METHOD working-storage
persists across all object instances - and I hope it does actually.
One should NOT use Working-Storage in a method (under any
implementation) if it EVER changes values -- that data, if single use
during the method invocation -- should be local-storage, or for those
who don't yet have it, working-storage in the object.

I'll post here the results of my Fujitsu test - it may be a little
while though.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OO - "schematic" of Data Division sections in a Class

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-04-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38ea8c1b.79588255@news.cox-internet.com>`
- **References:** `<8ccqi4$4fn$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <38ea546f.65334016@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
I could not wait.  My tests with Fujitsu COBOL yielded the following
behavior.

Working-Storage at every level except the method level behaves as the
standard defines and as we suspect.

HOWEVER - any working-storage defined at the METHOD LEVEL, behaves
like LOCAL-STORAGE, it is in it's first used state with each execution
of the method.  This means that if you DEPEND on this behavior with
Fujitsu COBOL OO, when the standards conforming compiler, offering
support for Local-Storage, is relased, your code may be broken.  It
might be a good idea NOW, to code a *LOCAL-STORAGE SECTION, (commented
out) in each method with WS so that later, to get the same behavior,
you can uncomment the Local-Storage, and comment out the
Working-Storage.
```

<a id="t-9822fa715f"></a>
## Thread: Network Modems / Modem Pools — 1 msgs

### Network Modems / Modem Pools

- **From:** Doug Blaze <Doug.Blaze@merchants-fla.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<m2qjesgsplfj10vu2jhpnes1u6sr6vgkh1@4ax.com>`

```
Hi All,

After doing quite a bit of hunting around and even contacting the
author of a book on serial communications, I'm still trying to find
out how to handle network modems/modem pools in my COBOL app - or find
out even if I have to!  The app is written in MF Cobol 4.0.32 and has
been working fine for the past 1.5 years with modems connected to the
PC it' running on.  It uses Windows API calls to talk to the COM port
and uses a GUI front end provided by MPACT from ESI.

From what I can find out, it looks like in order for an application to
talk to a network modem, there has to be some kind of a 'redirector'
resident on the PC from which the app is running which tells Windows
that this COM port is really somewhere else on the network - sort of
like mapping a printer port.  Is this correct?  The libraries that
I've found, Marshallsoft, Sax, Crescent, etc. have no mention of
network modems/modem pools in them.  You connect to a COM port and
that's it.  It also appears that the 'redirector' can vary by which
brand of network you have.  This would also imply that the end user
would have to be responsible for setting it up before my app could
access a network modem.

Any information or experience anyone has had with network modems would
be greatly appreciated.  I'm not even sure I have to do anything to my
app other than update the help file!

Doug
```

---

<a id="t-5b90758808"></a>
## Thread: COBOL vs C — 12 msgs

### COBOL vs C

- **From:** billycanas@cs.com (BillyCanas)
- **Date:** 2000-04-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000404232431.20126.00001076@ng-ca1.news.cs.com>`

```
Hi,

Well first of all, I'm a college student majoring in Computer Information
Systems. I've had lots of trouble programming in C language. I need to make a
career decision right now, and I really don't want to keep taking C courses.
Instead I want to go in another field that uses only COBOL, but I don't have a
clue how COBOL works.
My question is:
Is programming in COBOL easier than in C?
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL vs C

- **From:** frlsfly@aol.com (FRLSFLY)
- **Date:** 2000-04-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000405030624.09048.00000831@ng-ft1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<20000404232431.20126.00001076@ng-ca1.news.cs.com>`

```
>Hi,
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>Is programming in COBOL easier than in C?

First of all, go where the money is, and right now it seems to be in active
Server Pages, VB, and SQL server.

Just remember that where ever Microsoft documentation uses the word "object"
you can substitute "thingie" and it will make more sense.

Second, on a theoretical level,  I have to say what's easier, 'C' or COBOL,
depends on what you are trying to do. 

'C' is great for small routines that do small logically discrete mathmatical
tasks.  You want a little item to calculate area of a circle and display it,
'C' is the way to go.
'C' is also more vesatile in some respects, and runs less overhead in terms of
setting up data areas and libraries for small tasks.

However, when you go to large systems of logically complex ( or in the case of
many operational systems, irrational) programs and tasks, COBOL is far easier
to work with.  

It's sort of like the difference between a hand-axe and a chain saw: if you
just want
to chop down a sapling and split a little cordwood, the hand axe is a nice way
to go.  But if you want to log several acres, then you go  to the trouble of
getting out that big old chainsaw, the gas, oil, sharpener, kevlar gloves.
goggles,  & all the other things.

And if you have to, you can pound nails with an axe- don't recommend you try
that with a chainsaw.
Nathan Meyer
Berkeley, CA
"Big Endian" is what I like.
```
