```

James J. Gavan <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
news:38EE29DA.E13BA309@home.com...
>
>
> docdwarf@clark.net wrote:
> >
> > Here in the States there's something called 'direct deposit'...
which I do
> > my best to avoid, as it gives the other party unfettered access to
one's
> > account which is *very* difficult to remove.
>
> What Charles is referring to was available in UK - ooh - 30 years
ago.
> We called it DIRECT CREDIT putting your pay cheque into your
account. No
> way Jose that it gave anybody the authority to DEBIT your account.
OK
> they probably now have, as we do in N.America - DIRECT DEBIT where
you
> authorize automatic payment of budgeted utility bills, instalment
> property taxes etc. - but that is with YOUR sanction. (Not to be
> confused with Direct Debit cards that you present at a supermarket
> check-out etc....) They CAN NOT willy-nilly zing your account. (So
I'm
> curious what type of 'Debiting' system you have in the Sates that
lets
> them do that).
>
What happens here in the states (sometimes), you authorize direct
deposit of your pay.
The Company deposits, and then immediately withdraws a trial deposit
(test run).
After that, they start depositing your pay.
Apparently anyone who deposits into your account, can also reverse the
deposit.
My guess is that this is to allow correction of misdirected deposits.

The banks do not question withdrawals made bank to bank, thus any bank
can debit your account, if they know the account number.
The person withdrawing the money is supposed to have written
authorization to do so.
This is not always the case.
You can dispute a withdrawal, AFTER THE FACT, but you may have already
have had your checks refused by your bank.
Giving out your checking account number may be hazardous to your
financial health.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: working-storage vs. local-storage

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2000-04-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000407185824.09697.00000200@nso-ci.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<uRCoeaMo$GA.227@cpmsnbbsa03>`

```
In article <uRCoeaMo$GA.227@cpmsnbbsa03>, "DennisHarley"
<LegacyBlue@email.msn.com> writes:

>
>The banks do not question withdrawals made bank to bank, thus any bank
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

There are ACH clearing house rules for validating and approving Deposit or 
Withdrawal transactions.   Normal procedure is to send a test transaction 
with a ZERO amount to ensure all the related routing information is correct.
If I recall correctly, they don't issue a withdrawal to retrieve a mis-directed
deposit.  They have to issue a Credit Reversal with all the correct related
routing info from the original transaction to ensure the correct item gets 
backed out of your account.
```

```
I am speaking from experience.
I had direct payroll deposit.
One pay period they deposited, and a day or 2 later withdrew the
money.
I was really angry, called the main office, and picked up a check the
next day.

I had once (years ago) given my checking account number to an ISP-like
provider to pay my account. When my computer broke down, I tried to
contact them by phone to cancel, but could not get through. (nice
music though).
So I emptied my account and moved to another bank.
When their transactions were rejected, they split the payment into
smaller amounts, and the bank covered them. I told the bank that they
were out the money. My account was closed as far as I was concerned.
I had never signed the agreement they sent me, allowing them to debit
my account.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: working-storage vs. local-storage

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2000-04-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000407232513.29593.00000103@nso-fx.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<ena0vFPo$GA.232@cpmsnbbsa03>`

```
In article <ena0vFPo$GA.232@cpmsnbbsa03>, "DennisHarley"
<LegacyBlue@email.msn.com> writes:

>One pay period they deposited, and a day or 2 later withdrew the
>money.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>

An ACH item can be reversed as much as 7 days (if I remember correctly,
could be longer).  Still the TRAN CODE had to be a reversal.
We experienced something like this as part of the Y2K scare.  Payroll setup
two runs of ACH with different dates to 'get a jump' on possible Y2K 
system crashes.  Some folks were lucky enough to get double posted 
payroll deposits.  The next day a notice was published internally to NOT 
make use of the 'extra' funds on penalty of dismisal.  The ACH processing
vendor ran both tapes rather than just the regular disbursement.


Usually in the online environment, when giving the R-T and Account
number to your account you are implicitly giving that vendor/service 
the authorization to automatically debit your account.  It gets set up
as a recurring transaction and requires some advance notification to 
discontinue ACH processing.   Some tricks that have been perpetrated
by some companies is to continualy alter the transaction amount.
This basically defeats the ability of putting STOP Payment on a vendor
for  a specific amount.
```

```
NOT

When I lived in Denver Colorado, the law in Colorado was; if a bank
made a mistake in your favor, and you withdrew the money, it was
yours.
We had a double posting of ACH transactions, payroll was deposited
twice. Some people saw the error, and withdrew the money, it was
theirs.
More states should follow suit, it would make payday something like a
lottery.
```

```
Some years ago (20 actually), they were already doing direct deposit
for Social Security checks.  I happened to work in a town where the
population was older.  One could not live within the city limits
unless over the age of 55.  

One day I noticed an unknown deposit in my checking account.  I asked
the bank about it, and it took them two weeks to research and
determine that it was some poor souls social security check.  3 months
later the deposits finally stopped and th excess money disappeared
from my account.  I wonder how long the individual waited for their
social security money?
```

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>

- **References:** `<8bhkat$621$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <MPG.13568491900b34249896a8@news.freedombird.net> <HN3H4.16432$0o4.108356@iad-read.news.verio.net> <MPG.1357ac7de1923e789896b4@news.freedombird.net> <IBpH4.16507$0o4.109133@iad-read.news.verio.net> <38EE29DA.E13BA309@home.com> <38ee3c1e.138570661@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote in message

>Some years ago (20 actually), they were already doing direct deposit

<snip>

Funnily enough, I had a very similar thing happen.  When the kids were small
(about twenty years back), I used to take my daughter with me to the bank on
Friday.  She would have been about six.

I opened a bank account for her, and I'd give her a fiver to deposit in her
bank account each week, to teach her about saving.  One week, she proudly
opened up her account book, and handed it to me saying "look how much money
I have saved daddy".  My eyes just about bugged out of my head.  Her balance
was $18,000.00 and change.

It seems some guys pay cheque was being deposited into her account, and had
been for close to six months. We got it all cleared up, eventually, but I
have often wondered how the guy lived for all that time without noticing
that he was not getting paid.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: working-storage vs. local-storage

- **From:** Charles F Hankel <noos@hankel.freedombird.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.135aba90e5318359896d1@news.freedombird.net>`
- **References:** `<8bhkat$621$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <MPG.13568491900b34249896a8@news.freedombird.net> <HN3H4.16432$0o4.108356@iad-read.news.verio.net> <MPG.1357ac7de1923e789896b4@news.freedombird.net> <IBpH4.16507$0o4.109133@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
I noticed that docdwarf@clark.net as docdwarf@clark.net said...
> In article <MPG.1357ac7de1923e789896b4@news.freedombird.net>,
> Charles F Hankel  <noos@hankel.freedombird.net> wrote:
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> According to whose 'laws', pray tell?

Only pedantically, you understand, but you either overshot column 72 or 
undershot column 8 - hence my inserted scale line.  I've worked on 
systems where the columns weren't constrained like this.  I'm not sure 
if it's still in the standards or not... and Bill's gone to California.
 
> >> >How could proper use possibly be considered excessive? :)
> >> 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> That's because they are written by folks with tenure, who don't have such.

Well, I don't have tenure, that's for sure.  I've got a bigger pile of 
paperwork than I have ever needed for a whole year's contracting, and I 
don't start until Monday.  This is turning out to be a bigger culture 
shock than I expected.
 
> >> >It's a perversion, maybe, and it's on the payroll too.
> >> 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> that fits what one finds at http://www.m-w.com, definition 1.

Oh dear, I haven't been suborned by company-speak already, have I?  I'll 
try harder but should point out that the word "benefit" is a simile for 
"millstone" in my vocabulary.
 
> >> >it's 
> >> >a life I might like to get used to.  I'll let you know after payday.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> account which is *very* difficult to remove.

This seems to have spawned a few comments so let me clarify, if I can.  
There are several ways of making payments via the bank account here.

1.  Cheque - simple and readily understandable, even if spellings differ 
on different sides of the Pond.  Oddly enough, I caused a stir among 
some Swiss friends who were most impressed by my having a chequebook as 
they didn't use them at all.  Apparently you needed to be really rich to 
warrant a chequebook but maybe this has all changed since then.

2.  Standing Order.  You instruct the bank to pay a regular amount 
into someone else's bank account at a regular time such as 1st day of 
the month or 17th June or whatever.  You are able to cancel this at any 
time.

3.  Bank Giro Credit.  Giro is a throwback, it's the original name for 
the Bankers Aautomated Clearing System, or BACS, as it's now known.  
This is used for one-off transmission of funds to someone else's 
account.  The key here is that you instigate payment as and when you 
want.  This is what is used for salary payments and the like.  I don't 
think that reversals can be effected but I'm not a real expert and I 
don't have a manual to hand.

4.  Direct Debit.  This is one that might cause you concern.  It's a 
common enough method over here and is usually used for regular bill 
payments like utility bills, insurance premiums and the like.  You can 
specify a fixed sum or you can leave it open.  You specifically sign an 
authorisation for your bank to pay it.

For instance, my phone bill is paid by DD (Direct Debit, not you Doc, 
though any contributions would be gratefully received and genuine 
recognition of your munificence given to anyone who might slight you), 
and this is paid quarterly.  The phone company sends me their bill about 
ten days before they apply for payment.  This gives me the opportunity 
to accuse them of being cheats and liars, and I generally do.

In the final analysis, I could get really caned but the element of trust 
has been hard to establish - thirty years ago, there were plenty of 
mistakes but nowadays the system is much more reliable.  I'd be careful 
about who I signed a DD mandate for though, even now.  The banks prefer 
DD as a payment method, presumably because it's cheapest for them, and 
so there's a lot of effort put into making sure that it works.

Added to these basic methods, there's all sorts of fast payment schemes 
both inter-bank and intra-bank.  I have a dial-up to the bank, as I 
mentioned and can instigate any of these depending on the situation.  It 
would have made my paying Network Solutions for a domain name a whole 
lot simpler if they could have had a similarly decent money receiving 
system.  I don't use credit cards - probably wouldn't get one now, even 
if I wanted one, because I don't borrow money and so have no line of 
credit.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: working-storage vs. local-storage

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 2000-04-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38F0E4E7.392388C3@melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<8bhkat$621$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <MPG.13568491900b34249896a8@news.freedombird.net> <HN3H4.16432$0o4.108356@iad-read.news.verio.net> <MPG.1357ac7de1923e789896b4@news.freedombird.net> <IBpH4.16507$0o4.109133@iad-read.news.verio.net> <MPG.135aba90e5318359896d1@news.freedombird.net>`

```
Actually there is nothing in the COBOL standard that says column
72 has to be the right margin. There has to be a right margin but
the specific value is an implementation decision. It could even
be spceified on a compile by compile basis, or maybe even on a
line by line basis.

I assume 72 came from punched cards which were 80 columns, and
people usually used 73-80 for sequence numbers so you could sort
the cards when you dropped them on the floor :-)

In cobol 2002 you can have variable length lines,

Tim Josling


Charles F Hankel wrote:

> > >> READ-MAST1.
> > >>     READ INMAST1 AT END GO TO READ-MAST2.  IF INMAST-CM2581 = 'M' OR 'Q'
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
  Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au> wrote:
> I assume 72 came from punched cards which were 80 columns, and
> people usually used 73-80 for sequence numbers so you could sort
> the cards when you dropped them on the floor :-)

Hi:

Actually, the sequence number was in 1-6. Columns 73-80 would
contain the program-id, if used.

>
> In cobol 2002 you can have variable length lines,
>

I use MF COBOL and BRIEF. BRIEF lets you specify your own line
length and I use 256.  This lets me put in comments starting
in column 73 to document changes made to programs.  The MF
compiler doesn't mind this, but the Fujitsu compiler does.

Thanks

TOny Dilworth


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: working-storage vs. local-storage

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 2000-04-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38F2402B.F91E9521@melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<8bhkat$621$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <MPG.13568491900b34249896a8@news.freedombird.net> <HN3H4.16432$0o4.108356@iad-read.news.verio.net> <MPG.1357ac7de1923e789896b4@news.freedombird.net> <IBpH4.16507$0o4.109133@iad-read.news.verio.net> <MPG.135aba90e5318359896d1@news.freedombird.net> <38F0E4E7.392388C3@melbpc.org.au> <8csic5$pd2$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Actually you could use columns 1-6 and 73-80 however you liked. I
have seen both used for all sorts of things, even including
comments about other people's code. But I have certainly seen
73-80 used as line numbers, in Cobol and elsewhere. ISPF editor
has various options here.

Tim Josling
```

- **From:** Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-11T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<r3c5fsocr496gos21e82ub82tenfqsrmqs@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<8bhkat$621$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <MPG.13568491900b34249896a8@news.freedombird.net> <HN3H4.16432$0o4.108356@iad-read.news.verio.net> <MPG.1357ac7de1923e789896b4@news.freedombird.net> <IBpH4.16507$0o4.109133@iad-read.news.verio.net> <MPG.135aba90e5318359896d1@news.freedombird.net> <38F0E4E7.392388C3@melbpc.org.au> <8csic5$pd2$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8ctngf$t9d$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`

```
On Tue, 11 Apr 2000 01:20:26 +0200, "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk> wrote:

>>
>The confusion could be that when you say 'Number Off' in ISPF, it will stop
>numbering in 73-80.

An of course in ISPF you would use NUM ON COB to have the sequence numbers in 1 to 6.
This has the advantage of displaying the file beginning at col 7.  It has the disadvantage
to display the cols 73 to 78 as well, which makes it simple to write into an area ignored
by the compiler - well - you win some, and you loose some
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: working-storage vs. local-storage

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-04-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38F325AB.FE78C36D@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<8bhkat$621$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <MPG.13568491900b34249896a8@news.freedombird.net> <HN3H4.16432$0o4.108356@iad-read.news.verio.net> <MPG.1357ac7de1923e789896b4@news.freedombird.net> <IBpH4.16507$0o4.109133@iad-read.news.verio.net> <MPG.135aba90e5318359896d1@news.freedombird.net> <38F0E4E7.392388C3@melbpc.org.au> <8csic5$pd2$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8ctngf$t9d$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`

```
Ib Tanding wrote:
> 
> >Actually, the sequence number was in 1-6. Columns 73-80 would
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> numbering in 73-80.

It stops numbering - wherever the current numbering is.  NUM STANDARD is
columns 73-80.  Often JCL has this setting.  NUM COBOL is numbers 1-6.  

One way to put spaces in columns 73-80 is to enter NUM STANDARD;UNNUM;   

UNNUM removes the numbers.  (UNNUM just stops paying attention to what's
in those columns)

> In the old days, pre-compilers checked carefully that you remembered to put
> numbers there. Then they commented your code, and inserted their own code
> with numbers out of sequence.

I missed that.  I do remember programming my punched cards to put
sequential numbers in 1-6 and the job name in 73-80.  I have worked
places where the standards were to put your initials, date, and/or
request # in either 1-6 or 73-80 (varying by shop).

It has been a long time since I have dropped my cobol source on the
floor and had to resort it, but I still put sequence numbers in my code
to make it easy to find lines from my listing to my source. (This is due
to copy statements filling out the listing).  Of course copy statements
mean that I will get warnings telling me that my numbers are out of
sequence.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: working-storage vs. local-storage

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 2000-04-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8cvijf$a9m$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<8bhkat$621$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <MPG.13568491900b34249896a8@news.freedombird.net> <HN3H4.16432$0o4.108356@iad-read.news.verio.net> <MPG.1357ac7de1923e789896b4@news.freedombird.net> <IBpH4.16507$0o4.109133@iad-read.news.verio.net> <MPG.135aba90e5318359896d1@news.freedombird.net> <38F0E4E7.392388C3@melbpc.org.au> <8csic5$pd2$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8ctngf$t9d$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <38F325AB.FE78C36D@cusys.edu>`

```
Yes Howard,
I know about the cobol numbering. some levels up in the thread I saw some
confusion about the editor's numbering in 73-80, and I responded to that.

The point you missed was not important, just that we had a 3rd party product
and a pre-compiler to that product. It took our ANS Cobol and expanded with
some library source. The pre-compiler had a sequence validation for
numbering - it checked 73-80, but didn't care about 1-6.All the source from
that product was entered WITHOUT numbers in 73-80. Of course the product
remembered to renumber 1-6. The point was, that the target for their
validation was in the wrong end of the record.

Regards
Ib

Howard Brazee skrev i meddelelsen <38F325AB.FE78C36D@cusys.edu>...
>Ib Tanding wrote:
>>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>> The confusion could be that when you say 'Number Off' in ISPF, it will
stop
>> numbering in 73-80.
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>> In the old days, pre-compilers checked carefully that you remembered to
put
>> numbers there. Then they commented your code, and inserted their own code
>> with numbers out of sequence.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>sequence.
```

```
Tim Josling wrote:
> 
> Actually there is nothing in the COBOL standard that says column
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> the cards when you dropped them on the floor :-)

When I worked on VAX computers, we had a compile option which gave us 80
columns to work with.  Column 1 was either column 6 or 7 depending on
context, and area B went to column 80.
```

```
> >> >> >It's a perversion, maybe, and it's on the payroll too.
> >> >> 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…

Unfortunately it's looking like things are moving in the other direction 
- I hate the hotel where we seem to have to stay, I can think of better 
places to spend my evenings than a holiday inn in the middle of an 
industrial estate, especially when the offices where we are working are 
in town.  I could do the overnight stay at a fraction of the price in 
altogether more human surroundings.  There's a real danger that I'll 
overstep the mark.  Indeed, maybe I ought to try a pseudonym for future 
postings of this nature.
```

```
I noticed that docdwarf@clark.net as docdwarf@clark.net said...
> In article <MPG.135d28ab5a114e99896e0@news.freedombird.net>,
> Charles F Hankel  <noos@hankel.freedombird.net> wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> *else* to do what you enjoy and leave you where you are.

Well, it gets still worse than this.  There are three of us in the same 
metaphorical boat.  The other two are doing something in COBOL but I've 
become the change management system, the source police or whatever you 
prefer.  Yes, I'm Infoman.  Or Changeman.  Or SCLM even.  Oh yes, and I 
get to write my own JCL... well, some of it.

In fairness the client's guy with whom I'm working was so embarrassed 
about it that he apologised _before_ outlining what I was going to be 
doing - he'd only seen my CV _after_ being told that someone was coming 
on board to help him out.  I feel as sorry for him as I do for myself, 
since he also has a fair old technical background as well.

It's not been a brilliant first week so things can only get better.  Er, 
can't they?
```

## Thread: The Wrong People — 2 msgs

### Re: The Wrong People

- **From:** "Eileen Preston" <epreston@lear.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s8edf206.048@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```


>>> Charles W. Cribbs II 04/03 2:36 pm >>>
you can be sure that most computer scientist have never written business
applications, just look at the books they write.  most of the good books on
business applications are written by junior college instructors who come
from business backgrounds.

Foodman <foodman123@aol.com> wrote in message
news:8canls$i42$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> Hi:
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
<major snippage>

Welp - I happen to know a 'computer scientest' personally.  A young man (I call him young because he's younger than I am) whose work done in obtaining his PhD can be found in things we use today, and whose work after receiving his degree is also found in things we use today. The names of these things would be recognizable to many people here, but I refrain from using them to protect his privacy.  He's never done any business applications as we have done, but without his contributions we wouldn't be doing some of the things we are today.  Yes, he's done some strange writings and I've attempted to read some of his books and found them out of my league if you catch my drift, but his reply to my complaints was 'you don't get published you don't get paid' (he was working as a professor at an Ivy League college at the time) made sense.  He wasn't too pleased either but that's the way the game is played.  He is also about 75% reponsible that I'm a programmer today.

Not all comuter scientests are old fogies just being obtuse.  Some are even real people!

Eileen


 Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
 Before you buy.
```

<a id="t-790e23aa82"></a>
## Thread: Thank you - re:help reading negative values in data file — 1 msgs

### Thank you - re:help reading negative values in data file

- **From:** "David Cole" <david-rc@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sXcH4.1288$8v5.80463@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Thank you for all your comments  and suggestions.  I got the problem
resolved today by writing disk files, then examining them to see what
happened using various code methods and compiler directives.  Then used
other programs to sucessfully read and process the data items.  A lot of
what I uncovered is what I found in my e-mail and groups posts when I got
home a few minutes ago. ( I cannot access newsgroups or home e-mail from my
college office).  What I don't yet understand is    (a) Why I never had this
problem in previous years of teaching COBOL, mostly on EBCDIC mainframes,
but with some coverage using microcomputers,  and,    (b)  Why is there not
any coverage of this subject in the textbooks that use microcomputer cobol.
I find I have a new lecture subject to bring out in my classes.

In summary, I found that only lowercase letters would work, p thru y for
last digits of 0 thru 9, when the field is negative.  The clause 'sign is
trailing' is unnecessary.  No runtime compiler directives ($SET commands)
affected my outcomes.

Again, I thank you for your support.  I will certainly be aware of you
contributions in assistlng and broadening my approaches to microcomputer
cobol.  As Net Express arrives on the scene, I can envision that cobol will
indeed have a slow death, not the rapid death predicted by many of my C++
friends.

Per your comments:
Volker Bandke  --  your detailed e-mail was most appreciated - wish I could
have magically read it last week.  I am sending separate e-mail.

Tony Dilworth, Thane Hubbell  --  I wasn't worried about file status in this
test code.  It is certainly used appropriately in commercial code for most
I/O.

Michael Mattias  --  A Read is actually treated as a group operation in
COBOL.  The actual run time error occurs when you attempt to use the
'corrupt' data item...  say, when my test code or student programs attempt
to move or add an item.  Using Read Into wouldn't alleviate the problem, but
can allow more ways to validate an incoming data value.

William Klein  --  ASCII is the default of MF Cobol, which I have verified
many times over the past years.  The $SET compiler directive SIGN"ASCII" did
not affect results.  It is the default if not overturned.  The CHARSET was
returned as invalid the one attempt to use - did not pursue as I started
getting good responses from the disk writing trials by then.

Ray Smith,  Pat  --  The code conversion (EBCDIC to ASCII) idea is good, and
the conversion code supplied by Pat (which she graciously credited to this
newsgroup) is going into my library - I like the conversion method!  I
already have a use in mind.

Again, thank all of you.  I really appreciate you.  I hope I can return the
contribution to this group soon.

David.
```

---

## Thread: looking for an aligment tool for cobol sources — 1 this month / 5 total

_Thread started [2000-03](2000-03.md#t-1c0d8968a1)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: looking for an aligment tool for cobol sources

- **From:** Vadim Maslov <vadik@siber.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38ED5CBD.7BE184A2@siber.com>`
- **References:** `<AMPC4.37829$jb7.768749@carnaval.risq.qc.ca> <38DD5658.C801827C@siber.com> <8bmvrp$f0h$2@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
RUSSELL STYLES wrote:
> 
>     But, unless there is a trick I have not found, if it finds something it
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> >

In general, if we cannot parse a particular Cobol dialect,
it needs to be fixed in the parser. We could try to pass
the source code to the beautified output 
without really understanding (parsing) it,
but this is more dangerous than replacing it with 
error message, because if smth is lost from the code,
you will find it at run time at a great cost.

So we decided to make such things explicit at compile time
where they are easy to catch.

Vadim Maslov
```

<a id="t-a3a0b6777e"></a>
## Thread: Help: to set printer spacing in NetExpress when using 'printer_redirection'? — 17 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help: to set printer spacing in NetExpress when using 'printer_redirection'?

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<R4MH4.465$b_2.770223@news3.mia>`

```
I'm new to Net Express.  I recently moved an old application to NE
3.0.  Everything works fine, except that one user's (the boss :) PC
gets an error message (9/013: file not found) when it tries to print.
In an attempt to solve this, I am testing using the run-time tunable:

    set printer_redirection=TRUE

which sends the printer output through the Windows printing subsystem
instead of directly to the printer.  This is working, because it now
prints to the Windows default printer instead of the local one.  I
think this may clear up my client's problem.  However, the printed
output, while 10 cpi, is not exactly 6 lpi, making the data overflow
the end of the page onto the next sheet.  One report is specifically
designed to fit (barely) onto two pages, so adjusting page length is
not a good solution.

Can anybody tell me an (easy?) way to tell the printer to print at
6 lpi?  I know how to do this in VB.  Other options, fonts, etc.,
would be nice to know, but now I only need to set lines per inch.
Thanks!
```

#### ↳ Re: Help: to set printer spacing in NetExpress when using 'printer_redirection'?

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 2000-04-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8coibq$oqs$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<R4MH4.465$b_2.770223@news3.mia>`

```
There's a font called 'line-printer'. I beleive, I have seen it on Lexmark
and HP. It is my guess that  you could use it to emulate a 6Lpi.

Hope it helps.

Regards
Ib

>3.0.  Everything works fine, except that one user's (the boss :) PC
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
```

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
> 
> I'm new to Net Express.  I recently moved an old application to NE
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I really don't know the answer to this one Judson - that's why I use
PC_PRINT :-)

Mail me if you would like a copy - and I'll send my PC_PRINT class.
Agreed it's an OO class but you could invoke it from a structured
program or hack it around to be your own structured called sub-program -
perhaps with entry-points instead of methods ? (The class is a
conglomeration of all the PC_PRINT calls you will already be aware of,
plus all the printer error codes).

The thing I like about it is I don't have that worry of which printer,
and can quickly change fonts, PC_PRINT doing the handshaking with
Windows to give me what I want. Unless you are into printing 'forms'
it's not a bad tool, in my opinion.

Jimmy
```

#### ↳ Re: Help: to set printer spacing in NetExpress when using 'printer_redirection'?

```
Judson,

Are you using the PC_PRINTER... library routines? You are
running into a problem that I have finally solved.

Are you running the print software on varying different systems
with different printers? If so, you should use COURIER NEW. This
is a font common to all windows systems (I don't know your
platform).

Are you trying to fill in a pre-printed form? If so, check out
FLEXUS. They have a form printing software that will give you
the capability to setup and complete a form.

Regards,
Rich
```

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>

- **References:** `<R4MH4.465$b_2.770223@news3.mia> <0024edc0.695034eb@usw-ex0105-037.remarq.com>`

```
Rich,

Sorry, I missed your message earlier.  No, I'm not printing forms or
graphics or any fancy stuff, only plain text.  It is just that when
I tell the Net Express runtime to use the Windows printing subsystem
instead of going directly to the printer, it doesn't print at exactly
6 lpi, more like 5.5 lpi.  This causes a single page of any report to
overflow onto the next sheet.  If Net Express doesn't use the Windows
printing subsystem, it prints directly to the printer, and one PC at
my client's location gets an I/O error when printing that way.  The
printer works fine with everything else, and the software works fine
on all the other PCs.  Unfortunately, that one PC belongs to the boss.
:-)  If I could just open the printer FD, then somehow specify the
printer use exactly 6 lpi, it would fix that problem.  One other
annoyance is that one of the reports is 132 columns wide, and the
client wants to print it on an 8.5"x11" sheet.  To make that work,
I need to specify the font size too.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help: to set printer spacing in NetExpress when using 'printer_redirection'?

- **From:** Rich Rohde <richNOriSPAM@richware.net.invalid>
- **Date:** 2000-04-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0ff31f28.df9d91f0@usw-ex0105-037.remarq.com>`
- **References:** `<R4MH4.465$b_2.770223@news3.mia> <0024edc0.695034eb@usw-ex0105-037.remarq.com> <Rf6J4.1566$C47.2529749@news3.mia>`

```
Judson,

I dropped you an e-mail with a routine that I wrote to print
reports of various sizes. Based on the width of the report, I
automatically drop the font size using COURIER NEW until the
width will fit. The user can select to print either landscape or
portrait, making the characters more readable.

COURIER NEW was used since it appears to be a universal fixed
spacing font. I did find printing from one printer to another
did produce different results (e.g. line spacing).

Regards,
Rich

The fastest and easiest way to search and participate in Usenet - Free!
```

#### ↳ Re: Help: to set printer spacing in NetExpress when using 'printer_redirection'?

```
Thanks for the suggestions, everybody.  I also sent a request to Merant
at the same time, and they gave me a work around that I hope my client
will accept.  They said Net Express alone did not currently support
setting the font on the printer when you use a standard printer
assignment and used the "printer_redirection" tunable.  I used this
approach because it is old code from around 1985, and I don't want to
change the code for printing unless I have to.

The work around was to add a 'generic/text only' printer to LPT1, and
make it the default printer.  Works great, but I'm not sure if it
will interfere with my client's use of his printer for other things,
say, if he prints graphics.

Does anybody know a way to easily set the default Windows printer from
Net Express 3.0?  Also, there is another report the same client (but
another person) uses that prints at 16cpi.  Currently they are setting
the printer font manually before and after printing that report.  The
program did that itself before Windows came along.  If I could only
access the printer as an object, I could use the attributes and methods
to do it, line in VB.

You OO COBOL gurus out there, if this were in OO COBOL, would the
printer FD be an object?

Thanks again!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help: to set printer spacing in NetExpress when using 'printer_redirection'?

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<NhEI4.456$wk.831376@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<R4MH4.465$b_2.770223@news3.mia> <AEoI4.4108$Yo4.264060@news1.mia> <38F29AF1.BD1C55E6@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote"
>
> Judson McClendon wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> an object for the print-file with traditional FD section etc.

In writing your Printfile class, did you use the PC_PRINT_xxx routines,
or another method?

> Judson - Don't know whether or not you can get any hints from demos in
> the Dialog System or OO Common Dialog routines - but if you could call
> 'Common Print' dialog without actually displaying and set your fonts and
> sizes ????

I'll check them out.  I assume you are referring to the PC_PRINTER_xxx
routines?  Apparently they are not compatible with an FD print file,
because to use them you must work with a numeric 'printer handle', and
I can't see how to get that from an FD, or vice versa.  It seems they
are two completely different methods for using the printer, and can't
be used together on one print file.  Anybody please correct me if I am
wrong here.

Net Express is impressive in a lot of ways, but I am frustrated that
there does not seem to be a smooth, balanced approach, to printing at
least.  Did nobody ever think that since we're now working in 32 bit
environments, it might be nice to simply set the font and print
margins, in addition to opening, printing and closing?  Why force us
to use a completely different approach to printing, just to do that?
Of course, certain kinds of printing (multiple fonts, bold, etc.)
would probably require a new approach.  But anybody might want to
use compressed or large print for a report.  You could implement that
using the VALUE OF clause under the FD without leaving standard COBOL.

The Net Express documentation gives you lots of big pictures and tiny
details, but can be sketchy about how the tiny details relate and
function with each other to make the big picture.  It's as if you can
find the microscopic details of the bark, and maps of the forest, but
the intermediate details, like how the bark or limbs fit onto the
trees, can be missing, or difficult to find.  There seems to be the
implicit assumption that if you explain the little details and the big
pictures, the reader will automatically know the middle level details
without being told.  For me, at least, this is not always obvious.
Just a paragraph explaining how the PC_PRINT routines relate to FD's
would be great.  The routines are explained, but I can find no context
description.  Instead, I must make assumptions, or deduce the context
for myself.  I have written enough documentation to know it is not
easy to always be clear.  No matter how clearly you write, there will
always be some moron who will misunderstand what you say.  But you
must at least say it. :-)  The first assumption you should make in
writing documentation is that the reader doesn't know anything about
what you're documenting except what you tell them, including its
purpose.  How many employees have been handed a new software package
and told to "learn this" with little or no idea about what it does?
More than a few, I'll bet. :-)
```

```
Merant,

I hope you are reading what Judson writes below. You have a great
product but I said it a long time ago. Go more for the old Borland
approach, Paradox etc. and all the other stuff addressed to non-tech
end-users. Same applies to us developers - like it Ir. not, treat us as
idiots, spelling it out in painful detail, step-by-step. Of course you
don't have to tell us we are idiots :-) .

And I believe I did make the comment in last year's questionnaire -
employ technical writers on documentation/on-line help who preferably
know nothing about computing and certainly NOTHING about COBOL - then
they can turn to developers with the query "What did Horace say Winnie
?" to translate techo-speak into plain English.

Judson - I'll e-mail you separately about PC_PRINT.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help: to set printer spacing in NetExpress when using'printer_redirection'?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Ken Mullins" <KenMullins@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8d2add$dk4$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<R4MH4.465$b_2.770223@news3.mia> <AEoI4.4108$Yo4.264060@news1.mia> <38F29AF1.BD1C55E6@home.com> <NhEI4.456$wk.831376@news1.mia> <0774657f.f72b3ba1@usw-ex0105-037.remarq.com> <38F36A07.6BB942F1@home.com> <197cb59e.3cab4022@usw-ex0105-037.remarq.com> <38F3C3AD.8226A7D5@home.com>`

```
for those with difficult printing requirements, have you considered using
something like Crystal Reports...If memory serves me correctly, CR has the
ability to read in a text file using the ODBC driver supplied my
Microsoft...You app can produce a line sequential file, then invoke the
32bit CR DLL for print previewing and actual printing...Encompasses pretty
much all the requirements that I can think of...If you need special spacing
and fonts, they can be set in the form to be printed...

I remember developing an application back under windows 3.1 with their 16
bit product, following the above guidelines...Worked pretty well, even with
multiple print previews, etc...

Printing and even simply drawing text in a window is rather difficult using
native APIs...There are hundreds of APIs dealing with this stuff...I try to
keep things simple and use the dialog editor for screens, etc...

kenmullins
```

```
With Data2Cr http://www.siber.com/sct/datafile/
Crystal Reposrts can read Cobol data files directly
for MF, FSC, Acu and RM data files.

Regards,
Vadim Maslov
```

```
Marte,

If you are wanting to access MVS Cobol data files from Windows, our
company offers an API that can be used with Visual Basic, Visual C++, or
Cobol (MF, Fujitsu, Realia) to do various things like this: Transfer
files to/from, obtain catalog listings, obtain dataset attributes,
catalog/rename/delete datasets, submit MVS jobs from the PC, etc.  It
seamlessly interfaces with both standard PDS's and CA-Librarian
libraries.

Please check out our web-site at http://www.c-cubed.net if you are
interested...

Todd Lucas
```

```
Hi John,

we're using Parkway Odbc driver to access our Cobol files. If your
Cobol files are residing on a Unix machine it is possible to access the
files through a Server using Tcp/Ip, which is really fast.

Cause it's ODBC you are able to use your files within all ODBC
supporting programms.

To contact Parkway please mailto: parkway_software@compuserve.com or
visit their homepage on www.parkway-software.de (german/english).
```

## Thread: executables — 2 msgs

### executables

- **From:** "joachim boesmans" <boesmans@freegates.be>
- **Date:** 2000-04-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<x0NH4.26$xp.1489@ns2.gip.net>`

```
I have a schoolversion of acubench cobol, and I can't make executables.  Can
someone tell me where I can find this converter???

Thank you
Boesmans Joachim
```

#### ↳ Re: executables

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 2000-04-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8crd0h$rbj$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<x0NH4.26$xp.1489@ns2.gip.net>`

```
"joachim boesmans" <boesmans@freegates.be> wrote in message
news:x0NH4.26$xp.1489@ns2.gip.net...
> I have a schoolversion of acubench cobol, and I can't make executables.
Can
> someone tell me where I can find this converter???

You can't create executables with Acucobol, only intermediate files to use
with their runtime. For that purpose you will need a compiler (usually named
ccbl32.exe).

Once compiled, you may execute the code with their runtime engine, usually
named wrun32.exe.

Cheesle
```

#### ↳ Re: PowerCobol + Marshallsoft

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
Yes, I am. The following is from my Cobol.CBR file, where "WEIGH IN" is the
program id.  That should solve it for you.

[WEIGH IN.ENTRY]
SioBaud=WSC32.DLL
SioDTR=WSC32.DLL
SioGetc=WSC32.DLL
SioParms=WSC32.DLL
SioReset=WSC32.DLL
SioRTS=WSC32.DLL
SioDone=WSC32.DLL
```

<a id="t-1addbb784c"></a>
## Thread: COBOL conversion — 26 msgs

### COBOL conversion

- **From:** softpaww <softpawNOsoSPAM@telenet.net.invalid>
- **Date:** 2000-04-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<03fc63cc.ea1ff9c8@usw-ex0107-049.remarq.com>`

```
I have NO idea how to do what I am going to ask about and would
appreciate any input.  I utilize a software in my business which was
converted from a Unix platform to a PC environment several years ago. 
At that time, it ran strictly in DOS on a pier to pier lan network.  It
was a few years ago made to run (or appear to run) in Windows
3.1...then eventually, Windows 95, 98, NT....on pretty much any kind of
network any user has.  Here is what I have been told but know nothing
about this kind of stuff and am just a user.  It was/is written in
Cobol and uses a GUI interface to present as a "WINDOW" application. 
(You can NOT open multiple windows of the application, so it is not
true windows).  I have also been told it is COBOL on the "back end",
C++ OR visual basic on the front end and uses MICRO FOCUS or FOCUS for
the "windowing"...I am probably using all the wrong terms and I
apologize for my ignorance in this regard.  What I am trying to find
out is what it would take to convert this program from its present
state to a CURRENT programming language.  This is NOT a scientific
program with complex algorithms (sp) as far as I know.  It does very
basic things like calender files, compile statisticial figures....it is
actually almost an accounting program, but is proprietary.  I am NOT
trying to steal the software.  I actually work for the company as a
trainer and have lots of people ask me when I travel why it is "STILL"
in COBOL....so, I am going to try to do some research and see what it
would take to get it out of COBOL. Also, there is a version of the SAME
software that is ORACLE ready which they charge more for.  My brother
tells me that if there is an ORACLE version ready that works, that most
of the COBOL would have to be gone in order for ORACLE to work as
ORACLE is an "open arcitechture".... ANY help/input will be MOST
appreciated.  I will be happy to answer or clarify anything which may
be unclear.  softpaw@telenet.net THANKS!


* Sent from RemarQ http://www.remarq.com The Internet's Discussion Network *
The fastest and easiest way to search and participate in Usenet - Free!
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL conversion

- **From:** jets@nbnet.nb.ca (Tony M. Mina)
- **Date:** 2000-04-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38f0e8bd.12747604@allnews.nbnet.nb.ca>`
- **References:** `<03fc63cc.ea1ff9c8@usw-ex0107-049.remarq.com>`

```
My client had several DOS systems running on a Novell network years ago.
These systems were also connected to a Unisys mainframe.  We did a study
whether it's worth converting to another system and found out that the
cost is not justifiable.  So what we did is to convert the existing
Cobol system amd make it Windows.  We went from Micro Focus DOS Cobol to
Fujitsu Cobol and used Cobol SP2 for the Windows interface and used
FormPrint QPR for the reports.  Also connected to a Unisys mainframe.
The cost was way below the cost if we convert to another system.  The
system is running on a Windows NT server.

It's been running now for serveral years and my client is very happy
with the decision to stay in Cobol especially the cost as well as the
maintenance of the system.

If you need more info, please email me privately.

Thanks

Tony M. Mina
Email: jets@nbnet.nb.ca
Web:  www.mc-sys.com

…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
```

```
Well, given that COBOL is a CURRENT programming language, and that Micro
Focus is a CURRENT GUI environment, it won't take anything to convert your
application into a CURRENT system because it already is CURRENT.





> program with complex algorithms (sp) as far as I know.  It does very
> basic things like calender files, compile statisticial figures....it is
> actually almost an accounting program, but is proprietary.  I am NOT
> trying to steal the software.  I actually work for the company as a
> trainer and have lots of people ask me when I travel why it is "STILL" in
COBOL
<snip>

Maybe because it works, is still working, and will keep on working?



> would take to get it out of COBOL. Also, there is a version of the SAME
> software that is ORACLE ready which they charge more for.  My brother

The company that makes this software in various versions does not provide a
way to switch between them?  I don't understand.  Could you clarify things a
bit?
Who is the application creator (your company/another company)?
Who is the application user (your company/another company)?


> tells me that if there is an ORACLE version ready that works, that most
> of the COBOL would have to be gone in order for ORACLE to work as

  What exactly is being discussed here?  Why would COBOL have to be "gone"
from your current application in order for you to use completely different
version of the same software?  The new software does not contain COBOL code,
so I don't see how taking the COBOL code out of the original application
helps (other than to stop the original application from working unless other
code was added in, but then the code would just be working the way it had in
the first place, right?).



  It sounds like you want a data transformation utility to convert your
current files into Oracle database files which can be used by this "new"
software version.
  You don't need to do any COBOL code conversion to do this.
  Just a few posts upstream someone mentioned a utility called DBMS/Copy
that does this sort of thing, and other people mentioned other products.
  Do a powersearch on www.deja.com and do a search on DBMS/Copy on this
newsgroup and look into all the messages in that thread to find the names of
the other products.  Or do a web search.

  But honestly, if the vendor of your application is some 3rd party, and you
are paying ongoing license/support fees, this should be something they at
least give you solid advice on, if not actually do it themselves.


> ORACLE is an "open arcitechture".... ANY help/input will be MOST
> appreciated.  I will be happy to answer or clarify anything which may
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
```

```
"ChrisOsborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:OTZQsMko$GA.235@cpmsnbbsa03...
> Well, given that COBOL is a CURRENT programming language, and that Micro
> Focus is a CURRENT GUI environment, it won't take anything to convert your
> application into a CURRENT system because it already is CURRENT.

I do agree in the first and the third statement, but claiming Micro Focus to
be a GUI environment is to pull the trigger too far. Micro Focus is (and
probably an excellent variant too) a development tool for Cobol, providing
support FOR GUI environment yes, but it has never been a GUI environment
itself.
Windows, OS/2 presentation Manager, Apple System x, are all examples of GUI
environments.

Cheesle
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL conversion

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38F14D31.F0663DEB@home.com>`
- **References:** `<03fc63cc.ea1ff9c8@usw-ex0107-049.remarq.com> <OTZQsMko$GA.235@cpmsnbbsa03> <8crdcs$rsb$1@news.cerf.net>`

```


Cheesle wrote:
> 
> "ChrisOsborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com> wrote in message
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Cheesle

Bob - jump in and say something nasty about AcuCOBOL <G>. Ib - Next time
you are in his direction - swipe this fellow-Viking over the head with a
2 x 4 <G>

Mr. Cheesle have I been wasting my time. M/F NetExpress is very much
CURRENT as regards GUIs - but take your point about it not being an O/S.
Now here's the problem - got 3 years to find out all the nuances of
Windows ? N/E provides a complete hierarchy of classes for all of the
Windowing controls with methods to flip-them, churn-them, do
what-the-hell you want - if you've got the time. At the moment these
classes are independent of one another grouped under 'GUI' - each doing
its own thing. Remember why Thane went the SP2 route - because Flexus
have done that research bit and provide him with the quick leverage into
Windows.

M/F's Dialog System goes about 80-90% of the way for Windowing - but
then you have to familiarize yourself with OO GUIs to handle the
exceptions - some just don't want the challenge of OO, preferring to
stick to structured. (And I don't blame them).

Currently I'm working on a class MyDialog.cbl which uses a very small
subset of those Windows controls - but here's the interesting bit - if
M/F were to take my Mickey Mouse approach and develop a Merant Class
MFDialog.cbl with sub-classes for each of the controls, (using the many,
many methods they already have coded), they would have a very serious
contender up against Flexus Sp2, Norcom ScreenIO - and dare I say it -
leave your 'dual screens' in the dust <G>

I haven't the time to dabble - but I'm also pretty confident that
Activex controls could probably be built from the GUI classes, using
drawing and painting methods.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL conversion

```
Did you know that we normally are friendly, quiet people in Scandinavia. I
would never use a 2x4 to anything like that, and on the other hand - he
would probably just shout 'The door's open'.

Now I am a little confused about when to call something a GUI environment.
When I start my GUI Workbench 16- or 32-bit I invoke my GUI-Edit-Animate
using Drag'n-Drop. That is a GUI environment to me, and I beleive that the
operating system supports that environment - even the one from Billy
Windoza.

Likewise NetExpress and Mainframe Express are GUI environments - even if we
use them to develop character based applications. My point is, that when the
environment I am in as a developer is GUI all over, I will call it a GUI
environment - even if it's not the operating system itself.

That is what G-U-I stands for, isn't it?

Regards
Ib
```

```
It's not. A 2x4 is a piece of wood used (for example) in framing house walls, 
so-called because its dimensions are nominally 2 by 4 inches. Actual size is 
about 9cm wide by 4cm thick; length varies, of course, but 8 feet (244cm) is 
typical. Getting whacked with one would hurt.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL conversion

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 2000-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38fdc20a.7138485@news.epix.net>`
- **References:** `<03fc63cc.ea1ff9c8@usw-ex0107-049.remarq.com> <OTZQsMko$GA.235@cpmsnbbsa03> <8crdcs$rsb$1@news.cerf.net> <38F14D31.F0663DEB@home.com> <38fb4fce.13570072@news.epix.net> <8di402$n3j$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
"Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no> wrote:

>"Bob Wolfe" <rtwolfe@flexus.com> wrote in message
[snip]
>Sounds good, I ain't sure what a 2x4 means, but it sure don't sound good to
>be swiped by :-)

Cheesle:

It's not fun to be swiped by a 2 x 4.  Now...for a description.  A 2 x
4 (or "two by four") is an object fashioned from cellulose, which can
be acquired in varying lengths, typically utilized as a structural
component in the development of various 3 dimensional structures.
They are commonly used to erect residential or commercial edifices.
Multiple 2 x 4's are generally fastened together by small sharpened
pins crafted from iron ore (or recycled Yugos) for the purpose of
creating three-dimensional chambers.

In other words, a 2 x 4 is board that is used to build homes among
other things.

The reason that they are called 2 x 4's is because the width of a
standard 2 x 4 is 3.5 inches (89 mm) and the depth is 1.5 inches (39
mm)  I think that they should be called 39 by 89's.  It would be far
more accurate.

Please don't blame me....I didn't make the rules!

2 x 4's come in varying lengths which can be cut to suit your specific
needs, although current technology does not allow them to be
lengthened.  This will become possible when the brilliant Doc Dwarf
perfects his patented "board stretching device"

>> Hey, Cheesle....are you planning to travel to San Diego for the
>> Acucorp users conference?
>
>Nah, that's pretty far isn't?

It is....we are still trying to decide.

[snip]


Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL conversion

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Warren Simmons" <w.g.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<NapL4.13284$PV.903715@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<03fc63cc.ea1ff9c8@usw-ex0107-049.remarq.com> <OTZQsMko$GA.235@cpmsnbbsa03> <8crdcs$rsb$1@news.cerf.net> <38F14D31.F0663DEB@home.com> <38fb4fce.13570072@news.epix.net> <8di402$n3j$1@news.cerf.net> <38fdc20a.7138485@news.epix.net>`

```
This is a fairly comprehensive description of 2 by 4's.  However, the
authors missed a couple of facts that add nothing to the discussion of
cobol that
may be fascinating to some.

1.  In early days of saw mill operations, lumber was cut to measure for a
variety of uses using whole numbers.  Two by four inches, two by six
inches, etc.

2.  It became apparent that Object orientation would stretch the supply of
lumber, and do the same job.  New dimensions were established, and
the old names were retained to avoid retraining.

3. Other materials besides lumber are used to build 2 by 4's.  Example:
steel.

4.  If you want to see the old style lumber, visit an old house, and take a
trip to the cellar and carry your tape measure.

Warren Simmons
"Bob Wolfe" <rtwolfe@flexus.com> wrote in message
news:38fdc20a.7138485@news.epix.net...

> [snip]

to
> >be swiped by :-)
>
…[39 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL conversion

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 2000-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38feff78.1826544@news.epix.net>`
- **References:** `<03fc63cc.ea1ff9c8@usw-ex0107-049.remarq.com> <OTZQsMko$GA.235@cpmsnbbsa03> <8crdcs$rsb$1@news.cerf.net> <38F14D31.F0663DEB@home.com> <38fb4fce.13570072@news.epix.net> <8di402$n3j$1@news.cerf.net> <38fdc20a.7138485@news.epix.net> <NapL4.13284$PV.903715@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
"Warren Simmons" <w.g.simmons@worldnet.att.net> wrote:

>This is a fairly comprehensive description of 2 by 4's.  However, the
>authors missed a couple of facts that add nothing to the discussion of
>cobol that
>may be fascinating to some.

You're absolutely correct!

>1.  In early days of saw mill operations, lumber was cut to measure for a
>variety of uses using whole numbers.  Two by four inches, two by six
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>the old names were retained to avoid retraining.

I don't believe that object orientation was responsible for the new
dimensions.  I believe that the dimensions were changed when an
upstart sawmill called the Microwood Corporation introduced a new and
improved 2 x 4 with smaller dimensions (new ideas and innovations) and
declared it to be the standard.

Soon, all the other sawmills mysteriously went out of business and
everyone was forced to adopt the new standard immediately because it
was the only thing available.

;-)  (Ain't I the cynic today!)

>3. Other materials besides lumber are used to build 2 by 4's.  Example:
>steel.

Yes, but those 2 x 4's were developed by Sun Microsteel and the debate
rages on whether to use wooden studs from Microwood or steel studs
from Sun Microsteel.

>4.  If you want to see the old style lumber, visit an old house, and take a
>trip to the cellar and carry your tape measure.

Tell me about it!  Years ago, my wife talked me into buying a large
110 year old house because it was really cool.  But I'm the guy who
has to repair everything all the time.  Nothing is standard and
nothing is square.  I've wiped out more cedar trees through excessive
use of cedar shims than I want to think about.




Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

```
Note: 2x4's are 2inch by 4inch after they have been cut, and before they
have been planed smooth.  A full size 2x4 is rough to the touch, not smooth.

This is not unlike the hamburger places advertising uncooked weight,
but selling cooked patties that weigh much less.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL conversion

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 2000-04-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
Hey,
I can see your problem. Sombody made an application, and suddenly you find
out that the data cannot be shared, and 2 million people in your company
would *luw* to maintain it, if it just was made in Oracle Forms.

Now, your present application is something. It has been worth to move it
from Unix to DOS, From DOS to Windows 3.x. Someone has reprogrammed the
application to put in the GUI-dialog. The structure in an old Character
based application is very different from the one you need have, when you put
on the GUI.

I am telling you this, so you can see that your company has made investments
in the present application.

It is probably quit easy to change your current filesystem to an Oracle
database. Letting your present application use Oracle gives you data
sharing, and it will be possible to extract date for other purposes by the
use of Oracle Forms.

If you consider the present application to be ready for major reprogramming,
and if you choose Oracle because some wise guys are saying that Cobol is old
technology,  I must say that their knowledge is built up from reading
colored magazines, not from education. We are nowadays making very modern
applications in Object Oriented Cobol, and in traditional Cobol with various
GUI fronts.

You can choose Oracle if that is the skills your company prefer, but if you
have cobol programmers as well, you should consider to use an Oracle
Database for your data.

Please let your analysis be based upon facts, instead of feelings and
fashion.

Regards
Ib
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL conversion

- **From:** Charles F Hankel <noos@hankel.freedombird.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.135ae90f84f0dd5f9896d6@news.freedombird.net>`
- **References:** `<03fc63cc.ea1ff9c8@usw-ex0107-049.remarq.com>`

```
I noticed that softpaww as softpawNOsoSPAM@telenet.net.invalid said...
> I am NOT
> trying to steal the software.  I actually work for the company as a
> trainer and have lots of people ask me when I travel why it is "STILL"
> in COBOL....so, I am going to try to do some research and see what it
> would take to get it out of COBOL.

There's at least one misconception to correct here and that is that 
COBOL is very much a current language.  It is more modern than, say, C 
and C++, because the standard moves with the times.

It is most important that you understand that there is absolutely 
nothing wrong with COBOL and that it is far more desirable to use it for 
business oriented software than just about any other language out there.  
If anyone suggests that COBOL is dead, old-fashioned or other such 
derogatory term, refer them to this newsgroup.

> Also, there is a version of the SAME
> software that is ORACLE ready which they charge more for.  My brother
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> be unclear.  softpaw@telenet.net THANKS!

It is more likely that, if there is an Oracle version, it is also in 
COBOL as there would be little point in rewriting the software 
completely.  However, without knowing more about the product, I can't be 
categorical about this.
```

```
do not know how to communicate between the mainframe and PC(server, not
terminal).

who can help me . THANK VERY MUCH !
```

#### ↳ Re: how does mainframe communicate with PC ?

- **From:** "ChrisOsborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<#p5OsCko$GA.225@cpmsnbbsa03>`
- **References:** `<8cq36f$bmq$1@news.cz.js.cn>`

```
Well, if your PC is connected to the same network as the mainframe, and both
platforms have FTP deployed, use FTP.

Microsoft has their SNA Server software, which should pretty much hookup
with any mainframe also running SNA (of course, it has to be set up on both
sides).

There are other methods, of course.  A variety of vendors (Barr comes to
mind right away, but all my Enterprise Systems Journals are off in storage,
so that's all I can think of off hand) sell software and/or hardware either
for the PC or mainframes that allow communications between the two.

Try doing some web searches to find out more exact information.



"zhengjc" <zhengjc75@263.net> wrote in message
news:8cq36f$bmq$1@news.cz.js.cn...

I
> do not know how to communicate between the mainframe and PC(server, not
> terminal).
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: how does mainframe communicate with PC ?

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7T3I4.156638$Hq3.3854241@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<8cq36f$bmq$1@news.cz.js.cn>`

```
Two options: SNA or TCP/IP.

SNA requires a gateway server on the PC. Microsoft, IBM and Novell provide
SNA gateway software. Other names to know are IRMA and RHUMBA.

TCP/IP requires TCP/IP running on the mainframe. Once you've got that
installed then there are a bunch of ways to make it happen. If 'twere me I
would strongly consider IBM's MQ series to manage the PC/mainframe
communications. It'll be responsible for guaranteeing that all messages get
where they're going.

Karl

I
```

#### ↳ Re: how does mainframe communicate with PC ?

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-04-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38F1C3B6.3CEC200B@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<8cq36f$bmq$1@news.cz.js.cn>`

```
zhengjc wrote:
> 
> I want to build a bank(virtual and simply) on the mainframe of
> OS/390. But I do not know how to communicate between the mainframe
> and PC(server, not terminal).
> 

My corporate intranet is run on a Domino Web Server on OS/390.  The
front end could easily be built using either CGI programming or by
Javascript in your browser and SSL (encrypt).  The HTTP protocol that
Browsers communicate with the Web server is dead simple.  You can
simply put in a call to a CGI program passing and receiving the
appropriate data.

For more tied applications you might want to investigate LU6.2, about
all I know is that it was designed for smart terminal traffic and can
hook up with an application on both ends.  There are various tools
that support this, I cant name any though I was never close enough to
the action.

For simple data transfer, as noted elsewhere, simple FTP is the best
method.  The mainframe can push data to your PC system which can
monitor it's arrival and kick off a process and vice versa.  I do this
quite a lot and it works well.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: how does mainframe communicate with PC ?

- **From:** Joseph Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2000-04-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38F209E7.4A290630@iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<8cq36f$bmq$1@news.cz.js.cn> <38F1C3B6.3CEC200B@zip.com.au>`

```

Ken Foskey wrote:
> 
> zhengjc wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Ok,
1. Forget SNA and APPC/LU6.2.
   You would be tying yourself into a protocol that may not have a future.
2. Use TCP/IP for your transport.
   IBM mainframes have it built in (OS/390) along with a UNIX layer.
3. Use DB2 on the mainframe to hold your data.
4. Use CICS on the mainframe to run your transactions.
5. Use MQ Series to communicate over TCP/IP from your PC to CICS.
   You can go direct to CICS via either Sockets or CICS Web Server.

You did not say how many transactions per second you wish to run.
If the number is high, then you have to break the transaction up into
validation and execution phases.

You have to validate your incoming transaction. If it is allowed to
proceed, you send your response back to the PC allowing the transaction,
you queue the validated transaction to one of many work queues, logging the
transaction details in a journal. The actual work of updating accounts happens
some time later when the queued transaction is processed.

This allows for a fast turn around of the users transaction, and the steady
update of users accounts as the update processes work their way through the
validated transaction queues.
```

<a id="t-eaa2a4b236"></a>
## Thread: File Class In OO COBOL — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo)_

### File Class In OO COBOL

- **From:** "Westley Lodge Pty Ltd" <wlodge@hotkey.net.au>
- **Date:** 2000-04-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38f056b3@news.iprimus.com.au>`

```
Hi there all.

Just wondering if it is possible to create a class (FUJITSU COBOL in
particular), with all of the methods and properties to handle file
processing. The idea is rather than having a module for each file I use, I'd
rather have the one class, which I use to initialize an instance for each
file I would use, and use the methods and properties to process the file.

I had a go at it, and I have a property to set the file name. However, I am
not quite sure on how to have the one/single class to handle each filess
DIFFERENT record layout (FD).

Any comments / examples will be greatly appreciated.

Regards

Theo
```

#### ↳ Re: File Class In OO COBOL

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-04-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38F064A3.109F78E3@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<38f056b3@news.iprimus.com.au>`

```
Westley Lodge Pty Ltd wrote:
> 
> Hi there all.
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> Theo

Jimmy has a set of routines for this.

If you need to process a series of files you develop a base class and
through inheritance on that base class you specialize the objects to
do what you need.

The other option depending upon your needs is that the file instance
is contained in another object (you have a reference to the file
object inside you class).  This is because the file is not the primary
focus of the object just an incidental method of holding the data.

Could you explain a little of what you are trying to do. This will
give us a clearer picture on how to structure the application.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

#### ↳ Re: File Class In OO COBOL

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38F0AABB.C4C7EBE2@home.com>`
- **References:** `<38f056b3@news.iprimus.com.au>`

```


Westley Lodge Pty Ltd wrote:
> 
> Hi there all.
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> Theo

Theo,

#1 - Get yourself a copy of Wilson (Will) Price "Elements of OO COBOL".
I don't know if the book has been updated but he issued an addendum for
the file handling you are after. The book is published by
cobolreport.com/objectz.com. The text is NetExpress based - but you can
most certainly adapt if for F/J. Should you have any problems converting
to F/J - consult with Thane Hubbell who is experimenting with F/J OO.

(Just as background, Thane and I have found we can 'swap' F/J and N/E OO
COBOL - I can get a clean compile if I set ISO 2000 directives in N/E -
but it wont work in N/E until I substitute Class is ..... instead of F/J
Repository, plus I need to add a Factory). 

#2 - Check out the recent message I wrote which has a zipfile on OO file
handling - Re: 3-Tier, March 30. Note my code is one class per file -
with research it is probable that you can create one class for ALL files
and use instances. However, this is where I suggest caution. If you get
too clever then the bloody thing can become too complicated - causing
you no small amount of headaches in understandibility and
maintainability.

Good luck !

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: File Class In OO COBOL

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-04-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38fb7d65.29253361@news.cox-internet.com>`
- **References:** `<38f056b3@news.iprimus.com.au>`

```
The "Best" solution I have "Concieved" of is to have one generic file
handling class, and code override methods for each FD in a new
override class.  Still more work than I want to do as well.


On Sun, 09 Apr 2000 10:08:51 GMT, "Westley Lodge Pty Ltd"
<wlodge@hotkey.net.au> wrote:

>Hi there all.
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: File Class In OO COBOL

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8dg36u$aji$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<38f056b3@news.iprimus.com.au> <38fb7d65.29253361@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
My tendency would be to have a class for each file, then a superclass that
inherited all files for a system.  Much easier to work with.

As for the problem of multiple record sizes, it should be fairly trivial.
The record buffer is always set to the size of the largest record, and so
should the method argument. The other records are redefinition's of that
record anyway.

I also have a "sub-class" inherited by all file classes.  That particular
class has a method for handling and storing file-id variables, plus the
standard get a file id method that it inherits from the base.  It stores the
file id in a fixed, system wide Isam file, indexed by the filename.

Thane Hubbell wrote in message <38fb7d65.29253361@news.cox-internet.com>...
>The "Best" solution I have "Concieved" of is to have one generic file
>handling class, and code override methods for each FD in a new
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>>processing. The idea is rather than having a module for each file I use,
I'd
>>rather have the one class, which I use to initialize an instance for each
>>file I would use, and use the methods and properties to process the file.
>>
>>I had a go at it, and I have a property to set the file name. However, I
am
>>not quite sure on how to have the one/single class to handle each filess
>>DIFFERENT record layout (FD).
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```

  Judy Forbes <jaforb@ptdprolog.net> wrote:
> I am writing a paper on the pro's and con's of external sorting vs
```

#### ↳ Re: sorting

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-04-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38F1E6D0.B4B2110F@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<38F1889A.FC4B077D@ptdprolog.net>`

```


Judy Forbes wrote:
> 
> I am writing a paper on the pro's and con's of external sorting vs cobol
> sorts. I know all about the differance in run times. I have had little
> luck in looking in the books at our libary. so can any one give me a few
> others. thanks

Please tell us something about your environment - it makes a difference.

I use external sorts whenever I have a sort by a field in a file:
Advantages:
     I can change the sort without changing the program
     Several simple steps are easier to maintain than fewer, more
complicated steps
     I can use the same program with different sorted files when sort
order should change
Disadvantages:
     External sorts do not use the field names that we see in COBOL
     I can programmatically select one of multiple sorts in an internal
sort.

I use internal sorts whenever I have a sort by a value which is not in
my file.
This can be a calculated value, it can be a value from looking up
something in a database.  
It can also be in a sorted report which keeps track of pages and/or
columns.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: sorting

- **From:** "Pat" <patp@cpinternet.com>

```

>I use internal sorts whenever I have a sort by a value which

>I use external sorts

We just completed (or I did, anyway) our text chapter in school on sorting,
just using the SORT SORT-FILE, ASCENDING KEY ETC.....

Was wondering if someone could clue me in on what internal and external
sorts are????  What is the SORT clause, as I mentioned above??  Not for
```
