Thanks,

Pat
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: sorting

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-04-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38F252EA.555409EB@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<38F1889A.FC4B077D@ptdprolog.net> <38F1E6D0.B4B2110F@cusys.edu> <uwrI4.215$3E5.1133@newsfeed.slurp.net>`

```


Pat wrote:
> 
> Howard Brazee wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> homework, just for my information.  My homework is done (Yay!)

An internal sort is one coded in the COBOL source as above.  In the
mainframe environment, it actually calls a sort program, passing data as
needed.

An external sort is a utility program.  You pass a file to it along with
some parms, and it creates sorted output.  In this discussion, that
sorted output is then read by a Cobol program.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: sorting

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Pat" <patp@cpinternet.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<R6xI4.607$3E5.3169@newsfeed.slurp.net>`
- **References:** `<38F1889A.FC4B077D@ptdprolog.net> <38F1E6D0.B4B2110F@cusys.edu> <uwrI4.215$3E5.1133@newsfeed.slurp.net> <38F252EA.555409EB@cusys.edu>`

```
thanks, Howard, for the answer to my sort question.

Pat
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: sorting

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Charles F Hankel <noos@hankel.freedombird.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.1364425926070c6e9896f6@news.freedombird.net>`
- **References:** `<38F1889A.FC4B077D@ptdprolog.net> <38F1E6D0.B4B2110F@cusys.edu> <uwrI4.215$3E5.1133@newsfeed.slurp.net> <38F252EA.555409EB@cusys.edu> <R6xI4.607$3E5.3169@newsfeed.slurp.net>`

```
I noticed that Pat as patp@cpinternet.com said...
> thanks, Howard, for the answer to my sort question.

There's a further dimension on mainframes where the sort program is 
actually in three phases; input, sort, output.  There are several exits 
available via which COBOL and other programs can be called, but the 
relevant ones are E15 and E35.

The E15 passes unsorted data records from the input phase while the E35 
passes sorted records from the output phase.  Thus, it is perfectly 
feasible to write a COBOL program that receives sorted records via the 
E35 and produces an informative report for management.  It could even be 
enhanced by a routine dropping irrelevant records via the E15 so that 
only those needed are actually sorted.

There's loads of scenarios that can be fulfilled in this way but, in 
reality, with the sample above, most people don't know about the sort 
exits and simply specify a program that ignores records, sorts what's 
left and reports on the remains.

How you put the spin on this depends on your point of view really 
because the compiler, in doing the latter task, actually does the former 
but with a greater overhead.  So, you could recommend external sorts for 
efficiency, or internal sorts for homogeneity in applications.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: sorting

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8dirkq02fq@enews3.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<38F1889A.FC4B077D@ptdprolog.net> <38F1E6D0.B4B2110F@cusys.edu>`

```
I can think of another good reason for external:
In my environment COBOL II OS390, when a program with an internal sort
abends, the Abend-Aid usually ends up in the middle of a very large dump
from SyncSort, sometimes 500,000+ lines.

I just finished writing a daily job with 40 steps.  17 steps are programs, 6
steps are IDCAMS, 1 step is IEFBR14, 2 are IEBGENER and the rest (14) are
simple SyncSort steps.  1 of the programs uses 1 internal sort, another
program uses 2 internal sorts.  Most of the programs are under 500 lines of
code.  Performance is decent, nothing spectacular.  Reading all those files
over and over again is technically reproduction of work which is very bad
from an efficiency standpoint, and from a common-sense standpoint.  I know
this job would be alot faster, at least twice as fast, if I had used VSAM
files.  But the difference in maintainability and simplicity is large enough
for me to have taken the sequential processing, external sorts route with
this job.  It is what THIS PARTICULAR JOB required.

If anybody ever tells you one is better than the other (external vs.
internal), remember it all depends on what tool better gets the job done.  I
would not drive a nail into a wooden board using my head.

Jeff

----------
In article <38F1E6D0.B4B2110F@cusys.edu>, Howard Brazee
<howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote:


>
>
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> columns.
```

#### ↳ Re: sorting

- **From:** georgelnj@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-04-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8dgrtq$im2$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<38F1889A.FC4B077D@ptdprolog.net>`

```
In article <38F1889A.FC4B077D@ptdprolog.net>,

  Judy Forbes <jaforb@ptdprolog.net> wrote:

> I am writing a paper on the pro's and con's of external sorting vs cobol

> sorts. I know all about the differance in run times. I have had little

> luck in looking in the books at our libary. so can any one give me a few

> others. thanks

>

>





One drawback Ive had over the years with INTERNAL sorts which

I strongly avoid is that you CANT see the intermediate file(s)

which the program reads. With the JCL you can preserve and catalog

the file to see the DATA and be sure the program behaves correctly.



Also you can slice and dice a LARGE file to extract just

the fields you need rather than reading a file with a large record length,
etc.  SAVES I/O space.........



Also I find programming with EXTERNAL sorts quicker to program

and maintain
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: sorting

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-04-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38FC5EC1.9E862C0B@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<38F1889A.FC4B077D@ptdprolog.net> <8dgrtq$im2$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
georgelnj@my-deja.com wrote:
> 
> One drawback Ive had over the years with INTERNAL sorts which
> I strongly avoid is that you CANT see the intermediate file(s)
> which the program reads. With the JCL you can preserve and catalog
> the file to see the DATA and be sure the program behaves correctly.

I only needed to look at an internal sort's intermediate file once,
almost 20 years ago.  I was converting from small Burroughs to large
Burroughs and had a record size which was too small to be sorted.  It
took a while to find out why my results were bad.
 
> Also you can slice and dice a LARGE file to extract just
> the fields you need rather than reading a file with a large record length,
> etc.  SAVES I/O space.........

Internal sorts work exactly the same way here.  In fact, internal sorts
are much more likely to be sliced and diced to contain exactly the
fields you need.  After all, they are named fields.  Most all (90+%)
external sorts I have seen simply sort the input file (with includes and
excludes, but with all of the fields).
 
> Also I find programming with EXTERNAL sorts quicker to program
> and maintain

Agreed.  Except for translating from your copy book to the figure out
the positions of your key fields.  

And obviously, except for cases where you have to calculate the sort
field.
```

"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
>   <snip>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> See your current (SORT)programming guide for information on this facility.

I have seen an example of this, but traditions die hard.  A sufficient
improvement would be if the named fields used the exact same copy
members as the COBOL uses, so that there wouldn't be double
maintenance.   

In my case, we use the data dictionary more than we use copy members
though.

I do put comments in my sorts to assist maintainers, e.g.:
  SORT FIELDS=(74,07,A,       TERM/INST  
               32,05,A,       SUBCODE
               19,06,A,       HOURS
               04,12,A),      MISC
         FORMAT=CH
  SUM FIELDS=NONE                     ELIMINATE DUPLICATES
  INCLUDE COND=(01,3,CH,EQ,C'T11')
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: sorting

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** fyaeger5@vnet.ibm.com
- **Date:** 2000-04-18T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<8di44i$v8d$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<38F1889A.FC4B077D@ptdprolog.net> <8dgrtq$im2$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38FC5EC1.9E862C0B@cusys.edu> <8dhpii$1oa$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:

> FYI (for IBM-environment people),
>I know that the latest release of DFSort ... now supports the use of
>"named" fields in SORT statements.
> See your current (SORT)programming guide for information on this
>facility.

Yes, DFSORT Release 14 supports symbols for fields and constants in
DFSORT and ICETOOL statements.  For a brief overview, see:

http://www.storage.ibm.com/software/sort/srtmr14.htm#sfc

For complete details, see Chapter 7 of "DFSORT Application Programming
Guide":

http://www.s390.ibm.com:80/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/BOOKS/ICECA107/7%2e0

Frank Yaeger - DFSORT Team (Specialties: Symbols, Y2K, OUTFIL, ICETOOL)
DFSORT is on the web at URL:
  http://www.ibm.com/storage/dfsort/

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: sorting

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Frank Yaeger <fyaeger5@vnet.ibm.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38FC964B.4784767E@vnet.ibm.com>`
- **References:** `<38F1889A.FC4B077D@ptdprolog.net> <8dgrtq$im2$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38FC5EC1.9E862C0B@cusys.edu> <8dhpii$1oa$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:

>   <snip>
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> See your current (SORT)programming guide for information on this facility.

Right. DFSORT Release 14 supports symbols for fields and constants in
DFSORT and ICETOOL statements.  For a brief overview, see:

http://www.storage.ibm.com/software/sort/srtmr14.htm#sfc

For complete details, see Chapter 7 of "DFSORT Application Programming
Guide":
    http://www.s390.ibm.com:80/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/BOOKS/ICECA107/7%2e0

Frank Yaeger - DFSORT Team (Specialties: Symbols, Y2K, OUTFIL, ICETOOL)
DFSORT is on the web at URL:
    http://www.ibm.com/storage/dfsort/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: sorting

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 2000-04-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VB%K4.17287$0o4.118736@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<38F1889A.FC4B077D@ptdprolog.net> <8dgrtq$im2$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38FC5EC1.9E862C0B@cusys.edu>`

```
In article <38FC5EC1.9E862C0B@cusys.edu>,
Howard Brazee  <Please, do, not, e-mail, replies, to, Howard, Brazee, post,
 	them!!> wrote:

[snippage]

>Agreed.  Except for translating from your copy book to the figure out
>the positions of your key fields.  

I've always found the following to be useful in IBM mainframe
environments, especially the more primitive ones which do not
have tools like FileAid that will give you field-displacements... have,
among your skeletons, a SORTSKEL program in COBOL; in the SD you COPY
(layoutnm) or just bring it in with an editor COPY line-command.  The
PROCEDURE DIVISION is two statements:

SORT SORTFIL (ascending keys/descending keys based on what was COPY'd into
the SD) USING INFIL GIVING INFIL.
GOBACK.

Compile and link this to your own USERID.TEST.LOAD library and do some
olde-fashionede 'object oriented programming'... by BROWSEing the load
module.  Issue the line command F 'FIELDS'... it might not give you
*everything* you need, right off the bat, but it is a start.

DD
```

- **Message-ID:** `<01a94420.ef823f23@usw-ex0107-049.remarq.com>`

     At my business we have an AcuCobol database.  (one that no one
here knows how to program in)  We are using a product called
CyberScience to write reports off of the database.  My problem is the
report writer is not very flexible.  So, I was told to look into a way
to access this database using MS Access or VB.  I'm not even sure if
it's possible, or if it is, what I would need to make it happen.  If
anyone can be of assistance I would appreciate it.  Thanks in advance
for your help.


* Sent from RemarQ http://www.remarq.com The Internet's Discussion Network *
The fastest and easiest way to search and participate in Usenet - Free!
```

#### ↳ Re: Quick question

- **From:** "ChrisOsborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uydZww0o$GA.225@cpmsnbbsa03>`
- **References:** `<01a94420.ef823f23@usw-ex0107-049.remarq.com>`

```
Since you have mentioned MS Access and VB I'll assume (oh so dangerously)
that you're working on a wintel system.

If you could please post the name (or vendor name) of the database AcuCOBOL
is currently being used with, more information would be forthcoming.

If all you're looking to do is transform one RDBMS into another, there are a
whole set of 3rd party software packages that can do this for you.

Use www.deja.com and do a powersearch on this newsgroup with the keywords
DBMS and database and look through the posts over the past week or so . . .
questions similar to yours have been asked and answered only days ago . . .
feel free to check them out.

Also check out www.faqs.org (because I can't remember Mr. Kleins faq URL)
and look up comp.lang.cobol; the FAQ contains more information related to
your question as well as info on how to get the most of the newsgroup.  As I
recall, the FAQ was posted to this newsgroup only days ago, and it too can
be found through www.deja.com.

Oh, and if your newsreader, like mine, can pull back the last couple of
weeks worth of messages the first time you pull back messages from a
newsgroup with it, you can skip going to www.deja.com or www.faqs.org, and
get that information right from the newsgroup messages.

Hi,
    If your data files are under Windows system, then you just need
AcuODBC to let MS Access or VB contact with Acucobol's
data files. If your data files are under UNIX, you need to add an
AcuServer positioned in UNIX.
Hope this can help you. If you need more information, you
can contact with Acucorp, or visit www.acucorp.com

#### ↳ Re: Quick question

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 2000-04-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8cvg1m$82e$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<01a94420.ef823f23@usw-ex0107-049.remarq.com>`

```
"H. B. Corder" <bcorderNObcSPAM@sgtc.com.invalid> wrote in message
news:01a94420.ef823f23@usw-ex0107-049.remarq.com...
>      At my business we have an AcuCobol database.  (one that no one

Acucobol is no database, but a Cobol dialect. Their filesystem is named
Vision. I presume that is what you refer to.

> here knows how to program in)  We are using a product called
> CyberScience to write reports off of the database.  My problem is the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> for your help.

You may access Vision files from any ODBC compliant front end utility if you
have AcuODBC, which is the ODBC driver for Acucobol Vision files.

If you don't know of this option, it is likely that you don't have it. In
that case you may want to purchase it (www.acucorp.com), you will however,
need to have access to the source of your applications to generate the
mapping tables of your vision files to be used in ODBC.

Cheesle
```

I was told that we are running the database in Windows NT.  Also, they
were told (by whom I don't know) that they had to have a AcuCobol
programmer come in and set up "pointer tables" so that the AcuODBC
could function.  I guess my question is, is that true and if so would
it be possible for someone with out any experience with cobol to
accomplish this?  And if not, what are the steps involved.  Thank you
all you helped.



* Sent from RemarQ http://www.remarq.com The Internet's Discussion Network *
The fastest and easiest way to search and participate in Usenet - Free!

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Quick question

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 2000-04-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8d7m28$gmn$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<01a94420.ef823f23@usw-ex0107-049.remarq.com> <8cvg1m$82e$1@news.cerf.net> <18a476d4.40399491@usw-ex0107-049.remarq.com>`

```
"H. B. Corder" <bcorderNObcSPAM@sgtc.com.invalid> wrote in message
news:18a476d4.40399491@usw-ex0107-049.remarq.com...
> I was told that we are running the database in Windows NT.  Also, they
> were told (by whom I don't know) that they had to have a AcuCobol
> programmer come in and set up "pointer tables" so that the AcuODBC

I guess that the "pointer tables" they are referring to are the XFD files,
the purpose of these files are to map the Cobol file such that when the ODBC
filter receives a record it uses the XFD file to identify the fields in the
record.

> could function.  I guess my question is, is that true and if so would
> it be possible for someone with out any experience with cobol to
> accomplish this?  And if not, what are the steps involved.  Thank you
> all you helped.

It is technically possible for someone that does not have Cobol experience
to do it, but it will be highly preferable to have knowledge to Acucobol in
particular. As I first said, you would need access to the source and an
Acucobol compiler to have the files made. It *is* also possible to create
the files manually, but, it will require an experienced Acucobol programmer,
and that person would also need access to the definition of the vision file.

Cheesle
```

Part of Procedure Division

       403-WRITE-HEADING-LINES.
           MOVE 1 TO LINE-COUNT-I.
           ADD 1 TO PAGE-COUNT-I.
           MOVE PAGE-COUNT-I TO HDG-PAGE-NUMBER.
           WRITE PRINT-LINE-I FROM HEADING-LINE-ONE
                 AFTER ADVANCING PAGE
           WRITE PRINT-LINE-I FROM HEADING-LINE-TWO

           WRITE PRINT-LINE-I FROM HEADING-LINE-FOUR
           WRITE PRINT-LINE-I FROM HEADING-LINE-FIVE
               AFTER ADVANCING 2 LINES

           MOVE STATE-CODE     TO CR-STATE-CODE

           MOVE CONTRIBUTION   TO CR-CONTRIBUTION
           WRITE PRINT-LINE-D FROM DETAIL-LINE
               AFTER ADVANCING 2 LINES.
           ADD 1 TO LINE-COUNT-I.

## Thread: How to open a QSAM file in append mode — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### How to open a QSAM file in append mode

- **From:** zsl_deja@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-04-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8cs4s5$be8$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hi all,
  Is there a method to allow us to open a QSAM file in append mode
instead of opening it in I/O mode,and read at the end of the file to
append a record?If it is possible,please tell me.

Thanks
Victor Zhang


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: How to open a QSAM file in append mode

- **From:** "DennisHarley" <LegacyBlue@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<#nK1R1wo$GA.207@cpmsnbbsa03>`
- **References:** `<8cs4s5$be8$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
As mentioned in previous posts DISP=MOD will do it for you.

You can also try OPEN EXTEND in your program.

Another option is to use GDGs.
Create a new Generation as each process writes to the file.
When you need the entire file, concatenate all Generations by not
specifying a Generation in the input DD.


<zsl_deja@my-deja.com> wrote in message
news:8cs4s5$be8$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> Hi all,
>   Is there a method to allow us to open a QSAM file in append mode
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

- **Date:** 2000-04-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eQrJW9wo$GA.260@cpmsnbbsa04>`
- **References:** `<8cs4s5$be8$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
As mentioned in previous posts DISP=MOD will do it for you.

You can also try OPEN EXTEND in your program.

Another option is to use GDGs.
Create a new Generation as each process writes to the file.
When you need the entire file, concatenate all Generations by not
specifying a Generation in the input DD.

Since you mention QSAM I can assume Mainframe.  Use a DISP of MOD in
your JCL and open the file for output.  The MOD will append to the end
of the file.  If you open I-O it will behave differently.

## Thread: Comparison to all 9's — 4 msgs

### Comparison to all 9's

- **From:** Corey Edwards <cedwards@gw.housing.umass.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-04-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38F2803F.C10769EB@gw.housing.umass.edu>`

```
COBOLMVS

if you have a field GROSS-ANNL-PREM that is PIC S9(7)V99,
and in the procedure division have code like:

IF GROSS-ANNL-PREM > 9999999.99
   NEXT SENTENCE
ELSE
   MOVE -9999999.99 TO MGROSS-A-P

how could GROSS-ANNL-PREM ever be more than all 9's?
i'm analyzing someone else's code and am trying to determine
the reason for these lines.  the person is no longer part of my group.

thanx,
lina

lguzman-edwards@usa.net
```

- **References:** `<38F2803F.C10769EB@gw.housing.umass.edu>`

```

never be true.  As to the reason for those lines, your guess is as good as
anyone's!

#### ↳ Re: Comparison to all 9's

- **From:** "Searcher" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yhvI4.301$t13.224@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<38F2803F.C10769EB@gw.housing.umass.edu>`

```
It can IF the Item is :
(from the IBM Language Reference Manual - and it has been this way for
YEARS)

    Note:  BINARY, COMPUTATIONAL, and COMPUTATIONAL-4 data items can be
affected by the BINARY and TRUNC  compiler option specifications.  For
information on the effect of these compiler options, see the IBM COBOL
Programming Guide for your platform.  (The BINARY compiler option is only
applicable to OS/2 and Windows  programs.)

From The COBOL Programming Guide:

You should use the TRUNC(OPT) option only if you are sure that the data
being moved into the binary areas will not have a value with larger
precision than that defined by the PICTURE clause for the binary item.
Otherwise, truncation of high-order digits may occur.  This truncation is
performed in the most efficient manner possible; thus, the results will be
dependent on the particular code sequence generated.  It is not possible
to predict the truncation without seeing the code sequence generated for a
particular statement.

The TRUNC(BIN) option applies to all COBOL language that processes USAGE
BINARY data.  When TRUNC(BIN) is in effect:

   BINARY receiving fields are truncated only at halfword, fullword, or
    doubleword boundaries.

   BINARY sending fields are treated as halfwords, fullwords, or
    doublewords.

   The full binary content of the field is significant.

   DISPLAY will convert the entire content of the binary field, with no
    truncation.

Note:  TRUNC(BIN) is the recommended option when interfacing with other
products that may place values in COBOL binary data items that are larger
than that defined by the data item's PICTURE clause (such as DB2, FORTRAN,
and PL/I).  For CICS considerations, see "Recommended Options" in
topic 3.11.3.

Example 1:

  01  BIN-VAR     PIC 99 USAGE BINARY
      .
      .
      .
      MOVE 123451 to BIN-VAR


+---------------------------------------------------------------------------
-----------+
� Figure 101. Values of the Data Item after the MOVE                     �
+---------------------------------------------------------------------------
------------�
�                                   �      Decimal �          Hex
�   Display      �
+---------------------------+--------------+-------------------------+------
--------�
� Sender                        �       123451 �  00|01|E2|3B    � 123451
�
+---------------------------+--------------+--------------+--------------�
� Receiver TRUNC(STD) �        51 �        00|33 �  51           �
+---------------------------+--------------+--------------+--------------�
� Receiver TRUNC(OPT) �  -7621 �      E2|3B �   2J           �
+---------------------------+--------------+--------------+--------------�
� Receiver TRUNC(BIN)   �  -7621 �      E2|3B �  762J         �
+------------------------------------------------------------------------+


A halfword of storage is allocated for BIN-VAR.  The result of this MOVE
statement if the program is compiled with the TRUNC(STD) option is 51; the
field is truncated to conform to the PICTURE clause.

If the program is compiled with the TRUNC(BIN) option, the result of the
MOVE statement is -7621.  The reason for this unusual looking answer is
that the nonzero high-order digits were truncated.  In this case, the
generated code sequence would merely move the lower halfword quantity
X'E23B' to the receiver.  Because the new truncated value overflowed into
the sign bit of the binary halfword, the value becomes a negative number.

This MOVE statement should not be compiled with the TRUNC(OPT) option
because 123451 has greater precision than the PICTURE clause for BIN-VAR.
If TRUNC(OPT) was used, however, the results again would be -7621.  This
is because the best performance was gained by not doing a decimal
truncate.

Example 2:

  01  BIN-VAR     PIC 9(6)  USAGE BINARY
      .
      .
      .
      MOVE 1234567891 to BIN-VAR


+---------------------------------------------------------------------------
-----------+
� Figure 102. Values of the Data Item after the MOVE                     �
+---------------------------------------------------------------------------
------------�
�                                   �      Decimal     �          Hex
�         Display      �
+---------------------------+--------------+--------------+-----------------
----------�
� Sender                    �   1234567891 �  49|96|02|D3   � 1234567891   �
+---------------------------+--------------+--------------+--------------�
� Receiver TRUNC(STD)    �       567891 �  00|08|AA|53 � 567891       �
+---------------------------+--------------+--------------+--------------�
� Receiver TRUNC(OPT)       �       567891 �  00|08|AA|53 � 567891       �
+---------------------------+--------------+--------------+--------------�
� Receiver TRUNC(BIN)       �   1234567891 �  49|96|02|D3 � 1234567891   �
+------------------------------------------------------------------------+


When TRUNC(STD) is specified, the sending data is truncated to six integer
digits to conform to the PICTURE clause of the BINARY receiver.

When TRUNC(OPT) is specified, the compiler assumes the sending data is not
larger than the PICTURE clause precision of the BINARY receiver.  The most
efficient code sequence in this case performed truncation as if TRUNC(STD)
had been specified.

When TRUNC(BIN) is specified, no truncation occurs because all of the
sending data will fit into the binary fullword allocated for BIN-VAR



Corey Edwards <cedwards@gw.housing.umass.edu> wrote in message
news:38F2803F.C10769EB@gw.housing.umass.edu...
> COBOLMVS
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>

## Thread: Sorting dilema... — 108 this month / 109 total

### Sorting dilema...

- **From:** "Morgan Erickson" <rockies1@gvi.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<iHRI4.3475$_H2.104531@news.swbell.net>`

```
Can anyone point me towards some examples of how to use COBOL for a
sort...I'm using a PC, Microfocus compiler, if that makes a difference...

Thanks!!
```

#### ↳ Re: Sorting dilema...

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38F3F864.428AAB6E@home.com>`
- **References:** `<iHRI4.3475$_H2.104531@news.swbell.net>`

```


Morgan Erickson wrote:
> 
> Can anyone point me towards some examples of how to use COBOL for a
> sort...I'm using a PC, Microfocus compiler, if that makes a difference...
> 
> Thanks!!

Morgan, check your SORT verb syntax for more help - BUT - From
NetExpress on-line help :-

SET ANS85

select ifile assign to ipf
   organization is line sequential
   file status is ipstat.

select sfile assign to "temp.dat".

Select ofile assign to opf
   organization is line sequential.

fd ifile.
01 irec           pic x(80).
fd ofile.
01 orec           pic x(80).
sd sfile.
01 srec           pic x(80).

working-storage section.
01 ipstat.
  03 iskey1       pic x.
  03 iskey2       pic x.
01 ipf            pic x(20).
01 opf            pic x(20).
01 ext            pic x(20).

01 clin           pic x(132).
01 len            pic 9(2)   comp-x.
01 a              pic 9(2)   comp-x value 0.
procedure division.
    accept clin from command-line
    unstring clin delimited by space into ipf, opf, ext
    if ext not = space
        display "too many arguements
    end if

sort sfile on ascending srec
    input procedure sortin
    output procedure sortout

stop run.

sortin section.
    open input ifile
    read ifile
    perform until ipstat not = "00"

        move irec to srec
        release srec
        read ifile
    end-perform
    close ifile.

sortout section.
    return sfile at end go to sortout-exit
    display srec
    go to sortout.
sortout-exit.
    display "Done".


Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting dilema...

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 2000-04-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Tb_I4.16808$0o4.113320@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<iHRI4.3475$_H2.104531@news.swbell.net> <8d1oaa$9f5$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In article <8d1oaa$9f5$1@nnrp1.deja.com>, Foodman  <foodman123@aol.com> wrote:
>In article <iHRI4.3475$_H2.104531@news.swbell.net>,
>  "Morgan Erickson" <rockies1@gvi.net> wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>useful file.

... and, perhaps, a far slower process.  Pardon me if I'm repeating myself
but my memory is, admittedly, porous:

The other day I put together an EasyTrieve job (yes, I know the IO
routines might not be identical to those found in various IGZEs) which
reads three concatenated flat files, does a primary-key READ on a VSAM
file, populates a record and writes yet another flat file.  There are
about 400K input recs and, accordingly, 400K VSAM READs.

This job chows down about 1.5 CPU min on... some kind of IBM mainframe, I
am unaware of the system's specifics.

I modified the job and turned it into a COBOL 101 match/merge assignment
complete with GO TO loop... like (please pardon me, I am about to quote
EasyTrieve in public):

READ-LOOP                                                 
*                                                         
IF EOF FRPVINCB                                           
    GO TO LEAVE-LOOP                                      
END-IF                                                    
*                                                         
IF TRK-TRANS-NO > MF-TRAN-NO                              
    GO TO LEAVE-LOOP                                      
END-IF                                                    
*                                                         
IF TRK-TRANS-NO = MF-TRAN-NO                              
    IF TRK-TAX-YEAR = MF-TAX-YEAR                        +
     AND TRK-SOC-SEC-NO = MF-SSN-1                        
        IF TRK-JOBB-DT NE 0                               
            MF-DATE-RET-RECVD = TRK-JOBB-DT               
            GO TO LEAVE-LOOP                              
        END-IF                                            
    END-IF                                                
    PERFORM PROCESS-REC                                   
    GO TO JOB                                             
END-IF                                                    
*                                                         
GET FRPVINCB                                              
IF TRK-JOBB-DT NOT NUMERIC                                
    TRK-JOBB-DT = 0                                       
END-IF                                                    
*                                                         
GO TO READ-LOOP                                           
                                                          
LEAVE-LOOP                                                
*                                                         
PERFORM PROCESS-REC                                       
GO TO JOB                                                 
*                                                         

... and CPU time dropped down to about .5 min.

Make of this what you will.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting dilema...

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-04-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38F47BBD.66C60BE2@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<iHRI4.3475$_H2.104531@news.swbell.net> <8d1oaa$9f5$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <Tb_I4.16808$0o4.113320@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
docdwarf@clark.net wrote:
> 

> This job chows down about 1.5 CPU min on... some kind of IBM mainframe, I
> am unaware of the system's specifics.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> EasyTrieve in public):

...

> ... and CPU time dropped down to about .5 min.
> 
> Make of this what you will.
> 
> DD

That is to be expected.  Indexed files are a wonderful invention which
allow us to do certain tasks (random access) quite effectively.  But
they weren't designed to replace the far more efficient SORT MATCH/MERGE
processes.  

Keeping output in a multiply indexed file ready for an efficient report
can occasionally be useful, but administration is much easier if we
simply extract the data from the input database, and recreate the report
as needed, using temporary sort files which go away when the job is
done.

Adding on an index every time you want your data in a different order,
is a process guaranteed to slow down updates, to create tremendous
overhead, and to complicate the system.   On the other hand, it is
slightly less work for the initial programmer, who might not care about
his customers and the administrators.

There are many good reasons for adding this overhead.  Multiple indices
can make random data retrieval effective.  But sorting a report is not
one of these good reasons.
```

TOny DIlworth

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<_b0J4.176495$Hq3.4242134@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<iHRI4.3475$_H2.104531@news.swbell.net> <8d1oaa$9f5$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <Tb_I4.16808$0o4.113320@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8d20ir$ina$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

"Foodman" <foodman123@aol.com> wrote in message
news:8d20ir$ina$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> In article <Tb_I4.16808$0o4.113320@iad-read.news.verio.net>,
>   docdwarf@clark.net () wrote:
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.

Perhaps the flat file is shipped from some other system

I once worked on a telephone billing system that handled several hundred
million records per month. The records came from the telephone switchgear,
via some unix boxes, to the mainframe. The records arrived sorted
chronologically (i.e. in the order in which they were created). The result
was your telephone bill.

If you seriously believe that creating indexed files prior to processing is
the correct way to handle that kind of volume then you have a lot to learn
about large scale systems development.

- Karl
```

- **From:** amosgreg@my-deja.com

```
In article <8d254k$nvr$1@nnrp1.deja.com>,
  Foodman <foodman123@aol.com> wrote:
> In article <_b0J4.176495$Hq3.4242134@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>,
>   "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com> wrote:
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Your right Tony, it is hardly typical for Day to Day operations, but
we are probably talking monthly production so the comment of hardy
typical does not make any sense.

Production batch processing generally falls into a window of time and
anything that extends that window is unacceptable. Period. Full Stop.

These systems have generally been place for years and the volume has
been steadily increasing so the time frame available is steadily
shrinking.

This said there are times for Indexed and there are times for sort,
process sequentially re-sort if necessary.

I am also talking Mainframe predominantly.

  Greg

Hi:

Yes, I don't consider a system for telephone company billing 'day-to-
day'. I think the majority of programmers work on more mundane stuff
for smaller companies.  I have designed systems for companies as
diverse as Estee Lauder, Time-Warner, Exxon Research & Engineering,
National Comics and Ballantine Books. All of the applications I
ever did were 'day-to-day' dealing with billing, order processing,
and all the other stuff. The volume of data never approached the
telephone company applications.

I think any VERY LARGE system will require special approaches.

Thanks

Tony Dilworth
>
>

In article <8d2kkn$av2$1@nnrp1.deja.com>, Foodman  <foodman123@aol.com> wrote:
>In article <P12J4.16831$0o4.113488@iad-read.news.verio.net>,
>  docdwarf@clark.net () wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>day'.

Then, Mr Dilworth, our definitions are *very* different.  I would say that
a telephone company is a rather common entity (as there are quite a few of
them floating about) and that customer billing is also a mundane,
meat-n-potatoes application; consider the following evaluation:

'Generating and sending out bills?  Hey, companies do that every day!'

>I think the majority of programmers work on more mundane stuff
>for smaller companies.

You are welcome to think what you please, of course... as noted above,
telephone companies are not exotic, generating and sending out bills is
about as mundane as one gets (well, maybe payroll...)... and as for what
the 'majority' does I have no idea but would be interested in seeing a few
numbers and sources to support this.

>I have designed systems for companies as
>diverse as Estee Lauder, Time-Warner, Exxon Research & Engineering,
>National Comics and Ballantine Books. All of the applications I
>ever did were 'day-to-day' dealing with billing,

Now let me get this straight... billing for Time-Warner or Ballantine
Books is 'day-to-day' but for a telephone company is not?

>order processing,
>and all the other stuff. The volume of data never approached the
>telephone company applications.

Oh, I see... a matter of volume.  Since you brought up former clients, let
me ask... would it be a mundane thing for, say, Nabisco to want to keep
track of daily shipments of products to various customers?  Quite a few
bags of chips there, as my memory, admittedly porous, tells me... or, say,
General Foods... they'd like to be able to keep up-to-date books on their
suppliers... is that mundane?  What about, oh... Traveller's Insurance and
the way they deal with premiums and claims... day in and day out and now
they even have 24-hr Info Lines!

Are those... mundane, day-to-day processes?

>
>I think any VERY LARGE system will require special approaches.

And there's the rub, Mr Dilworth... my experience, small as it might be,
tells me that for many corporations dealing with VERY LARGE quantites of
data is Just Another Day At The Office.

But hey... if I wanted only agreements I would speak only with my mirror,
or what is this UseNet thingie all about?

DD

- **References:** `<iHRI4.3475$_H2.104531@news.swbell.net> <8d1oaa$9f5$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <Tb_I4.16808$0o4.113320@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8d20ir$ina$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <_b0J4.176495$Hq3.4242134@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
In article <_b0J4.176495$Hq3.4242134@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>,
  "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com> wrote:
>
> "Foodman" <foodman123@aol.com> wrote in message
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > routines might not be identical to those found in various IGZEs)
which
> > > reads three concatenated flat files, does a primary-key READ on a
VSAM
> > > file, populates a record and writes yet another flat file.  There
are
> > > about 400K input recs and, accordingly, 400K VSAM READs.
> >
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> I once worked on a telephone billing system that handled several
hundred
> million records per month. The records came from the telephone
switchgear,
> via some unix boxes, to the mainframe. The records arrived sorted
> chronologically (i.e. in the order in which they were created). The
result
> was your telephone bill.
>
> If you seriously believe that creating indexed files prior to
processing is
> the correct way to handle that kind of volume then you have a lot to
learn
> about large scale systems development.
>
> - Karl
>
>

I concur with Karl about the large systems.
Sometimes you have to take what is avaliable and do something else with
it.

I once had to run 16 million address records (550 bytes each) into a
postal validation process. After cleansing, each record was 990 bytes.
These then needed to be re-inserted into a dataware house on a UNIX box.

When I got the records they were order of processing date and time.
The Postal software needed the records in ascending Zip +4 order.
The Warehouse needed them in ascending Primary Key.
On the Mainframe the processing and 2 sorts took just over 1/2 hour.
The first time I tried this without the zip code sort about 4 million
records ran in a bit over 4 hours.

Point is indexes are fine if they are cost effective but in order to
create and load the indexed file I would have spent more processing
time than the final 1/2 hour total (wiht 2 sorts)run time.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

> million records per month. The records came from the telephone switchgear,

…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

I also worked for a company who clients were telephone companies.  FWIW, the
program that read switch data would write it to one of several indexed files.
In fact very few of our files were in anything except indexed format unless
they were read or written outside of our system.  Those decisions were made
before I got there but even our sorts consisted of building then reading a
temporary indexed file.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting dilema...

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 2000-04-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<xZ0J4.16823$0o4.113286@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<iHRI4.3475$_H2.104531@news.swbell.net> <8d1oaa$9f5$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <Tb_I4.16808$0o4.113320@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8d20ir$ina$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In article <8d20ir$ina$1@nnrp1.deja.com>, Foodman  <foodman123@aol.com> wrote:
>In article <Tb_I4.16808$0o4.113320@iad-read.news.verio.net>,
>  docdwarf@clark.net () wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>these flat files all the time.

Well, the simplest reason is that 'because those are the files that the
person who signs my timesheets told me to use'... but I noticed that your
question neglected the rest of the example, which showed a decrease of
approximately 66% in CPU use when an indexed-file lookup is changed to a
sort/merge process.

I fail to see the relevance of the driver-files' organisation to the
processing described; each record has to be processed so all records must
be read.  Do you believe that indexing the input would speed up processing
in any way?

>
>Do people, at the time of original data-entry, write flat files?

Yes.

>Do the flat files come from some ancient system?

... and yes, data are entered both by on-site personnel and by contracted
organisations; the format of the data supplied is specified both by
contract and law.

>Does the flat file which you create require subsequent sorts? 

The output of the process is, once again, immaterial... or are you trying
to say that it would be sped up by writing to an indexed file?

>
>(To me a flat file is a non-indexed sequential thing like a deck of
>cards.)

It has been said that if all one has is a hammer then all problems tend to
look like nails... and hammers, nails and decks of cards all have their
uses, as well.

But back to the 'meat' of the tale... changing the processing from flat
file reads/primary key index reads to sort flatfiles/sort indexed file to
flat file/match-merge the whole mess resulted in a 66% decrease in CPU
use; might you be so kind as to state how you can, in the face of such
gains in efficiency, unilaterally suggest use of indexed files?

DD
```

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

Hi:

Thanks for the answers.

No, I would not suggest the unilateral use when you get stuff
coming at you in the forms you describe. In fact I wouldn't
say that anything must be used unilaterally. The phone company
example someone gave of billions of records created by phone
company equipment would be something which requires a specialized
approach.

My questions in this area have all centered around the design
of NEW business applications, perhaps I have failed to make
that clear. I have no problem with legacy stuff requiring sorts.

Obviously if you are getting all these flat file thingies thrown
at you, you have to respond appropriately, as you seem to do. But
I would hope that a NEW system design would eliminate all that
sorting stuff.

I also wonder if newcomers look at these older system with their
sorts and stuff and assume that that is how things are done so
they should do the same.

I suppose I should also add that when I refer to ISAM, you can
read it as DBMS.  I can't afford to use a DBMS and don't need
one, but can see that a DBMS is used more often than poor, old
overlooked ISAM a lot of the time.

By the way, I dimly recall that IBM never had multi-key ISAM
but offered CICS instead. Do modern mainframe COBOL compilers
support multi-key ISAM?

I also have not designed anything for other than the PC since
1983. Although, I did develop a system on a Tandy 2000 (80186)
which was destined to run on a VAX.

Thanks

TOny Dilworth









Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.

The IBM mainframe went from ISAM to VSAM years ago.
VSAM does support multiple Alternate Indexes.
However, performance is poor when you have large files.

Many shops are replacing VSAM systems with DB2 systems.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting dilema...

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "DennisHarley" <LegacyBlue@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<#Qby8mNp$GA.306@cpmsnbbsa04>`
- **References:** `<iHRI4.3475$_H2.104531@news.swbell.net> <8d1oaa$9f5$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <Tb_I4.16808$0o4.113320@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8d20ir$ina$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <xZ0J4.16823$0o4.113286@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8d2k08$a92$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <#mLxT$Lp$GA.90@cpmsnbbsa04> <38F4E09F.EBC02CC6@home.com>`

```

James J. Gavan <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
news:38F4E09F.EBC02CC6@home.com...
>
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> That's interesting. Would you big users care to comment. Was the
switch
> to DBs perceived as a 'desired need' or was it to cover a deficiency
in
> the way the O/S handled the indexed file system. (It reads a bit
like
> 'We went Visual Basic/C++ etc..... because COBOL didn't have the
> necessary features.....').
>
It was basicly to have a more efficient, and effective technology.

Let me give you an example:
I replaced a system which had alternate indexes with a relational
product.
There was a need to see if a potential customer had been declined in
the past,
or to find an account number for a customer.
Using the alternate indexes, the user could select from 3 alternate
paths: Date. Telephone Number, or Customer Name, to search for the
customer.
Using relational technology, all 3 fields could be used at one time,
and wild card searches could be used.
The user could look for all customers who had applied between 2 dates,
and had a telephone in the 212 area code, with the word "PIZZA" in
their business name. It would also be possible to supply only the last
4 digits of the phone number.
The users loved the system, because they could use the computer to
filter the results they needed.
Under the old system, spelling errors or slight differences in the
name often made finding the customer by name difficult.

If I want all customers with telephone numbers ending in 1234 and with
the word PIZZA in the customer name, I could code:
SELECT CUST_NO, CUST_NAME
FROM CUST_TABLE
WHERE CUST_TEL_NO LIKE '%1234'
AND CUST_NAME LIKE '% PIZZA %'


Another difference is that DB2 can be running your query while you are
processing a previously returned record (row). That is DB2 is getting
your next record while you are processing.
DB2 can also be doing parallell I/O. If your table is split into
several physical datasets (Partitions).
DB2 can be doing I/O against multiple partitions simultaneously. It is
similar to having multiple copies of your program running, with each
reading a portion of the Indexed file.

There are other feature which allow the totaling of amounts or counts
by department or any other field (column).

SQL is a powerful tool.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting dilemma...

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38F560CB.284A08D2@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<iHRI4.3475$_H2.104531@news.swbell.net> <8d1oaa$9f5$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <Tb_I4.16808$0o4.113320@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8d20ir$ina$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <xZ0J4.16823$0o4.113286@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8d2k08$a92$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <#mLxT$Lp$GA.90@cpmsnbbsa04> <38F4E09F.EBC02CC6@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" wrote:
> 
> DennisHarley wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy

Where I work we had a CICS application that queried an ESDS VSAM file
with five alternate indexes.  It was fast enough for query, but under
high transaction load the base file and alternate indexes could not be
updated in realtime, and the most recent date on queries would be as
much as 30 minutes out of date.

This application was converted to DB/2 a few months ago.  I tend not to
like database, except for bill-of-material type applications, but I was
impressed.  DB/2 performs much better than VSAM with alternate indexes. 
DB/2 is able to sustain a much faster rate of update and the queries are
quicker too.  This application is a network monitor and transactions are
date/time stamped to the nearest second.

Binds are kind of problem to manage with Endevor.  For a single key
file, VSAM is a lot easier to set up.  DB/2 might still be faster for
random update and inquiry.  

Getting back to SORTs, this application also manages data in in-core
tables.  The tables need to be resorted for each CICS query, because
transaction count is the sort key.  The in-core sort was too slow, but I
found an improved algorithm on the ETK website that solved that
problem.  We used "combsort" and liked the results:
http://www.etk.com/papers/sorting/sorting.htm
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ The DocDwarf Runtime Performance Challenge

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38F8067F.49003AF8@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<iHRI4.3475$_H2.104531@news.swbell.net> <38F560CB.284A08D2@worldnet.att.net> <68jJ4.16886$0o4.114150@iad-read.news.verio.net> <38F6A0AF.B52AFC49@worldnet.att.net> <WXEJ4.16989$0o4.114902@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
docdwarf@clark.net wrote:
> 
> In article <38F6A0AF.B52AFC49@worldnet.att.net>,
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> and ran smack into NIH = NFG.

"Not Invented Here" equals "No Freaking Good".  I think that falls under
the classification of ... human nature.

> 
> >Doc, I really appreciated your story about converting VSAM to flat files
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD

Well, I'm not sure I can make it mysterious enough.

This batch job runs twice a week.  It has several files, including a
random-access KSDS VSAM file that is updated and used to print summary
reports.  The primary input file ranges from 10 million to 17 million
records per run.  The fixed LRECL is about 600 bytes and the file
occupies several IBM-3490 tape cartridges.  There are three parameter
files which are used to modify the processing, which is basically a big
edit that prints a couple of summary reports.  The parameter files may
be edited by the users using ISPF, the standard mainframe programming
text editor.  The program uses a couple of internal tables, one of
10,000 entries with a lot of duplicate keys, and another of about 100
entries or so.  Tables are loaded from user parameter files during
program initialization.  There's also a date card, but it appears to
only be used to set the processing date on the report different from the
runtime current date.

With 10 million input records the job runs for about 7.5 hours of
wall-clock time, but consumes about 6.5 hours of CPU time.  The program
was written in VS COBOL II in 1995.  It has no GO TO's, ALTER'S, or
PERFORM THRU EXIT's, but appears to have gone through some
redesign/rework as user requirements changed during development.  It's
about 5,000 lines of fairly dense code, and is not particularly easy to
understand.  

Any suggestions?
```

to

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

2 tihings strike me as worth looking into.
1) Can the tape files be written to temporary DASD (I/O)
2) Is the Random Read really  necessary? (that SORT step process
sequentially thing)

Sugesstion if the site has STROBE avaliable, next tme you run use
STROBE to run a analysis on the process and concentrate on the
adjusting the process or paragraphs that are show undue percentage of
use.

For those unfamiliar with STROBE it is a utility that once set up with
the compiled source avaliable like Expaditor, will analyze the programs
processing.
You set the number of samples and the amount of time and it takes
a "snapshot" of execution that many times during the time frame and
then gives a formated report showing by paragraph or process- waiting
on I/O -- where the program is spending it's time.

Having run into a similar issue I would be willing to bet a Hot Fudge
Sundae that you are spending most of your time in I/O state on your
VSAM reads and writes.
 Let us know
             Greg


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The DocDwarf Runtime Performance Challenge

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-04-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38FCF8EE.4717A8B1@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<iHRI4.3475$_H2.104531@news.swbell.net> <38F560CB.284A08D2@worldnet.att.net> <68jJ4.16886$0o4.114150@iad-read.news.verio.net> <38F6A0AF.B52AFC49@worldnet.att.net> <WXEJ4.16989$0o4.114902@iad-read.news.verio.net> <38F8067F.49003AF8@worldnet.att.net> <8d9ju4$t6c$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38fb866a.31562698@news.cox-internet.com> <38FC5EDB.257F072@zip.com.au> <38fcd6b4.15986463@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
Having done VSAM tuning in the not that distant past, I can guarentee
that it can make a tremendous difference.  I have seen it cut an hour
off a run where the program was randomly accessing elements of a table
file.  Because the entries covered more than one data control interval
(CI) normal random-access VSAM caused a LARGE amount of physical I-O as
the control intervals were overlaid and re-overlaid.  (Normal VSAM will
only keep one data control interval or buffer in memory plus use a
second for splitting the information between two buffers during an
update if needed).  Since batch LSR will keep more than one data control
interval in memory as well as multiple index control intervals including
sequence set intervals, almost all of the physical reads were
eliminated.  This was almost as effective as building an in-core table
which I had done as the first attempt at optimization.  Since batch LSR
was only a JCL change and didn't require user approval, the in-core
table solution was scrapped even though it further improved
performance.  

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services Inc., cfmtech@istar.ca
Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> On Tue, 18 Apr 2000 23:10:51 +1000, Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> job accessing the file.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Use of batch LSR for performance Re: The DocDwarf Runtime Performance Challenge

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38FF6BBE.9F0D8263@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<iHRI4.3475$_H2.104531@news.swbell.net> <38F560CB.284A08D2@worldnet.att.net> <68jJ4.16886$0o4.114150@iad-read.news.verio.net> <38F6A0AF.B52AFC49@worldnet.att.net> <WXEJ4.16989$0o4.114902@iad-read.news.verio.net> <38F8067F.49003AF8@worldnet.att.net> <8d9ju4$t6c$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38fb866a.31562698@news.cox-internet.com> <38FC5EDB.257F072@zip.com.au> <38fcd6b4.15986463@news.cox-internet.com> <38FCF8EE.4717A8B1@istar.ca> <38fdc9bd.11101125@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
I have only used batch LSR (local shared resource) for KSDS (key
sequenced data set) files.  Since there is only one component to an RRDS
, I would assume (perhaps dangerously) that local shared resources bring
no benefit to the optimization.  Similarly I doubt that ESDS (entry
sequenced data sets) will benefit from LSR pooling.  

While this discussion is oriented to MVS/OS390 systems, some principle
may apply to other environments.  I used SMF (systems management
facility) information from type 30 (hex 1E) subtype 4 - step termination
and type 64 (hex 40)  - VSAM close records to determine the areas where
use of BLSR would help.  These records had been extracted into DB2
tables by the database group and formatted for easier use.  I used
DYL280 jobs to target job steps by execution time and others to find
files with a large amount of physical I-O in relation to the logical
read-write activity on either the data component or the index
component.  In the shop in question, I found that there were cases where
BLSR gave drastic improvements and others where it actually caused worse
performance.  It was difficult to determine the type of access in many
cases because the access was DYNAMIC for the major programs.  This was
because the package used one I-O subroutine for each file that had all
types of logical access (random, sequential or dynamic).  The shop had a
standard of coding the AMP parameter on the DD to improve the buffering
over the default for 2 data control intervals and 1 index control
interval.  I also improved VSAM performance in sorts by eliminating the
AMP parameters and allowing the SORT program to do the optimization
(SYNCSORT from SYNCSORT, Inc.).  I assume that DF/SORT and CA-SORT have
similar capabilities.

Note that in MVS the indexed file and relative record file systems are
components of the operating system.  There is one OEM replacement that
is used by many shops although at none I have been at.  On other
platforms, it may be worth gathering performance statistics and tuning
file access.  One advantage of this type of tuning on the mainframe is
that no program changes are required and the change control and
verification requirement are relatively low in many shops.  I would be
interested in seeing any postings on the I-O tuning done on other
platforms.

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services, Inc.,
cfmtech@istar.ca         
Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> Thanks Clark.  I may recommend this to the owner of the offending
…[45 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> job accessing the file.
```

There are fairly frequent threads about various SMF records in IBM-MAIN.  I
also have heard of some applications BESIDES MIXG (?) from SAS for handling
them.  If this is really of interest to anyone in this NG, I suggest you post
your questions there.  (This TENDS to be a "systems programmer" thing, while
this NG "tends" to be an application programmer domain.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Using system information for tuning was Re: Performance tuning on IBM

_(thread depth: 22)_

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-04-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3909C8D1.AB68B211@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<iHRI4.3475$_H2.104531@news.swbell.net> <38F560CB.284A08D2@worldnet.att.net> <68jJ4.16886$0o4.114150@iad-read.news.verio.net> <38F6A0AF.B52AFC49@worldnet.att.net> <WXEJ4.16989$0o4.114902@iad-read.news.verio.net> <38F8067F.49003AF8@worldnet.att.net> <8d9ju4$t6c$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38fb866a.31562698@news.cox-internet.com> <38FC5EDB.257F072@zip.com.au> <38fcd6b4.15986463@news.cox-internet.com> <38FCF8EE.4717A8B1@istar.ca> <38fdc9bd.11101125@news.cox-internet.com> <38FF6BBE.9F0D8263@istar.ca> <3906D68D.544233D1@zip.com.au> <8e735k$j85$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
The following comments are aimed primarily at the COBOL community for
IBM's MVS or OS390 operating systems but the methodology and principles
should be common to many platforms.  

While most programmers rarely even check generated code to see if they
are doing things efficiently (and doing things efficiently is becoming
less important), there are times when an applications group is asked to
investigate problems with performance.  In general you should be
checking with your systems programming/administration/support groups to
find out what tools are available.  The database and transaction monitor
(CICS etc.) people also should be consulted.  This way you may be able
to use the tools they already have to get the information you need to do
the job.  In the IBM world, you can find out the record layouts for all
of the standard IBM system measurement facility records online because
all of the manuals are online.  You can also read the library normally
called SYS1.MACLIB for the assembler record descriptions (they begin
with IFA as I recall) if you have read access.  However you may well not
have read access to the files where these are stored and you shouldn't
be reading the online collection files to which you probably shouldn't
have access.  Similar comments apply to the other environments.  

In specific, I have used the SMF 14/15 (hex '0E' and '0F') - non-VSAM
data set read and write records primarily for usage information (what
job or tso-id) is reading or writing the data set or library member.  I
have also used the information on these records to find badly blocked
files and small tape files that could be put on disk.  The SMF 30 (hex
'1E') sub-type 4 step termination and subtype 5 (job termination or
started task termination) have be used to measure overall cpu and
run-time plus physical input/output operations for job steps, as well as
submitter and owner of jobs.  The SMF 64 (hex '40') VSAM close records
have a wealth of information on I-O activity, both physical and logical
(now if only they kept track of read not found and write duplicate key
conditions).  CICS cuts a type 110 (hex '6E') record with various
sub-types that may contain useful information.  DB2 also writes various
records.  Many years ago I used the GTF trace load, link and attach
records to find heavily used dynamically called vendor and in-house
subroutines.  I was surprised at the low overhead of this trace.  I have
written COBOL programs that used the types 14, 15, 30 and 64 records. 
There may be records being recorded and saved by various monitoring
packages that can be used.  

The use of some of the records can be interesting, especially the SMF 30
record where the job and step identification information is located in a
part of the record with a variable offset from the beginning of the
record.  COBOL 85 handles this record easily but it has to be
reformatted for general use such as sorting.  In one shop I was on the
systems programming staff when I was using the information.  In another
shop I was on the application maintenance team and Year 2000 project
where I used the systems information for both application tuning and
obtaining program and data set usage.  In this posting I have focused on
the records with information that I believe is useful in tuning at the
application level and finer (transaction, job, etc.). Note that any
improvements that can be made without changing code are far easier to
sell and implement.  The information I was looking at helped find the
areas of opportunity.

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services Inc.,
cfmtech@istar.ca       

"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> There are fairly frequent threads about various SMF records in IBM-MAIN.  I
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> > http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Using system information for tuning was Re: Performance tuning on IBM

_(thread depth: 23)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ed5fh$rir$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<iHRI4.3475$_H2.104531@news.swbell.net> <38F560CB.284A08D2@worldnet.att.net> <68jJ4.16886$0o4.114150@iad-read.news.verio.net> <38F6A0AF.B52AFC49@worldnet.att.net> <WXEJ4.16989$0o4.114902@iad-read.news.verio.net> <38F8067F.49003AF8@worldnet.att.net> <8d9ju4$t6c$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38fb866a.31562698@news.cox-internet.com> <38FC5EDB.257F072@zip.com.au> <38fcd6b4.15986463@news.cox-internet.com> <38FCF8EE.4717A8B1@istar.ca> <38fdc9bd.11101125@news.cox-internet.com> <38FF6BBE.9F0D8263@istar.ca> <3906D68D.544233D1@zip.com.au> <8e735k$j85$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <3909C8D1.AB68B211@istar.ca>`

```
Personally, I would hope that application programmers would have access to
STROBE (or similar) performance tuning tools (in an IBM mainframe world)
before they started studying SMF records.  The old CA-Optimizer had another
excellent tool for determining where (range of COBOL statements - or within
system run-time modules) a BATCH COBOL program was spending its time.

This is NOT to say that SMF couldn't/shouldn't be used for application
tuning.  It just seems to me (from my experience) that it tends to be a "last
resort" and that there are better tools to do this - at least as a first
approach.
```

Same here!  The shop that I am in has just a BARE bones installation of OS/390
and the only runtime diagnostic we have are the DEBUG syntax that we code into
the source.

As an alternative, we purchased FujiCOBOL and do a lot of coding and testing on
the PC.  Bring sample data down and convert it from EBCDIC to ASCII, then use
the FujiCOBOL Debugger to single step thru the program or watch specific data
fields as they move thru the code.  It has been a great help in identifying
what got broken in the last set of revisions.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Using system information for tuning was Re: Performance tuning on

_(thread depth: 26)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>

Personally, I do *not* see Fujitsu COBOL (or Micro Focus or CA-realia) as a
good way to do "performance tuning" of IBM mainframe applications.  They are
a great way to do maintenance, development, and debugging - but not
performance tuning.

There is an interesting thread that is just starting in IBM-MAIN about people
who can't afford STROBE and want to do analysis with SMF records - but just
as they can't afford STROBE, they also can't afford MXG (from SAS) which
specializes in SMF analysis.  Some how, *if* a shop wants/needs to do
performance tuning, I just find it hard to believe that paying for
"programmer" time to re-invent the tools is cheaper than buying the tools to
do it - but then again, I haven't had to sell management on "purchase"
requirements (either as user or vendor) in several years, so I can't/won't
say that this "penny wise and pound foolish" approach is actually wrong.
```

Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> I did a lot of research into this once.  I learned about hiperspace
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> job accessing the file.

I worked on a badly written application that hit the same keys a lot
in a single execution.  I reduced a one hour run time down to 15
minutes. I typically got about 30% run time improvements on heavy
random file access programs.  I never made it work across multiple
jobs.

I am tinkering with Hiperspace now and it really does not appear to do
much for me at this stage.

Batch pipes is another tool that might be interesting, concurrent jobs
one writing output and a second reading it concurrently.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The DocDwarf Runtime Performance Challenge

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Michael C. Kasten" <mck9@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38F85666.3CF7@swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<iHRI4.3475$_H2.104531@news.swbell.net> <38F560CB.284A08D2@worldnet.att.net> <68jJ4.16886$0o4.114150@iad-read.news.verio.net> <38F6A0AF.B52AFC49@worldnet.att.net> <WXEJ4.16989$0o4.114902@iad-read.news.verio.net> <38F8067F.49003AF8@worldnet.att.net>`

```
Arnold Trembley wrote:
> 
> Well, I'm not sure I can make it mysterious enough.
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> Any suggestions?

Here's my guess:

The CPU spent most of its time doing sequential searches on the 
10,000-entry table.  You couldn't just do a simple SEARCH ALL because 
of the duplicate keys.

Solution: contrive some clever trick to optimize the searches.  There
are various ways to do so, according to taste and circumstance. 

Michael C. Kasten	mck9@swbell.net
http://home.swbell.net/mck9/cobol/cobol.heml

> --
> Arnold Trembley
> http://home.att.net/~arnold.trembley/
```

- **From:** Jonathan Nash <jnash@qis.net>

How about...

Convert the large table with many duplicate keys into two 
tables. The first table would contain only non-duplicate 
keys along with a number which would be a "pointer" (subscript)
to a linked list in the second table which would contain all
of the non key data.

 KEY TBL   DATA TBL
  key1 ----> data 
  key2 ----> data ----> data ----> data
  key3 ----> data
  key4 ----> data ----> data

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ The Winner is...Re: The DocDwarf Runtime Performance Challenge

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38FAA727.8419FE74@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<iHRI4.3475$_H2.104531@news.swbell.net> <38F560CB.284A08D2@worldnet.att.net> <68jJ4.16886$0o4.114150@iad-read.news.verio.net> <38F6A0AF.B52AFC49@worldnet.att.net> <WXEJ4.16989$0o4.114902@iad-read.news.verio.net> <38F8067F.49003AF8@worldnet.att.net> <38F85666.3CF7@swbell.net> <Pine.GSO.3.96.1000415142117.3651C-100000@eclipse>`

```
Jonathan Nash wrote:
> 
> How about...
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>   key4 ----> data ----> data

This is essentially how the program was fixed.

The search of the 10,000 entry table was especially inefficient.  The
table was searched serially with PERFORM UNTIL for each input record (10
million to 17 million input records).  The table was always searched
until the last entry was checked, even if the table entry's customer
number was greater than the customer number of the current record.  That
added an extremely long instruction path to the program.

The big table was loaded from a file that was in sequence by customer
number, but code was added to verify its sequence and abend if out of
order.  It was relatively simple to build the index table while loading
the primary table.  The original PERFORM UNTIL was replaced by a SEARCH
ALL on the index table to obtain the subscript into the primary table. 
The primary table was then processed sequentially until a control break
on customer number.

The end result was approximately a 93% reduction in CPU time and a 75%
reduction in wall clock run time.  No I/O optimization was attempted. 
The program was moved into production without any JCL changes being
required.  The original program tied up three tape drives for the entire
duration of the job, so any performance improvement frees up quite a lot
of system resources.

Most of you picked up immediately on table searching as a primary
suspect.  The big clue for me was the excessively high CPU time.  A
typical batch COBOL program is I/O bound, and should consume only a few
seconds or a few minutes of CPU time for each hour of wall clock run
time.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The Winner is...Re: The DocDwarf Runtime Performance Challenge

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** Charles Haatvedt Jr. <clastname@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.1364f88e3872c8339896c6@news>`
- **References:** `<iHRI4.3475$_H2.104531@news.swbell.net> <38F560CB.284A08D2@worldnet.att.net> <68jJ4.16886$0o4.114150@iad-read.news.verio.net> <38F6A0AF.B52AFC49@worldnet.att.net> <WXEJ4.16989$0o4.114902@iad-read.news.verio.net> <38F8067F.49003AF8@worldnet.att.net> <38F85666.3CF7@swbell.net> <Pine.GSO.3.96.1000415142117.3651C-100000@eclipse> <38FAA727.8419FE74@worldnet.att.net>`

```
[This followup was posted to comp.lang.cobol and a copy was sent to the 
cited author.]


Arnold,

your solution should work fine with perhaps one small concern. Since you 
mentioned that the table you are loading will have multiple keys, the 
"SEARCH ALL" verb will just find a key that is "equal", not necessarily 
the "first" occurance of that key...

I have used the same process many times in production...   the process is 
that AFTER the search all, then search sequentially backwards in the 
table to locate the first entry with the key you are processing and THEN 
process forward until a control break occurs...


In article <38FAA727.8419FE74@worldnet.att.net>, 
arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net says...
> Jonathan Nash wrote:
> > 
…[45 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The Winner is...Re: The DocDwarf Runtime Performance Challenge

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38FC031B.4A07CAA1@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<iHRI4.3475$_H2.104531@news.swbell.net> <38F560CB.284A08D2@worldnet.att.net> <68jJ4.16886$0o4.114150@iad-read.news.verio.net> <38F6A0AF.B52AFC49@worldnet.att.net> <WXEJ4.16989$0o4.114902@iad-read.news.verio.net> <38F8067F.49003AF8@worldnet.att.net> <38F85666.3CF7@swbell.net> <Pine.GSO.3.96.1000415142117.3651C-100000@eclipse> <38FAA727.8419FE74@worldnet.att.net> <MPG.1364f88e3872c8339896c6@news>`

```
"Charles Haatvedt Jr." wrote:
> 
> [This followup was posted to comp.lang.cobol and a copy was sent to the
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> the "first" occurance of that key...

Actually, the index table contains a pointer (subscript) to the first
occurrence of each unique customer ID in the larger table, so the
problem doesn't arise.  But you are absolutely correct about duplicate
keys and binary search.  They don't mix.
  
> 
> I have used the same process many times in production...   the process is
> that AFTER the search all, then search sequentially backwards in the
> table to locate the first entry with the key you are processing and THEN
> process forward until a control break occurs...

More than one way to skin a cat.  I like the index table, and the
results seem to have worked out favorably.  

Thanks for your response.
```

Yes, this happened to me the other day with a 50,000 entry table.  Filtering
the data down to a "mini-table" only when the key changed cut the run time
of the program 50% wall and CPU.

Jeff

- **From:** "DennisHarley" <LegacyBlue@email.msn.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<O7xtwa5p$GA.269@cpmsnbbsa04>`
- **References:** `<iHRI4.3475$_H2.104531@news.swbell.net> <38F560CB.284A08D2@worldnet.att.net> <68jJ4.16886$0o4.114150@iad-read.news.verio.net> <38F6A0AF.B52AFC49@worldnet.att.net> <WXEJ4.16989$0o4.114902@iad-read.news.verio.net> <38F8067F.49003AF8@worldnet.att.net> <38F85666.3CF7@swbell.net>`

```
>
> The CPU spent most of its time doing sequential searches on the
> 10,000-entry table.  You couldn't just do a simple SEARCH ALL
because
> of the duplicate keys.
>
Actually you can use SEARCH ALL even with duplicate keys.
You just won't be pointing at the first one all the time.
Do the search all.
index back to the one before first one.
(use an in-line perform)
they set the index up by 1, and you r there.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The DocDwarf Runtime Performance Challenge

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8dcmnv$17m6$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<iHRI4.3475$_H2.104531@news.swbell.net> <38F560CB.284A08D2@worldnet.att.net> <68jJ4.16886$0o4.114150@iad-read.news.verio.net> <38F6A0AF.B52AFC49@worldnet.att.net> <WXEJ4.16989$0o4.114902@iad-read.news.verio.net> <38F8067F.49003AF8@worldnet.att.net> <38F85666.3CF7@swbell.net> <O7xtwa5p$GA.269@cpmsnbbsa04>`

```

Very true.  I used this technique a few years ago.  There was a possibility
of as many as 3 duplicate keys, so after the SEARCH ALL, I set the index
down by 3, then set the index up by 1 until I was either at the end of the
table or the key changed.  Works great.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The DocDwarf Runtime Performance Challenge

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "dennis_minette" <dennis_minette@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<#CWWZU$p$GA.305@cpmsnbbsa04>`
- **References:** `<iHRI4.3475$_H2.104531@news.swbell.net> <38F560CB.284A08D2@worldnet.att.net> <68jJ4.16886$0o4.114150@iad-read.news.verio.net> <38F6A0AF.B52AFC49@worldnet.att.net> <WXEJ4.16989$0o4.114902@iad-read.news.verio.net> <38F8067F.49003AF8@worldnet.att.net>`

```
I'm glad to see you accepted DD's suggestion that you might have worked a
situation that we could all potential learn from.  Here are a few efficiency
improvement ideas just off the top of my head:

1.  Optimize nested IFs to place in order by most likely to be true
2.  Evaluate use of EVALUATE to replace some nested IFs
3.  Optimize organization of the "internal tables" for efficient lookups
4.  Make sure you are not doing anything twice (or thrice) that could
logically be done only once.
5.  Make sure you are not performing any operations on each detail item that
could be optimized to be performed just once at program initialization (and
I have examples of that sort of thing!)

CCCO

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ma9cfs88i56offatbjmlif4jebm3osvgv5@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<iHRI4.3475$_H2.104531@news.swbell.net> <8d1oaa$9f5$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <Tb_I4.16808$0o4.113320@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8d20ir$ina$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <xZ0J4.16823$0o4.113286@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8d2k08$a92$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <#mLxT$Lp$GA.90@cpmsnbbsa04> <38F4E09F.EBC02CC6@home.com> <38F560CB.284A08D2@worldnet.att.net> <9DFC6004EEC20B62.7701AE69AA9D1706.B0FD1CA74DE8B8DF@lp.airnews.net>`

```
On Thu, 13 Apr 2000 14:44:11 -0400, SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam> wrote:


>
>An ESDS file does not have keys.  A KSDS file does.  ESDS = Entry
>Sequenced Data Set.  I can be directly read if you know the RBA of the
>record you want, but there is no key.  Just a typo, Arnold?

No typo - you may easily have an alternate index on top of an ESDS:

Look at an Employee file - The employee number is in cols 10 to 20 of each record in the
ESDS.  When the ESDS is defined, you can define an alternate index/path for this ESDS with
thisd property.  When a record is written to the ESDS, VSAM will store the "key" (whatever
is in  between cols 10 and 20) together with the RBA of the record just written to the
alternate index automatically.  Thus you can access a record in the ESDS via a "key"

been there, down that
