- **From:** amosgreg@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-04-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8d4e4s$71l$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<iHRI4.3475$_H2.104531@news.swbell.net> <8d1oaa$9f5$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <Tb_I4.16808$0o4.113320@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8d20ir$ina$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <xZ0J4.16823$0o4.113286@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8d2k08$a92$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <#mLxT$Lp$GA.90@cpmsnbbsa04> <38F4E09F.EBC02CC6@home.com>`

```
In article <38F4E09F.EBC02CC6@home.com>,
  "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:
>
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> That's interesting. Would you big users care to comment. Was the
switch
> to DBs perceived as a 'desired need' or was it to cover a deficiency
in
> the way the O/S handled the indexed file system. (It reads a bit like
> 'We went Visual Basic/C++ etc..... because COBOL didn't have the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

It depends on what the use is. As has been stated later on(or earlier)
in this particular thread, the switch from VSAM to DB2 was for
relational reasons not necessarly performance. But again it all depends
on the data you have to work with, how it is set up and what you need
out of it.

Something that most people don't realize about DB2 is the "search
engine" and "index structure", is in fact VSAM on the mainframe.

If I have a single key or small amount of data then I will if possible
use VSAM if there are multiple records to combind or as Arnold stated
partial information then SQL can be a very powerful tool.

Again to restate the obvious, use to most efficient tool to process the
data in the most efficient manner to solve the process at hand.

     Greg


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

amosgreg@my-deja.com wrote:
> 

> It depends on what the use is. As has been stated later on(or earlier)
> in this particular thread, the switch from VSAM to DB2 was for
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> engine" and "index structure", is in fact VSAM on the mainframe.

One advantage of having a commercial database such as DB2 is that you
tend to have DBAs controlling the structure and monitoring performance
of our data management.  This is much less likely to be done effectively
when you have a bunch of separate files.  I think this is a significant
factor.

Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> > Something that most people don't realize about DB2 is the "search
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> factor.

The discussions I've been reading about DBs have been compelling. You
will also appreciate that not being in the mainframe world one is at a
disavantage to experiment/experience the benefits.

However, the one thing I had suspicions about was the 'new' title DBA.
To be a truly effective operative, it would suggest to me that the DBA
has to be an ideal cross-over between an analyst and a programmer, no,
not analyst/programmer. Ideally the DBA has to know a large
corporation's philosphy plus at the same time being tuned into the
technology associated with DBs.

Have DBAs in general been effective - or are there glaring examples
where they have performed less than adequately in one or other role as
mentioned above ?

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: DBAs : WAS Re: Sorting dilema...

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Warren Simmons" <w.g.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MJrJ4.6561$fV.572062@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<iHRI4.3475$_H2.104531@news.swbell.net> <8d1oaa$9f5$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <Tb_I4.16808$0o4.113320@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8d20ir$ina$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <xZ0J4.16823$0o4.113286@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8d2k08$a92$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <#mLxT$Lp$GA.90@cpmsnbbsa04> <38F4E09F.EBC02CC6@home.com> <8d4e4s$71l$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38F5CCE3.3125880@cusys.edu> <38F5E966.C739FDCA@home.com>`

```
Jimmy,

DBA's have been around a long time (relatively).
IDS, Total, and other systems pioneering this concept had lots of users,
and yes the DBA must be  more than a programmer/Analyst.  Yet, the major
tools dealt with are resources, and schedules with backup, and recovery
major considerations.

Presumably, the DB2 and other Relational systems are easier to support than
the network models.  A great deal of experience among the Early Christians
must have made the recent (last 20 years) a little easier.

Biggest problem I've seen is the need to have a well defined need.  Like
our OO learning curve,
Data Base keeps showing us new benefits, but speed and cost of storage are
always trade off elements.  Large data bases are of course the most
demanding in all aspects.

Warren Simmons

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
news:38F5E966.C739FDCA@home.com...
>
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> > of our data management.  This is much less likely to be done
effectively
> > when you have a bunch of separate files.  I think this is a significant
> > factor.
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy

- **References:** `<iHRI4.3475$_H2.104531@news.swbell.net> <8d1oaa$9f5$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <Tb_I4.16808$0o4.113320@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8d20ir$ina$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <xZ0J4.16823$0o4.113286@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8d2k08$a92$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <#mLxT$Lp$GA.90@cpmsnbbsa04> <38F4E09F.EBC02CC6@home.com> <8d4e4s$71l$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38F5CCE3.3125880@cusys.edu> <38F5E966.C739FDCA@home.com>`

"James J. Gavan" wrote:
> 
> Howard Brazee wrote:
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy

If you have an "old" technology, such as IDMS, it is hard to keep good
DBAs.  And many times a company finds it hard to fund enough DBAs so
that you can have one sitting in with the analysts in design sessions. 
Ideally, you should send programmers to database classes and
conventions, but that doesn't happen much.

I think it is very valuable to have trained DBAs as an active part of
design.  Systems which I have worked with that have done this have been
better systems.  Also, I have learned how to do better systems through
this experience as well.

Our business is data.  The GUIs are nice, but data retrieval and data
input are our core business.  Even tools such as COBOL are secondary to
the actual data.  Data and business rules are all we need to switch are
whole process.  Therefore we need to make damn sure our business rules
are correct and that we understand our data flow and processes before
anything else.
```

Ah well, life is still good.  Got a $100 bonus today simply for working on a
project, and at age 24, that is quite gratifying.

Peace!

Jeff

Jeff Baynard wrote:
> 
> But conversion to DB2 is "out-of-scope" for our entire system.  So I guess
> we'll have to deal with the old-tech, shite IDMS DBAs.

That is a real problem.  15 years ago, I worked with IDMS DBAs who loved
their jobs and believed they were an important part of an important
technology.  The company I worked for had enough to have them work with
analysis and design teams as well as their normal work.  They liked
their jobs and were well paid.

Now I work with IDMS DBAs who get paid about the same as everybody
else.  The company does not have enough for them to participate in
design decisions.  They consist of older people who have settled in for
the duration.  They no longer believe the technology is vibrant,
growing, and has a future.

This is an extreme condition of what is happening some places with COBOL
programmers.  The work isn't that different than it was 20 years ago -
but perceptions are.  And perceptions are real and have a real impact on
our industry.
```

```

amosgreg@my-deja.com wrote in message <8d4e4s$71l$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>In article <38F4E09F.EBC02CC6@home.com>,
>  "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:

>Something that most people don't realize about DB2 is the "search
>engine" and "index structure", is in fact VSAM on the mainframe.
>

Yes, it IS VSAM. But it is NOT VSAM Key Sequenced Data Sets (KSDS). Many
people reading this and unfamiliar with mainframe environments will assume
that VSAM is the equivalent of ISAM on PCs, and will mistakenly assume that
mainframe DB2 uses indexed datasets like ISAM.

For non-mainframers:

VSAM (the "Very Silly Access Method", a bit less silly than ISAM (see
below), or "Virtual Storage Access Method" if you like, is comprised of
three parts:

ESDS = Entry Sequenced Data Sets - unordered data sets randomly accessible
on Relative Byte Address (RBA)

RRDS = Relative Record Data Sets - accessible on relative record number
(direct hashing)

KSDS = Key Sequenced Data Sets - accesible on sequential key just like ISAM
on the PC.

DB2 is implemented using VSAM ESDS and is NOT based on a Key Sequenced
method.

To clear this point and hopefully resolve confusion:

Mainframe ISAM (the "Incredibly Silly Access Method" or "Indexed Sequential
Access Method" if you like...) came first and was a system which used
Indexes on sequential files to provide random access via a key.

It suffered because it's maintenance methods were poor, with records to be
inserted placed in "overflow" areas with pointers to them. This meant that
manual housekeeping had to be performed at regular intervals to reclaim and
re-organize the underlying files. This was such a pain that ISAM in the
mainframe world is now almost (I nearly typed "virtually" there...<grin>)
extinct.

VSAM (see above) was the next step and was infinitely superior because it
was "self re-organizing" . No need for housekeeping. All of the "flavours"
of VSAM detailed above are self re-organizing.

This is achieved by binary splitting of control areas and control intervals
to distribute free space throughout the file.  (Housekeeping happens
automatically as you use the file and you just have to cop the overhead.
Actually it is very good because it only does it when it HAS to, and it only
does what is necessary (unlike ISAM, which always required the ENTIRE FILE
to be re-organized.)

The nearest thing I can think of to compare it to in the PC world would be
B-TRIEVE. Unlike ISAM, VSAM is alive and well in the mainframe world and is
so good it has been around for 20 years and, as the underlying access method
for the major RDB offerings in Mainframe Land, will probably be around for
the next 20.

So when we talk about ISAM in the PC world we are talking VSAM KSDS  as the
equivalent in the mainframe world (PC ISAM files today, are also self
re-organizing, based on the B-TRIEVE model.)

Relational Data Bases on the mainframe (DB2, UDB) do NOT use Indexed
Sequential access methods. (These are archaic methods which mainly exist
today as part of the COBOL file system [yeah, I know, you all love it and
think its wonderful, but it is NOT an industry standard and isolates any
systems you write which use it.])

The reason I advocate using ODBC and embedded SQL in COBOL programs is
because you can then attach your system to ANY relational DB and get all the
benefits of the other tools which now exist (like, why write reports in
COBOL when your users can call off WHATEVER they want quickly and easily
using tools like Crystal Reports?)

Database changes? No problem. Generate the new COBOL record definition using
DECLGEN and recompile.(no maintenance of existing code required).

Volumes increase and consequent performance degradation? No problem. Move
the users to another database (say from MS-ACCESS to ORACLE or INGRES or
DB2 or UDB or whatever. Duplicate the table structure on the new database
and reset ODBC to point at it. Done! Not even a re-compile!

Multi user system? No problem. Locking and scheduling of multiple threads is
automatically handled by the database software. So is recovery and
transaction integrity. Why sweat it with manual locking when the system can
do it for you? Not only that, but you are GUARANTEED integrity.

And please don't insult my intelligence by telling me horror stories of
terrible performance using RDB.

That was then, this is NOW.

If your RDB doesn't perform, there are a plethora of tools to analyse what
the problem is and get it fixed. Any DBA group worth its salt will solve the
problem within a day, and most of them will prevent it happening in the
first place by making sure the corporate data resource (whether distributed
around the organization or centralised) is within agreed design guidelines.

These days, when I have to handle data I ask myself the following:

Need random access to this? Yes; RDB (ODBC and embedded SQL). No; LINE
SEQUENTIAL. End of story (at least for me...I respect your right to get it
wrong...<grin>)

COBOL file system is NOT an option (for me) for any new development.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting dilema...

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-04-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38F5B9C5.7DB400D5@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<iHRI4.3475$_H2.104531@news.swbell.net> <8d1oaa$9f5$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <Tb_I4.16808$0o4.113320@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8d20ir$ina$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <xZ0J4.16823$0o4.113286@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8d2k08$a92$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <#mLxT$Lp$GA.90@cpmsnbbsa04> <38F4E09F.EBC02CC6@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" wrote:
> 
> DennisHarley wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> didn't have the necessary features.....').

DB2 (and others) and speed:  DB2 buffer files in memory in a common
address space.  If you have multiple programs hitting the same data at
the same time it is in memory and very quick.  It does chew a lot of
memory as a result of course.

DB2 has transaction control.  I can do a complete update and then
something goes wrong and I can roll back to the start of the entire
process.  Great for program crashes, just rerun it after correcting
the problem.

DB2 has SQL.  Joins across tables then you simply get a set of values
back in the order you specify ready to process.  This virtually
eliminates extract sort report logic.

DB2 and Indexing:  You can add and remove indexes without changing any
code.  

DB2 and Indexing 2: Often the path you think is efficient given a set
of indexes is not the fastest way to the data.  For example you might
start at customer, locate orders, and then locate order items and
check for outstanding flag.  DB2 collects statistics (runstats) that
tell it to reverse the traversal because it narrows the search
faster.  It also uses parts of the index without returning data to
subset searches, you simply cannot do this with VSAM files.

DB2 and binds:  They are a pain but they make the access very
efficient.  If you do not run 'runstats' it can be incredibly dumb as
well.  With the good comes the bad.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting dilema...

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-04-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38F4DFF8.24759B15@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<iHRI4.3475$_H2.104531@news.swbell.net> <8d1oaa$9f5$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <Tb_I4.16808$0o4.113320@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8d20ir$ina$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <xZ0J4.16823$0o4.113286@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8d2k08$a92$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Foodman wrote:
> 
> Thanks for the answers.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> approach.

Glad to see that retraction.
 
> My questions in this area have all centered around the design
> of NEW business applications, perhaps I have failed to make
> that clear. I have no problem with legacy stuff requiring sorts.

What's the difference between NEW business needs and old ones?
 
> Obviously if you are getting all these flat file thingies thrown
> at you, you have to respond appropriately, as you seem to do. But
> I would hope that a NEW system design would eliminate all that
> sorting stuff.

NEW systems requirements have banks FTPing flat files to us.
NEW systems requirements require some efficiency.
NEW systems requirements desire ease of maintenance.
NEW systems requirements require administration.
NEW systems require updating data in real time.  (do a lot of updating
on a file with a dozen indices and see what happens).
 
> I also wonder if newcomers look at these older system with their
> sorts and stuff and assume that that is how things are done so
> they should do the same.

I wonder if others look as systems full of files with a bunch of indices
on them, and assume that that is how things are done so they should do
the same.  Let me take that back, the word is "other", singular.
 
> I suppose I should also add that when I refer to ISAM, you can
> read it as DBMS.  I can't afford to use a DBMS and don't need
> one, but can see that a DBMS is used more often than poor, old
> overlooked ISAM a lot of the time.

Then what you have been saying doesn't make any sense.  You referred to
extracting data for a report into a file, finding out how it is to be
sorted, and slapping an index on it, leaving the file out there in case
someone else wanted the report sorted a different way.  (The report is
sorted on a calculated field).  DBMSs tend to be normalized so they
don't contain much calculated data - certainly not all the calculated
data users want reports sorted on.
 
> By the way, I dimly recall that IBM never had multi-key ISAM
> but offered CICS instead. Do modern mainframe COBOL compilers
> support multi-key ISAM?

KSDS is multi-keyed.  CICS is a method of handling pseudo conversational
code.  Yes.
 
> I also have not designed anything for other than the PC since
> 1983. Although, I did develop a system on a Tandy 2000 (80186)
> which was destined to run on a VAX.

So who administers all of your extra files?  It has been almost 10 years
since I did any COBOL work on a PC.  At that time, users did not have
the skills or inclination to do the work of cleaning up leftover files,
nor the patience to update multi-indexed files. 

> Thanks
> 
> TOny Dilworth

We have given lots of reasons to choose to sort a flat file rather than
to put an index on it.  The only reason I have seen from you to do the
opposite is that you claim that our systems are not NEW.

I have to admit that we do have some legacy ISAM (actually KSDS - same
thing) files at our shop, but the last one should be gone in the autumn,
as we modernize our system.  But we aren't getting rid of ISAM files
because we care what is NEW or what is OLD, we are getting rid of ISAM
files because we have better tools such as databases and sorts.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting dilema...

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8d4dqs$6hv$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<iHRI4.3475$_H2.104531@news.swbell.net> <8d1oaa$9f5$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <Tb_I4.16808$0o4.113320@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8d20ir$ina$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <xZ0J4.16823$0o4.113286@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8d2k08$a92$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38F4DFF8.24759B15@cusys.edu>`

```

> > My questions in this area have all centered around the design
> > of NEW business applications, perhaps I have failed to make
> > that clear. I have no problem with legacy stuff requiring sorts.
>
> What's the difference between NEW business needs and old ones?

Hi:

When I say a NEW system, I mean a system which is being designed,
programmed and implemented from scratch without using anything from
anywhere else. Whether or not it is a new NEED or not doesn't matter.
It might be a replacement for an existing system. Again, I may have
led a charmed life, but I have never done 'maintenance', only new
systems as defined here.



>
> > I suppose I should also add that when I refer to ISAM, you can
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Then what you have been saying doesn't make any sense.  You referred
to
> extracting data for a report into a file, finding out how it is to be
> sorted, and slapping an index on it, leaving the file out there in
case
> someone else wanted the report sorted a different way

No, that is not what I meant. Again, I am sorry to annoy, but
I do NOT 'extract data for a report into a file, leaving the file
out there in case someone else needed it'.

Believe it or not, I have designed quite a lot of successful systems
in the past. Maybe I have been lucky but, when I design the system
I know in advance what reports the user wants and design the files
so that they can be produced directly from my beloved ISAM files
without extraction or sorting. As I have mentioned before, if a
new requirement arose which required a sequence not provided for,
I would reformat the file. Again, maybe I am lucky, but reformatting
a file is never a problem. Sure if you are talking about American
Express or the phone company, then you probably need a DBMS. I don't.
I have four vertical market systems which I live off. They are
basically order-entry systems for different types of food-service
businesses. The primary file in these systems was designed in 1986
when only one of the systems was in existence. Development of
the three additional systems did not require reformatting of that
file and there has never arisen a need to do so.

> So who administers all of your extra files?  It has been almost
>10 years since I did any COBOL work on a PC.  At that time, users did
>not have the skills or inclination to do the work of cleaning up
leftover files, nor the patience to update multi-indexed files.

Implicit in what I said above, I don't have any 'extra files'. Unlike
you I have worked exclusively on the PC since 1983. The PC freed
me from the tyranny of the employer. Any data processing system
is going to require management of the files, i.e., purging old
records, making backups etc. Every system I design has the
provision to purge, as required, and to make backups of the files.
'Cleaning up' is simple and efficient. You might recall that the
hopeless Great Programming Contest required that the entry included
a provision for backing-up/restoring. Nor do my applications require
that the user have 'patience'.

I provide free telephone assistance with an 800 number to approximately
550 users all over the USA and overseas. The reason I provide free
support is because the software does not require support. If it did
I would have to charge for support and hire people (I have no
employees). The users do everything they need to do without me. I would
guess that 90% of the few support calls which I receive are related to
hardware problems. I have customers who have been using my systems
for more than 10 years and have NEVER called for assistance.

Thanks

Tony Dilworth





Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting dilema...

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** postingid@dissensoftware.com (Binyamin Dissen)
- **Date:** 2000-04-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jf7cfs8q82anee111n0bs0ojuu162u90h7@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<iHRI4.3475$_H2.104531@news.swbell.net> <8d1oaa$9f5$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <Tb_I4.16808$0o4.113320@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8d20ir$ina$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <xZ0J4.16823$0o4.113286@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8d2k08$a92$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38F4DFF8.24759B15@cusys.edu> <8d4dqs$6hv$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
On Thu, 13 Apr 2000 12:16:38 GMT Foodman <foodman123@aol.com> wrote:

   [ snipped ]

:>Believe it or not, I have designed quite a lot of successful systems
:>in the past. Maybe I have been lucky but, when I design the system
:>I know in advance what reports the user wants and design the files
:>so that they can be produced directly from my beloved ISAM files
:>without extraction or sorting. As I have mentioned before, if a
:>new requirement arose which required a sequence not provided for,
:>I would reformat the file. Again, maybe I am lucky, but reformatting
:>a file is never a problem. Sure if you are talking about American
:>Express or the phone company, then you probably need a DBMS. I don't.
:>I have four vertical market systems which I live off. They are
:>basically order-entry systems for different types of food-service
:>businesses. The primary file in these systems was designed in 1986
:>when only one of the systems was in existence. Development of
:>the three additional systems did not require reformatting of that
:>file and there has never arisen a need to do so.

The basic difference between your systems and other systems is that your
system has minimal updaters pounding on the database and minimal report needs.

A system which has many updaters will have performance problems as the number
of indexes increase. It will also have more complex requirements for
backup/restore. By reducing the indexes to those required for the transactions
that are the most common issued interactively one speeds up the entire system
and simplifies backup/recovery. True, the batch reports will run a little
longer, but that is worth it to reduce the interactive response.

Such a system may also have batch updates that were not issued against the
live file and come from many sources, for example the stores of a chain may
transmit their sales data at night rather than interactively updating the
corporate database for each purchase as it happens. By sorting the input into
the order of the database key one greatly speeds up the batch update and
reduces the batch window.

   [ snipped ]
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting dilema...

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Michael C. Kasten" <mck9@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38F6839B.6D0B@swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<iHRI4.3475$_H2.104531@news.swbell.net> <8d1oaa$9f5$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <Tb_I4.16808$0o4.113320@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8d20ir$ina$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <xZ0J4.16823$0o4.113286@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8d2k08$a92$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38F4DFF8.24759B15@cusys.edu> <8d4dqs$6hv$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <jf7cfs8q82anee111n0bs0ojuu162u90h7@4ax.com>`

```
Binyamin Dissen wrote:
> 
> On Thu, 13 Apr 2000 12:16:38 GMT Foodman <foodman123@aol.com> wrote:

[snip -- quoting Tony Dilworth]
 
> The basic difference between your systems and other systems is that your
> system has minimal updaters pounding on the database and minimal report needs.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> reduces the batch window.

I'm joining the fray late, and arbitrarily attaching my post to Mr.
Dissen's, to relate a war story from another job long ago.

I developed a timesheet entry system for a mid-size company -- about 600
employees -- so that the managers could get manhour reports quickly.  
The reports, of course, needed to be sorted and summed in various ways.  

The environment was CICS, and in CICS you're not supposed to use the
SORT verb.  (At least you weren't back then; I don't know about now.)  
To get around that problem, I used a VSAM file as a temporary sort 
space.  I built a key depending on the kind of report and wrote the 
records to the VSAM file.  Then I read them back sequentially,
prepared the report, and deleted the records.  By including a task id
in the sort key I could prepare multiple reports concurrently without 
getting them mixed up.

It was all terribly clever and it worked, but it was an absolute dog. 
Some reports took hours to run and cost hundreds of dollars in bills 
from the service bureau which ran the computer for us.  We wound up 
writing JCL to an internal reader, including the records as instream 
data, and preparing the reports with ordinary batch jobs submitted from 
the CICS region.  That way the reports showed up on people's printers
within minutes.

The lesson I learned was: VSAM is a terrible sort utility.

Mr. Dilworth might argue that we should have built a VSAM file with
multiple alternate keys, instead of using VSAM as a temporary sort 
space.  We didn't try that.  Perhaps it would have worked.

Others have noted, correctly, that large volumes of data may call for a
sort rather than for indexed files.  However the volumes in my example 
weren't terribly large -- maybe tens of thousands of records, or 
hundreds of thousands if a report covered a long time period.  (That 
was before I started working for a telephone company.)

The point raised by my example is the timing of a sort.  If you need to
do a bunch of sorting all at once, a good sort utility will beat an 
indexed file every time.  It will probably beat a database, too.  On the 
other hand if you rely on indexes to do your sorting for you, then you 
distribute the sorting over time, as you add records (or update their 
key fields).  If you add records a few at a time, as in a typical online
environment, the overhead of updating the indexes probably won't be
noticed.  If you add large numbers of records in a batch process, the 
overhead may well be intolerable.

Michael C. Kasten       mck9@swbell.net
http://home.swbell.net/mck9/cobol/cobol.html
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting dilema...

- **Message-ID:** `<38F731DB.773DB574@larich.com>`
- **References:** `<iHRI4.3475$_H2.104531@news.swbell.net> <8d1oaa$9f5$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <Tb_I4.16808$0o4.113320@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8d20ir$ina$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <xZ0J4.16823$0o4.113286@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8d2k08$a92$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38F4DFF8.24759B15@cusys.edu> <8d4dqs$6hv$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <jf7cfs8q82anee111n0bs0ojuu162u90h7@4ax.com> <38F6839B.6D0B@swbell.net>`

```
"Michael C. Kasten" wrote:
(snip)
> 
> I'm joining the fray late, and arbitrarily attaching my post to Mr.
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

I will follow your lead and also jump in this late...  Over the years I
have removed many (perhaps dozens of) indexed work files used as quick
ways to sort data for reports.  In EVERY CASE replacing the indexed file
by a flat file/sort resulted in a performance increase, often of
dramatic proportions.

It is much easier on the programmer to use an indexed file, but is
certainly not always the best solution.  As with most programming
efforts, a little extra work up front pays off for years afterwards.
```

In article <8d4elg$7lq$1@nnrp1.deja.com>,
  amosgreg@my-deja.com wrote:
> Tony, This is a very good point you make and also one that is stirring
> the pot. You are PC based and as such a large data set is one thing.
> I on the other hand am Mainframe based and to be your large datasets
> would be a small test file.
>      Greg

It is gradually dawning on my dimness that the majority of the
CLC throng work in mainframe environments and do not use Windows
for development and that their point-of-view is a lot different from
mine.

This leads me to ask - if a company has a mainframe, are NEW
systems developed for the mainframe or the PC?. Would it be
company policy to put all applications on the mainframe just
because they had one?

Although a mainframe may have powers the PC can only dream about,
the days of multiple gigabyte drives and large RAM on the PC have been
here for some time.  If a NEW application, with tons of data, would
fit on the PC and execution time was OK, would consideration
be given to designing for the PC rather than the mainframe
and therefore using tools not available on the mainframe?

Thanks

Tony Dilworth


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/

- **References:** `<iHRI4.3475$_H2.104531@news.swbell.net> <8d1oaa$9f5$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <Tb_I4.16808$0o4.113320@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8d20ir$ina$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <xZ0J4.16823$0o4.113286@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8d2k08$a92$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38F4DFF8.24759B15@cusys.edu> <8d4dqs$6hv$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8d4elg$7lq$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8d4had$ach$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

"Foodman" <foodman123@aol.com> wrote in message
news:8d4had$ach$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> In article <8d4elg$7lq$1@nnrp1.deja.com>,
>   amosgreg@my-deja.com wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> because they had one?

Oh, definitely.  Last summer I spent two months doing contract work for a
multi-billion dollar California company doing a very large scale project
with totally new development in COBOL.  The project must have been costing
well over two or three hundred thousand dollars in resources every month
(30+ programmers).  Other projects were being done in COBOL, as well.  All
the GUI front end stuff for OEM POS PCs was done in VB 5.0, with Unix server
back ends feeding data to the mainframes (via C programs).


>
> Although a mainframe may have powers the PC can only dream about,
> the days of multiple gigabyte drives and large RAM on the PC have been

Just how easy would it be for your desktop PC to connect to multiple
Terrabytes of data?  PCs have come a long ways, and so has mainframe storage
(which today is usually over 100MB/sec fibre optic cables, or if they're
slow, over 17MB/sec optic cables, with a few holdouts still working on 70s
technology bus and tag cables with a horrifyingly slow 4MB/sec rate).  I
can't remember for certain what the ulitmate limit on the number of fibre
channels that can be fitted to G5 or G6 (a number not related to Apples
Power PC processor numbers) mainframes, but I think with the frame expansion
it can go up to what, 256 channels?  That's a max theoretical 25,600MB/sec
or 4,352MB/sec total data transfer capability (although you'd have to have
and use a lot of storage boxes to hit that limit).  PCs have no chance to
catch up with that today, even if one were stuff with six PCI SCSI adapter
cards and each card running to six RAID Arrays packed with 27gig hard
drives.  And what's more, on such a large system, you'd also probably have
at least 8 full processors with 2 more microcode altered to serve as "SAPs"
(System Assist Processors, cheaper by license and designated in microcode to
spend their time taking CPU hits for I/O requests, freeing the other
processors to avoid costly switches to I/O instructions).  A G6 mainframe
with 10 full processors rates out at 1,000+ MIPs, and those are MIPs with
which a PC can never compare, and is only the size of your fridge (or two
fridges with the expansion frame).

> here for some time.  If a NEW application, with tons of data, would
> fit on the PC and execution time was OK, would consideration
> be given to designing for the PC rather than the mainframe
> and therefore using tools not available on the mainframe?

Why would I want to split mission critical business systems into lots of
little machines?  I can't imagine a major mission critical system (Payroll,
General Ledger) ever being designated for deployment on someone's PC.  Now,
a server on the other hand might do it (maybe), but you'd have to do cost
analysis and be fair (don't laugh) and include all real costs, including all
personnel and network management costs, to be sure of what you should do
from a money perspective.


>
> Thanks
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Mainframes vs PCs

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-04-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38F5D91B.42C231FF@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<iHRI4.3475$_H2.104531@news.swbell.net> <8d1oaa$9f5$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <Tb_I4.16808$0o4.113320@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8d20ir$ina$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <xZ0J4.16823$0o4.113286@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8d2k08$a92$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38F4DFF8.24759B15@cusys.edu> <8d4dqs$6hv$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8d4elg$7lq$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8d4had$ach$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Foodman wrote:
> 
> This leads me to ask - if a company has a mainframe, are NEW
> systems developed for the mainframe or the PC?. Would it be
> company policy to put all applications on the mainframe just
> because they had one?

If a company has a mainframe, it is for reasons based upon cost
effectiveness for large amounts of transactions, centralized data
security, and centralized data control, disaster recovery, automatic
backups, etc.  There are mainframe java machines running as Web
servers!!!

So, no.  Company policy isn't to put it on the mainframe just because it
is there.  Company policy is to put it on the location which works
best.  This usually means where the data exist, and where the security
is set up.


> Although a mainframe may have powers the PC can only dream about,
> the days of multiple gigabyte drives and large RAM on the PC have been
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> and therefore using tools not available on the mainframe?
 
I imagine some of your applications are created to run on LAN servers
where multiple users have access to them.  Think of a mainframe as a
very efficient  (especially I/O which is where they really shine), very
reliable, secure LAN server for large transaction environments.  Then
put powerful database tools on that machine as well as a wealth of
experience in archiving, disaster recovery, & security.

So the value of developing for the mainframe is obvious.  Now the
question comes up, how do we maximize the benefits of PCs and PC tools?

Some of this is being done with data marts.  The mainframes can be
primarily data management tools which accept and pass out data via tools
(COBOL is one of these) between the database and PCs.  This is why I am
in favor of a COBOL extension to work with ODBC/JDBC databases.  Put the
high transaction/high contol/high reliability data manipulating on the
mainframe, input or extract the data between there and your PC tools,
and have the best of each world.
```

I don't know about 'the majority', Mr Dilworth... but there are a
couple-three Big Iron Jockies out here, from what I've seen.

>
>This leads me to ask - if a company has a mainframe, are NEW
>systems developed for the mainframe or the PC?. Would it be
>company policy to put all applications on the mainframe just
>because they had one?

That would depend on the company, I'd imagine... different places,
different policies.  (Or even on the time... same place, different times,
different policies)

>

…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

To give a rigid, definite answer... in my experience and depending on the
place and the time... maybe.  Among other things in the mainframe/PC
debate there's the questions of data integrity and security; Big Iron has
a tradition for both (which is, of course, frequently violated... but
that's besides the point) and a COBOL-coder has habits - say, like
checking the File Status after tying a shoe - which a Java-jockey might
not.

DD

But you do realize that speed is not really what makes the mainframe such a
great machine.  What about reliability, throughput, stability, bandwidth,
ease of recovery, expandability, etc.  We have mainframe jobs that do
nothing but ftp and execute KornShell scripts on the Unix machines.  The
mainframe has evolved into a very versatile machine for business.

Jeff

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting dilema...

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-04-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38F5CC1E.D4708C62@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<iHRI4.3475$_H2.104531@news.swbell.net> <8d1oaa$9f5$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <Tb_I4.16808$0o4.113320@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8d20ir$ina$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <xZ0J4.16823$0o4.113286@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8d2k08$a92$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38F4DFF8.24759B15@cusys.edu> <8d4dqs$6hv$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Foodman wrote:
> 
> When I say a NEW system, I mean a system which is being designed,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> systems as defined here.

That would explain much.  Programmers who have done maintenance tend to
value systems designed for easy maintenance.  They get called in to make
programs more efficient so that they can run in a time frame and they
learn to make process flows as simple as possible.  See, maintenance
programmers live with the results of the original design and quickly
adjust their design priorities (usually blaming the original designers
for flaws).

But when we design a new system, or when we add new jobs to an old
system, our values are the same.  My reasons for considering sorts are
exactly the same either way.  I would expect your reason(s) for not
considering sorts would be the same.  I can't say for sure as I don't
think I have seen your reasoning yet.

Please tell me if I have missed anything:
1.  Sorts are obsolete.  If you want your data in a new order, slap a
new index on the file.
2.  Sorts are obsolete.  We should program NEW methods, which is ISAM.
3.  Sorts are obsolete, but after a bunch of discussions, you modified
your original statement that they should never be used to make
exceptions for large quantities of data or for legacy operations.

Our replies were to mention the overhead of adding extra keys, to preach
simplicity, and to give examples of flat files used in new systems.

I stated flatly that if the only reason for a key is to make an
inefficient sort, the indexed file is being misused, and you accused me
of being arrogant.

But, you never gave any business reasons why that statement is wrong
(being modern is not a business reason in of itself).  We mentioned
costs, you mentioned NEW.   

I suspect your reasoning is implicit in your statement that you've never
done maintenance.  It is marginally easier to slap on an index than to
sort an input file.  

Do you have any other reason which we can relate to business needs
and/or costs?

On the other hand, it is much harder to create an output file with a
calculated value in it, just to index that file by that calculated value
to feed into a report program.  I don't see any other way to do a report
ordered by average value (without using sort or SQL).

P.S.
Someone else in this thread said he was glad to see a new interesting
discussion, and I agree with him - I sometimes have to be careful in a
medium where we cannot hear inflection, I want to be friendly.  If it
doesn't come through that way, please make favorable assumptions about
my character!!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting dilema...

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8d4osa$j2s$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<iHRI4.3475$_H2.104531@news.swbell.net> <8d1oaa$9f5$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <Tb_I4.16808$0o4.113320@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8d20ir$ina$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <xZ0J4.16823$0o4.113286@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8d2k08$a92$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38F4DFF8.24759B15@cusys.edu> <8d4dqs$6hv$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38F5CC1E.D4708C62@cusys.edu>`

```
In article <38F5CC1E.D4708C62@cusys.edu>,
> I stated flatly that if the only reason for a key is to make an
> inefficient sort, the indexed file is being misused, and you accused
>me of being arrogant.

I am sorry but I never accused anyone of being arrogant. I have
been accused of being arrogant and self-serving, however (Life is
like a buffet, if you don't serve yourself, you'll starve.)


> I suspect your reasoning is implicit in your statement that you've
never
> done maintenance.  It is marginally easier to slap on an index than to
> sort an input file.
>
> Do you have any other reason which we can relate to business needs
> and/or costs?

The thought of having to do maintenance on the systems of the sixties
was too horrible to contemplate, so, I always sought positions where
I could do NEW stuff.

Cost is the primary consideration I think. Surely it has to cost more
to find someone who knows umpteen things, to cost more to train
people to know umpteen things, to cost more to acquire those umpteen
things, and so on.

From time to time I look at the want ads for COBOL programmers. I
couldn't get a job if I tried because I don't know umpteen
things.

If there were a universal tool that people could learn easily and
quickly, DP costs could plunge. Of course, that is why there are dozens
of languages, DBMSs etc. Each in pursuit of the wholly grail. I like to
think of COBOL and SP2 as that tool. If you go over all the different
threads in here, it is amazing how many different problems are
described and how many different approaches are taken to, essentially,
the same thing.  It seems so many people are working on trying to make
a COBOL program interact with Oracle or C++ or on solving similar
TECHNICAL problems. The time associated with those activities is, I
think, non-productive. It has to be looked at as expensive overhead
which could be eliminated by simplicity - instead the emphasis is on
complexity with umpteen things all talking to each other.

> P.S.
> Someone else in this thread said he was glad to see a new interesting
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> my character!!


I have the same complex. I wonder if people think I'm a luny or
something. I wouldn't be here if I did not find it stimulating
and interesting. I was also 'educated' by sadistic nuns in Lancashire
so my phrasing sometimes reflects my Victorian upbringing.


Thanks

TOny Dilworth


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting dilema...

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 2000-04-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3PaJ4.16868$0o4.114167@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<iHRI4.3475$_H2.104531@news.swbell.net> <8d20ir$ina$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <xZ0J4.16823$0o4.113286@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8d2k08$a92$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In article <8d2k08$a92$1@nnrp1.deja.com>, Foodman  <foodman123@aol.com> wrote:
>In article <xZ0J4.16823$0o4.113286@iad-read.news.verio.net>,
>  docdwarf@clark.net () wrote:
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
>Thanks for the answers.

You're welcome... it was the only civil thing to do, of course.

>
>No, I would not suggest the unilateral use when you get stuff
>coming at you in the forms you describe. In fact I wouldn't
>say that anything must be used unilaterally.

And would you say that... unilaterally?

>The phone company
>example someone gave of billions of records created by phone
>company equipment would be something which requires a specialized
>approach.

Mr Dilworth, that is a common situation in some businesses and is handled
by junior programmers, after a bit of training... in sorts, of course.

>
>My questions in this area have all centered around the design
>of NEW business applications, perhaps I have failed to make
>that clear.

I don't recall your having mentioned this 'centering' half as many times
as I recall your suggesting the substitution of an ISAM file for a sorted
one, no.

> I have no problem with legacy stuff requiring sorts.

This was never suggested, Mr Dilworth... and given that 80% (or so) of
your average mainframe shop's budget goes for *maintenance*, not 'NEW
business applications' then the conclusion should be obvious.

>
>Obviously if you are getting all these flat file thingies thrown
>at you, you have to respond appropriately, as you seem to do. But
>I would hope that a NEW system design would eliminate all that
>sorting stuff.

Mr Dilworth, permit me to try this again.  Imagine that you are working in
a data-center, using a mainframe to accomplish the tasks assigned to you
by various organisations within the company... inventory, payroll,
billing, customer relations, etc.

One of these organisations (or 'users', as some call them) comes to you
and says 'We need to build spreadsheets on a daily basis for our
trend-analysis.  What you need to do is get all the transaction-numbers
for a day's work and consrtuct a record containing that transaction
number, the associated part number ordered, the customer number and the
time the item was packed... how you do this is up to you.'

Now, Mr Dilworth, to make things easy for you... there exists a
transaction master file, primary-keyed on transaction number, which
contains transation details... amongst them part number ordered, customer
number and shipping time.  The obvious solution is to read through that
list of transaction numbers, do a key read nd build your output record for
ftp to the PC, right?

I say 'wrong'... for just the reasons I have given.  That approach, as
shown by the example I gave in EasyTrieve, will chow down 1.5 CPU minutes
per run; sort/sort/match-merge processing cuts that down to .5 CPU
minutes.

Do tell, Mr Dilworth... how does one go about justifying a solution which
requires the user to be charged-back for three times as much CPU as is
necessary to get the job done?

>
>I also wonder if newcomers look at these older system with their
>sorts and stuff and assume that that is how things are done so
>they should do the same.

If it cuts CPU use to a third it can be argued that it is a Very Good Way
To Do Things, indeed... or are they teaching courses nowadays without any
lessons in match/merge processing?

>
>I suppose I should also add that when I refer to ISAM, you can
>read it as DBMS.

Mr Dilworth, that makes even less difference... if you so desire we can
begin to talk about the kind of overhead one gets from DB2 but I'll not be
able to give examples as precise as those I've given.

>I can't afford to use a DBMS and don't need
>one, but can see that a DBMS is used more often than poor, old
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>but offered CICS instead.

Your recollection seems to be rather dim indeed, Mr Dilworth.

>Do modern mainframe COBOL compilers
>support multi-key ISAM?

The standard IBM Access Method Services supports multiple keys (primary
and alternate indices), aye.

>
>I also have not designed anything for other than the PC since
>1983. Although, I did develop a system on a Tandy 2000 (80186)
>which was destined to run on a VAX.

There is a rather different world out there, Mr Dilworth... one in which
millions of records are slogged through on a daily basis and files are to
be kept the way some men prefer their women... small, flat and fast.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting dilema...

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-04-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8d4ebr$7ef$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<iHRI4.3475$_H2.104531@news.swbell.net> <8d1oaa$9f5$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <Tb_I4.16808$0o4.113320@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8d20ir$ina$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <xZ0J4.16823$0o4.113286@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
It's so good to see another lively thread in the NG, I thought I'd stick 2
cents in (though my contribution may not be worth that much...)


>But back to the 'meat' of the tale... changing the processing from flat
>file reads/primary key index reads to sort flatfiles/sort indexed file to
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Doc, this is a fine example of an actual case where sorting wasn't such a
bad solution. But be careful.  In that last paragraph you're starting to
sound like Foodman....<grin>

As has been pointed out by many in the thread, there is certainly still a
place for sorting (especially when large volumes are involved, and no, Tony
there are NOT exceptions to every rule; the telephone billing example was a
perfectly valid one...

"The exception proves the rule" uses the verb "to prove" in the ancient
sense of "to Test". You could replace it with "The exception TESTS the rule"
not, as is often used in (losing) arguments, "The exception just proves that
the rule is right...", but I digress. What I was going to say is that
Foodman is also right, there are occasions when using an indexed dataset is
appropriate.
(Shock! Horror! Here's Dashwood, who has often bewailed the COBOL file
system as archaic and outmoded actually springing to the defence of INDEXED
files! Well, not exactly...please indulge me for moment or two...)

I think Howard listed just about every kind of possible solution and I
endorse 100% what he said.

We all know the drawbacks of Indexed systems (even sexy self-reorganising
implementations like VSAM KSDS, and B-TRIEVE, light years beyond the
original primitive ISAM implementations with their second level overflows,
and need for constant manual housekeeping, still have to carry that index
overhead. )

However, there are times when this is not so bad. I guess I just wanted to
point out that although Tony might have made his point in a gentler way, he
isn't absolutely wrong. I guess what rankles us is the suggestion that this
is the ONLY way...

I remember when there were no disk drives and consequently, no ISAM files or
random access of any kind.

(The early attempts at randomly accessible media were very reminiscent of
those early flying machine movies...hilarious failures which, nevertheless,
left you amazed at the resourcefulness and inventiveness of human beings. I
wrote an article on this subject some years ago for a computer magazine in
Australia and it had a huge reader response. People like to look at how
other people solve problems, even when the attempts fail.)

Anyway, in the days I'm talking about, before disk drives, the ONLY solution
was to SORT and reSORT sequential files. Remember that in those days,
processors were nothing like as powerful as they are now and SORTing took
forever.

(I remember working as a junior programmer in an organization which had to
sort and report on 100,000 stock items a month. The sorting took 7 HOURS!
(To be fair it was using a 1401 sort on an IBM system 360-30 running in
compatibility mode...) but it was still a very long time.)

Consequently, "SORT" was a very dirty word (some would say a "4 letter"
word...<groan>) and many people (including yours truly) tried to get rich by
writing better sort programs.
(Ask me about Fibonnaci series and Tri-partite balanced merges, go on...)

Eventually, disk drives came along and guess what we did to replace the
SORTS?

Yep, we created ISAM files.... We thought it was the best thing since sliced
bread until somebody actually did some timings...there were red faces all
around. So much for new technology. (See, it wasn't the technology that was
at fault here, it was our misunderstanding of it....sound familiar?)

 I am firmly convinced that Foodman is a time traveller who is stuck in
1968...Can you deny it, Tony <grin>?

Although you may accuse me of sniffing glue and writing science fiction, I'M
not burning my draft card and marching against the War....

Excuse me, my Canned Heat album just finished so I'll go and pop Iron
Butterfly on...Ah, In a Gadda Da Vida...!!!!

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting dilema...

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-04-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38F6140E.14A598E1@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<iHRI4.3475$_H2.104531@news.swbell.net> <8d1oaa$9f5$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <Tb_I4.16808$0o4.113320@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8d20ir$ina$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <xZ0J4.16823$0o4.113286@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8d4ebr$7ef$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`

```
Part of the discussion on sorting depends on volumes.  In another
context I looked at a mainframe job that was grossly inefficient but the
total CPU and elapsed times were so small that it made no difference.  

In the context of the files that Tony is dealing with there are several
differences between the PC world and the mainframe world that I am
familiar with. 

1.  The Indexed Access methods (B-trieve, etc.) may well be more
efficient than moderately tuned VSAM.  AIX's (alternate index files) are
especially hard to tune.  I am guessing that sufficient read-write
buffers are retained for both the data areas and each of the indexes so
that if there is sufficient locality of reference the buffers stay in
memory.

2.  My guess is that there is relatively low concurrent usage of files
by multiple users in each of Tony's systems.

3.  The systems have relatively low insert/delete volume on the files
because this is where the time is eaten and depending on the underlying
ISAM architecture intriguing file growth can occur.

4.  In keeping with my earlier statement, the use of sort/extract
instead of multiple keys and other designs alluded to earlier might
bring vast but unseen and unneeded efficiencies to Tony's systems. 
Given the nature of the PC and the relatively low amount of concurrent
processing and the interactive nature of most processes, the response
time perceived by the person at the keyboard may be the only important
metric.  

5.  Definitely on the mainframe and probably in the PC environment,
relational databases can offer some distinct advantages over indexed
files.  If each table is considered the logical equivalent of a file,
the enforcement of relationships (you can't delete the customer until
all orders for that customer go away, etc.) and potentially new ways of
indexing can both improve performance and reliability.      

6.  On modern systems with large amounts of both real and virtual memory
available, both PC and mainframe, internal sorts should be about as fast
as external sorts and you can tell what fields you are sorting on.  In
Tony's environment, they might best be used for the oddball request
where the frequency of use doesn't warrant setting up another key
(index).  In Tony's case an external sort might be dangerous because the
customer might attempt to change it and the amount of documentation
necessary for the customer to do so safely could be interesting.     


Clark F. Morris, Jr. - CFM Technical Programming Services Inc.
                       cfmtech@istar.ca

PS. Over 35 years in the field and still learning, even on the Year 2000
project.

prior discussion snipped
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting dilema...

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 2000-04-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8d5pu1$mgo$2@ssauraaa-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<iHRI4.3475$_H2.104531@news.swbell.net> <8d1oaa$9f5$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <Tb_I4.16808$0o4.113320@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8d20ir$ina$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <xZ0J4.16823$0o4.113286@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8d4ebr$7ef$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`

```
re sort vs isam:

    At least on the typical pc, using ISAM (not database product), A sort
will us
as much memory is available, since the sort has controll of the thread (at
least) for
the duration of the sort.

    But an ISAM module will need to minimize (within limits) memory use
in order to be usefull.

    Since more memory is (normally) available to the sort module,
it tends to be faster.

    Also, work files are just a snapshot, ie the older the work file,
the more out of date it is.
```

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>

In article <8d4ebr$7ef$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>,
  "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
> However, there are times when this is not so bad. I guess I just
wanted to
> point out that although Tony might have made his point in a gentler
way, he
> isn't absolutely wrong. I guess what rankles us is the suggestion
that this
> is the ONLY way...



Hi:

I am sorry if I created the impression that it is the only way. That
would be dim.

Put yourself in my position. Everything I do has an effect on the
bottom line. It is my money. Unlike people who work in installations
where all sorts of things are available, I have to have a good reason
to adopt anything new.

If I had a business card, it would say 'Programmer'. I fell into
programming accidently in 1960 and thank the Lord that I found
something I enjoyed and had an aptitude for. Although you think
I do not have interests beyond my sphere, that is not true. If I
had no interest in things, I would not be in CLC.

At 64, I still enjoy writing NEW programs. Java and OO and all the
other stuff will not make my life better, save me money, or anything
else. Everything I need to do I can do with what I have.

Thanks

Tony Dilworth


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting dilema...

_(thread depth: 7)_

```
Foodman wrote:
> 
> Put yourself in my position. Everything I do has an effect on the
> bottom line. It is my money. Unlike people who work in installations
> where all sorts of things are available, I have to have a good reason
> to adopt anything new.

The bottom line for us is money as well.  But since we have to live with
our work, we are more aware of the costs of maintenance and
administration.  As programmers, we are virtually always fighting for
opportunities to work with new technologies - to help our careers, but
our employers balk at losing our time from the work which is more
valuable to them.  I don't know that we have "all sorts of things"
available.  We have to sell our employers of the value of buying new
products.

The relationship between "new" and "good" is tenuous at best.  (as is
the relationship between "old" and "good").
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting dilema...

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-04-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8d6v7u$aqs$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<iHRI4.3475$_H2.104531@news.swbell.net> <8d1oaa$9f5$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <Tb_I4.16808$0o4.113320@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8d20ir$ina$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <xZ0J4.16823$0o4.113286@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8d4ebr$7ef$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <8d4i43$bb5$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

Foodman wrote in message <8d4i43$bb5$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...

>
>At 64, I still enjoy writing NEW programs. Java and OO and all the
>other stuff will not make my life better, save me money, or anything
>else. Everything I need to do I can do with what I have.
>
OK Tony, that's a very fair statement (and I now forgive you for the
"science fiction" and "glue sniffing" cracks....hey! I'm too old to carry
grudges...I don't have enough time left.)

So, starting with a clean slate and no axes to grind, I would like to make
the following observations:

1 You cannot in fairness say that things which you have no knowledge or
understanding of "will not make my life better, save me money, or anything
else. " You can believe that (and you obviously do) but you cannot KNOW it
unless you have spent some time and done some fair research.

2. You justify it by saying "Everything I need to do I can do with what I
have." I certainly can't quarrel with that; it is fair and it is true for
you.

The corollary from this statement, however, is that you do not aspire to
EVER do anything more than you do currently.

Obviously, this is a personal decision and I respect it as such.

What would happen if you DID need to do more than you do now? Won't happen,
right? You control what you undertake and you undertake what you know.

So all of that is fine and we have no issue.

But there are others (certainly speaking for myself) who DO want to go
somewhere. Who are NOT happy with doing what we know. Who realise that there
is much which can be improved and want to be part of improving it.

People who are happy to apply imagination and vision and effort to learning
and evaluating new tools (maybe even devising some...), to increasing our
own and, hopefully, other people's productivity, who want to see computers
empowering ordinary (non technical) people to have better lives.

(OK it may sound corny to you, but I am very sincere about this and am
dedicating a large amount of my time and personal wealth towards doing
it...)

And this is where the trouble starts. You see your world being threatened by
these new advances and make a call to arms..."These people (Allen Holub) are
deciding YOUR future.."

Nope. Allen's not. I'm certainly not. Nobody I know is deciding your future.

I have no problem with you continuing to use whatever you want to. I don't
see the OO Police being formed to drag people kicking and screaming away
from structured coding if they don't want to go. There is no threat. There
IS a Luddite reaction against things new.

Even if EVERY single COBOL programmer in the world was to decide to
implement the new (currently proposed) standards, how would it stop you from
using what you use now, to do what you do now? It wouldn't.

So let's agree to live and let live.

OK?

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting dilema...

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-04-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38F4AB33.5E72C6D8@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<iHRI4.3475$_H2.104531@news.swbell.net> <8d1oaa$9f5$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <Tb_I4.16808$0o4.113320@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8d20ir$ina$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Foodman wrote:
> 

> Sorry to be a pest, but, I don't understand why one would use
> these flat files all the time.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> TOny DIlworth

Examples:

1.  The bank FTP's over a flat file listing credit accounts.  (any FTP'd
file will do)
2.  The file is an extract which we won't be keeping (to save
administrative costs).  It will be read into a report program then
automatically deleted.
3.  The file is part of an unload/reload of a database or ISAM file.  It
is unloaded, and deleted from the database, sorted and reloaded (for
efficiency).
4.  The file is designed to be edited with Write (for instance the file
which contains users added words to the spell checker).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting dilema...

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8d1t06$sgj$1@news.igs.net>`

```

Foodman wrote in message <8d1oaa$9f5$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>
>Unless there is some unavoidable reason to sort, write an ISAM
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Considering the long discussion we have been having on this topic, the above
qualifies as rather arrogant.  For all you know, the purpose is to answer a
quiz question of "How do I sort in Cobol?".

There is a difference between having an opinion and thinking you are the
only one in the world qualified to have one.

I don't use sorts either, and I use good old fashioned Isam for most of my
data structures.  That is because I know the systems that I am programming,
and I know what to do to get them to work.

It did not take me 30 years to figure out, though, that listening to others
is also a fairly important technique.  Listening to requirements works even
better than Isam for deciding what to do in a new situation.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting dilema...

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-04-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38F47830.ABC27F01@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<iHRI4.3475$_H2.104531@news.swbell.net> <8d1oaa$9f5$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
 
> Unless there is some unavoidable reason to sort, write an ISAM
> file instead, it's easier, and you can end up with a far more
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Tony Dilworth

A file is far more useful only if you use it.  Keeping it is a cost, if
you don't use it , it is a waste.

You can avoid using a sort, or you can avoid using an ISAM file.  Don't
avoid either one, but instead be familiar enough with the various
methods that YOU can pick the best solution for your situation.

When I have needed sorted data, I have found each of the following has
advantages in different situations:
1.  Create an ISAM file.
2.  Tell my database to provide me my results sorted.
3.  Use an external sort and a temporary file.
4.  Use an internal sort.
5.  Sort by hand.
6.  Create an index for an already existent ISAM file.

Over the years, my use of ISAM files has dropped considerably, while my
use of databases has increased.  My use of sorting has remained pretty
constant.  Administration costs generally say, don't create files -
instead manage data.

Your Mileage May Vary.  Certainly, you don't want to assume that either
Tony's environment nor mine will apply to all situations.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting dilema...

- **From:** dxmixxer@netdirect.net (Douglas Miller)
- **Date:** 2000-04-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tQjK4.670$T9.9398973@news.netdirect.net>`
- **References:** `<iHRI4.3475$_H2.104531@news.swbell.net> <8d1oaa$9f5$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In article <8d1oaa$9f5$1@nnrp1.deja.com>, Foodman <foodman123@aol.com> wrote:
>In article <iHRI4.3475$_H2.104531@news.swbell.net>,
>  "Morgan Erickson" <rockies1@gvi.net> wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I'm guessing that you don't have a lot of experience with performance 
analysis, as this is, to be quite honest, a really BAD suggestion. If the file 
is of any respectable size, "sorting" it by writing it to an ISAM file will 
take far longer (due to block splits) than actually sorting it and then 
copying the *sorted* file to an ISAM file if that is needed.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Last recently used

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-04-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38F47F7E.6356E13A@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<38F3184E.C17869DF@zip.com.au> <8cvs7c$7no$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Foodman wrote:
> 
> In article <38F3184E.C17869DF@zip.com.au>,
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Wot is one of them?

You would be asking me to do my own homework...

OK an LRU is a buffer that for instance you would store say the last
say five customers read, as you get random transactions in for various
customers you check the buffer first.  If the customer you seek is not
in the buffer then you discard the one that has not been touched the
longest time.  If you have truly random input this would perform a
minor increase in speed, in reality though data tends to cluster so
you get really good improvements by knocking out IO time.

I am interested it the approach people have taken for expiring the old
buffers and counting the usage.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Last recently used

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-04-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8d4i9p$7k5$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<38F3184E.C17869DF@zip.com.au> <8cvs7c$7no$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38F47F7E.6356E13A@zip.com.au>`

```
Ken,

I wrote one of these years ago (we referred to it as "LEAST recently used"),
I think you'll agree that LAST recently used is kind of redundant... (the
LAST used buffer MUST have been "recent").

In fact, now I think about it, it probably should be "Least used recently".

I had a search around the oldest notebook I have, to try and find the COBOL
source I wrote but no success. I've probably archived it to tape and the
tape is in store in Valparaiso or somewhere equally unlikely...(There are
warehouses all over the World with personal effects of yours truly, most of
which have something to do with computer technology thru the ages <grin>)

It really doesn't warrant a whole program/module. A subroutine to be
performed I think, ...

Ah, but then you're in the OO World, right?

So, an object with Methods and Properties. Internals encapsulated.

Suggested Methods:

1. Get the Buffer which this key controls. (Registered as "bufShuff")

(Checks if the passed Key already has an appropriate buffer loaded. Returns
buffer address and a Boolean indicating no buffer purge. (therefore no need
to reload)

If it's not loaded, returns buffer address of the LRU and a Boolean
indicating LRU purge. Could set buffer to low values if required, but would
need another property: buffer length )

NOTE: Actual "i/o" (loading of buffers) must be done by the communicating
object. The communicating object ALWAYS receives a buffer address and a flag
indicating whether to "load" it or not.

Visible (shared) properties:

1. Number of buffers.
2. Array of buffer addresses.
3. Offset of Key within buffer.
4. Length of key.
5. Key to be matched.
6. Return buffer address
7. Return Boolean indicator

Called as:

 INVOKE bufShuff USING   {1 thru 7 above}
                                RETURNING RETURN-CODE
I guess it would be ideal as a component.

How urgent is your need? (I would consider redeveloping it, but I'm pretty
busy myself right now...)

Presumably you want the COBOL source so you can compile it into whatever
environment?

The algorithm maintains a count for each buffer and increments it when the
buffer is used. When a flush is required, the buffer with the lowest count
is used (in the case above, it's address is returned with an indicator so
the caller simply loads into it. The internal count is set to 1 (not zero as
you might expect) in the expectation that the buffer will be used.

It is also possible to reverse the algorithm and flush the MOST recently
used buffer. This is appropriate for some applications where the expectation
is that once a buffer has accumulated multiple hits it is unlikely to be
required again. (In this case the algorithm has a constant for the "Flush
level" (like say, after 3 hits, flush it...)

If what I have outlined here is close enough to your requirement and you can
wait a few days,
Let me know.

Pete.

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Last recently used

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Michael C. Kasten" <mck9@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38F6819D.4695@swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<38F3184E.C17869DF@zip.com.au> <8cvs7c$7no$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38F47F7E.6356E13A@zip.com.au> <8d4i9p$7k5$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`

```
Peter E. C. Dashwood wrote, concerning a least-recently-used algorithm:

> The algorithm maintains a count for each buffer and increments it when the
> buffer is used. When a flush is required, the buffer with the lowest count
> is used (in the case above, it's address is returned with an indicator so
> the caller simply loads into it. The internal count is set to 1 (not zero as
> you might expect) in the expectation that the buffer will be used.

Maybe I'm missing something, but it looks to me like the algorithm above
does *not* pick the least recently used buffer -- it merely picks the 
*least used* buffer.

Any buffer which has been used enough times becomes effectively immune
to flushing, even if it is never used again, just because its counter 
got big.

Likewise you might have just used a buffer a few nanoseconds ago, but
since it hasn't been around very long it hasn't accumulated much of a 
count.  In fact the buffer to be flushed is likely to be the one *most* 
recently used.

To me the most obvious way to implement an LRU algorithm is to use a
linked list of buffers (probably, but not necessarily, a doubly linked 
list).  Taking the example Mr. Foskey brought up, where he caches some 
number of customers:

1. When you search the cache and find a hit, transfer the customer to
the tail of the list.

2. When you search the cache and don't get a hit, discard the customer
at the head of the list, fill that buffer with data for the new 
customer, and move it to the tail of the list.

With this approach, the buffer at the head of the list is always the one
least recently used (apart from the initial loading of the list) -- it
got there by working its way through the ranks without being used.

Variations are possible in which you would might remove a buffer from
the list altogether, at least temporarily, in order to protect it from 
flushing. In fact that's how Unix manages its system file buffers.

Linked lists are more typical of C, but they're entirely possible in
COBOL.  If you're comfortable with pointers and dynamic allocation of 
memory, and you're not worried about the portability problems these 
techniques probably entail, by all means use them.

Otherwise you can implement a linked list within a conventional COBOL
table.  Declare each table element to have one or two members used as 
subscripts or indices referring to other table elements.

For more details, see the Data Structures section of my website.

Michael C. Kasten       mck9@swbell.net
http://home.swbell.net/mck9/cobol/cobol.html
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Last recently used

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 2000-04-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8d6cbb$6oi$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```

Michael C. Kasten <mck9@swbell.net> wrote in message
news:38F6819D.4695@swbell.net...
> Peter E. C. Dashwood wrote, concerning a least-recently-used algorithm:
>
> > The algorithm maintains a count for each buffer and increments it when
the
> > buffer is used. When a flush is required, the buffer with the lowest
count
> > is used (in the case above, it's address is returned with an indicator
so
> > the caller simply loads into it. The internal count is set to 1 (not
zero as
> > you might expect) in the expectation that the buffer will be used.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

    Rather then  incrementing the counter that belongs to that buffer,
you increment a global counter, and move that to the buffer's
local counter.  When the global counter overflows, you can either
decrement all local counters by a fixed amount, or reset all to zero.

   This is not as elegant as a linked list, but works well enough.


> Any buffer which has been used enough times becomes effectively immune
> to flushing, even if it is never used again, just because its counter
…[41 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Last recently used

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Michael C. Kasten" <mck9@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38F7C584.6988@swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<38F3184E.C17869DF@zip.com.au> <8cvs7c$7no$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38F47F7E.6356E13A@zip.com.au> <8d4i9p$7k5$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <38F6819D.4695@swbell.net> <8d6cbb$6oi$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
Russell Styles wrote:
> 
> Michael C. Kasten <mck9@swbell.net> wrote in message
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> decrement all local counters by a fixed amount, or reset all to zero.

Ah.  Yes, that would work.  Whenever you need to pick a buffer, you
scan the buffer pool and take the one with the lowest counter.

>    This is not as elegant as a linked list, but works well enough.

...and it would be easier for a novice to code or maintain.  For a 
smallish cache of a half-dozen buffers, this approach is probably the 
way to go.

For a larger cache, a linked list has an advantage in performance 
because it operates in constant time.  The buffer to pick is always
right there at the head of the list, and all you have to do is juggle
a few pointers.  Using a counter, by contrast, you have to scan the
entire buffer pool to look for the lowest counter -- unless you code
something more complicated than a linked list would have been in
the first place.

Michael C. Kasten	mck9@swbell.net
http://home.swbell.net/mck9/cobol/cobol.html
```
