Michael C. Kasten wrote in message <38F6819D.4695@swbell.net>...
>Peter E. C. Dashwood wrote, concerning a least-recently-used algorithm:


>Maybe I'm missing something, but it looks to me like the algorithm above
>does *not* pick the least recently used buffer -- it merely picks the
>*least used* buffer.
>
You're not missing anything Michael.

You are completely correct and I was wrong. (Comes of writing pseudo code at
3 in the morning...<grin>)

 That's why I said it should be called "least used recently" It DOES find
the least used buffer.


>Any buffer which has been used enough times becomes effectively immune
>to flushing, even if it is never used again, just because its counter
>got big.
>
To be fair to myself, here, I would've included a check to prevent this
happening within the internals of the module. It would be done by limiting
the counters and checking time.

>Likewise you might have just used a buffer a few nanoseconds ago, but
>since it hasn't been around very long it hasn't accumulated much of a
>count.  In fact the buffer to be flushed is likely to be the one *most*
>recently used.
>
Yes. On the basis of what I described, this is totally accurate.

I would've found this out when I debugged it, but your assessment is
completely right.

>To me the most obvious way to implement an LRU algorithm is to use a
>linked list of buffers (probably, but not necessarily, a doubly linked
>list).

I agree this would work. But the fact that a buffer gets pushed
progressively down the list does not mean you are necessarily achieving what
you intend to. It really depends on whether you are concerned about the
buffers, or the contents of the buffers.

Using a linked list is definitely a superior approach. (I believe that the
original solution I wrote employed this, but I couldn't find it and I'd
forgotten all about it until Ken mentioned it.)

OK Guv', it's a fair cop. My face is red... (hangs head in shame...but then
consoles himself with  the thought "Hey! I was only trying to help and
whatever I delivered to Ken would've worked because I would've made sure it
did before I delivered it...")

So how about helping Ken out and giving him the code <grin>?

Regards,

Pete.

```
You woul need a 'C' or 'Assembler' subroutine to allocate the memory.
HOWEVER, what you are stating really dones NOT make sense.

Do you mean that 'sometimes, in a file of VARIABLE-LENGTH BLOCKED records'
the actual Blocksize is GREATER than that calculated for the FD'?

That can be handled by using
(O L D)
FD SOMEFILE
       RECORD CONTAINS 50 TO 250 CHARACTERS
       BLOCK CONTAINS 4 RECORDS.
(==> calculates a Blocksize of 1020 Characters (250 * 4 + ( 4bytes * 4
records) + 4 bytes for block)
FD SOMEFILE
       RECORD CONTAINS 50 TO 250 CHARACTERS
       BLOCK CONTAINS 620 CHARACTERS.
(==> 2 * 50 + 2 * 250 = 100 + 500 = 600 + ( 4 * 4bytes + 4 bytes)

If the file was created in method 1 ( O L D )
and read in method 2, then you will get a buffer that is larger than
calculated.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dynamic allocation of memory

- **From:** Rich Rohde <richNOriSPAM@richware.net.invalid>
- **Date:** 2000-04-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<08f36484.c7245296@usw-ex0105-037.remarq.com>`
- **References:** `<38F32042.5E5556F2@NOSPAM.thewatercooler.com> <8d238f$nml$1@newssrv.otenet.gr> <38F595A0.B7BD32DF@NOSPAM.thewatercooler.com>`

```
Harish,

Did you see my response early in the thread? I will give you an
example now on how to use the library routines to allocate and
free memory using NetExpress.

--------- Start of code --------------
.
.
working-storage section.
01 mem-ptr usage pointer.
01 mem-size pic x(04) comp-5.
01 mem-flags pic x(04) comp-5.
.
.
linkage section.
01 table-area.
05 ta-entries occurs 4096 times.
10 ta-data pic x(256).
.
.
procedure division.
.
.
move zeros to mem-flags
*> compute mem-size
move length of table-area to mem-size
if mem-size < 8192
move 8192 to mem-size
end-if

call 'CBL_ALLOC_MEM' using mem-ptr
by value mem-size mem-flags

set address of table-area to mem-ptr
*> now you have the memory assigned to the table. Note, table-
area is in the linkage section
*> now do all of your processing and when done, you should free
memory

call 'CBL_FREE_MEM' using by value mem-ptr
stop run
.
------------- End of code ----------------

Regards,
Rich

* Sent from RemarQ http://www.remarq.com The Internet's Discussion Network *
The fastest and easiest way to search and participate in Usenet - Free!
```

## Thread: NetExpress 3.0 Client-Server application — 2 msgs

### NetExpress 3.0 Client-Server application

- **From:** "Alluyn Peter" <pa@schaubroeck.be>
- **Date:** 2000-04-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gnFI4.29$vs5.876@nreader3.kpnqwest.net>`

```
We are using NetExpress 3.0 for developing our client-server application. We
use pc-clients (windows 95,98,nt) and unix-server.  We use mfserver en
mfcient from Micro-Focus.  The mfserver is running on the server and every
time a client makes a connection a child mfserver is created.   Is there a
possibility to find out wich client started wich mfserver-child ?
```

#### ↳ Re: NetExpress 3.0 Client-Server application

- **From:** Peter Scheurer <pmscheurer@gmx.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8d1tr3$ff4$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<gnFI4.29$vs5.876@nreader3.kpnqwest.net>`

```
Hi,

After establishing the connection to a server process, cs-binding gives
you some information in lnk-client-id and lnk-tagname. afaik lnk-tagname
is built combining the servername from mfclisrv.cfg and a consecutive
number.
If you have any further questions, you may ask me.
CS Binding - to my mind - is a very easy and cheap way for small client
server solutions, once it is set up correctly (!).
The documentation, that comes with NetExpress is very poor, especially
if you want to do real two-way communication with your server. So you
depend on try and error :-(


Peter

I sent by e-mail the following response and am posting it here also.

The following Links on the IBM web site might help.
At first I thought that 4.1 might not support the Gateway, the second
link is the specifications sheet on the Gateway and it lists ESA 4.1 as
supporting the Gateway.

You should be able to use your existing CICS server.

I am also posting this to Comp.Lang.COBOL for anyone else.

http://www-4.ibm.com/software/ts/cics/platforms/desktop/

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<8cus7h$voj$1@news.cz.js.cn>`

```
In article <8cus7h$voj$1@news.cz.js.cn>,
  "mainframe" <rushwoo@hengsys.com.cn> wrote:
> hi!
> A question please !
> our mainframe is S/390 running OS/390, CICS/ESA 4.1, and we have CICS
> Transaction Gateway 3.0.3 for Windows NT and CICS Universal Client for
> Windows NT, but we have no CICS Transaction Server for OS/390! We
want to
> communicate with CICS directly nor through CICS Transaction Server for
> OS/390, what should I do ? If the CICS Transaction Server is
required ?
> Please answer me or mail to me, my E-mail address is
rushwoo@hengsys.com.cn
> Thanx !!!
>

Does your NT platform support APPC (LU6.2) using Mapped Conversations?
It's a very robust and industrial strength connection methodology,
upward compatible to CICS/TS.

Cheers,

WOB

<a id="t-4f52423580"></a>
## Thread: OT - Ultimizer Utility and Re-entrant COBOL programs and Turbo abends(or hard abends) with Ultimizer on. — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### OT - Ultimizer Utility and Re-entrant COBOL programs and Turbo abends(or hard abends) with Ultimizer on.

- **From:** "Taig" <mmalin@fbfs.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8d0pc002n6o@enews4.newsguy.com>`

```
This is off topic but I am wanting to know if anyone has had these problems
in their shop. We have the Ulitmizer utility from ULTIMIS and when running
our CSC Life-Comm system on long 4 or 5 day cycles or update steps are
abending. We have "Turbo" installed so we take three abends at the same PSW
and then Turbo says enough. It spools out the dumps gives us relevant data
(Policy numbers and such). The policies are always consecutive or very close
(depending if they had processing that was occuring). We turn Ultimizer OFF
in that step and we just crank on through with no abends. The kicker is that
we have some files that are ran through some COBOL programs that have are
compiled as being "not re-entrant" in their options. (Life-Comm is written
99% in assembler). I am not directly on the team that is dealing with this
but our most experienced inhouse programmers and consultants cannot figure
out how to fix this. Has anyone had to fix this or set compiler/assembler
options that will jibe with ULTIMIZER? Please respond using my e-mail, if
possible. Thanks!
```

---

are testing here:

"The ICONS conversion tool will convert approximately 80% of the code
from the following input:  BTG's (Back-up Transfer Generator for the
data base structure), COBOL programs, IDEAL Panels, Reports, and
Programs, batch COBOL Programs, JCL, PARMLIBS, PROCLIBS, COPYLIBS,
etc.."


or you have probably heard form Ira by now:

We offer the DMS Reengineering Toolkit, which is intended
to define custom translations for large-scale (millions of lines) legacy
systems in multiple languages such as yours.
See http://www.semdesigns.com/Products/DMS/DMSToolkit.html.
Such tools can achieve much higher percentage conversion rates, because
one can design custom translations for the idioms found in your code
base,
not just the general idioms the "shrink wrap" translator vendor thought
of.
We also offer custom consulting and  contract services related to using
DMS
for these tasks.

I'd be happy to chat with about your problem,  to get a better
understanding
of your conversion problem, and sketch where DMS might be useful.

<a id="t-b7a4a0209b"></a>
## Thread: Exit codes from COBOL modules — 7 msgs

### Exit codes from COBOL modules

- **From:** david_dawkins@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-04-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8d21gt$jo9$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
We are getting a return code of 67 from the
following line of COBOL (according to a customer):

    CALL "MyModule".

The MyModule source always exits via the following
line of code

    A000-EXIT. EXIT PROGRAM.

What is it about the way one exits a module that
sets a return code?

Any help appreciated!
Thanks and regards,
David Dawkins


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Exit codes from COBOL modules

- **From:** "Searcher" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hv6J4.657$t13.412@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<8d21gt$jo9$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Usually a preceeding 'MOVE 67 TO RETURN-CODE.'
or some other value.

<david_dawkins@my-deja.com> wrote in message
news:8d21gt$jo9$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> We are getting a return code of 67 from the
> following line of COBOL (according to a customer):
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Exit codes from COBOL modules

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<NG0J4.176772$Hq3.4246114@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<8d21gt$jo9$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Questions:

1) Is there anything in MyModule that moves 67 to RETURN-CODE?

Failing the obvious things get more difficult...

1) What operating system and compiler are you using?
2) Is MyModule actually executing? The call format looks like it's a static
call but compiler directives may cause it to be dynamic. Perhaps a 67 means
that the module failed to load.
3) If it's a static call, did the linker report any errors?

- Karl


<david_dawkins@my-deja.com> wrote in message
news:8d21gt$jo9$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> We are getting a return code of 67 from the
> following line of COBOL (according to a customer):
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Exit codes from COBOL modules

- **From:** tracy_jenkins@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-04-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8dhvgd$pkk$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8d21gt$jo9$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <NG0J4.176772$Hq3.4246114@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
Thanks for the replies so far.

There is nothing in the code which moves 67 to RETURN-CODE,
that's the problem, where does 67 come from.

I believe MyModule is being called.  (This is a customer
problem which we cannot reproduce, so we only have the
information they give to us:-)

We are using MF COBOL on HPUX, so I am looking into
the indexed file not open idea.

Thanks again

Tracy


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Exit codes from COBOL modules

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-04-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38F55CE3.993@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<8d21gt$jo9$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
david_dawkins@my-deja.com wrote:
> 
> We are getting a return code of 67 from the
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> sets a return code?

Perhaps it isn't your module that is setting the
return code and exiting, perhaps it is the run-time
exiting with code 67 because, for example, it can't
find 'MyModule'.
```

#### ↳ Re: Exit codes from COBOL modules

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 2000-04-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8d31nr$ib2$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<8d21gt$jo9$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
A wild, wild guess. You are trying to do something with an indexed file, but
you didn't open it first.
RTS 067 if you use Merant (Micro Focus) filehandler.

I think it's about time we get the name of your compiler and information
about your operating system.

Regards
Ib

david_dawkins@my-deja.com skrev i meddelelsen
<8d21gt$jo9$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>We are getting a return code of 67 from the
>following line of COBOL (according to a customer):
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>Before you buy.
```

<a id="t-67dd389801"></a>
## Thread: Data file from AS/400 to PC — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`AS/400, iSeries, RPG`](topics.md#as400)_

### Data file from AS/400 to PC

- **From:** "Steven Kalemkiewicz" <senseisk@flash.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d99J4.23447$hh2.534206@news.flash.net>`

```
Today I decided to pull a flat data file from our AS/400 and save it as a
text file on my PC.  (I figured I could speed up writing and testing my
programs by doing some of the work at home and then coding them on the 400
at school.)  I opened the file to take a look at it and the entire file is
HEX (EBCDIC)   The original file (in COBOL terms) was USAGE DISPLAY

Well my question is, wasn't there someone out there with a EBCDIC to ASCII
converter?  If there is, would you be willing to give me the source?

-Steve Kalemkiewicz
```

#### ↳ Re: Data file from AS/400 to PC

- **From:** "Searcher" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Kv9J4.688$t13.551@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<d99J4.23447$hh2.534206@news.flash.net>`

```
When you download the file, set the file type to 'TEXT' not Binary, then it
will be translated.

Steven Kalemkiewicz <senseisk@flash.net> wrote in message
news:d99J4.23447$hh2.534206@news.flash.net...
> Today I decided to pull a flat data file from our AS/400 and save it as a
> text file on my PC.  (I figured I could speed up writing and testing my
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Data file from AS/400 to PC

- **From:** terrywin.nospam@pronetisp.net (Terry Winchester)
- **Date:** 2000-04-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38f82f9c.1086499@news.pronetisp.net>`
- **References:** `<d99J4.23447$hh2.534206@news.flash.net> <Kv9J4.688$t13.551@news4.mia>`

```
On Wed, 12 Apr 2000 21:32:04 -0700, "Searcher"
<mangogwr@bellsouth.net> wrote:

>When you download the file, set the file type to 'TEXT' not Binary, then it
>will be translated.
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Searcher is correct if your using FTP, but, if your using Client
Access you need to check your code-page (CCSID) to see if it is set 
properly (65535?) for the data transfer (and possibly your job
description).

Good Luck
Terry
```

#### ↳ Re: Data file from AS/400 to PC

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<RTsJ4.59832$QJ3.7262760@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<d99J4.23447$hh2.534206@news.flash.net>`

```
If you are unable/unwilling to let the communications software handle the
EBCDIC-ASCII conversion (i.e., because there is binary or BCD data in the
file), I have a freeware(*)  32-bit Windows DLL that will handle the
conversion on the PC side on a field-by-field basis. (It will also convert
COBOL numerics to IEEE numerics, but it doesn't sound like you'd need that).

E-mail direct if interested.

Michael Mattias
Tal Systems
Racine WI USA
michael.mattias@gte.net

* Well, not totally free: I do ask each person who uses this DLL to promise
to let me know how it worked out.

## Thread: Cobol - Data Exception (Conversion Data). — 4 msgs

### Re: Cobol - Data Exception (Conversion Data).

- **From:** "Eileen Preston" <epreston@lear.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s8f4c154.045@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```
/rant mode on

Dang Deja.com - I got this at 6pm EDT on 4/12

/rant mode off

>>> Binyamin Dissen 04/06 8:44 am >>>

On Wed, 05 Apr 2000 15:40:08 -0400 "Eileen Preston" <epreston@lear.com> wrote:

:>>>> Peter E. C. Dashwood 04/01 8:53 pm >>>
 
:>Sorry, but I couldn't let this go...

:>docdwarf@clark.net wrote in message ...
:>>In article <8bng6o$11p$1@news.flashnet.it>,
:>>Gianluigi Angotti <giangol@tin.it> wrote:


:>>...A check which would work across all platforms I know of is:

:>>05  AREA-TEST     PIC X(5).
:>>05  AREA-TEST-NUM REDEFINES AREA-TEST PIC 9(5).

:>>...

:>>MOVE DATO-NUM TO AREA-TEST-NUM.
:>>IF AREA-TEST NOT NUMERIC
:>>    DISPLAY 'TUTTI DI FROMAGGIO!'.

:>This almost certainly WON'T work across all platforms. (I'd be surprised if
:>it worked on ANY platform, and it is certainly NOT a solution to the
:>underlying problem).

In which platform will a NUMERIC class test as above incorrectly succeed or
fail?

I think this must, by definition, work.

:>It is predicated on the (I believe false) hope that moving an invalid packed
:>field to a non-packed target will NOT cause a data exception.             

One cannot guarantee that such a move WILL fail.

It may fail, but it may not.

:>                                                              In other
:>words, an 0C7 can only be raised during a PACK operation. (Maybe someone in
:>the NG who has access to a mainframe can confirm or deny whether this is the
:>current situation...?)

And 0C7 CANNOT be raised during a pack operation.

Neither PACK nor UNPK require numeric operands.

:>This will not work on a mainframe.  If DATO-NUM is invalid data (packed or 
:>not packed) there will be a sock seven (S0C7 data exception on a mainframe).

>Why?

>How can a NUMERIC class test cause a numeric data exception? 

>Makes the class test kind of useless, doesn't it?

The MOVE will cause a S0C7 not the class test.  Movement of invalid numeric data to a numeric field causes the S0C7.

:>Also asking if a PIC X field is numeric will give a false answer IF the field 
:>is signed because a sign always is alpha in a PIC X field so doing the numeric 
:>check on ARE-TEST won't work on a signed field or if DATO-NUM was packed.

> Why do you consider that to be false?

> It is a valid number.

Lets look at the data representation in EBCIDIC.

If  the number being looked at is +12345 then the EBCIDIC representation is in hex x'F1F2F3F4C5'.  Should the number be negative then the representation in hex is x'F1F2F3F4D5'.  In EBCIDIC the x'C5' equates to the letter E and the x'D5' equates to the letter N.  

If you put a display into the program to display AREA-TEST you would see 1234E or 1234N.  This same appearace is what is seen with a numeric check on AREA-TEST (the PIC X field).  Checking AREA-TEST-NUM would result in a valid numeric check assuming the data was valid.  If it wasn't valid the test would fail also if you haven't received the S0C7 in the previous MOVE.

I hope that clarifies the confusion.

:>I've gotten enough calls in the gawd awful hours of the morning on this :D.

I would like to see your example of a NUMERIC class test causing an 0C7.

Any such situation would be a bug in the compiler.
```

Maybe not quite the same topic (EBCIDIC) but close - I was testing the
following today - NetExpress - in the Dialog I focused on a
7-characte-field (5A + 2n - in theory) and pressed ENTER key without
entering anything, returning value to business logic :-

*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "EF-01-RMclient".                                    
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Local-Storage section.                                          
01 ls-key.                                                      
   05 ls-client                  pic x(05).                     
   05 ls-facility                pic 9(02).                     
                                                                
Linkage section.                                                
01 lnk-length                    pic x(4) comp-5.               
01 lnk-text.                                                    
   05 occurs 1 to MaxTextLength                                 
      depending on lnk-length   pic x(01).                      
                                                                
Procedure Division using lnk-length, lnk-text.                  
                                                                
 move lnk-text                      to ls-key                   
 move ls-client                     to ws-ClientNumber   (Pic x(05))    
move ls-facility)       	    to ws-Facility 	 (Pic 9(02))
 invoke os-Dialog "setfocus-default"                            
Exit Method                                                     
End Method "EF-01-RMclient".                                    
*>--------------------------------------------------------------

 *> I would have assumed the above would have given me an error, moving 
 *> non-numeric to numeric - but presumably it wasn't caught because of 
*> a 'group move'. The Animator showed Hex 20 20 for the ls-facility.  
*> - I check on the validity of these two fields later in the
program                                                          
No big deal, I got round it by recoding :-

*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "EF-01-RMclient".                                    
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Local-Storage section.                                          
01 ls-key.                                                      
   05 ls-client                  pic x(05).                     
   05 ls-facility                pic x(02).                     
                                                                
Linkage section.                                                
01 lnk-length                    pic x(4) comp-5.               
01 lnk-text.                                                    
   05 occurs 1 to MaxTextLength                                 
      depending on lnk-length   pic x(01).                      
                                                                
Procedure Division using lnk-length, lnk-text.                  
                                                                
 move lnk-text                      to ls-key                   
 move ls-client                     to ws-ClientNumber          
 compute ws-FacilityNumber = function numval(ls-facility)       
 invoke os-Dialog "setfocus-default"                            
                                                                
Exit Method                                                     
End Method "EF-01-RMclient".                                    
*>--------------------------------------------------------------

Jimmy, Calgary AB

#### ↳ Re: Cobol - Data Exception (Conversion Data).

- **From:** postingid@dissensoftware.com (Binyamin Dissen)
- **Date:** 2000-04-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ucsdfso78vc5e5dn1m7jtb53t8dbu95sib@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<s8f4c154.045@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```
On Wed, 12 Apr 2000 18:32:36 -0400 "Eileen Preston" <epreston@lear.com> wrote:

   [ snipped ]

:>:>Also asking if a PIC X field is numeric will give a false answer IF the field 
:>:>is signed because a sign always is alpha in a PIC X field so doing the numeric 
:>:>check on ARE-TEST won't work on a signed field or if DATO-NUM was packed.

:>> Why do you consider that to be false?

:>> It is a valid number.

:>Lets look at the data representation in EBCIDIC.

:>If  the number being looked at is +12345 then the EBCIDIC representation is in hex 
:>x'F1F2F3F4C5'.  Should the number be negative then the representation in hex is 
:>x'F1F2F3F4D5'.  In EBCIDIC the x'C5' equates to the letter E and the x'D5' equates 
:>to the letter N.  

:>If you put a display into the program to display AREA-TEST you would see 1234E or 1234N.
:>This same appearace is what is seen with a numeric check on AREA-TEST (the PIC X field).
:>Checking AREA-TEST-NUM would result in a valid numeric check assuming the data was valid.
:>If it wasn't valid the test would fail also if you haven't received the S0C7 in the 
:>previous MOVE.

:>I hope that clarifies the confusion.

No, it does not.

If the picture is signed I would expect the numeric class test to allow a sign
in the sign position, typically the last digit.

If the picture is not signed I would expect the numeric class test to demand
all digits.

If a signed number is moved via group move to a unsigned field I would expect
that a numeric class test would work on the source field and fail on the
target field. If it was move directly the compiler should generate code to
remove the sign.

   [ snipped ]
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol - Data Exception (Conversion Data).

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 2000-04-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%0JJ4.17021$0o4.115398@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<s8f4c154.045@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM> <ucsdfso78vc5e5dn1m7jtb53t8dbu95sib@4ax.com>`

```
In article <ucsdfso78vc5e5dn1m7jtb53t8dbu95sib@4ax.com>,
Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote:
>On Wed, 12 Apr 2000 18:32:36 -0400 "Eileen Preston" <epreston@lear.com> wrote:
>
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
>all digits.

From:

<http://www.s390.ibm.com/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/BOOKS/IGYLR105/6%2e1%2e6%2e2?SHELF=IGYMS005>

(please excuse wonky formatting... straight cut-n-paste)

--begin quoted text

 ___ Format_____________________________________________________________
|                                                                        |
|>>__identifier-1_____________________NUMERIC________________________><  |
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
|________________________________________________________________________| 


identifier-1 
Must reference a data item whose usage is explicitly or implicitly
DISPLAY. 



X Identifier-1 can reference a data item whose usage is explicitly or 
X implicitly DISPLAY-1. 


If identifier-1 is a function-identifier, it must reference an
alphanumeric function. 


NOT 
When used, NOT and the next key word define the class test to be executed
for truth value. For example, NOT NUMERIC is a truth test for determining
that an identifier is nonnumeric. 


NUMERIC 
Identifier consists entirely of the characters 0 through 9, with or
without an operational sign. 


If its PICTURE does not contain an operational sign, the identifier being
tested is determined to be numeric only if the contents are numeric and an
operational sign is not present. 


If its PICTURE does contain an operational sign, the identifier being
tested is determined to be numeric only if the item is an elementary item,
the contents are numeric, and a valid operational sign is present. 


The NUMERIC test cannot be used with an identifier described as alphabetic
or as a group item that contains one or more signed elementary items. 


For numeric data items, the identifier being tested can be described 
X as USAGE DISPLAY or (as IBM extensions) USAGE COMPUTATIONAL-3, or 
X USAGE PACKED-DECIMAL. >

--end quoted text

DD
```

- **Date:** 2000-04-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8d0ipv$1ct0$1@news.cz.js.cn>`

```

Some questions please!
In the following configuration,I must install DFHCLNT resource group,
1. I was wondering if DFHCLNT is one of basic components of CICS/ESA 4.1 or
we must buy DFGCLNT in addition?
2. If CICS Transaction Server is required in the following configration?
3. Would you like to supply a sample to show me how to define the CICS
client base on tcp/ip in CICS/ESA?

   CICS      CICS   tcp/ip  CICS
   Gateway-->Client-------->ESA
   for NT    for NT
   V3.0.3                   V4.1

Please answer me or mail to me, my E-mail address is rushwoo@hengsys.com.cn
Thanx !!!
```

#### ↳ Re: If CICS Transaction Server is required?

- **From:** Jason Edmeades <edmeades@uk.ibm.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38F44481.A9DD0873@uk.ibm.com>`
- **References:** `<8d0ipv$1ct0$1@news.cz.js.cn>`

```
You cannot connect the CICS Client directly to a mainframe system via
tcp/ip. You can only use SNA (APPC) or TCP62 which is SNA over TCP/IP
and requires Anynet support on the mainframe side.

For configuration information use the helpful guides downloadable from:
http://www-4.ibm.com/software/ts/cics/library/manuals/ctg31dl.html

(See the 2nd section, configuration PDF's).
Jason

Hey Everyone,

Just to be upfront and frank I am a student, so bare that in mind.  I am
working on a simple interactive program using indexed files to add, chage,
query, and delete records.  I am using screen commands to display my menus
and data.  Everythign is working great for adding and quering records, but
unfortunatly I do not have an idea how to edit records using the screen
method.

What I am trying to do is display the original data and then be able to tab
or enter through each record and change it.  Currently when I cycle to ie
the Name field, the data is removed allowing me to reenter the string.  Is
ther a command to allow me to edit the existing text or hit return to keep
the current text.  The code I am using at present in my screen storage is as
follows:

       01  INPUT-SCREEN.
?
           05  LINE  7    COLUMN 30  PIC X(15)  TO WS-LAST-NAME
?
             REVERSE  NO HIGHLIGHT  REQUIRED  AUTO.
?
           05  LINE  7    COLUMN 60  PIC XX     TO WS-INITIALS
?
             REVERSE  NO HIGHLIGHT  REQUIRED  AUTO.
?

?
             REVERSE  NO HIGHLIGHT  REQUIRED  AUTO.
?

?
             REVERSE  NO HIGHLIGHT  REQUIRED  AUTO.
?
           05  LINE 12    COLUMN 40  PIC 9(8)   TO WS-SALES-AMOUNT
?

All suggestions would be appreciated.

Mark Hanlon

#### ↳ Re: SCREEN SECTION MENUS and Changing Text Interactively through Menu.

- **From:** "Gael Wilson" <gael.wilson@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8d1jro$rb7$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<qwSI4.35432$m5.669675@news1.rdc2.on.home.com>`

```
Mark,

If you want your Screen Section initially to contain the values of the WS-
items you will need to change the syntax you are using.

The <blah> TO WS-item syntax just returns the values entered to your
working-storage.
Adding <blah> FROM WS-item will set the values in the screen to the item in
working-storage. Or rather than having both TO and FROM statements you can
have USING which is the combination of both.

Lastly, depending on compiler/vendor you may need to do a DISPLAY prior to
the ACCEPT to get the appropriate values into the screen fields.

I hope this helps.


Mark Hanlon <great_cdn_guy@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:qwSI4.35432$m5.669675@news1.rdc2.on.home.com...
> Hey Everyone,
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> What I am trying to do is display the original data and then be able to
tab
> or enter through each record and change it.  Currently when I cycle to ie
> the Name field, the data is removed allowing me to reenter the string.  Is
> ther a command to allow me to edit the existing text or hit return to keep
> the current text.  The code I am using at present in my screen storage is
as
> follows:
>
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: SCREEN SECTION MENUS and Changing Text Interactively through Menu.

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8d19b4$25ns$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<qwSI4.35432$m5.669675@news1.rdc2.on.home.com>`

```
Instead of TO WS-LAST-NAME, use USING WS-LAST-NAME.

Screen items with FROM data-name are display-only.
Screen items with TO data-name are accept-only.
Screen items with USING data-name are both display and accept.
------------------
Rick Smith

>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…

as
>follows:

…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

## Thread: Presenting: TESTING COMPUTER SOFTWARE (TCS2000) Conference -- Featured Speakers — 1 msgs

### Presenting: TESTING COMPUTER SOFTWARE (TCS2000) Conference -- Featured Speakers

- **From:** uspdi@earthlink.net (USPDI)
- **Date:** 2000-04-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.forth
- **Message-ID:** `<uspdi-1204001009340001@1cust5.tnt6.tco2.da.uu.net>`

```

           17th International Conference and Exposition on

                     TESTING COMPUTER SOFTWARE
         Theme: Testing Technology vs. Testers' Requirements 

                         June 12-16, 2000
      (Conference: June 14-16; Workshops/Tutorials: June 12-13)
 
          Washington, D.C. * Hyatt Regency (Bethesda) * USA 
  

In cooperation with:
Association for Computing Machinery (ACM) SIGSoft
American Society for Quality (ASQ) Software Division
IEEE Reliability Society
Software Technology Support Center (STSC)

                            * * * * * *

The general sessions of the Conference on Testing Computer Software will
address major issues of concern to the developers and users of software.
The theme of the Conference "Testing Technology vs. Testers' Requirements"
focuses on providing practical and essential solutions to the software
development profession, and the users of computer software at all levels.

Shari Lawrence Pfleeger will give the Conference keynote address: "From
Art to Practice: Transferring Testing (and Other) Technology." She will
discuss that technology transfer requires a good idea, the generation of
evidence, analysis of that evidence, good packaging and support, and
careful consideration of the audience for the technology. Pfleeger
explores how to learn from other disciplines, so that we can devise a
transfer strategy that fits organizational as well as technological needs.

Louise Tamres's lunch address will present "If I Knew Then What I Know
Now." She will explain how to improve quality in your organization; how to
fight the right battles; and what are the pitfalls to avoid.

Lee Copeland's topic will be "When Helping Doesn't Help: Software Testing
as Codependent Behavior." He defines codependent behavior as "a way of
getting needs met that doesn't get needs met. We do all the wrong things
for the right reasons." This presentation will enable participants to: 1.
Understand the various aspects of codependency. 2. Recognize codependent
behavior in themselves and others. 3. Evaluate the negative effects of
their own codependent behavior. 4. Respond more appropriately through
establishing their own boundaries of behavior.

Bob Binder will discuss "Testing Object-Oriented (OO) Systems: Lessons
Learned." His talk will cover the lessons learned, best practices and
challenges in test design, test automation and test process for
object-oriented systems. Best practices available as test design patterns
are summarized. The state of the testing practice is explored with
practical improvement strategies and examples.

Simon Mills will explore "Is an Ounce of Test-plan Worth a Ton of Tools?"
His talk covers the story of his seeking an automated utopia, where it
went wrong and where it did not. He will review the history of test
automation, and the history of attitudes towards automation -- from pipe
dream to panacea -- and have a look at the reality. Mills will explore
automation as a portion of an overall testing strategy. Plenty of object
lessons from hard-won experience will be shared.

Tom Gilb's topic will be "Powerful and Pitiful Measures of Software." He
will summarize the powerful measures needed to help management achieve
their purposes as managers of software engineering teams. Gilb will
discuss how to identify objectives and appropriate scales of measurements
to evaluate whether those objectives have been met. A variety of measures
will be discussed. Twelve "tough questions" will be identified to help
keep our projects on track towards quality.

The Conference will conclude with the wrap-up session: "Conference
Reflections - and a few surprises thrown in!" Marnie Hutcheson will
expertly summarize: What we did; Where we've been; Where we're going.

Thirty-two parallel Track Sessions will cover a wide variety of topics on:
management, advanced topics, practitioners and automation. The Conference
sessions were designed to meet the needs of software professionals, ADP
managers and their organizations.

Three 2-day Workshops and six full day Tutorials will present
comprehensive technical sessions for software testing practitioners. These
sessions will provide detailed training on major software testing
technologies, tools and methodologies.

Networking with participants interested in specialized topics will be
facilitated by Birds-of-a-Feather Sessions and Lunch Discussion Groups.

Finally, the Testing Tools vendors exhibit will present the latest in
products and services to support software and applications testing.

For a continuously updated program check out on the Web:
     http://www.uspdi.org/conference

A printable version of the Conference brochure may be downloaded from:
     http://www.uspdi.org/conference/printbrochure.html

The Conference brochure and other information may also be requested by:

Regular Mail: USPDI, 612 Ethan Allen Avenue, Suite 100; Takoma Park,
              MD 20912-5400 (USA)
Phone:        (+1) 301-270-1033
Fax:          (+1) 301-270-1040 
E-mail:       admin@uspdi.org (Subject: TCS2000)

                 ***
```

## Thread: I want to see a mainframe — 44 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### I want to see a mainframe

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8d4f9k$8a9$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```



Hi:

Having worked 'At Home Alone' since 1983, I am out of touch with
the world of mainframes.

If there is anyone in the group who works in a mainframe environment,
in the New York metropolitan area, I would be very pleased to visit
your mainframe to see what they look like. The last mainframes I
saw were 360s and I would like to see what a 390 looks like and
to see a modern datacenter.

If anyone could arrange that, please email me.

Thanks

Tony Dilworth


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/

##### ↳ ↳ Re: I want to see a mainframe

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 2000-04-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5CEJ4.16988$0o4.115114@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<8d4f9k$8a9$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <O$aKdZbp$GA.238@cpmsnbbsa03>`

```
In article <O$aKdZbp$GA.238@cpmsnbbsa03>,
DennisHarley <LegacyBlue@email.msn.com> wrote:
>I work with mainframes all the time.
>It's been years since I have actually seen one.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>center was in the same building.

Hee hee hee... this is why I learned to concatenate ISPF line-commands; I
was taught 'Your terminal is in Omaha and the datacenter's in
Altoona; every time you hit 'Enter' you want to send as *much* as possible
to keep your own lag-time to a minimum.'  The typical example (used from
the long command-line of the ISPF Main Menu) is:

=2;;S CMP;C PROGNAM1 PROGNAM2 ALL;SUB;CAN;SPLIT;SDSF H;PRE MYUID*

... which is, of course, Very Obvious to some and Utterly Incomprehensible
to others.

(explanation: it invokes the ISPF editor panel, where your last library's
name can be left over by default, enters the selection screen for members
of that library, selects the member 'CMP' (for CoMPile, of course),
changes all occurrences of PROGNAM1 to PROGNAM2, submits the job to the
internal reader, cancels out of the changes (so you don't have to remember
PROGNAM2), splits the screen (similar to invoking a second session, sorta
kinda almost maybe), invokes SDSF (the System Display and Search Facility
one uses to monitor batch jobs which have been submitted or have already
run, among other things) to Display Active jobs and sets the
viewing-prefix to MYUID* so I can watch my compile run)

DD

>
>Foodman <foodman123@aol.com> wrote in message
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> Interestingly - quoteing you runs off the screen.  Reading does not.
> Eilene's posts run off reading and posting.  I use Free Agent.
> 
Oh dear. Must correct this one on behalf of Eileen Preston and my
Eileen. They get it right in UK - but N. Americans do seem to have a
problem with spelling Eileen :

	Helen		= original
	Scottish	= Aileen
	Irish		= Eileen ( as in Marureen, Kathleen etc.)

Doesn't mean either of the Eileens I refer to is Irish. Go back long
enough and mine is a Viking from the Isle of Wight.

Contrary to correct spelling of Eileen in UK - my last name gets spelled
correctly 80-90% of time in N.America but they manage to do a 'real
number on it' in UK - Gavin, Gaven, Gowan, Cowan, Caven, Govin etc..
etc.. take your pick, just mix the vowels.

Jimmy
```

  mixxerdw@eye_eye_echs.com wrote:
> It seems  Foodman <foodman123@aol.com> keyed the following on Thu, 13
Apr 2000 15:27:13:
>
> > Just get rid of the overalls, the undies can stay.
> >
> LOL!  Really, they are so darn small and have such low power
requirements that it takes a *really* big system to look impressive.  I
run about a $6M/mo business on an OS/390 box that really does, except
for the Xerox 4590 printer, fit into a large-ish clothes closet,
including the four 3480 tape drives and a couple thousand tapes.

Hi:

Can you give us the basic specs for your 390, please?

I am sort of prepared (if I get invited) to be somewhat
disappointed. After all, it would have to be pretty impressive
to compare to the big IBM data centers of the sixties.

TOny Dilworth

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: I want to see a mainframe

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38F6A3EE.4C313212@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<8d4f9k$8a9$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <1tGZ16mMk7i4-pn2-pxwjhJtc67Yf@localhost> <8d4ovr$jbg$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <1tGZ16mMk7i4-pn2-OX5SldJlpdxR@localhost> <8d5foc$e5g$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Foodman wrote:
> 
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> to compare to the big IBM data centers of the sixties.

Just a few days ago I was in the data center where I could actually rub
my hands on three of them.  Two Amdahl CMOS mainframes and a new IBM
G6.  You wouldn't be impressed, none of them were much bigger than the
E-ten-K (a large refrigerator), and there are no banks of blinking
lights.  The Amdahls are beige and the IBM G6 was black like the
monolith in "2001: A Space Odyssey".  I didn't notice the yellow racing
stripe.  

I understand these boxes are water-cooled, but don't require an external
chiller.  Each of them has 2-4 gigabytes of RAM (old-timers still call
it CORE).  The DASD farm is in a separate room, and total capacity is
measured in terabytes.

The things that really take up floor space are the SILOS, robot 3490
tape managers each as large as a truck.  Video cameras are mounted on
the actuating arms.  I get dizzy watching the monitors as they grab a
cartridge, accelerate, spin, mount, dismount.

Tape storage is probably measured in thousands of terabytes.  
  
> 
> Thanks
> 
> TOny Dilworth
> 
```

I mean a heater of barns operating by radiation as opposed to convection or conduction and barns, at least those of my youth (no "last ice age" jokes, please!) tended to be drafty and quite unpleasant when feeding goats at 0430 in January.

X1=L, X2=L & the domain is phonetic

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: I want to see a mainframe

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38F80165.A89E9D33@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<8d4f9k$8a9$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <1tGZ16mMk7i4-pn2-pxwjhJtc67Yf@localhost> <8d4ovr$jbg$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <1tGZ16mMk7i4-pn2-OX5SldJlpdxR@localhost> <8d5foc$e5g$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38F6A3EE.4C313212@worldnet.att.net> <uj6gprmp$GA.207@cpmsnbbsa03>`

```
ChrisOsborne wrote:
> 
> "Arnold Trembley" <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Have all the color schemes been changed?

I expect you're right and I'm wrong.  I didn't notice any racing stripe,
and when I mentioned my data center visit to a colleague he asked about
the racing stripe.  Perhaps he's seen a multiprise and thinks a G6 looks
like it.

> 
> >
> > I understand these boxes are water-cooled, but don't require an external
> 
> IBM G6 Mainframes are air cooled.

I once worked for a company that had an IBM-3083.  It was water-cooled
and required an external chiller.  Prior to the CMOS mainframes I'm
pretty sure the Amdahl boxes had a sealed internal liquid cooling system
with a sort of a radiator aimed down into the hollow floor.  When I'm
lucky enough to visit the glass room I try not to abuse my privileges by
opening the mainframe's access panels or popping the floor tiles for a
"look-see".

I'm not sure if the CMOS boxes have a similar internal liquid cooling
system.  I can imagine they might, but I know there are no external
chillers.

Anyone who really knows the hardware can easily correct me.  I'm lucky
to ever see the boxes at all.

Last Thursday, I think, the Wall Street Journal had an article on
"overclockers", PC hardware hackers who add liquid cooling to run their
Pentiums at higher clock speeds.  One guy went so far as to immerse his
motherboard in mineral oil.  Some use water, and occasionally they have
problems with leaks.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: I want to see a mainframe

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "ChrisOsborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OJXIrJxp$GA.304@cpmsnbbsa04>`
- **References:** `<8d4f9k$8a9$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <1tGZ16mMk7i4-pn2-pxwjhJtc67Yf@localhost> <8d4ovr$jbg$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <1tGZ16mMk7i4-pn2-OX5SldJlpdxR@localhost> <8d5foc$e5g$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38F6A3EE.4C313212@worldnet.att.net> <uj6gprmp$GA.207@cpmsnbbsa03> <38F80165.A89E9D33@worldnet.att.net>`

```



"Arnold Trembley" <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message
news:38F80165.A89E9D33@worldnet.att.net...
> ChrisOsborne wrote:
> >

<snip>

>
> Last Thursday, I think, the Wall Street Journal had an article on
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> problems with leaks.

Water?  Doesn't that short out the motherboard and/or peripheral cards?  Or
do they spray the motherboard with a non-conductive polymer of some sort
first and then immerse it?  Of course, I'm assuming that the power supply
and similar components are not in this bath, too.

I would think using a bath of a flourocarbon<sp> substitute would work
better, as all components would be immune to shorting in one of these
non-conductive fluids, but that's just me.

Besides, I'm not really interested in overclocking, I'd really rather buy a
faster processor.  Overclocking can do bad things to the microscopic chip
curcuit pathways that have nothing to do with heat (although heat is the big
culprit).  However, I also don't have big must-get-done jobs sitting on my
desk, either.  Back when I consistently read about graphics professionals
jobs when I ran a Mac as my main computer, the magazines consistently
hammered home the Speed is Money principle.  The faster the graphics got
processed, the faster your job was done and you could get paid and start on
the next.


>
>
> --
> Arnold Trembley
> http://home.att.net/~arnold.trembley/
```

No, it is the impurities that do that, like salts etc.  I have found
mouse wee to be the most destructive water based product.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: I want to see a mainframe

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** johnl@iecc.com (John R Levine)
- **Date:** 2000-04-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8de6q6$q3r$1@xuxa.iecc.com>`
- **References:** `<8d4f9k$8a9$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38F9A95C.D62F6256@zip.com.au> <#6FS8i9p$GA.233@cpmsnbbsa03> <8de5so$9d4$2@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
>> Water?  Doesn't that short out the motherboard
>> >
>> > No, it is the impurities that do that, like salts etc.  I have found
>> > mouse wee to be the most destructive water based product.

Well, actually, the water in a water-cooled mainframe never gets
anywhere near the chips.  Typically the chips are either in a helium
atmosphere (helium's a really good heat conductor) or there are metal
"thumbs" to conduct the heat away, then there's a heat exchanger with
water pipes to cool that first-level cooling system.  It's plain old
tap water in the pipes.

There's some really spiffy technology in modern mainframes -- a
complex of 12 processors, 10 of which are active, the other two are
hot spares which are swapped in automatically by hardware if any of
the 10 fails, redundant fiber optic I/O buses, etc.  But as others
have noted, it's all packed up in a box that looks like a
refrigerator.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Relativity and RM Cobol

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<zUEK4.133$QS5.528@client>`
- **References:** `<38f6147a.934439@news.giganews.com> <N2HJ4.92$sj3.993@client> <8defqe$m1m$1@news1.xs4all.nl>`

```

Anton <a.rusbach@coss.nl> wrote in message
news:8defqe$m1m$1@news1.xs4all.nl...
> Hi Tom,
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Anton Rusbach

The explanation is actually in the Designer help file; start with the topic
"Defining Record Types".

Essentially, one uses COBOL 88 level(s) (which may be added with the
Designer if they do not exist in the original source) to associate the
particular values that identify the record type(s).  Complex expressions
involving several level 88 items may be used, as well as the possibility of
using several synthesized elements (e.g. whether a specific data item is a
valid numeric).  Usually, however, the record type is simply determined by
one or two values in the record and all this is quite straightforward.

After one has defined record types, the record type appropriate for a table
is selected, and then the dta elements for that table are dragged/dropped on
the table definition.

This is a short explanation, of a process that is documented in the help
file in all of its (possible) complexity.  I will be happy to answer
specific questions;  seeing the FD in question is usually helpful.

Best regards,
Tom Morrison
Liant Software Corporation   www.liant.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Relativity and RM Cobol

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<GdGK4.138$3T2.65671@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<38f6147a.934439@news.giganews.com> <N2HJ4.92$sj3.993@client> <8defqe$m1m$1@news1.xs4all.nl>`

```
As a general guide, you can handle multiple record types in a file by using
REDEFINES - either explicit if using working-storage, or implicit if using
multiple '01' level record descriptions in the file section FD.

To use implicit redefinition in the FILE SECTION, you might code...

FD  Multi-record-file.
01   Header-record        PIC X(100).
01   Detail-record          PIC X(100).

Each READ of the file fills both header-record and detail-record; it is your
task to figure out which it is. For this it is pretty common to use an '88'
level entry. If the first character of the record contains 'H' or 'D' to
identify header or detail record types, a revised file section entry might
now look like...

FD  Multi-record-file.
01   Header-record        PIC X(100).
01   Detail-record          PIC X(100).
01   FILLER                 PIC X(01).
        88 IS-HEADER-RECORD  VALUE IS 'H'.
        88 IS-DETAIL-RECORD     VALUE IS 'D'.

If the records are different sizes, all you really gotta watch out for is
that you list the largest record size first.

Usually I am more comfortable using a single buffer (FD), and reading the
data into working storage, but the data structures look about the same. That
may be more style than substance - although in "header" and "detail"
scenarios, you often need to keep the header record around while processing
multiple details, and if you use the implicit redefinition shown above, as
soon as you read a detail, the header record is no longer available to you.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Windows Printing and COBOL

- **From:** dlmax@netcom.com (David L Maxwell)
- **Date:** 2000-04-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8d6gvm$fir$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8d597v$6ir$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38F623A1.67718E52@home.com>`

```
In article <38F623A1.67718E52@home.com>, jjgavan@home.com says...
>
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>Jimmy, Calgary AB

below is some portions of code using MicroFocus Netx and escape sequences
defined in HP's PCL 4&5 Printer Language. It is used to drive HP LaserJet II &
III printers.  Notice that no Windows API calls are used.  This code works
on all versions of Windows including Windows 2000.

Large portions of code have been removed.  Enough has been shown to give a 
rough
idea on how HP's PCL escape sequences can be used to control their printers
without having to resort to calling Windows printing API's. As you can see it
takes a lot of code to do anything very fancy.

All of the escape values were obtained from the HP PCL 5 Printer Language
Technical Reference Manual.

Dave



-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
       FILE-CONTROL.
           SELECT Form-Printer ASSIGN TO Printer.
       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.
       FD  Form-Printer
           LABEL RECORDS ARE OMITTED
           Linage IS Line-Count
           Lines AT Top 0
           Lines AT Bottom 0.
       01  Print-Line.
           03 filler                 pic x(5).
           03 form-area              pic x(80).
           03 filler                 pic x(59).

       Working-Storage SECTION.
       01 Working-data.
           03 Line-Count             Pic 99 value 0.
           03 Line-Count-66          pic 99 value 66.
           03 Spacing                pic 99 value 0.
      *****************************************************************
      *          Variables Used to control HP LaserJet III Printer
      *
      *    Symbol set .... "10U" is the HP PC-8
      *    Symbol Type ... "s1p" for proportional Scaling
      *                    "s0p" for fixed
      *    Style ......... "1s" for ITALIC
      *                    "0s" for UPRIGHT
      *    Stroke Weight . "3b" for BOLD
      *                    "0b" for MEDIUM
      *    Typeface ...... "4101T" for CG TIMES (proportional)
      *                    "4148T" for UNIVERS  (proportional)
      *                    "3T"    for COURIER
      *                    "0T"    for Line Printer
      *****************************************************************
      *****************************************************************
      *         HP Symbol Set Constants
      *****************************************************************
       78 PC-8              value "10U".
       78 Roman-8           value "8U".
       78 ECMA-94           value "0N".
       78 PC-8-DN           value "11U".
       78 PC-850            value "12U".
       78 Legal             value "1U".
      *****************************************************************
      *         HP Symbol Type Constants
      *****************************************************************
       78 Fix               value "s0p".
       78 Proportional      value "s1p".
      *****************************************************************
      *         HP Style Constants
      *****************************************************************
       78 Italic            value "1s".
       78 Upright           value "0s".
      *****************************************************************
      *         HP Stroke Weight Constants
      *****************************************************************
       78 Bold              value "3b".
       78 Medium            value "0b".
      *****************************************************************
      *         HP Typeface Constants
      *****************************************************************
       78 Courier           value "3T".
       78 CG-Times          value "4101T".
       78 Univers           value "4148T".
       78 Line-Printer      value "0T".
      *****************************************************************
      *         HP Area Fill Commands
      *****************************************************************
       78 Shaded-Fill       value "2P".
       78 Erase-Fill        value "1P".
       78 Black-Fill        value "0P".
       78 Cross-Hatch       value "3P".
      *****************************************************************

           03 Copies-Esc-seq.
             05 filler              pic x(3) value X"1B266C".
             05 copies              pic x(2) value "1".
             05 filler              pic x    value "X".

           03 P-Esc-seq.
              05 filler              pic x(2) value X"1B28".
              05 P-Symbol-Set        pic x(3) value PC-8.
              05 filler              pic x(2) value X"1B28".
              05 P-Symbol-Type       pic x(3) value Proportional.
              05 P-Point-Size        pic x(5) value "24.00".
              05 filler              pic x    value "v".
              05 P-Style             pic x(2) value Italic.
              05 P-Stroke-Weight     pic x(2) value Bold.
              05 P-Typeface          pic x(5) value CG-Times.

           03 F-Esc-seq.
              05 filler              pic x(2) value X"1B28".
              05 F-Symbol-Set        pic x(3) value PC-8.
              05 filler              pic x(2) value X"1B28".
              05 F-Symbol-Type       pic x(3) value Fix.
              05 F-Pitch             pic x(5) value "10.00".
              05 filler              pic x    value "h".
              05 F-Point-Size        pic x(5) value "12.00".
              05 filler              pic x    value "v".
              05 F-Style             pic x(2) value Upright.
              05 F-Stroke-Weight     pic x(2) value Medium.
              05 F-Typeface          pic x(2) value Courier.

           03 Area-Fill-Seq.
              05 filler              pic x(2) value X"1B2A".
              05 filler              pic x    value "p".
              05 X-Pos               pic x(4) value "165".
              05 filler              pic x    value "x".
              05 Y-Pos               pic x(4) value "660".
              05 filler              pic x    value "Y".
              05 filler              pic x(2) value X"1B2A".
              05 filler              pic x    value "c".
              05 Area-Width          pic x(4) value "1980".
              05 filler              pic x    value "a".
              05 Area-Height         pic x(4) value "228".
              05 filler              pic x    value "b".
              05 Area-Fill-ID        pic x(2) value "15".
              05 filler              pic x    value "g".
              05 Area-Fill-Type      pic x(2) value Shaded-Fill.




       Procedure division using Basic-Rec Work-Areas No-Copies.
      *Procedure division.
           OPEN Output Form-Printer.
           PERFORM 4000-Reset.
           PERFORM 4010-Initialize.



      * Prepare to print The Header Line
           MOVE "24.00"      TO P-Point-Size
           MOVE Italic       TO P-Style
           MOVE Proportional TO P-Symbol-Type
           MOVE Bold         TO P-Stroke-Weight
           Move P-Esc-Seq    TO Print-Line
           PERFORM 6010-Printing.
           MOVE Header       TO Print-Line
           Perform 6010-Printing.

      * Prepare to print the qualifier line
           MOVE "12.00"      TO P-Point-Size
      *    MOVE "10.00"      TO P-Point-Size
           MOVE Italic       TO P-style
           MOVE Proportional TO P-Symbol-Type
           MOVE Medium       TO P-Stroke-Weight
           Move P-Esc-Seq    TO Print-Line
           PERFORM 6010-Printing.
           MOVE Qualifier    TO Print-Line
           Write print-line after advancing 2 lines

      * Change to Fixed Courier Font for Form Printing
           MOVE Italic       TO F-style
           MOVE Bold         TO F-Stroke-Weight
           Move F-Esc-Seq    TO Print-Line
           Perform 6010-Printing
      * Write First Block
           MOVE Print-Line-1 TO Form-Area
           Write print-line after advancing 1 lines
           MOVE Print-Line-2a TO Form-Area
           Write print-line after advancing 1 lines


      * Change to Proportional scalable CG Times Font for the Footer
           MOVE "12.00"      TO P-Point-Size
      *    MOVE "10.00" to P-Point-Size
           MOVE Upright      TO P-style
           MOVE Proportional TO P-Symbol-Type
           MOVE Medium       TO P-Stroke-Weight
           Move P-Esc-Seq    TO  Print-Line
           PERFORM 6010-Printing
           MOVE Footer TO Print-Line
           Write print-line after advancing 5 lines

      *----------------------------------------------------------------------
      * Examples of shading various areas of the form
      *----------------------------------------------------------------------

      * Area-Width = 30 x Number of characters wide (69)
      * Shade the Title Areas (Date Line)
           MOVE "165"  TO X-Pos
           MOVE "164"  TO Y-Pos
           MOVE "2070" TO Area-Width
           MOVE "61"   TO Area-Height
           MOVE "08"   TO Area-Fill-ID
           MOVE Shaded-Fill TO Area-Fill-Type
           MOVE Area-Fill-Seq TO Print-Line
           PERFORM 6010-Printing

      * Shade the Title Areas (Employee Line)
           MOVE "165"  TO X-Pos
           MOVE "300"  TO Y-Pos
           MOVE "2070" TO Area-Width
           MOVE "61"  TO Area-Height
           MOVE "08"   TO Area-Fill-ID
           MOVE Shaded-Fill TO Area-Fill-Type
           MOVE Area-Fill-Seq TO Print-Line
           PERFORM 6010-Printing

      * Shade the Title Areas (Job Line)
           MOVE "165"  TO X-Pos
           MOVE "436"  TO Y-Pos
           MOVE "2070" TO Area-Width
           MOVE "61"   TO Area-Height
           MOVE "08"   TO Area-Fill-ID
           MOVE Shaded-Fill TO Area-Fill-Type
           MOVE Area-Fill-Seq TO Print-Line
           PERFORM 6010-Printing

      * Shade the Title Areas (QTY Insp Line)
           MOVE "165"  TO X-Pos
           MOVE "572"  TO Y-Pos
           MOVE "2070" TO Area-Width
           MOVE "61"  TO Area-Height
           MOVE "08"   TO Area-Fill-ID
           MOVE Shaded-Fill TO Area-Fill-Type
           MOVE Area-Fill-Seq TO Print-Line
           PERFORM 6010-Printing

      * Shade the Title Areas (Inspection Type)
           MOVE "165"  TO X-Pos
           MOVE "752"  TO Y-Pos
           MOVE "2070" TO Area-Width
           MOVE "62"   TO Area-Height
           MOVE "08"   TO Area-Fill-ID
           MOVE Shaded-Fill TO Area-Fill-Type
           MOVE Area-Fill-Seq TO Print-Line
           PERFORM 6010-Printing.

      *-------------------------------------------------------------------
      *  large portions of code deleted
      *-------------------------------------------------------------------


       4000-Reset.
           MOVE 0 TO SPACING.
           MOVE X'1B45' TO Print-Line
           PERFORM 6010-Printing.

       4010-Initialize.
           MOVE Line-Count-66 TO Line-Count.
      *    This sets TOP Margin to 0 lines [esc &k0E] *
           MOVE X'1B266B3045' TO Print-Line.
           PERFORM 6010-Printing.
      *    This turns PERF Skip Off         [esc &l0L] *
           MOVE X'1B266C304C' TO Print-Line.
           PERFORM 6010-Printing.
      *    This sets VMI for 66 lines  [esc &l7.2727C] *
           MOVE X'1B266C372E3237323743' TO Print-Line.
           PERFORM 6010-Printing.
      *    Set up Number of copies
           MOVE No-Copies TO Copies
           move Copies-Esc-Seq TO Print-Line.
           perform 6010-Printing.
      *-------------------------------------------------------------


       6010-PRINTING.
           WRITE Print-Line AFTER SPACING.
           MOVE SPACES TO Print-Line.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
```

#### ↳ Re: Windows Printing and COBOL

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8d5dp2$c1f$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8d597v$6ir$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In article <8d597v$6ir$1@nnrp1.deja.com>,
  johnglennon@my-deja.com wrote:
> I'm looking for a way to print directly to the windows print api from
a
> COBOL program.  I want to be able to control the fonts within a print
> line. Also want to be able to invoke the printer Common Dialog box
from
> the cobol program.
>
> Thanks

Hi:

I dimly recall that Windows strips escape characters (027) in print
lines. To open a cash drawer, I have to send an escape sequence to
the printer (027, 008). If the escape sequence is the first
character of the print line, it doesn't work. Put the escape sequence
starting in column 3 or something and it works.

I also use a COBOL program to print envelopes on a laser jet. The
program inserts the PCL escape sequences in the print line. This
works fine selecting font and position on envelope.

The printer is assigned to LPT1.

Thanks

TOny Dilworth


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

and write an OLE application to do what you want!?!?!?

#### ↳ Re: Windows Printing and COBOL

- **From:** "Robert Heady" <r.heady@liant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vMGJ4.57$sj3.964@client>`
- **References:** `<8d597v$6ir$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
johnglennon@my-deja.com wrote in message <8d597v$6ir$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>I'm looking for a way to print directly to the windows print api from a
>COBOL program.  I want to be able to control the fonts within a print
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

RM/COBOL version 7.00.02 for 32-Bit Windows can do this.  A library of P$
subprograms have been added to provide a broad range of Windows printing
capabilites.  You can control fonts, draw boxes and lines, print bitmaps and
invoke the printer Common Dialog box.

For more details see:


http://www.liant.com/download/pdf/rmcobv7.pdf
```

#### ↳ Re: Windows Printing and COBOL

- **From:** "Pat Smith" <patsmith@seawise-isl.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<955728042.13287.0.nnrp-06.9e98341c@news.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<8d597v$6ir$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
<johnglennon@my-deja.com> wrote in message
news:8d597v$6ir$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> I'm looking for a way to print directly to the windows print api from a
> COBOL program.  I want to be able to control the fonts within a print
> line. Also want to be able to invoke the printer Common Dialog box from
> the cobol program.

You could be entering into a bit of a sledgehammer and a nut situation here.
I've written a COBOL wrapper around the 16bit WIN API using the MicroFocus
compiler. This works well enough on WIN3.1 95 98 and NT. It includes the
features you mentioned plus a couple more like retaining the last selected
font information on disk, and correctly calculating the rendering size for
the font on an output device. Unfortunatly I'm not at liberty to supply you
with the code here.

I can say that this is not something you'd really want to do unless you're
serious about using the Windows GDI.

You'll need:
    1) a COBOL compiler that supports Windows API programming, I've only
used MF's and can't comment on this support from other compilers.
    2) The windows development kit
    3) Understanding of C is useful for reading.....
    4)A book - Programming Windows 3.1 Charles Petzold or the later 5th
edition (WIN32 API) if your using a 32 bit compiler, are widely recommended.
Either of these books includes the windows development kit, I think.

If you are already writing Windows programs via the API from COBOL then
perhaps I can give you some of the information you require. However, if
you're not yet doing that then I'd advise looking at an alternative solution
or consider learning the Win API first.

hope this helps,

Pat
```

#### ↳ Re: Windows Printing and COBOL

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-04-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38fb83f6.30934209@news.cox-internet.com>`
- **References:** `<8d597v$6ir$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
On Thu, 13 Apr 2000 20:04:27 GMT, johnglennon@my-deja.com wrote:

>I'm looking for a way to print directly to the windows print api from a
>COBOL program.  I want to be able to control the fonts within a print
>line. Also want to be able to invoke the printer Common Dialog box from
>the cobol program.
>

You have (partially) described COBOL Form Print from Flexus -
http://www.flexus.com.  You have full control over fonts, you can
include pictures, you can call the printer selection dialog (or not)
as you desire!

Give it a try - 60 day free trial upon download from their site.
```

I'm working with IBM Cobol for VSE/ESA 1.1.0 on a IBM mainframe.
We have programs written in assembler that receive the record and block
sizes of input or output files from parameter cards and modify the dtf
accordingly.
Is there a way to accomplish this in Cobol?
```

#### ↳ Re: accepting record and block sizes

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8dg5vm$bp6$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<38F5A95D.BF20C4B8@bestweb.net>`

```
I know MVS (OS/390) rather than VSE - but you PROBABLY can pass the FD name
to your assembler program.  This works on MVS for QSAM but *not* VSAM files.
My guess is that it will also work for SAM (?) on VSE - but won't swear to
it.
```

…[13 more quoted lines elided]…

I heard somewhere that you could read any file by using the clause
block contains zero records and record contain zero characters. You
define your record to the largest possible size.

I have never tried it and I cannot remember where this thought came
from, caveat a emptor.  I have kept this concept to try when and if I
needed to.  If you try let me know the results.

## Thread: Removing spaces from fields — 5 msgs

### Removing spaces from fields

- **From:** "Joris Vanderhoeven" <stimpy@pandora.be>
- **Date:** 2000-04-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0JpJ4.78595$l35.163393@afrodite.telenet-ops.be>`

```
Hi !

I'm a student working on a school project.
We have to write a program about school administration.
All the hard work is done, but I still have one problem:

at the end of the program there should be a line which says:

You have worked xxx hours, xxx mins and xxx secs with the program

The problem is this :

the hours mins and secs can be > 100 < 100 or < 10
For example
when you have worked 1 h 1 m and 10 sec

I get:

You have worked    1 h,   1 min and  10 sec
instead of
You have worked 1 h, 1 min and 10 sec

I'm using CA Realia

Is there any simple way to remove the excess spaces from the line ?

Any help would be very welcome

Thanx
```

#### ↳ Re: Removing spaces from fields

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38F62A74.8505BE8D@home.com>`
- **References:** `<0JpJ4.78595$l35.163393@afrodite.telenet-ops.be>`

```


Joris Vanderhoeven wrote:
> 
> Hi !
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Try STRING - following is very crude ( but also check STRING syntax in
your textbook/reference manuals) :-

01 field-hours		pic zzz9/9999.
01 field-minutes 	pic zzz9/9999.
01 field-seconds	pic zzz9/9999.

initialize (move spaces to) output-string *> *** good idea to do this
String	 field-hours delimited by size	  
    	" h " delimited by size
	field-seconds delimited by size
    	" min  " delimited by size
	field-seconds delimited by size
	" sec " delimited by size
	into output-string
End-string

To get the 'value' strings reduced to 'actuals' check for leading zeroes
or blanks and then you can use REFERENCE MODIFICATION to move those.
For example - field hours 4 char field = 11 then you would be
'stringing' field-hours(3:2) from third character to the end which is
the '2', or another way to express it field-hours(3: ) - the second
missing ref mod implying move 'containing up to the last'. Obviously to
use this approach you need variables to denote the positioning :-

	string field-hours (x:y) delimited by size
		etc.....

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8nHJ4.111$sj3.654@client>`
- **References:** `<0JpJ4.78595$l35.163393@afrodite.telenet-ops.be> <RIuJ4.5602$_H2.134268@news.swbell.net>`

```

Jerry P <bismail@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:RIuJ4.5602$_H2.134268@news.swbell.net...
> The easy way is to copy the exiting line to a new line, byte by byte,
> discarding multiple blanks.

Perhaps a code fragment to show this using UNSTRING/STRING (I know it is
homework, but...)
[warning:  untested]

01  FIXED-FORMAT-LINE.
    03  pic x(16) value "You have worked ".
    03  FFL-HOURS pix Z9.
    03  pic x(4) value " h, ".
    03  FFL-MINUTES pic Z9.
    03  pic x(9) value " min and ".
    03  FFL-SECONDS pic Z9.
    03  pic x(10) value " sec".

01  CONSTRUCTED-LINE pic x(50).
01  HOLDER pic x(50).

01  F-POS  pic 99.
01  F-LEN  pic 99.
01  C-POS pic 99.
...


    move 1 to F-POS, C-POS.
    move 0 to F-LEN.
    inspect FIXED-FORMAT-LINE tallying F-LEN for characters.
    move spaces to CONSTRUCTED-LINE
    perform until F-POS >= F-LEN
        unstring FIXED-FORMAT-LINE
                delimited by ALL SPACE
                into HOLDER pointer F-POS
        string HOLDER delimited by space
                " " delimited by size
                into CONSTRUCTED-LINE
                pointer C-POS
    end-perform
```

KWilliams

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-04-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38F75F3C.FCBF56E8@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<20000303151450.02510.00000239@ng-cd1.aol.com> <38c02889.69848657@news.transport.com> <q0h0cs8i632g3o546c5voii59eiojptgb5@4ax.com> <38C0B894.A2E482B5@home.com> <7j43cson8697j65qdemgviu001u28gnqge@4ax.com> <38C1F245.BCE36D2B@home.com> <jjt5cskgfmu26ko4etdpk04ivmpgnk24oc@4ax.com> <8a16i4$l5k$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net> <1398cskr7docc5o2cg8qccagqbu2tmaep5@4ax.com> <38C531C9.5378276@home.com> <qRax4.4645$5Y2.70748@news3.mia> <Zkgx4.2615$HN.138687@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <38C66090.83A1B7DD@cusys.edu> <Hkyx4.41$lA4.1578@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <Vr6y4.25825$wk.1368603@news1.mia>`

```
I just found this posting below in my files and was prompted to ask if
the GUI interface for COBOL should be a web browser.  The interface
specifications are cross browser and cross operating system.  While they
are a moving target, they at least aren't controlled by a single vendor.
Also COBOL should probably support the use of XML.  Doing both of these
things would mean a learning experience for me because I understand
neither of them but they look like a supportable way to go.  I will be
interested in seeing opinions on whether this is a valid approach.

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services Inc., cfmtech@istar.ca

…[63 more quoted lines elided]…

…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> interested in seeing opinions on whether this is a valid approach.

This has been pretty much my position.  I also want to access ODBC/JDBC
with data & with meta-data.  COBOL is about business rules and data,
features which improve its ability to be clear and accurate with these
are very welcome.

But don't get bogged down in GUI details, there are other products which
are much better tools at hitting this moving target.  Tools such as
browsers, spreadsheets, or data mart tools should supplement our
business orientation.  So make it easy to link to those tools, and keep
Cobol doing what it does best.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL GUI interface was Re: Procedural vs Object-Oriented programming

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8dshvr$g4p$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<20000303151450.02510.00000239@ng-cd1.aol.com> <38c02889.69848657@news.transport.com> <q0h0cs8i632g3o546c5voii59eiojptgb5@4ax.com> <38C0B894.A2E482B5@home.com> <7j43cson8697j65qdemgviu001u28gnqge@4ax.com> <38C1F245.BCE36D2B@home.com> <jjt5cskgfmu26ko4etdpk04ivmpgnk24oc@4ax.com> <8a16i4$l5k$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net> <1398cskr7docc5o2cg8qccagqbu2tmaep5@4ax.com> <38C531C9.5378276@home.com> <qRax4.4645$5Y2.70748@news3.mia> <Zkgx4.2615$HN.138687@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <38C66090.83A1B7DD@cusys.edu> <Hkyx4.41$lA4.1578@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <Vr6y4.25825$wk.1368603@news1.mia> <38F75F3C.FCBF56E8@istar.ca> <38FB0EC8.2B30F618@cusys.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote in message

>But don't get bogged down in GUI details, there are other products which
>are much better tools at hitting this moving target.  Tools such as
>browsers, spreadsheets, or data mart tools should supplement our
>business orientation.  So make it easy to link to those tools, and keep
>Cobol doing what it does best.

I would have thought this exactly the what you were looking for with OOP.
Excel, for example, *is* an OLE object that Cobol can converse with via the
new standard.  Having the mainframe load up an Excel spreadsheet with data
and letting the user play about with it is one of the most interesting
things OOP can do. I cannot see why you are so resistant to it, and relegate
it to small systems only.
```

### RMCobol handling BIG indexed files

- **From:** "Anton" <a.rusbach@coss.nl>
