- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8d6lj8$rcv$1@news1.xs4all.nl>`

```
Is there a limitation on the size of indexed files?

We are the 256 user version of RMCobol 6.61 for Unix

It appears that we can not write new records in an indexed file that is
1.528.373.184 bytes big.


kind regards,
Anton Rusbach

like 2 or 4K to enable the maximum size.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RMCobol handling BIG indexed files

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Robert Heady" <r.heady@liant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<WyjL4.24$Od.540@client>`
- **References:** `<8d6lj8$rcv$1@news1.xs4all.nl> <IYGJ4.78$sj3.1022@client> <8defhu$lkr$1@news1.xs4all.nl> <8dia0g$e6t$1@news101.telia.com> <8diu7r$v0g$1@news7.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Anton,

For indexed files the maximum file size depends on the block size used for
the file.  This is the formula for computing the maximum size an indexed
file:

 (MAX_LOCK_BYTE / (BlockSize + 256)) * BlockSize = maximum file size

The default value for MAX_LOCK_BYTE for file versions 0 and 2 is  x7FFFEBFD
or decimal 2,147,478,525.

The larger the block size, the closer the file size will approach the
MAX_LOCK_BYTE.

Examples:

 BlockSize = 512
                       (2147478525 / (512 + 256)) * 512    =   1,431,652,350
bytes   (1.33 GB)

 BlockSize =  4096
                       (2147478525 / (4096 + 256)) * 4096 =   2,021,156,258
bytes  (1.88 GB)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RMCobol handling BIG indexed files

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Anton" <a.rusbach@coss.nl>

<a id="t-d3cde1c2f1"></a>
## Thread: AS/400 to Semi-GUI recommendations — 19 msgs

_Topics: [`AS/400, iSeries, RPG`](topics.md#as400)_

### AS/400 to Semi-GUI recommendations

- **From:** Dutch Owen <dutch@sysun.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OVGJ4.11701$x62.59006@typhoon.southeast.rr.com>`

```
     I'm a consultant and experienced COBOL programmer, also familiar
with the windows API and GUI.  I have a client who has a large-scale
mainly COBOL-based application running on the AS/400.  Management now
wants this application ported to the PC environment, with the main
requirement being that the formerly green-screen interactive programs
now look nice on the Windows platform.  They want to save as much
code as possible, and for the system design and operation of the 
programs to remain as close as possible to the original; it seems
the main goal is to 1) run on Windows, and 2) look like a Windows
app without actually buying into the expensive redesign and rewrite
necessary to give real GUI functionality.

     What I'm asking for is any recommendations on the best approach
to take, and what tools might be best for this project.  I've used
MicroFocus COBOL years ago, and we're getting a copy of Net Express
to evaluate.  I've also looked at the websites for tools like SP2.
But I have no real experience trying to graft what I would call a
Semi- or fake GUI onto a character based app.  I don't even know if
it's a practical idea, but it sounds like others may have tried 
before us and do know.

     To be a bit more precise, I think that screen formats with the fields
in similar locations to the original, but with nice colors and pretty fonts
and maybe a picture here and there, plus a few very restricted uses of real
GUI-type objects uses as a combobox for code lookups is what they are
looking for.  They don't want to transform a 1980s app into QuickBooks Pro,
they just want it to look good enough to sell without the prospects cringing
when they see the green screens.

     Recommendations to a) stay with green-screen, or b) go all the 
way and redesign for a proper GUI are not helpful, as management is
not interested in either of those options.

    Thanks for any advice /warnings / war stories, etc.

    Dutch
```

#### ↳ Re: AS/400 to Semi-GUI recommendations

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 2000-04-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8d7lqe$ghj$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<OVGJ4.11701$x62.59006@typhoon.southeast.rr.com>`

```
"Dutch Owen" <dutch@sysun.com> wrote in message
news:OVGJ4.11701$x62.59006@typhoon.southeast.rr.com...
>      I'm a consultant and experienced COBOL programmer, also familiar
> with the windows API and GUI.  I have a client who has a large-scale
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> necessary to give real GUI functionality.

I have no experience in porting from character AS400 Cobol to gui Cobol on
Windows, so I can't help you there, but there is one statement of yours,
that I paid attention to, that is that your management want to save as much
code as possible. By saying that you are narrowing down the path quite a
bit. To my knowledge, there is only one Cobol that allows GUI programming
with Cobol "like" syntax, that is Acucobol. For instance, to display a
combo-box you would do:

    DISPLAY COMBO-BOX LINE 5 COL 3 USING MY-DATA.

A push button:

    DISPLAY PUSH-BUTTON LINE 10 COL 15 SIZE 5 TITLE "Click" SELF-ACT.

An entry field:

    DISPLAY ENTRY-FIELD AT 0705 SIZE 30 USING MY-ENTRY UPPER.

This syntax may be used in procedure division or screen section, you may
have BEFORE, AFTER, EXCEPTION procedures, using events if you like. ACCEPT
is how you get your data and of course, it displays GUI. Most of it is
directly portable to character interface, in the event something is not, you
may specify two editions with the same name in your screen section
separating it using the reserved words CHARACTER and GRAPHICAL, the runtime
will then choose the appropriate instance.
To check it out, take a look at www.acucorp.com

You also got the option of using an external form utility, like FormSP,
which also will preserve most of your logic.

I may be wrong, but I believe that using Fujitsu or Micro Focus will require
OO programming to get GUI, for RM Cobol I don't know.

I am certain I will stay corrected if I am wrong.

Cheesle
```

#### ↳ Re: AS/400 to Semi-GUI recommendations

- **From:** terrywin.nospam@pronetisp.net (Terry Winchester)
- **Date:** 2000-04-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38f888d5.3077206@news.pronetisp.net>`
- **References:** `<OVGJ4.11701$x62.59006@typhoon.southeast.rr.com>`

```

On Fri, 14 Apr 2000 15:36:14 GMT, Dutch Owen <dutch@sysun.com> wrote:

>     I'm a consultant and experienced COBOL programmer, also familiar
>with the windows API and GUI.  I have a client who has a large-scale
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
>    Dutch

Cheesle's suggestion may meet your needs but IMHO it will require a
fair amount of work to convert the 5250 display file code/logic.  I
also work on an AS/400 and have yet to find anything that does all
that you want (or I want for that matter) in a *painless* way.

You might try looking at 5250 screen scrapers.  There seems to be
quite a few of them (try AS400Network.com), but, since you indicated
that your "selling" the software you'd have to make sure could bundle
the third party screen scraper package with yours at a reasonable cost
(which seems unlikely).

And of course you also have "workstation gateway" as a GUI option 
via a browser - but this is not a viable solution for anyone IMHO.

(If it was RPG you could have used Visual Age RPG & Code400 -- yuck!)


Good Luck, you're going to need it.

Terry
```

#### ↳ Re: AS/400 to Semi-GUI recommendations

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38F78DD4.917CAED7@home.com>`
- **References:** `<OVGJ4.11701$x62.59006@typhoon.southeast.rr.com>`

```


Dutch Owen wrote:
> 
>      I'm a consultant and experienced COBOL programmer, also familiar
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
>     Dutch

Well Cheesle offers one possible solution. Based on what you've written,
may we assume you are using Screen Section ? Whether you choose Acu,
Fujitsu or NetExpress, to achieve that 'Windows look alike', probably
your best approach is use SP2.

I'm copying to Thane so that he can make observations/recommendations. 
Assuming Screen Section, SP2 would allow you a typical structured
approach returning 'Windows' values to your structured. Now how tricky
it would be converting your screen code to SP2 'send/receive syntax' I
have no idea - perhaps Thane can e-mail you with an old Screen Section
program converted to SP2.

If you choose NetExpress you can still use SP2; don't attempt OO/GUIs or
Dialog System as they represent what you are trying to avoid - a new
learning curve. I'm not familiar with Fujitsu but if you are proficient
in APIs - then you could also use these in NetExpress - but I would
doubt that gives a quick switchover from Screen Section to Windows
style.

It goes without saying - nothing is quite as simple as it seems :-) Good
luck.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: AS/400 to Semi-GUI recommendations

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)

I'm thinking he is using SDA (Screen Design Aid) in which case he is
familiar with the WYSIWYG concept of the Sp2 panel editor.  SDA
operates with "calls" just like Sp2.  It should be an easy transition.
That said, my acticle in IEEE software shows a Screen Section
converted to SP2.
```

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-14T00:00:00

```
In article <OVGJ4.11701$x62.59006@typhoon.southeast.rr.com>,
  Dutch Owen <dutch@sysun.com> wrote:
>      What I'm asking for is any recommendations on the best approach
> to take, and what tools might be best for this project.
>     Dutch
>
>

Hi:

You mentioned that this was software 'to sell'. I know from experience
that selling that old-fashioned stuff is hard to do.

I converted four applications in MF COBOL from DOS to Windows and
used SP2.

I would recommend it highly.  You can keep your programs working
just the same way, but give them Windows-like appearances and
they will run under Windows just fine.

Try it, you'll like it.

If you want to know more, just email me.


Thanks

Tony Dilworth


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: AS/400 to Semi-GUI recommendations

- **From:** "Costas Giannoulis" <diavasi@otenet.gr>
- **Date:** 2000-04-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8d9jv6$4d4$1@newssrv.otenet.gr>`
- **References:** `<OVGJ4.11701$x62.59006@typhoon.southeast.rr.com>`

```

Hi,

First of all - as SFF5KEY points out - a great factor is how interface,
business logic and data managment (database or file handling) are mixed up.
From my experience, if the application uses screen section then  a tool like
SP2  may be a nice solution. On the other hand if you use standard
accept/display syntax mixed up with business logic then things are more
difficult.

Another thing is the time period you have to deliver the project. If you
have a logical time available (it depends on the complexity of the
application), i suggest you to redesign the application and if you can,
depending on the people will work you, have a look to OOD's way. This would
be a great step but the final project will be a powerful application with
reusable components. Not just another gui application.

As you wrote, managment decided to transfer the application on windows
environment. What about  if after 1 year managment decides to convert the
same programs into an Internet/Intranet based application ? Or what about if
they decide to change the data storage mechanism ?
You will rewrite the whole application having some versions of business
logic ?

I believe you should take a decision based on the  future plans (and the
future market), not only the gui appearance ....

Good luck
Costas

Dutch Owen <dutch@sysun.com> wrote in message
news:OVGJ4.11701$x62.59006@typhoon.southeast.rr.com...
>      I'm a consultant and experienced COBOL programmer, also familiar

…[20 more quoted lines elided]…

fonts
> and maybe a picture here and there, plus a few very restricted uses of
real
> GUI-type objects uses as a combobox for code lookups is what they are
> looking for.  They don't want to transform a 1980s app into QuickBooks
Pro,
> they just want it to look good enough to sell without the prospects
cringing
> when they see the green screens.
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

Actually, Metro Information Services' Nashville, TN office has created

screen section to sp2

same source program and same screen definitions to deploy the
application across the 'net as a web browser based client app or a
non-web browser based internet app.

The file handling should also be separated from thebusiness logic if
at all possible.

>You will rewrite the whole application having some versions of business
>logic ?

Not necessarily.  Our philosophy is that you should always maintain
only one set of source code regardless of where you need to deploy
your user interface.

>I believe you should take a decision based on the  future plans (and the
>future market), not only the gui appearance ....

I agree 100%

[snip Dutch's original posting]
Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

#### ↳ Re: AS/400 to Semi-GUI recommendations

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2000-04-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000415002923.12346.00000593@nso-fc.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<OVGJ4.11701$x62.59006@typhoon.southeast.rr.com>`

```
In article <OVGJ4.11701$x62.59006@typhoon.southeast.rr.com>, Dutch Owen
<dutch@sysun.com> writes:

>     What I'm asking for is any recommendations on the best approach
>to take, and what tools might be best for this project.  I've used
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I cannot speak from experience on this issue.  From what I have
read about here, there are several options for handling your screen
design processes (VB, Delphi, Java, SP2, CaseGen, Win32API).  
It all depends on how tightly your screen interaction code is laced 
thru your program logic.  If the screen build/present/response 
functions are broken out into separate parts, it should be fairly easy 
to design your screens with one of these tools and replace the 
perform with a call of the Screen module/DLL/whatever.
Unfortunately, a lot of these 'simple' things appear easy at the outset
and you later find a snake's nest once you are knee-deep into the 
process.
```

#### ↳ Re: AS/400 to Semi-GUI recommendations

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 2000-04-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38fb6d5a.21135102@news.epix.net>`
- **References:** `<OVGJ4.11701$x62.59006@typhoon.southeast.rr.com>`

```
Dutch Owen <dutch@sysun.com> wrote:

>     I'm a consultant and experienced COBOL programmer, also familiar
>with the windows API and GUI.  I have a client who has a large-scale
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>before us and do know.

Dutch:

sp2 does indeed directly invoke Windows API functions in order to
display GUI screens, therefore it is a real GUI screen which is
displayed.

The same situation applies regardless of whether you are speaking
about any other approach or toolset unless of cource you are directly
invoking the Windows API functions from the COBOL application.....this
can get a bit complex and messy.

I suspect that you will get a number of different recommendations from
a variety of people in this group, but preserving your business logic
and replacing the front end is indeed a wonderful idea.

You need to determine how much work is involved in separating the user
interface logic from the business logic.  If the screen handling in
the existing application is indeed isolated from the bulk of the
application, it may be a simple task.  If it is intertwined in every
program, it may be difficult.

We have assisted a lot of customers in making this transition.
Perhaps you can provide a bit more detail on how the screens are
displayed currently from the COBOL application.  This is important in
helping to make a determination as to the "path of least resistance."

>     To be a bit more precise, I think that screen formats with the fields
>in similar locations to the original, but with nice colors and pretty fonts
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>when they see the green screens.

That is generally the most desireable approach.  Later, the GUI
screens can be enhanced with additional features.  If the conversion
can be automated or partially automated, then that's even better.  Let
us know how the screens are currently displayed.  Depending upon your
current approach, one of my customers may be able to supply you with
an more automated approach to conversion.

>     Recommendations to a) stay with green-screen, or b) go all the 
>way and redesign for a proper GUI are not helpful, as management is
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: AS/400 to Semi-GUI recommendations

- **From:** khansen@screenio.com (Kevin Hansen)
- **Date:** 2000-04-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```

Dutch,

We've had many folks convert mainframe applications to the PC; it's
eminently practical.    Our new GUI ScreenIO completely isolates you
from the Windows side of the issue so that you can concentrate on your
business logic.

We can probably even import your panels into GUI format if they in any
way resemble SDA format.

You can download our GUI ScreenIO from our website and try it; it's
written completely in itself and COBOL so it gives you some idea of
how we work and what we can do.  Questions/comments are welcome, and
we're glad to provide excellent technical support on evaluations as
well.

http://www.screenio.com

Kevin 

>Dutch Owen <dutch@sysun.com> wrote:
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>>necessary to give real GUI functionality.

NORCOM COBOL Programming Tools
GUI ScreenIO; a cool COBOL/Windows Screen Manager
http://www.ScreenIO.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: AS/400 to Semi-GUI recommendations

- **From:** jets@nbnet.nb.ca (Tony M. Mina)
- **Date:** 2000-04-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38fbecde.44550112@allnews.nbnet.nb.ca>`
- **References:** `<OVGJ4.11701$x62.59006@typhoon.southeast.rr.com> <38fb6d5a.21135102@news.epix.net>`

```
This is exactly what we are doing now.  We are converting the green
screen to GUI using SP2 from Flexus.  The only difference is that ours
is a Unisys 2200 Clearpath mainframe.  On the client side we use Fujitsu
Cobol and on the mainframe side it is the same as Cobol.  The only thing
we are doing on the mainframe side is to remove the screen processing.
The business rules are intact.  We also replaced our form printing on
the mainframe with FormPrint from Flexus.

So far the conversion is very easy since we are not changing the
business rules.  We are averaging 1 program a day and this includes
cleanups as well.

Tony M. Mina




On Mon, 17 Apr 2000 20:13:08 GMT, rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe) wrote:

>Dutch Owen <dutch@sysun.com> wrote:
>
…[74 more quoted lines elided]…
>

##### ↳ ↳ Re: AS/400 to Semi-GUI recommendations

- **From:** Dutch Owen <dutch@sysun.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<QL3L4.7683$cZ.14629@typhoon.southeast.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<OVGJ4.11701$x62.59006@typhoon.southeast.rr.com> <38fb6d5a.21135102@news.epix.net>`

```
Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com> wrote:

> I suspect that you will get a number of different recommendations from
> a variety of people in this group, but preserving your business logic
> and replacing the front end is indeed a wonderful idea.

  There are too many replies to answer them all, but I'd like to
thank everyone who responded to my query.

  To answer a few questions that were asked, our application was
designed from the ground up to separate user interface, database
access, and business logic.  This principle has eroded a bit in
recently written programs by new programmers, but generally all
I/O whether database or screen is in copybooks.  This will ease the
conversion greatly.

  I got several offers to help us convert or to do the conversion
for us -- that's not what we are looking for at all, we *are*
consultants, and can convert it ourselves, I'm just looking for 
the best target platform to shoot for, and hoping to avoid false
starts.

  After considering our options and all advice, we are going to
do an experimental move of a small portion of the app to MF Net
Express using the built-in GUI tools, and if that turns out to
be inadequate we'll consider using something like SP2 to help.
One of the reasons for this decision is that many of the staff
members have experience with MF cobol.


  Thanks again for all your suggestions,
         Dutch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: AS/400 to Semi-GUI recommendations

- **From:** jets@nbnet.nb.ca (Tony M. Mina)
- **Date:** 2000-04-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38fd1342.44957930@allnews.nbnet.nb.ca>`
- **References:** `<OVGJ4.11701$x62.59006@typhoon.southeast.rr.com> <38fb6d5a.21135102@news.epix.net> <QL3L4.7683$cZ.14629@typhoon.southeast.rr.com>`

```
On Tue, 18 Apr 2000 20:41:20 GMT, Dutch Owen <dutch@sysun.com> wrote:

>Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com> wrote:
>
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>
You can still use MF Cobol with SP2.  We were on the same situation when
we converted our systems to GUI.  We were using MF Workbench that time.
Instead of going to NET Express we went with SP2 for the reason that it
supports different compilers.  I don't  know with Net Express if you can
move the codes to another compiler.  In our case when we moved to
Fujitsu there were only minor changes to the programs.  Mostly are
syntax related.  No changes to the SP2 codes were made. 


>  Thanks again for all your suggestions,
>         Dutch

Tony M. Mina
```

The best place to get a free compiler or to learn how to write COBOL
code is to buy Thane Hubbell's book, "Teach Yourself COBOL In 24
Hours!"

It is not only a great book, but also incudes 16 and 32 bit versions
of the Fujitsu COBOL compiler.  The ISBN number of Thane's book is:

0-672-31453-3

## Thread: intelligent reply only — 5 msgs

### intelligent reply only

- **From:** pennybridge_pioneer@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-04-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8d62qm$2ug$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I am looking for someone knowledgable to help me decompile command
interpeter, ie command.com.  I wish to translate it from assembly to
machine language to readable ascii to be able to read the information
contained within.  If you think this can be done and you are someone
who can help me invent a decompiler for this purpose or if you have any
questions mail me at <mailto: fenlxxtx@aol.com>


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: intelligent reply only

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-04-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8d6o5l$ahc$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<8d62qm$2ug$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
This can be done using any existing 8086 disassembler/debugger. (You
wouldn't use a decompiler as the code is NOT compiled, it is assembled.)

Try CODEVIEW which is the standard PC assembler deugging tool.

If you REALLY want to have an ASCII version of the machine language (and I
can't imagine why you would - how meaningful are several hundred thousand
ones and zeros?) then you could write a simple program to read the
command.com file byte by byte (use byte stream IO if you decide to do this
with COBOL) and simply transform each 8 bit "packet" into ASCII using a
simple table lookup of the 255 possibilities, or an intrinsic function which
converts a byte to a string, if your compiler supports this.

If you want an "intelligent reply" you must ask an "intelligent question".

You're a loony, right?

Pete.




pennybridge_pioneer@my-deja.com wrote in message

>interpeter, ie command.com.  I wish to translate it from assembly to
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

#### ↳ Re: intelligent reply only

- **From:** "Dana Repouille" <drepouille@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jYMJ4.3501$T4.9302@news1.rdc1.ne.home.com>`
- **References:** `<8d62qm$2ug$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

<pennybridge_pioneer@my-deja.com> wrote in message
news:8d62qm$2ug$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> I am looking for someone knowledgable to help me decompile command
> interpeter, ie command.com.

debug c:\command.com
-u
11BA:0100 06            PUSH    ES
11BA:0101 17            POP     SS
11BA:0102 BE1B02        MOV     SI,021B
11BA:0105 BF1B01        MOV     DI,011B
11BA:0108 8BCE          MOV     CX,SI
11BA:010A F7D9          NEG     CX
11BA:010C FC            CLD
11BA:010D B81B01        MOV     AX,011B
11BA:0110 06            PUSH    ES
11BA:0111 50            PUSH    AX
11BA:0112 06            PUSH    ES
11BA:0113 B81801        MOV     AX,0118
11BA:0116 50            PUSH    AX
11BA:0117 CB            RETF
11BA:0118 F3            REPZ
11BA:0119 A4            MOVSB
11BA:011A CB            RETF
11BA:011B E93221        JMP     2250
11BA:011E 7AC9          JPE     00E9
-
Keep pressing U to continue with the disassembly.
For help with debug, enter a ?
-?
assemble     A [address]
compare      C range address
dump         D [range]
enter        E address [list]
fill         F range list
go           G [=address] [addresses]
hex          H value1 value2
input        I port
load         L [address] [drive] [firstsector] [number]
move         M range address
name         N [pathname] [arglist]
output       O port byte
proceed      P [=address] [number]
quit         Q
register     R [register]
search       S range list
trace        T [=address] [value]
unassemble   U [range]
write        W [address] [drive] [firstsector] [number]
allocate expanded memory        XA [#pages]
deallocate expanded memory      XD [handle]
map expanded memory pages       XM [Lpage] [Ppage] [handle]
display expanded memory status  XS
-
```

Hiya DD,

I see you are still working that same old job -- requiring Dollars and Sense
info from borkers trolling for resumes.  I thought the request in French was
from a student looking for some COBOL 85 programming advice and besides, it
gave me an opportunity to renew my acquaintance with the AltaVista babelfish
language translation facilities.  It is really a hoot to translate English
into French, and then have AltaVista translate the French back into English.
It is really a practical demonstration of the familiar phrase "It loses
something in the translation."

CCCO

In article <e1alELoq$GA.306@cpmsnbbsa04>,
dennis_minette <dennis_minette@email.msn.com> wrote:
>Hiya DD,
>
>I see you are still working that same old job -- requiring Dollars and Sense
>info from borkers trolling for resumes.

'Tis not an easy task, me lad, but *someone's* needs ta be a-doin' it!

>I thought the request in French was
>from a student looking for some COBOL 85 programming advice

[snip]

Well, no wonder I got it wrong... I thought it was in German and was
simply requesting that a COBOL programmer send the author an email.  Since
the request was for email from a practioner of a particular Art I
concluded that the author needed to have some Art... practised; as
practising the Art of COBOL-coding is a COBOL-coder's job this is,
reasonably enough, a request for a COBOL-programmer to do a job... and one
does one's job for a rate, or range of rates.

Then again... I could be wrong and the author might only have wanted a
COBOL coder's opinion on the recent IMF conference in Washington,
DC... but I have been wrong before.

DD

>
> george leventakis <george.leventakis@chello.be> wrote in message

A word from your friendly NON-moderator.

There is NO "official" language of this NG.  HOWEVER, there are more readers
and contributors who speak English (well some American - both US and
Canadian, some British, and even an occasional Australian or New Zealander)
either as their 1st or 2nd language.  Therefore, you (any reader/poster) is
MOST likely to get a quicker and better reply if you post in English.  On the
other hand, I have not seen a post in ANY language (other than English) that
hasn't received at least a few replies.  (I don't remember seeing many
non-Latin alphabets, e.g. Japanese or Russian - but I do think we have even
had a few of those.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ADMIN - "Languare" of the NG (was: CHERCHE UN PROGRAMMEUR COBOL 85

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8e28oi$hp5$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<nw2K4.8642$BO2.166602@news.chello.be> <cqaK4.17091$0o4.116562@iad-read.news.verio.net> <e1alELoq$GA.306@cpmsnbbsa04> <AkDL4.17479$0o4.120723@iad-read.news.verio.net> <jhYL4.11720$BO2.218726@news.chello.be> <VF6M4.586$PP5.6403@news.swbell.net> <zIoM4.12475$BO2.231246@news.chello.be> <tDpM4.2827$4O2.23439@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <8dt9gu$bpt$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <390448B0.BE1A5006@cusys.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:390448B0.BE1A5006@cusys.edu...
> "William M. Klein" wrote:
> >
> > There is NO "official" language of this NG.
>
> Does that mean we can speak FORTRAN, BASIC, & C on this newsgroup?

ACTUALLY,
  The NG "charter" is explicit that you MAY talk about other computer
languages - but you are supposed to avoid "language wars".  Having said that,

1) As we in the NG found out a few years ago, the "charter" is worth its
weight in "bits", i.e. there is nothing to say that it should be current - or
changed if not current

2) I would say that your chance of getting EITHER useful information on
questions that are STRICTLY related to those other languages is minimal.
However, we do get medium frequent questions that are "limited" to JCL (for
which the "L" stands for language) and other questions about "how do you say
this COBOL thing in C" (or vice versa).
```

"men det bruger jeg ikke her" = "but I do not use it here"
"S� Leif, nu skal vi s'gu pr�ve deres overs�ttere" = "Well, Leif, now we'll
#@! test their translaters"

Even if it's allowed to use any language here, I feel that English is best
for most of us. That's why I translated our Danish joke - it's my way of
being polite. However, if English is difficult for someone, we have our
translaters.

<a id="t-af9e9fe09a"></a>
## Thread: COBOL Parsers — 7 msgs

### COBOL Parsers

- **From:** "Mark Rickan" <mrickan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.compilers
- **Message-ID:** `<00-04-120@comp.compilers>`

```
Does anyone have any insights/experience on options for parsing COBOL?
I am working on a project where we will need to extract data file
declarations and access these files using other applications using
multiplatform C/C++.

So far we've considered the CobolTransformer Toolkit from Siber
Systems (www.siber.org), the COBOL parser from CocoLab
(http://www.cocolab.de/) or rolling or own using Coco/R
(http://cs.ru.ac.za/homes/cspt/cocor.htm), Visual Parse++ from
Sandstone (www.sand-stone.com).

Any help would be greatly appreciated.

Mark
[There's some stuff in the FAQ.  The grammar for Cobol isn't particularly
complex, but it's very large with an enormous number of different tokens.
-John]
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Parsers

- **From:** Vadim Maslov <vadik@siber.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.compilers
- **Message-ID:** `<00-04-129@comp.compilers>`
- **References:** `<00-04-120@comp.compilers>`

```
Mark Rickan wrote:
>
> Does anyone have any insights/experience on options for parsing COBOL?
> I am working on a project where we will need to extract data file
> declarations and access these files using other applications using
> multiplatform C/C++.

In fact, grammar of Cobol is fairly tricky at times.

Example: Cobol grammar is non LALR(1), that is it requires lookaheads
of more than one.

Example: to distinguish between 2 forms of PERFORM statement
PERFORM A OF B TIMES COMPUTE X=Y+Z END-PERFORM and
PERFORM A OF B COMPUTE X=Y+Z
we need a lookahead of 4 tokens.

In the 1st form A OF B is data item name
that contains conuter for PERFORM ... TIMES ... END-PERFORM stmt.
In the 2nd form A OF B is paragraph name performed
by PERFORM statement.

It probably can be made with some heavy symbol-table-based trickery,
but it does not really work here, because is some dialects A OF B can
be both paragraph name and data item name (2 diffrent names can have
the same name!).

So really doing your own Cobol grammar -- the one that works -- is too
expensive, as there are many pitfalls on the way.  It tooks us 3 years
to get it right, which I would not qualify as easy.

Vadim Maslov
Siber Systems
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Parsers

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 2000-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.compilers
- **Message-ID:** `<00-04-145@comp.compilers>`
- **References:** `<00-04-120@comp.compilers> <00-04-129@comp.compilers>`

```
Vadim is quite correct. To parse the Cobol 85 nucleus alone, I had to
put many hacks into my code to help the standard tools along.

You can have a look at the details in my pre-pre-alpha Cobol compiler
if you are interested at
http://www.geocities.com/timjosling/cobol.html .

The file that has the description of the hacks is
gcc/cobol/cobctok.def. The grammar is in the same directory cobcprs.y

Similar problems exist in other parts of the language. It is not
actually rocket science but it does make it hard to provide good error
messages.

Tim Josling

> In fact, grammar of Cobol is fairly tricky at times.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> we need a lookahead of 4 tokens.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Parsers

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-04-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.compilers
- **Message-ID:** `<00-04-158@comp.compilers>`
- **References:** `<00-04-120@comp.compilers> <00-04-129@comp.compilers> <00-04-145@comp.compilers>`

```
On 20 Apr 2000 01:36:57 -0400, Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au> wrote:
>Similar problems exist in other parts of the language. It is not
>actually rocket science but it does make it hard to provide good error
>messages.

Speaking of error messages, I got an interesting one from the Fujitsu
v5 compiler the other day.  Had me laughing.

What it meant to say was "Required file is missing".  But what it said
(and remember these messages were translated directly from Japanese
into english):

A file that does not exist was found.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Parsers

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-04-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.compilers
- **Message-ID:** `<00-04-124@comp.compilers>`
- **References:** `<00-04-120@comp.compilers>`

```
Mark Rickan wrote:
>
> Does anyone have any insights/experience on options for parsing COBOL?
> I am working on a project where we will need to extract data file
> declarations and access these files using other applications using
> multiplatform C/C++.

I have one that handles the data division pretty well completely, in
cobol.  I creates a cobol array of the definitions so that you can use
the information gained.  It is freeware, you can use it but any
modifications must come back to the original source.  Bug reports
accepted and corrected as well.

Compiles under Fujitsu V3 (available from www.adtools.com)

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Parsers

- **From:** "John H. Lindsay" <eil@kingston.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.compilers
- **Message-ID:** `<00-04-130@comp.compilers>`
- **References:** `<00-04-120@comp.compilers>`

```
Hi folks:

This isn't really a compiler problem, but a simple text-processing
one.  Do have a look at the language SNOBOL 4, especially with any of
the Spitbol compilers.  See http://www.Snobol4.com .  Don't laugh if
you thought SNOBOL4 died; it's alive and well and doing the same sort
of amazing things (string processing, pattern recognition, data
structures ...) as always, especially one-off and special purpose jobs
like this one, although some amazing things have been done with it
like a compiler for a PL/I-like system language in ~700 lines!
There's a mailing list available via the above web site if you need
starters.

Mark Rickan wrote:
> Does anyone have any insights/experience on options for parsing COBOL?
> I am working on a project where we will need to extract data file
> declarations and access these files using other applications using
> multiplatform C/C++.
```

- **From:** isaac kattan <i.kattan@liant.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sfmbg6d45c2104@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<00-04-120@comp.compilers>`

```
mark,
If you are using Micro Focus or RM Cobol you can use a product called 
Relativity.  It makes your COBOL application appear to be a standard ODBC 
data source to your other applications like Crystal reports or MS Access 
etc... You can e-mail me at I.kattan@liant.com if you have any questions.
Thanks
Isaac

<a id="t-8a7363dc22"></a>
## Thread: MF COBOL on HPUX: large files — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MF COBOL on HPUX: large files

- **From:** "Michael C. Kasten" <mck9@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38F86B57.6037@swbell.net>`

```
I have a question for MicroFocus mavens.

According to the MicroFocus release notes on our HPUX 10.20 box, 
MF COBOL cannot access files larger than 2 GB.  (There are also
operating system issues surrounding the use of large files, but those 
are not my concern here.)  The recommended workaround is to replace the 
usual COBOL IO verbs with calls to C routines that are enabled for large 
files.

Fortunately this solution is reasonably doable for us, since we only
use COBOL for line sequential (i.e. text) files.  Indexed files or
variable-length record sequential files would entail a great deal 
more pain.

On the other hand we'd rather avoid this step if possible.

Soon we will upgrade to HPUX 11.x, which is much friendlier to large
files.

The question: is MicroFocus COBOL limited to file sizes of 2 GB or 
less, even under HPUX 11.x?  The release notes available to me are not
very explicit on this point.

Michael C. Kasten	mck9@swbell.net
http://home.swbell.net/mck9/cobol/cobol.html
```

#### ↳ Re: MF COBOL on HPUX: large files

- **From:** "Gazaloo" <gaz@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<XT7K4.22606$k5.469529@news1.frmt1.sfba.home.com>`
- **References:** `<38F86B57.6037@swbell.net>`

```
Michael C. Kasten" <mck9@swbell.net> wrote in message
news:38F86B57.6037@swbell.net...

> The question: is MicroFocus COBOL limited to file sizes of 2 GB or
> less, even under HPUX 11.x?  The release notes available to me are not
> very explicit on this point.

either get the latest Object COBOL Developer Suite (V4.1.30) or the new
Server Express product. both run under HPUX 11. OCDS provides support for
larger files via a proprietary striping technology. SX provides striping AND
native support for very large files.
```

#### ↳ Re: Enough already about comp.compilers

- **From:** johnl@iecc.com (John R Levine)
- **Date:** 2000-04-16T00:00:00

- **References:** `<8dcioe$seg$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`

```
>I hold documentation showing Mr. Levine as a abusive and arrogant person, so
>I refuse to support his business.

Aw, come on.

I'm the moderator for comp.compilers, and have been for close to 15
years.  I do it in my spare time as a service to the usenet community.
I have no idea what "business" Mr. Tanding's not supporting.

I have no idea what he thinks is abusive and arrogant, but at this
point, I don't see any point in beating this particular dead horse.
As best I can guess, it was the auto-ack sent in response to every
submitted comp.compilers message.  If you want to see what it said,
send a message containing "test" (so I know not to post it) to
compilers@iecc.com, the standard submission address for
comp.compilers.

Messages related to compiling Cobol have always been welcome in
comp.compilers.  It's never been a really hot topic, but if you search
through the archives, you'll find a few messages every year asking
about Cobol parsers or other compiler tools.  See
http://www.iecc.com/compilers for archive, FAQ, etc.  Also, please
check any introductory Internet book (I've written a few) for info on
how moderated newsgroups work.
```

Ib Tanding wrote in message <8dcioe$seg$1@news.inet.tele.dk>...
>Please note, that I will not support comp.compilers. Anyone who put a

will
>get no answer from me.

so

…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
For those who are unaware of who Mr. Levine is, John R. Levine
is the moderator for the newsgroup, < news:comp.compilers >. He is
the co-author (along with Tony Mason and Doug Brown) of "lex and yacc",
O'Reilly & Associates, Inc., 1990, 1992. 'lex' and 'yacc' are compiler
construction tools.

[He is also the co-author (along with Carol Baroudi and Margy Levine
Young) of "The Internet for Dummies, 6th Edition", IDG Books, 1999.
Carol Baroudi is the author of "Mastering COBOL", Sybex, 1999.]

Mr. Tanding seem to be stating that < news:comp.compilers > is "owned"
by Mr. Levine. This is not correct. As a moderated newsgroup, all posts to
< news:comp.compilers > are routed to < compilers@iecc.com > where
they are reviewed and commented on by Mr. Levine before posting to the
newsgroup. The exact procedures are stated in the FAQ that is available
through < www.faqs.org >.

It seems to me that, if Mr. Tanding has any complaints about Mr. Levine's
moderation of < news:comp.compilers >, those complaints should be
addressed in that forum rather than < news:comp.lang.cobol >. Should
Mr. Tanding feel uncomfortable sending his complaints to the newsgroup,
through Mr. Levine, there are other newsgroups available for posting and
discussing such complaints.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

```

Charles F Hankel wrote in message ...
>I noticed that Rick Smith as ricksmith@aiservices.com said...
>>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>attention of the group?

I would suggest a post to, or cross-post including,
< news:news.admin.net-abuse.misc >. The post should contain a
statement of the problem clearly detailing the alleged abuse with a
request for assistance in eliminating that alleged abuse. It should
avoid any opinions about the character of the alleged abuser.

There is no guarantee that such a post will eliminate the alleged
abuse; but what may happen is that the "elders" in the news admin
group may "pressure" the moderator(s) to post a fair statement of
the alleged abuse to the relevant group.

Everything else is dependent upon the statement.

If the regulars in the newsgroup agree with the accuser, the
moderator(s) may be "pressured" to allow such posts or resign their
position. If the regulars agree with the moderator(s), the accuser
may be "pressured" to not attempt such posts in the future.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: About comp.compilers

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>

Rick,

In fact, if you look into it, the moderator does effectively own
the newsgroup. They can change the charter, refuse to accept
postings for any or no reason, and can't be removed by any
democratic means. See
http://www.eyrie.org/~eagle/faqs/mod-pitfalls.html for the
details.

Of course, as moderator he can prevent posting of any material
critical of himself, so posting to that newsgroup, as suggested
below, may be of limited value.

Tim Josling

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: About comp.compilers

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ddu4t$2h6$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<8dcioe$seg$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <8dcmpe$1vdi$1@news.hitter.net> <38FA2DF7.7B524FC0@melbpc.org.au>`

```

Tim Josling wrote in message <38FA2DF7.7B524FC0@melbpc.org.au>...
[...]
>In fact, if you look into it, the moderator does effectively own
>the newsgroup. They can change the charter, refuse to accept
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>details.

A newsgroup with one moderator "controls" access to the
newsgroup and, therefore, may "effectively own ...".

However, a newsgroup with several moderators "controls"
access to the newsgroup; but cannot "effectively own ..." unless
they consort.

Moderators can be removed democratically or by fiat.

Democratically, this may be accomplished by a discussion and
Call For Vote (CFV) that is administered by usenet. Should a
different moderator be chosen, the previous moderator is out.

Should a moderator attempt to prevent a CFV or control the
discussion by refusing to post negative comments, usenet may
instruct ISPs to "unmoderate" the group. This allows the CFV
and discussions to take place without interference. Once the
vote is taken, the group can be "re-moderated" based upon the
vote. I do not know if the foregoing has ever been necessary; but
it is feasible.
------------------
Rick Smith

[...]
>Rick Smith wrote:
[...]
>> It seems to me that, if Mr. Tanding has any complaints about Mr. Levine's
>> moderation of < news:comp.compilers >, those complaints should be
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>> discussing such complaints.
```

```
Rick,

I agree this is how it should be, but it just aint so. You cannot
remove a moderator who does not want to go, other than by
shutting down the newsgroup, which usually noone wants to do.
There have been many long discssions about this is the
appropriate forums. The bottom line is that the powers that be
will not accept a proposal for unmoderation that is opposed by
the moderator. And the charter is not binding in any effective
way.

I would also like to clarify - I personally have no complaints
about how comp.compilers is moderated - he does a good job. But I
do not support changing an existing group to moderated status,
because it can lead to suppression of views. Try and criticise
feminism in soc.feminism for example - even though the original
charter when it was moderated specifically said debate was
allowed (the moderators later changed the charter without a
usenet vote).

Tim Josling

Rick Smith wrote:
>
> Moderators can be removed democratically or by fiat.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Rick Smith
```

In article <_ctL4.3395$nH2.29573@news.swbell.net>,
Jerry P <bismail@bisusa.com> wrote:
>One of the regulations governing newsgroups is that a thread may not
>be 'killed' until someone mentions Hitler. Oops! I just did. Sorry.

Almost... but not quite.

<http://www.netmeg.net/jargon/terms/g/godwin_s_law.html>

--begin quoted text:

Godwin's Law     prov.     [Usenet] "As a Usenet discussion grows longer,
the probability of a comparison involving Nazis or Hitler approaches
one." There is a tradition in many groups that, once this occurs, that
thread is over, and whoever mentioned the Nazis has automatically lost
whatever argument was in progress. Godwin's Law thus practically
guarantees the existence of an upper bound on thread length in those
groups. 

--end quoted text

DD

>
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

##### ↳ ↳ Re: About comp.compilers

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>

If you look through the thread, you will get all the answers, you need
except for the very abusive way Mr. Levine adressed me, an how the conflict
started.

I have read Mr. Levines entry, and for the first time I agree with him -
this thread shouldn't grow much longer. However, I will correct a few
details.

I have *never* intended to put an entry to comp.compilers. I responded to a
question in this NG (comp.lang.cobol), but the posting I responded to was
crossposted. The rules in comp.compilers and in comp.lang.cobol are
different - obviously my response was too short or the question too  long.

Mr. Levine adressed me as a moderator for comp.compilers. An automated
process showed that my response was less than a certain percentage of the
question, and I should do something about it.

My posting was visible on comp.lang.cobol, and I didn't care about the
restrictions in comp.compilers - a subject with two different views, ending
with the removal of my posting.

The result of the removal was that my posting to comp.lang.cobol was removed
as well. That is why I don't want to spend my time upon crossposted entries.
If you send me a crossposted message, the moderator on the other NG can
delete it from *both* NG's - that is the nature of crossposting.

I have *no* intention at all to start anything against Mr. Levine nor to ask
anyone to act different to comp.compilers. My statement was purely to tell
everyone that *I* will not respond to crossposted messages unless the rules
for postings are similar on the NG's in the crossposting.

Please understand that I want to help anyone, who needs help to understand
and work with Cobol. I like the discussions in comp.lang.cobol. I have a
full time job and I spend some time with family and friends. I have learned,
that crossposted messages can be waste of time, and that your postings can
be deleted - I could have helped 10-15 friends instead.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: HELP WITH VALUE OF KEY

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 2000-04-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8de5sn$9d4$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<rP7K4.31098$2D6.947171@news20.bellglobal.com> <ec2dmr9p$GA.238@cpmsnbbsa03>`

```
    For heavens sake - the Mf'ers amoung us know that he is talking about
MF cobol, and the arbitrary values assigned to the various function keys
within a particular system subroutine.

    The values 53 and 54 refer to values assigned to user function keys
pageup
and pagedown.  Up and down arrow are NOT (normally) user funtion keys,
but are instead ADIS funtion keys.

    I handle this by using the program keybcf to assign user function key
values to the keystrokes normally NOT a user key.  There are 127 potential
user function keys, but only 00 thru 56 and 65 thru 98 are defined by
default.

    If you do not have the documentation, you are at a severe disadvantage.
All of these things are in the manual, if you dig.

******************************************************************
ChrisOsborne <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:ec2dmr9p$GA.238@cpmsnbbsa03...
> If you're on a PC, try Start\Programs\Accessories\System Tools\Character
> Map.  This little app will help you find the value.
>
> If you have an IDE (any, not just COBOL), and you're on a PC, check the
help
> file.  VB, at least, has help entries for the ASCII character set.
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: HELP WITH VALUE OF KEY

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 2000-04-17T00:00:00

```
You are right, but MF has a lot of products, and some of them are OEM's. I
wouldn't start a discussion about ADIS an KEYBCF before knowing the
platform.

I can just add, that whenever the ADIS is changed, you will get a file
called ADISCTRL, and when you change KEYBCF you get a file called COBKEYMP.
The files must be present if you wille take advantage of the changed
user-keys and function-keys. Removing the files wil bring you back to
default.

Search for ADIS and KEYBCF.

Regards
Ib
Russell Styles skrev i meddelelsen
<8de5sn$9d4$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com>...

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: HELP WITH VALUE OF KEY

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 2000-04-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8dgat2$qo9$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<rP7K4.31098$2D6.947171@news20.bellglobal.com> <ec2dmr9p$GA.238@cpmsnbbsa03> <8de5sn$9d4$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <8defeh$q7u$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <xTHK4.13483$fV.1040109@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
ADIS is Micro Focus' AcceptDISplay implementation. It hold the rules for
accept and display. You are able to change the rules by configuring ADIS.
When you do that, ADIS puts your new rules in a file, named ADICTRL. You can
copy that file to all future projects and the Accept/Display behaviour will
be the way you have decided. If the file is not present, your Accept/Display
will turn back to default. Look up ADIS in your documentation.

On your keyboard you have about 100 keys. Most of them are letters and
digits, but some of them are function keys. Keys like F1-F12, PgUp, Home,
Cursor keys etc. etc. Micro Focus gives you a predefined keyboard
configuration. The configuration is a list of the keys with the a code
telling what kind of key that is. You can change that configuration with the
program called KEYBCF (KEYBoard ConFiguration). Changing the keyboard
configuration will give you a file, called COBKEYMP. You can use it in all
future applications. Look for KEYBCF in your documentation.

Regards
Ib

Warren Simmons skrev i meddelelsen ...
>
>"Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk> wrote in message
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

Ib is right - MF does have a lot of products.  I was assuming that you are
using a product
that is compatable with the DOS/windows/unix (mostly) products.

ADIS - system program that handles screen (text mode) in recent products.
KEYBCF - program to edit function key list.
COBKEYMP - data file that stores results of KEYBCF.


    Direct answer - it is not possible to trap up/down arrow as a user
function
key.  But they are available as ADIS function keys.

    It all depends on which type of accept you are using.  Try asking the
program, as
one of the other people suggested.
```

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>

```

place a display in the code and see what values the keys return.  It is
about ten thousand times faster to ask the program than to ask the
newsgroup.

<a id="t-cb134a4545"></a>
## Thread: Multiple Topics - One message -- CA-World discussed too! — 149 this month / 187 total

### Multiple Topics - One message -- CA-World discussed too!

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-04-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38fb651c.23035054@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
Time for some reflection on current and recently hot topics.

First, to quote a Michael Kelly Blanchard song...."The view out the
window is just a piece of the sky."....

As we discuss the issues of mainframe vs PC, and relationional DB vs
Indexed files and OO vs conventional structured .. we need to remember
that what we see of the COBOL and "Data procesing" (to use an aged
term) world is not nearly the entire big picture.

I had the extreme pleasure (Thanks Bob) of accompanying Bob Wolfe of
Flexus in his exhibitor booth at CA World.  If you don't know know
what CA-World is let me explain.  CA is a giant company (Computer
Associates) that has grown over the years (mostly by acquisition) to
service the IT industry.  Their products (many of which are the best
of class) are mainly geared toward large mainframe shops.  Once a year
they have a big convention where they conduct training and have a big
exhibition of new and old software products.  Non CA Vendors are
invited to give Educational sessions and to show off their wares on
the showroom floor.  Bob was nice enough to ask me to come this year
and help him out with the pre-concerence education sessions he was
giving and to help man the booth.  While CA provides a lot of food,
drink and entertainment - let me tell you, this was no walk in the
park.  These marketing guys earn their keep.  This is hard labor.

Bob's pre conference education topic was "Taking your COBOL
application to the Internet".  He intentionally did not use "Web"
because the "web" is not the internet, and the internet is not solely
the web.  What we learned in research and preparation is that there is
no one solution for this issue.  We also learned that many IT
departments are told to get there, when there may be no realy
identifiable need to be there.  However, we discussed all the tools we
could find to do this.  From IBM's CICS gateway, to CA-OPAL, to Flexus
Sp2 and Thin Client.  We touched on multi-tiered architectures and
direct emulation/connection from the mainframe (or whatever the host
platform happened to be).  We learned from our classes as much as they
learned from us.  As a presenter (sp) Bob got high marks from the
attendees.

I met a few "old friends" for the first time there.  Dave Stuckman
(sp, sorry Dave), Kevin Hansen, Ziggy, and Jeff Friedman (Sorry if I
mispelled that Jeff).  I can't tell you what a pleasure it was to meet
these people in the flesh.  Jeff and I discussed the standards and the
standards process.  I really enjoyed chatting with him.  With others
from CA, I discussed OO COBOL and the direction the standard was
taking.  I learned much from their views and the views of the
customers.  My one regret in that area was not being able to stick
around for the IBM J4 rep's talk on the future of COBOL.   I got to
take a look at GUI Screenio and had a nice talk with Kevin from
Norcom.  It was a great trip.

My observations - in as much of a nutshell as I can put them - boil
down to the following:

1. The mainframe is alive and well.  Especially in the international
arena.  CA-World had the most international flavor of any conference
of any kind that I have ever attended.  

2.  COBOL is alive and well.  It's usage in new development on the
mainframe is still vast.  It is far from abandoned.  COBOL is easy to
learn and to become productive in.  It is an ideal fit for business.

3.  The internet is where everyone is going -- and no one is sure they
should be going there-- but they are.

4.  OO COBOL has not been widely accepted as good or necessary,
especially in the mainframe world.  Most COBOL vendors are looking to
see what problems Fujitsu has with their OO Implementation.  It seems
to be generally accepted that in the area of destructors they lead the
pack.  The fast tracking of this in a technical letter is probably the
best thing for the standard.  Most IBM mainframers don't use and don't
care about OO COBOL.  So why have it?  Well, I ran into one thing at
CA-World that led me to realize that it IS needed for the future of
the language.

A person came by, who was a JAVA programmer.  He said that while at
CA-World he had heard a lot about COBOL (Bob's sign says "COBOL SP2")
and that he wanted to know what it was.  He asked if I could show him
some COBOL Code.  So I did.  We went through a small example, and he
kind of understood, but he lacked interest.  Then I showed him some OO
COBOL Code.  That got his attention.  It was familiar.  He said "I
could code using this.".  We need OO to attract users of other
languages to COBOL.
 
That's enough rambling for now.  I'm going to go play with my new
Athlon processor!
```

#### ↳ Re: Multiple Topics - One message -- CA-World discussed too!

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vv1L4.3099$zG.2419679@news1.mco>`
- **References:** `<38fb651c.23035054@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
>
> 4.  OO COBOL has not been widely accepted as good or necessary,
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> languages to COBOL.

Good post, Thane, thanks. :-)  But I must take issue with your last
sentence above.  COBOL has always been successful because it was the
best language for programming the applications COBOL is good for.  If,
as you suggest, OO is a very 'iffy' thing in the COBOL arena (I agree),
then why add a host of expensive (in cost of developing standards,
implementing them and learning/teaching them) features of dubious value,
simply to attract programmers from other languages?  If somebody is
using a different language, they certainly aren't going to move to COBOL
because it offers them some of the same things they already have.  They
will come because it offers them something better in one or more ways.
As I have said until everybody is tired of hearing it, I am convinced
that one of, if not 'the', primary advantages of COBOL has been its
simplicity.  Adding OO to COBOL shoots that in the head first thing.
Why throw away your best advantage in exchange for dubious benefit?
When structured design and programming had been out as long as OO has,
the debate over whether it was good or not had been settled long before.
Is there a reason why, in a profession that enjoys learning and using
new tools, OO is still under serious debate after what, around twenty
years?  The advantages of structured techniques were clear and easy
to measure, in time, money and quality.  In fact, many people, such as
myself, actually studied structured techniques *because* of such clear
results from real world trials.  Many of us turned our programming
techniques upside-down to achieve such results, and I never met anyone
who wanted to turn back.  On the other hand, we hear of project after
project (not all, of course, but many) where OO turned out a disaster.
"Well, they just didn't do it right" we are told.  Well, why was it so
difficult to "do it right", I wonder?  My very first structured attempt
was clearly better, even though I made many mistakes.  Only a little
practice showed it *much* better.  Using OO can be expensive in many
ways.  Reason suggests that, to be worth it, the benefits must be
comparably great.   Yet it is still in serious dispute that there *are*
real benefits.  Not that the benefits are great, but that the balance
is actually on the OO side at all.  If the benefits are not great enough
to be easily and clearly demonstrated, repeatedly and consistently, why
are we pursuing such a clearly expensive approach with such abandon?
Especially in a language whose major forte' is simplicity?  It is my
strongly held opinion that we *must* begin making programming easier,
not more difficult, or we will before too long reach a crisis point
where we simply cannot develop reliable, effective systems quickly
enough to meet business needs.  I'm not suggesting a regression "to
the old ways."  I'm suggesting progression "in a different path", where
*the* primary goal of language and programming tool development is
ease of use and learning, while improving productivity.  Those goals
are not mutually exclusive.

There is a pattern in technological development that is often seen.
The first phase products of a new technology are simple, and inefficient.
The second phase products are much more complex, but much more efficient.
The final phase is the relative simplicity of a 'proper' design, but even
more efficiency.  This final phase results from a better understanding of
the principles and processes involved.  I believe we are in the second
phase of programming languages and development tools.  We need to move to
the final phase as soon as possible.  I seriously doubt that OO is what
is going to do it, but something must, and soon. :-)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Multiple Topics - One message -- CA-World discussed too!

- **From:** khansen@screenio.com (Kevin Hansen)
- **Date:** 2000-04-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38fca9ff.12100129@wingate>`
- **References:** `<38fb651c.23035054@news.cox-internet.com> <vv1L4.3099$zG.2419679@news1.mco>`

```
On Tue, 18 Apr 2000 13:07:03 -0500, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc@bellsouth.net> wrote:

(snipped long rant re OO COBOL)

Judson,

I must agree with you regarding OO.  Doug Miller posted a paper back
in October, 1996 entitled "The Programmer's New Clothes" which
addressed much of the hype about OO.  It was a great article which,
alas, I can't find in the archives anymore.  Someone should post it
again.

As you observed, most TRUE improvements in programming technique are
obvious.  Structured coding, for example, is easy to grasp and
provides obvious benefits, even to the beginner.  If it's OBVIOUSLY an
improvement, it must REALLY be an improvement.

OO, on the other hand, invents its own obscure terminology, and
imposes huge up-front design/documentation burdens which are not at
all simple or, let's face it, practical in the real world of "average"
programmers.  If you can't succinctly explain OO, how do you expect to
see it widely adopted?

Furthermore, one of the big OO "advantages" is reusability of code,
but we've been able to do that for decades via site standard
subroutine libraries.  If they're good enough, easy enough to use, and
well-documented (and have management that requires their use), they
standardize the handling of common programming problems.  In my 30
years or so of experience, even site standard libraries cease to be
used when the programming/management staff changes.  

If the problem being addressed by Joe Programmer is not that common,
he's just as apt to roll his own solution without even looking into
whether a routine exists in the site standard library.  An OO solution
would suffer the same problem.  

After all, OO is really nothing more than a formal way of providing a
standard subroutine library, isn't it?  The way you invoke/call and
manage the standard subroutine/method/class is really just a matter of
semantics.  

You're correct that we need something to improve the quality of COBOL
software, and I agree with you that OO COBOL is not necessarily it.
The real solution, when it is developed, will be obvious to the
"average" programmer and not to just a few who have the experience,
mental capacity, and time (Lots!) to figure out the OO maze.

IMHO, OO and subroutine libaries work only when they provide a clear
advantage, are the only practical way to accomplish a task, and are
supported by an independent third party.  GUI products or third-party
software libaries, for example, which includes the Windows API itself;
simply another subroutine library when you get down to it.  Sure, you
could roll your own GUI interface, but it's FAR easier to call the
Windows API to do the detail stuff.  

A true OO implementation doesn't really change anything, and more
likely makes it more difficult to use even if you embrace the whole
concept, which many don't.  If OO was so great, it would have caught
on like wildfire but we've been hearing about OO for at least ten
years and I doubt that any business application of any significant
size has yet been successfully using OO.  QED

(end of rant) My 2 cents worth...

Kevin
NORCOM COBOL Programming Tools
GUI ScreenIO; a cool COBOL/Windows Screen Manager
http://www.ScreenIO.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OO COBOL and Future of COBOL (was: Multiple Topics - One message -- CA-World discussed too!

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8dig0j$8e4$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<38fb651c.23035054@news.cox-internet.com> <vv1L4.3099$zG.2419679@news1.mco> <38fca9ff.12100129@wingate>`

```
As someone who is NOT totally convinced of what the eventual impact of OO
COBOL will be for the future of COBOL, I did want to state one thing (that I
think I have said previously in the NG).

When/If "vendors" (not compiler-vendors) start providing LOTS of "standard
class libraries" for "business" needs (as well as GUIs, etc) - and ASSUMING
these libraries can be used by Standard COBOL programs, then I think this
will impact the future of COBOL.

As stated below, "re-usability" is certainly a well-proven technique in
existing "modular" COBOL applications.  HOWEVER, so far, it has (primarily)
been limited to "in-house" subroutine libraries (as stated below). If
"sub-routine libraries" for "general purpose" (business and otherwise -
vertical type environments) are provided, I think this will be of benefit for
application development.  (Not the "path" that Pete Dashwood is talking
about - but a possible stepping-stone to it.)

I believe that MOST new "functional libraries" are being created for OO (and
non-COBOL) environments.  Given OO's "easy" ability to provide both
inheritance and polymorphism, such libraries may WELL provide a well
advantage to the future of COBOL - in an OO COBOL world.

HAVING SAID THIS, so far most of the OO class libraries that I have heard of
so far, are *not* easy to use from multiple OO languages (e.g JAVA, and C++,
and SmallTalk).  This was/is certainly the promise of such things as
"CORBA" - but I don't know if the promise is - or will be - realized.
```

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8dj4ha$404$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<38fb651c.23035054@news.cox-internet.com> <vv1L4.3099$zG.2419679@news1.mco> <38fca9ff.12100129@wingate> <8dig0j$8e4$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <8dimgc$9gj$1@news.cerf.net>`

```

Cheesle wrote in message <8dimgc$9gj$1@news.cerf.net>...
>"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
>news:8dig0j$8e4$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net...
>> When/If "vendors" (not compiler-vendors) start providing LOTS of
"standard
>> class libraries" for "business" needs (as well as GUIs, etc) - and
>ASSUMING
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>That is an interesting perspective, sure it sounds glorious, but, will it
be
>more than words? After all, who is more resistant to adopt vendor specific
>extensions than Cobol developers?
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Maybe the problem is that I am looking at it from the other side.  I have
systems where I am responsible for them working, and I have customers that
demand access to their data. Even when you have professionals playing about
with your data bases then you are in trouble, if you have a software
maintenance contract.

On the other hand, I can give them a DLL that allows them to use my system
but on my terms.  I have rigorously defined methods and properties that they
can play about with, but the critical parts them cannot even see. I could
hand you, for example, a truck scale object that simply gave you a
gross/tare/net, or higher level data like an invoice record. I want to give
you an object that I can guarantee, and I cannot do that with source code
libraries.  The second I give you access to my source, I cannot warrantee
the system. If I give you an object library, I can.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO COBOL and Future of COBOL (was: Multiple Topics - One message -- CA-World discussed too!

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<LokL4.2839$wk.1523563@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<38fb651c.23035054@news.cox-internet.com> <vv1L4.3099$zG.2419679@news1.mco> <38fca9ff.12100129@wingate> <8dig0j$8e4$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <8dimgc$9gj$1@news.cerf.net> <8dj4ha$404$1@news.igs.net>`

```
donald tees wrote:
>
> Maybe the problem is that I am looking at it from the other side.  I have
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> the system. If I give you an object library, I can.

Donald, you raise a good point.  Obviously, that is a big plus for OO,
in those particular applications.  Perhaps this is more like the (in my
opinion) ill advised headlong rush into *total GUI*.  GUI, and perhaps
OO, should be viewed as tools suited for certain things, but not for
every application conceivable.  People seem to want answers that are, in
reality, too simplistic.  We can look at the 'health food' trends to see
this.  For many years it was 'calories, calories, calories'.  Later it
was 'low sodium, low sodium', then 'low cholesterol, low cholesterol.
Recently it has been 'low fat, low fat'.  All to the virtual exclusion
of other factors.  But a healthy diet is based on a good balance, not
narrow attention to just one area, ignoring the rest.  I believe that
our industry would be much better served by a broader mindset about our
programming tools.  If we saw a carpenter and a plumber arguing about
which is the better tool, a hammer or a wrench, we would think it very
silly.  The problem, I think, is that we seek for a simplistic answer,
"GUI/character is best" or "OO/procedural is best", when the question
should be the more complex "Which is best *for this application?"  When
we dump all our character based interfaces for GUI, we throw the baby
out with the bath water.  Some applications are simply much better as
character based than GUI, and vice versa.  I believe that OO is just
not worth it (at least in its current forms) for the majority of (at
least many) applications, because the costs outweigh the advantages.
But like any tool, there are areas where OO can shine.  Perhaps we're
just "tool bigots". :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO COBOL and Future of COBOL (was: Multiple Topics - One message -- CA-World discussed too!

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** khansen@screenio.com (Kevin Hansen)
- **Date:** 2000-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38fdeddd.95009776@wingate>`
- **References:** `<38fb651c.23035054@news.cox-internet.com> <vv1L4.3099$zG.2419679@news1.mco> <38fca9ff.12100129@wingate> <8dig0j$8e4$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <8dimgc$9gj$1@news.cerf.net> <8dj4ha$404$1@news.igs.net> <LokL4.2839$wk.1523563@news1.mia>`

```
On Wed, 19 Apr 2000 10:37:10 -0500, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc@bellsouth.net> wrote:

> (snip)

>> On the other hand, I can give them a DLL that allows them to use my system
>> but on my terms.  I have rigorously defined methods and properties that they
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
>Judson McClendon      judmc@bellsouth.net

Judson, I can't agree more.  My general take is that OO undoubtedly
has its place, especially for things that are heavily used from a
variety of places and where encapsulation is possible and important.

OTOH, doing EVERYTHING using OO just doesn't make sense to me.  As far
as I can tell, the prevailing sentiment is that an OO implementation
is considerably more difficult than a procedural implementation.
Would an OO implementation make sense for a one-off?  

The other thing is, I am not convinced the comparison between
structured and OO is really legitimate, either.  Structured coding is
just a way of organizing your procedural code, while OO (which
internally has to be done with procedural code) is more of a system
design philosophy.

To my (admittedly traditional) way of thinking, OO is swell in theory,
but probably too difficult to implement broadly.  I, in my diehard
traditionalist (ignorant?) way, don't really see the true distinction
between an OO class and a well-documented and implemented subroutine
or subroutine library, other than the somewhat esoteric properties of
"inheritance" etc.  

I mean, after all, the big attraction is just building black boxes
that do certain things in clearly defined ways through a clearly
documented interface, right?  Does it really matter, or even make
sense, if you add the conceptual difficulty of OO to the mix?

Someone commented that some of us may be getting to old to grok OO (no
offense taken), and that may be part of it.  'Course, 10 years ago I
commented that (after a first reading of Petzold's book) I was waaaay
to old to learn to program Windows, and now I are a Windows
programmer.  Maybe that will happen with OO, too.

As far as the GUI/character interface issue, I don't really see any
big deal.  If the user wants GUI and is willing to buy our tool to
implement, why not?  Keeps me from digging ditches to keep bread on
the table.

Kevin
NORCOM COBOL Programming Tools
GUI ScreenIO; a cool COBOL/Windows Screen Manager
http://www.ScreenIO.com
```

- **References:** `<38fb651c.23035054@news.cox-internet.com> <vv1L4.3099$zG.2419679@news1.mco> <38fca9ff.12100129@wingate> <8dig0j$8e4$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <8dimgc$9gj$1@news.cerf.net> <8dj4ha$404$1@news.igs.net>`

```
"donald tees" <donald@willmack.com> wrote:

>On the other hand, I can give them a DLL that allows them to use my system
>but on my terms.  I have rigorously defined methods and properties that they
>can play about with, but the critical parts them cannot even see.

i dunno if oo was ever meant for data handling. lets face it, if you
fool around with data that has any connection to physical products or
income, it has to work and it has to be setup with holds, locks, and
exclusive properties *before* it even gets a handle on the data. i
suppose you could write a handler which takes as an input a FD /
copybook and hold parameters and outputs data, but in the end, it's
just cheaper to hire a professional with *some* computer skills.
despite oo's popularity, somewhere there has to be a programmer
telling the computer what to do in procedural code. however, that does
not mean to not have it modular. just be sure to remember that the
majority of code will never fit the oo paradigm.

reply to email gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com remove the spam
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO COBOL and Future of COBOL (was: Multiple Topics - One message -- CA-World discussed too!

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8e2o600ant@enews2.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<38fb651c.23035054@news.cox-internet.com> <vv1L4.3099$zG.2419679@news1.mco> <38fca9ff.12100129@wingate> <8dig0j$8e4$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <8dimgc$9gj$1@news.cerf.net> <8dj4ha$404$1@news.igs.net> <uqprfs4ebu3201qmej2ent9da0qhviaq3s@4ax.com> <8dntfh$p9v$1@news.igs.net> <ruk1gs48242jgui0lpqhljmrgot8uonr7s@4ax.com>`

```
I would like to make 3 points...

1) Code is nice, but the finished product is what's important.  No user
gives a damn what language it's programmed in.  People at my company can't
believe that our 16 year old database and largest application (a
Merchandising system at 1,800 DC/COBOL ADS/O, and batch programs) is based
almost totally on COBOL.  A good, well designed system will provide years of
service at a low cost and I hate to say it, but a good programmer can always
still find better ways to do things.  You are not a better programmer just
because you know all the cool tools.

2) There's nothing you can do in OO COBOL that you can't do in regular COBOL
II.  So stop crying.  It's no big deal.

3) Cut and Paste is reuse too.

Jeff

----------
