###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Error in Call-ed

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Michael C. Kasten" <mck9@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3904F0A4.1CC8@swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<38FABA1D.3F5C0C1E@personal.unicaja.es> <LnEK4.204187$Hq3.5094148@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <38FBB6E0.6E3@swbell.net> <8diioo$6le$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`

```
Ib Tanding wrote:
> 
> Making a low level module to be used as widely as you describe is a
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> handle the error.

It was exactly this approach which drove me to devise something better.

When I first joined a certain application group, they used a cycle
checking subprogram which, if it encountered an error, returned one of
a long series of error codes (and no message).  Almost every load 
module in the application included this subprogram.  Program after 
program contained hundreds of lines of tedious, repetitious code to 
translate error codes into the corresponding messages.  Sometimes this
code took up more space than all the rest of the PROCEDURE
DIVISION.

With a few exceptions it didn't really matter which error code turned 
up -- any kind of error required that we abort the job -- except that 
they needed different messages.

After I rewrote the subprogram to provide its own error messages, I
could quickly eliminate thousands of lines of code in the calling
programs.  The new messages were also more informative because they
could include details which were otherwise internal to the subprogram.

Here's another consideration: what happens if you change the internal
implementation of the subprogram?  For example, maybe you start using
a database instead of indexed files.  You're likely to find yourself 
with a different collection of error conditions -- but the calling 
programs only understand the old error conditions, and their messages 
are obsolete or worse.

My rule is: the module which detects an error should be the one to 
define the error message, because that's the module that knows what 
the error is.  How to deliver the message is another question, which 
different people answer in different ways.

Usually my subprograms provide different error codes for different
error conditions, just in case the calling program needs to treat 
different conditions differently.  In my experience, however, it is a 
rare program that cares which kind of error it gets, as long as it can 
deliver an appropriate message and shut down.  (That may not be true of 
interactive programs such as a GUI.  My experience is mostly in the 
batch world.)

Michael C. Kasten	mck9@swbell.net
http://home.swbell.net/mck9/cobol/cobol.html
```

- **References:** `<38FABA1D.3F5C0C1E@personal.unicaja.es> <LnEK4.204187$Hq3.5094148@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <38FBB6E0.6E3@swbell.net> <8diioo$6le$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <3904F0A4.1CC8@swbell.net> <39050DB5.30E0910C@home.com>`

```
"Michael C. Kasten" wrote:
>
> Usually my subprograms provide different error codes for different
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> batch world.)

I posted an example of a called program which accesses KSDS files.  I
haven't the foggiest idea why this program was built, but it was
included with our ERP.  At any rate, we call it with various codes, to
use the VSAM file.  It does the command and sets return codes.  I ran it
with the code "O" to open, and it returned a "9", saying that the open
failed.  Looking at the dump, I discovered that VSAM returned a "97"
which means "OPEN statement execution successful:  File integrity
verified.".  That program only checked for "00".

So the only thing that I can figure that this called program did is stop
me from finding out that the open worked correctly in an unforeseen
way.  My program DID care what kind of error it got.

And of course, database commands which convert to calls certainly DO
care what kind of return code they get.  Sometimes "record not found" is
an error, sometimes it is what we expect.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Error in Call-ed

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-04-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39087A2F.B6EBCBD6@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<38FABA1D.3F5C0C1E@personal.unicaja.es> <LnEK4.204187$Hq3.5094148@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <38FBB6E0.6E3@swbell.net> <8diioo$6le$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <3904F0A4.1CC8@swbell.net> <39050DB5.30E0910C@home.com> <390598F2.36E87F81@cusys.edu>`

```
I think that the sub-routine should have handled the 97 and passed zero
back to you unless they want to take special action because the 97 was
received.  Since the 97 means that VSAM detected the file wasn't closed
by the previous user but that VSAM was able to successfully close and
then open the file (implicit verify) this would be a policy
determination either by the user or the vendor.  The vendor of software
in my previous shop was not consistent in this regard.  If we got a
programmed termination due to the 97, we verified all files used in the
step and restarted. 

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services Inc., cfmtech@istar.ca

Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> "Michael C. Kasten" wrote:
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> an error, sometimes it is what we expect.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Error in Call-ed

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-04-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39088D20.F49D81FA@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<38FABA1D.3F5C0C1E@personal.unicaja.es> <LnEK4.204187$Hq3.5094148@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <38FBB6E0.6E3@swbell.net> <8diioo$6le$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <3904F0A4.1CC8@swbell.net> <39050DB5.30E0910C@home.com> <390598F2.36E87F81@cusys.edu> <39087A2F.B6EBCBD6@istar.ca>`

```
That would have been preferable to what happened.  But what the
sub-routine should have done was to pass back the status code and let ME
handle it however I wanted.  IDCAMS creates all these useful numbers,
and this code obfuscated them.  What is the advantage in making my
decision for me?  What is the advantage of making me have to look at the
dump to determine what happened?  Think of what would have happened
without a dump.  The job would have aborted, and I would restart and it
would work.  If I try to debug the rerun, I would see that everything
worked nicely.  But every once in a while the production job would abort
(depending on some other job ending poorly).

If a subprogram or an object throws an error condition - it should also
pass on all of the information which it had in determining that there
was an error.  I want to be able to override its decision.

"Clark F. Morris, Jr." wrote:
> 
> I think that the sub-routine should have handled the 97 and passed zero
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
> > an error, sometimes it is what we expect.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Error in Call-ed

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8eabkv$scn$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<38FABA1D.3F5C0C1E@personal.unicaja.es> <LnEK4.204187$Hq3.5094148@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <38FBB6E0.6E3@swbell.net> <8diioo$6le$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <3904F0A4.1CC8@swbell.net> <39050DB5.30E0910C@home.com> <390598F2.36E87F81@cusys.edu> <39087A2F.B6EBCBD6@istar.ca>`

```
FYI,
  The next Standard allows for "implementor defined SUCCESSFUL" file status
codes.  I sure hope that IBM move "97" to "0x" or something similar.  It
really is "successful" but the current Standard just doesn't support such a
construct.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Implementor defined successful was Re: Error in Call-ed

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-04-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3909BBB6.17622A11@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<38FABA1D.3F5C0C1E@personal.unicaja.es> <LnEK4.204187$Hq3.5094148@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <38FBB6E0.6E3@swbell.net> <8diioo$6le$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <3904F0A4.1CC8@swbell.net> <39050DB5.30E0910C@home.com> <390598F2.36E87F81@cusys.edu> <39087A2F.B6EBCBD6@istar.ca> <8eabkv$scn$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
This feature shows the problems with status codes.  In IBM COBOL 74, if
you opened a VSAM file I-O that had been defined and never opened, you
got a 9X status code.  With IBM COBOL 85 compilers, you get a status
code 35.  This obsoleted all of the code built into one vendor's I-O
subroutines that were checking for the specific code and if detected,
opening the file output, writing one record, closing it, opening it I-O,
deleting the record and maybe closing the file and reopening it.  I
don't know what the vendor has done for current releases.  In the case
of a status code 97, if IBM changes to using a 0x status code for the
same condition, a large amount of code will be broken. My method of
getting around the problem for any new code that I write is to move the
file status code to a common status code that has 88 levels defined for
all of the legal status values.  I have defined both 00 and 97 for
OPEN-SUCCESSFUL so that in these programs adding the new 0x code would
be simple.  

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services Inc.,
cfmtech@istar.ca   

"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> FYI,
…[49 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > an error, sometimes it is what we expect.
```

Frank Swarbrick wrote:
> 
> On Tue, 25 Apr 2000 07:09:06 -0600, Howard Brazee
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
> VERIFY feature is for?)

It happens when the previous application didn't close the file
correctly.  It is sort of like when you turn off your Windows PC instead
of shutting it down.  When you boot up again it does some testing they
your machine is up.  A code of 97 means the open was successful - but it
did test the file first.  The subprogram told my program "the open
failed".  But it didn't.
```

Hmm, interesting.  I know that if the program abends that COBOL will
still close the files.  I think it may even if someone cancels it
manually.  The only thing I can think of is if someone IPLs the
machine while the job is running.  What I'm trying to say is that I
can think of only a handful of our programs that check for status '97'
along with status '00'.  Not saying that that's good, only that it's
true.  I've never heard of any of the programs having a problem
because of it, though.  I will certainly be adding '97' to my
"successful" codes, though.  Thanks for the tip.  (Thane too.)

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Error in Call-ed

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-04-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3909D3A1.C3B9B7D0@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<38FABA1D.3F5C0C1E@personal.unicaja.es> <LnEK4.204187$Hq3.5094148@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <38FBB6E0.6E3@swbell.net> <8diioo$6le$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <3904F0A4.1CC8@swbell.net> <39050DB5.30E0910C@home.com>`

```
While I would have agreed with you 10 years ago, today I would have the
actual error message external to the program if the infrastructure for
doing so were there.  They would be stored in an external file that has
both message number and language as parts of the key.  This is for
internationalization.  While I benefit from the English (Ameriglish?)
language predominance in the computer field, it is arrogant on my part
to presume everybody speaks English.  Indeed I am amazed at the success
of COBOL in non-English speaking countries.  

I also note that there are both normal errors and catastrophic errors. 
The first group of errors are conditions that can be handled by the
caller at least sometimes (record or view not found on a read for
example).  The second are of the type where the application should quit
before digging the grave deeper (physical I-O error on file for
example).  In some cases, only the caller will have the logic to
determine the handling of the error.

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services Inc.,
cfmtech@istar.ca   
"James J. Gavan" wrote:
> 
> "Michael C. Kasten" wrote:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Error in Call-ed

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "mangogwr" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aI7O4.9036$A75.1887439@news1.mco>`
- **References:** `<38FABA1D.3F5C0C1E@personal.unicaja.es> <LnEK4.204187$Hq3.5094148@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <38FBB6E0.6E3@swbell.net> <8diioo$6le$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <3904F0A4.1CC8@swbell.net>`

```
Well, all good and nice after every single module is changed again.
EXCEPT now, still the module has THOUSANDS of lines of code (and probably
hundreds of thousands  of bytes of storage0 that is almots NEVER needed.

They way we 'enlightened True Professional Systems Designers'
do this is:

The subroutine returns ONLY data to a structure (if needed) and a SEPERATE
RETURN CODE.  If the RC is NOT OK (sometimes that's -1, sometimes 0)
THEN the CALLER (or the CALLED) invokes ANOTHER MODULE PASSING
either JUST the error/msg number or (depending upon installation size)
[APP-CODE],[PGM-NAME],ERROR-MSG-NO/RC-CODE

This NEW routine READS a READ-ONLY KEYED FILE and returns to caller
the MSG TXT.

THIS REMOVES DEPENDENCIES from between caller and called for
error-msg txt,
REMOVES ALL THAT CODE FROM MEMORY UNTIL NEEDED, then
ONLY a small program is loaded.

Michael C. Kasten <mck9@swbell.net> wrote in message
news:3904F0A4.1CC8@swbell.net...
> Ib Tanding wrote:
> >
> > Making a low level module to be used as widely as you describe is a
> > challenge. I really think that the module is a service to all the
different
> > platforms, and knowing that you will have to be restrictive.
> >
> > Produce the module and tell potential users that the module can be used.
You
> > tell them the contents of the request-structure, and you give them a
list of
> > returned values. In this discussion a list of return codes will be
relevant
> > (0=OK, 2=...., 4= ..... etc).
> >
> > The message is: "Dear user of my module, please give correct input, and
I
> > will supply you with a result. Make meaningfull error messages based
upon my
> > return code"!!!!
> >
> > That, I beleive, will give a clear cut on any platform, and the caller
can
> > handle the error.
>
…[41 more quoted lines elided]…

First of all - your modesty to use of memory is admirable. Please give the
idea to mr. Gates.
If I need 2K of memory to give the user a well designed program with
excellent error-handling, I wouldn't hesitate a second - not even if I had
to use 20K.

Making error-handling in submodules has been done for 30 years or more - no
big deal. However, making good and user-friendly applications with
understandable error messages is undoubtable a problem these years.

Remember, we are making programs for users - not to fulfill our own
technical ambitions.

Regards
Ib

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Error in Call-ed

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Michael C. Kasten" <mck9@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<390A3BC6.146C@swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<38FABA1D.3F5C0C1E@personal.unicaja.es> <LnEK4.204187$Hq3.5094148@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <38FBB6E0.6E3@swbell.net> <8diioo$6le$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <3904F0A4.1CC8@swbell.net> <aI7O4.9036$A75.1887439@news1.mco>`

```
mangogwr wrote:
> 
> They way we 'enlightened True Professional Systems Designers'
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> error-msg txt,

With the approach I described earlier, the called program provides
the text of an error message on request.  With your approach it
provides an error code on request.  These dependencies look identical 
to me, except that your approach adds a dependency on an external file
to provide the message text.

> REMOVES ALL THAT CODE FROM MEMORY UNTIL NEEDED, then
> ONLY a small program is loaded.

This approach has both advantages and disadvantages.

One advantage, as you have pointed out, is that the memory requirements
are potentially reduced.  I say "potentially" because the code needed
to fetch the message from an indexed file may itself occupy a
non-trivial amount of memory.  Depending on the COBOL dialect, of
course, you may be loading much of the IO subsystem anyway even if
you don't use it.  Alternatively you could call the message program 
dynamically, so that (again depending on the dialect) it wouldn't be
loaded until it was needed.

Another advantage is that you can change the messages by changing the
message file, without a recompile.  As Mr. Morris has pointed out in a 
related posting, this approach is especially useful for 
internationalization.

Now for the disadvantages:


1. Messages built or defined within a program serve as documentation,
describing the error being reported.  Once you separate the error
message from the program which detects the error, it may not be 
obvious just what the error is.  For clarity you would have to add
comments which would otherwise be redundant.

2. Hard-coding a message into a program is quick and easy.  Providing
a reference to a message stored elsewhere is not.  Not only do you 
have to add an entry to an external file somewhere, you also have to
assign an error code for it, and make sure that the error code is
unique across the system (or maybe find a message which has previously
been defined, and reuse its error code).  The sheer tedium of
maintaining messages in this fashion is likely to discourage 
programmers from providing good messages.

3. If you accidentally return the wrong error code, or if the message 
file becomes out of sync with the program, you're unlikely to notice, 
because you never see the text of the messages unless you look for it.

4. A new release of the program will likely involve a new release of
the message file as well, creating additional opportunities for
mistakes.

5. Often a generic error message that you could easily store in a file 
will not be as useful as a more specific message that incorporates 
information from the specific incident.  For example, a complaint
about an invalid date format will be much more useful if it includes
the invalid date.  If you build the error message locally you can
easily insert this information.  You can't do so with externally
stored messages unless you make the messaging system rather complicated.


My own COBOL experience has been mostly in the mainframe world, on
programs for use only within the company.  Neither memory constraints
nor internationalization has been an issue.  In this context the
use of a message file has little to offer.  One can imagine other
situations where it would make more sense.

Michael C. Kasten	mck9@swbell.net
http://home.swbell.net/mck9/cobol/cobol.html
```

<a id="t-7cac6179b3"></a>
## Thread: NOP — 16 this month / 17 total

### NOP

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-04-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38FCD6BE.3986FDEA@cusys.edu>`

```
We have a lot of programs with Exit paragraphs.  It isn't the way I
would program, but they are there nevertheless.

Sometimes I will debug by putting a display in the exit paragraph.  To
compile, the EXIT statement has to be deleted.  But EXIT is a No-OP
statement, the same as CONTINUE. 

Where we have code with empty EXIT paragraphs, we get compiler warnings,
so we generally stick in EXITs.  Or I have been putting in CONTINUEs. 
The latter have the advantage in that we can stick in a DISPLAY without
commenting out the EXIT.  I guess they're both NOPs, but one gives a
compiler check and the other doesn't.

Oh, well.  Here's to the death of GO TO's in COBOL.
```

#### ↳ Re: NOP

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8dip9g$o7e$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<38FCD6BE.3986FDEA@cusys.edu>`

```
Just so it is clear for those who might follow up on this thread, there is a
world of difference between "exit paragraphs" (part of the current and past
Standards) and the "Exit Paragraph" statement (currently an extension in some
implementation - and will be included in the next Standard).

The former is something like:

  0123-Whatever-Exit.
       Exit.

(which is usually used as the 2nd part of a statement like
   Perform 0123-Whatever thru 0123-Whatever-Exit)

while the latter is something like

0123-Whatever.
    If Something = "A"
        Read something
            At End
               Exit Paragraph
           Not At End
              Perform Process-Rec
        End-Read
   Else
      Perform Not-An-A
   End-If
   Perform Either-A-or-Not-but-not-EOF
     .
02345-Something-Else.
      ....

       ****

Back to the original note, there are "rules" about when/how the "EXIT"
statement (in an "exit paragraph" may/must be coded).  These are QUITE
different from the rules on when/how to use a CONTINUE statement.
    Perform Even-if-no
```

Actually, EXIT ISN'T a NOP, it is a LABEL
Correct sytnax is

PERFORM T100 thru T100-EXIT.

T100.
    MOVE ..
   COMPUTE..
T100-EXIT.   <= tis is just a label
         EXIT.     <== this when Performed thru generates a BRANCH stmt
        w/o, it you just FALL-THRU or get an error.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NOP

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ebrh7$3rs$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<38FCD6BE.3986FDEA@cusys.edu> <390418F6.96670691@personal.unicaja.es> <7N7O4.9041$A75.1895690@news1.mco> <8eb2nr$2c3$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <39092fb1.173251375@news.radiks.net>`

```
If you have an implementation where the EXIT statement (in an EXIT-paragraph)
"does affect execution" - then you have a COBOL compiler that does NOT
conform to the ANSI Standard.  The following is part of the definition from
the draft of the next Standard (which I believe matches exactly the current
Standard).

"an EXIT statement has no ... effect on the compilation or execution."

As for "bloated," I suggest that you compile the following (ANSI CONFORMING)
COBOL program and check its generated assembler (or any other test of
"bloat"). (This says NOTHING about the USEFULNESS of this program - only
about the "bloat" from the EXIT statement.)

Identification Division.
 Program-ID. TestBloat.
Procedure Division.
 Para1.
        Exit.
Para2.
        Exit.
 Para3.
        Exit.
 Para4.
        Exit.
 Para5.
        Exit.
 Para6.
        Exit.

(You may add a STOP RUN at the end - but it isn't required by the Standard)
```

- **References:** `<38FCD6BE.3986FDEA@cusys.edu> <390418F6.96670691@personal.unicaja.es> <7N7O4.9041$A75.1895690@news1.mco> <8eb2nr$2c3$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <39092fb1.173251375@news.radiks.net> <8ebrh7$3rs$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <390C02CB.73FEE663@melbpc.org.au>`

```
*if* the program is CALLed as a subprogram, the Standard requires it to act
as if an EXIT PROGRAM were coded.  (This is how/why the entire Procedure
Division is "optional" in the '85 Standard).

If it is your "main" program that doesn't have a Stop Run (or if even if you
have a GO TO that causes you to "fall thru the bottom of your code") then
"results are undefined" (and ABENDing is as good as anything)
```

William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:8eb2nr$2c3$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net...
> I don't understand what you are trying to say. The "T100-EXIT" is a label,
> but the "EXIT." statement is a statement that doesn't "impact" execution
> (which sounds like a NOP) to me.

(IANAALP) (I am not an assembly language programmer)

On big iron, I would think EXIT would generate a jump (is that a 'branch?')
to a variable based on what PERFORMed the preceding paragraph. On micros,
EXIT probably results in a 'ret' or 'retf', which are stack-based jumps.

NOP would not return to the PERFORMing line, 'cuz NOP don't do nuthin.

MCM
```

The EXIT statement is not a return.  It is a Nop.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NOP

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-04-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<390993C0.3C848134@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<38FCD6BE.3986FDEA@cusys.edu> <390418F6.96670691@personal.unicaja.es> <7N7O4.9041$A75.1895690@news1.mco> <8eb2nr$2c3$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <lAeO4.41$l51.12326@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <8ebvvo$bu2$1@news.igs.net>`

```
The EXIT statement is a NOP for which no code is generated.  The
instruction(s) that causes the return to the statement after the PERFORM
and rest the return to a fall through.  Note the reset may be generated
before the statement after the invoking PERFORM statement (this is the
practice of the IBM COBOL compilers for the S360-S390 except where
eliminated by the optimizer when not needed).  Note also that an
optimizing compiler may do even more interesting things.

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services Inc., cfmtech@istar.ca
 
donald tees wrote:
> 
> Michael Mattias wrote in message ...
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> The EXIT statement is not a return.  It is a Nop.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NOP

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ebngd$ckf$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<38FCD6BE.3986FDEA@cusys.edu> <390418F6.96670691@personal.unicaja.es> <7N7O4.9041$A75.1895690@news1.mco> <8eb2nr$2c3$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <lAeO4.41$l51.12326@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
In a well-formed COBOL program, an EXIT statement does
nothing. There is a technical explanation you might find interesting.

Given:
t100-exit.
    exit.

the compiler will see the tokens,
paragraph-name
period
separator-space
exit-symbol
period
separator-space

The combination: paragraph-name period, will be replaced
by the equivalent of a label in assembler.

The first separator-space may be replaced by code for
tracing execution or debugging, through generally this
separator-space is discarded.

The combination: exit-symbol period, is discarded.

The second separator-space is replaced by code to terminate
the paragraph.

So, quite literally, the EXIT statement does nothing. It has no
effect on compilation or execution. This is why the EXIT statement
is not required by some compilers, except for conformance to
the standard.

The confusion, with the meaning of the EXIT statement, occurs
because many do not see the separator-space following the
EXIT statement and, therefore, associate the end of paragraph
code with the EXIT statement rather than the separator-space
that follows.
------------------
Rick Smith

Michael Mattias wrote in message ...
>William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
>news:8eb2nr$2c3$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net...
>> I don't understand what you are trying to say. The "T100-EXIT" is a
label,
>> but the "EXIT." statement is a statement that doesn't "impact" execution
>> (which sounds like a NOP) to me.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>NOP would not return to the PERFORMing line, 'cuz NOP don't do nuthin.
```

- **Date:** 2000-04-28T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<3909A35C.9ADD7A@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<38FCD6BE.3986FDEA@cusys.edu> <390418F6.96670691@personal.unicaja.es> <7N7O4.9041$A75.1895690@news1.mco> <8eb2nr$2c3$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <lAeO4.41$l51.12326@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```


Michael Mattias wrote:
> 
> William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> MCM

It is the PERFORM which generates the branch, at whatever point the
perform ends.  There may be a real command at the end of the paragraph
or section, or there may be an exit.  EXIT and CONTINUE are NOPs which
can stand alone without a PERFORM.
```

- **From:** "Vicar" <varias@mbox.rrc.mb.ca>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8dhmdd$goj$1@canopus.cc.umanitoba.ca>`

```
Could you please give me some information regarding left and right
justifying when using the Micro Focus Animator Version 2.

I am a student at Red River College in Winnipeg, Manitoba and need your help
to complete a COBOL project. I need to be able to display or accept a users
input on the screen, left or right justifed.

Thank you, and I look forward to hearing from you.
```

field (which may contain leading spaces) to a field that is defined as
JUSTIFIIED, and the spaces will disappear, no?

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Right and left justify

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8dl9qo$9gu$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8dhmdd$goj$1@canopus.cc.umanitoba.ca> <eqQZQrbq$GA.230@cpmsnbbsa03> <38fe273d.0@news.axs2k.net>`

```
No - sorry, just not the way that the COBOL "justify" clause works.  It
*only* works when you are moving a smaller field to a larger field (and
leading/trailing spaces are considered part of each field).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Right and left justify

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38FE2F60.E176BB5A@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<8dhmdd$goj$1@canopus.cc.umanitoba.ca> <eqQZQrbq$GA.230@cpmsnbbsa03> <38fe273d.0@news.axs2k.net>`

```
Solar wrote:
> 
> I don't know Cobol that well, but it seems to me that you can just move a
> field (which may contain leading spaces) to a field that is defined as
> JUSTIFIIED, and the spaces will disappear, no?
> 

No, JUSTIFIED doesn't know the difference between spaces and any other
data.  It seems to be designed for use when you move a small field to a
large one.
```

O.k o.k. here's my problem. If a user descides to put leading spaces in
 a field, I have to be able to remove them. This is what I came up with.

MOVE ZERO TO Counter
INSPECT Product-Name-Add TALLYING counter FOR LEADING ' '
ADD 1 TO Counter
UNSTRING Product-Name-Add
    INTO Product-Name-Add WITH Pointer counter
END-UNSTRING.

If you can help me find a shorter way to shift information to the
left most part of a field, that would great. If not o'well this seems to
work.

VA

You might also consider reference modification which can be more efficient
than UNSTRING. Your code then becomes:

MOVE ZERO TO Counter
INSPECT Product-Name-Add TALLYING counter FOR LEADING ' '
MOVE Product-Name-Add(counter + 1:) to Product-Name-Add

- Karl

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Right and left justify

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8dij3f$n7e$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<8dhmdd$goj$1@canopus.cc.umanitoba.ca> <8di3vf$ncj$1@canopus.cc.umanitoba.ca> <hM0L4.17293$0o4.118787@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8di613$oe3$1@canopus.cc.umanitoba.ca> <Ml1L4.17297$0o4.118857@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8di92d$pu7$1@canopus.cc.umanitoba.ca>`

```
You can unstring without knowing the number of leading spaces by using
unstring x delimited by all " ".  If you are going to go to the effort of
counting the leading spaces with an inspect, then I would just use reference
modification for the move.  Either would be faster.  If you want to be
fiendishly clever and write fancy code, create an object that shifts left
one if there is a leading space, then invoke it iteratively.<snicker>.

truth to tell, I have (inherited) programs that do it exactly that way,
I have never seen a reason to revise functional code. I may have to
reconsider now that I have seen some alternatives.
		
	Susan A
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Right and left justify

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Warren Porter <wb999port@bellsouth12.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38FF09A6.840811EC@bellsouth12.net>`
- **References:** `<8dhmdd$goj$1@canopus.cc.umanitoba.ca> <8di3vf$ncj$1@canopus.cc.umanitoba.ca> <hM0L4.17293$0o4.118787@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8di613$oe3$1@canopus.cc.umanitoba.ca> <Ml1L4.17297$0o4.118857@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8di92d$pu7$1@canopus.cc.umanitoba.ca> <8dij3f$n7e$1@news.igs.net> <38FE1BD2.FAEF38A2@boeing.com>`

```
Susan Allen wrote:

> donald tees wrote:
> >
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> reconsider now that I have seen some alternatives.

This worked for me a few years back.

01  FIELD-1          PIC ZZ,ZZZ.99.
01  FIELD-1-R REDEFINES FIELD-1.
   05 TEST-SPACE        PIC X.
   05 FIELD-1-RIGHT PIC X(8).
01 FIELD-2.
   05 FIELD-2-LEFT PIC X(8).
   05 FILLER             PIC X VALUE SPACE.
--------------------
    MOVE whatever TO FIELD-1.
    PERFORM UNTIL TEST-SPACE NOT = SPACE
        MOVE FIELD-1-RIGHT TO FIELD-2-LEFT
        MOVE FIELD-2 TO FIELD-1
    END-PERFORM.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Right and left justify

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 2000-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<IzFL4.17487$0o4.120610@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<8dhmdd$goj$1@canopus.cc.umanitoba.ca> <8dij3f$n7e$1@news.igs.net> <38FE1BD2.FAEF38A2@boeing.com> <38FF09A6.840811EC@bellsouth12.net>`

```
In article <38FF09A6.840811EC@bellsouth12.net>,
Warren Porter  <wb999port@bellsouth12.net> wrote:

[snippissmus]

>This worked for me a few years back.
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>    END-PERFORM.

Zounds, Mr Porter... that brings back a blast from the past, both
barrels!  I cannot recall the details - my memory is, admittedly, porous -
but I recall working with a bunch of code, years ago, where the programmer
had set up in working-storage, right at the top - after the 'BEGIN
WORKING-STORAGE PROGRAM XXXXXXXX' literal - two 500-byte chunks o' filler,
called TRACE-RIGHT and TRACE-LEFT... and the first had a 30-character
subordinate field called THIS-PARA... and every paragraph began:

0100-HOUSEKEEPING.

    MOVE '0100-HOUSKEEPING' TO THIS-PARA.
    MOVE TRACE-RIGHT TO TRACE-LEFT.

... and the result was that when the program blew up one could check the
dump and see *exactly* what had been going on.

Damnation... I wish I remembered the details of this... I'm old and
crochety enough by now to start doing this again.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Right and left justify

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8dnaq8$s7m$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8dhmdd$goj$1@canopus.cc.umanitoba.ca> <8dij3f$n7e$1@news.igs.net> <38FE1BD2.FAEF38A2@boeing.com> <38FF09A6.840811EC@bellsouth12.net> <IzFL4.17487$0o4.120610@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
When I was working for Micro Focus, I created a "test" program to see exactly
what COBOL statements would cause S0C7 ABENDs - depending upon which types of
input values (i.e. spaces in numeric fields vs low-values vs "garbage") - AND
depending upon which specific settings of compiler options.  This ended up
with about 1000+ separate paragraphs - each with a single (possible S0C7
statement)  and with various "initialization" of the sending fields.

What I then did was at the beginning of each paragraph, check what the LAST
paragraph did and write it out to a file as "successful" (no S0C7) - if it
worked OK.  Then I would write to a file that I was starting this paragraph -
then I would try the COBOL statement.  If, it didn't cause a S0C7, it would
indicate that this statement worked OK and the program would go on to the
next paragraph.  If, on the other hand, the program ABENDED, I would then
restart the program and it would check (my temp file) to see which paragraph
had started OK, but not finished - and write this out as a combination that
caused a S0C7.

Talk about a "strange" application design <G> !!!  (and thank goodness, no
real application would ever have to be designed this way)
```

news:38FF398C.AE5ADB14@cusys.edu...
> docdwarf@clark.net wrote:
> >
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> I wondered if he had 1000 separate OPEN APPEND and CLOSE statements.

I had one OPEN APPEND statement - which was performed at the beginning of
each "run".

As for the "lost record in buffer" issue, this never (well never in my
experience) happens on the PC - and I coded BLOCK CONTAINS 1 RECORD to avoid
the problem on MVS.

- **From:** aflinsch <avflinsch@att.net>

```
docdwarf@clark.net wrote:
> 
> In article <38FF09A6.840811EC@bellsouth12.net>,
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> 


my personal preference is:
05  Z-FIELD                PIC ZZZ,ZZ9.99. 
05  STRING-IN.
    10  STRING-FIRST       PIC X(01).      
    10  STRING-LAST        PIC X(09).      
05  STRING-OUT.                            
    10  STRING-LAST        PIC X(09).      
    10  STRING-FIRST       PIC X(01).      

MOVE Z-FIELD TO
STRING-IN                                                   
PERFORM UNTIL STRING-FIRST OF STRING-IN NOT = SPACE     
        MOVE CORR STRING-IN TO STRING-OUT               
        MOVE STRING-OUT TO STRING-IN                    
END-PERFORM                                             




> Zounds, Mr Porter... that brings back a blast from the past, both
> barrels!  I cannot recall the details - my memory is, admittedly, porous -
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

01  DEBUG-TRACE.                                  
    05  DEBUG-STACK.                              
        10  THIS-PARA          PIC X(0030).       
        10  REST-PARAS         PIC X(4550).       
    05  FILLER PIC X(50) VALUE ALL '*'.           
    05  TRACE-STACK            PIC X(4550).       

first few lines of each paragraph
 MOVE DEBUG-STACK TO TRACE-STACK       
 MOVE TRACE-STACK TO REST-PARAS        
 MOVE '0000-MAIN ' TO THIS-PARA        


when you get a dump everything before the **************************
line is a paragraph trace.

Personally I liked 'STATE,COUNT,FLOW' in the compile options better...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Right and left justify

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8e73gf$2n8$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8dhmdd$goj$1@canopus.cc.umanitoba.ca> <3906D57B.9B480386@zip.com.au> <N_BN4.18333$0o4.133378@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3906EFE2.CCDFFF3B@zip.com.au> <ohDN4.18369$0o4.134083@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
In case anyone doesn't understand all of this (and actually CARES), - I
assume that Ken and DD do already understand.

The new TEST options are part of the LE run-time support (available for ALL
LE enabled languages - not just COBOL).

For COBOL this requires the use of:
   IBM SAA AD/CYCLE COBOL/370 (no longer supported)
   IBM COBOL for MVS & VM
   IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM

(either of the last two with or without MLE - Millennium Language
Extensions).

I also believe it is supported with the COBOL FOR VSE - but haven't
researched that.

P.S.  Just a friendly reminder to any VS COBOL II programmers out there.  Did
you remember that VS COBOL II goes "out of service" in less than one year
now, i.e. March 31, 2001?
```

```
In article <8di92d$pu7$1@canopus.cc.umanitoba.ca>,
Vicar <varias@mbox.rrc.mb.ca> wrote:
>O.k o.k. here's my problem. If a user descides to put leading spaces in
> a field, I have to be able to remove them. This is what I came up with.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>END-UNSTRING.

Now *there's some code... and it might even work!  UNSTRINGING something
into its'self is... interesting, to say the least, but I notice the use of
an END-UNSTRING delimiter so I guess this means I can use them newfangled
COBOL '85 thingies... how about an inline PERFORM, say:

MOVE 1 TO SUB2.
MOVE SPACES TO WK-PRODUCT-NAME-ADD.
PERFORM VARYING SUB1 FROM 1 BY 1
 UNTIL SUB1 > (LENGTH OF PRODUCT-NAME-ADD)
    IF PRODUCT-NAME-ADD(SUB1:1) NOT = SPACES
        MOVE PRODUCT-NAME-ADD(SUB1:1) TO WK-PRODUCT-NAME-ADD(SUB2:1)
        ADD 1 TO SUB2
    END-IF
END-PERFORM
MOVE WK-PRODUCT-NAME-ADD TO PRODUCT-NAME-ADD.

... and that, too, just might work.

>
>If you can help me find a shorter way to shift information to the
>left most part of a field, that would great. If not o'well this seems to
>work.

I'm not sure if this is 'shorter' or not... but I *do* know which one I'd
rather pass on to a two-year programmer for maintenance.

DD

><docdwarf@clark.net> wrote in message
>news:Ml1L4.17297$0o4.118857@iad-read.news.verio.net...
…[53 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Right and left justify

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8dit37$2ne4$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<8dhmdd$goj$1@canopus.cc.umanitoba.ca> <8di613$oe3$1@canopus.cc.umanitoba.ca> <Ml1L4.17297$0o4.118857@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8di92d$pu7$1@canopus.cc.umanitoba.ca> <RK8L4.17356$0o4.119322@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```

docdwarf@clark.net wrote in message ...
>Now *there's some code... and it might even work!  UNSTRINGING something
>into its'self is... interesting, to say the least, but I notice the use of
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> UNTIL SUB1 > (LENGTH OF PRODUCT-NAME-ADD)
[...]
>... and that, too, just might work.

LENGTH OF is not COBOL '85, it is a common extension.
Use FUNCTION LENGTH (data-name) for COBOL '85
(with the later intrinsic function enhancements).

And yes, it just might work even without changing the code.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Right and left justify

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8digcp$sg6$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8dhmdd$goj$1@canopus.cc.umanitoba.ca> <Ns%K4.17285$0o4.118703@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8di3vf$ncj$1@canopus.cc.umanitoba.ca> <hM0L4.17293$0o4.118787@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8di613$oe3$1@canopus.cc.umanitoba.ca>`

```
Vicar,
 You might want to check out the COBOL FAQ and read what it says about asking
homework questions.  It makes it clear that these ARE welcome - but that you
will only (well usually) get "useful" replies if you show how much you have
done - before asking for help.

Both the question on "how to submit homework questions" and how to do
left/right justify appear in this newsgroup fairly frequently.  If you don't
know about it, you may want to check

   www.deja.com

for questions that already have been answered in the newsgroup.  This may
speed up the response that you get for "common" questions.
```

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>

```


Vicar wrote:
> 
> Could you please give me some information regarding left and right
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thank you, and I look forward to hearing from you.
-----------------------------------------------------------------------
Your text-book or reference manual should contain the syntax below, or
similar.  ACCEPT MYFIELD WITH LEFT-JUSTIFY ( or RIGHT-JUSTIFY ) :-

.......................................................................
The ACCEPT statement causes data keyed at the console or supplied by the
operating system to be made available to the program in a specified data
item.

Formatï¿½5 (Screen Item) 

ACCEPT identifier-1

    ï¿½AT ï¿½LINE NUMBER ï¿½identifier-2 ï¿½ï¿½ ï¿½ï¿½COLUMN ï¿½ NUMBER ï¿½identifier-3 ï¿½ï¿½
ï¿½
    ï¿½   ï¿½            ï¿½integer-1    ï¿½ï¿½ ï¿½ï¿½COL    ï¿½        ï¿½integer-2    ï¿½ï¿½
ï¿½
    ï¿½                                                                   
ï¿½
    ï¿½AT ï¿½identifier-4 ï¿½                                                 
ï¿½

    ï¿½   ï¿½integer-3    ï¿½                                                 
ï¿½

    [FROM CRT] [MODE IS BLOCK]

    ï¿½      ï¿½ ï¿½AUTO      ï¿½                      ï¿½    ï¿½
    ï¿½      ï¿½ ï¿½AUTO-SKIP ï¿½                      ï¿½    ï¿½
    ï¿½      ï¿½ ï¿½BELL ï¿½                           ï¿½    ï¿½
    ï¿½      ï¿½ ï¿½BEEP ï¿½                           ï¿½    ï¿½
    ï¿½      ï¿½  BLINK                            ï¿½    ï¿½

    ï¿½      ï¿½ ï¿½FULL         ï¿½                   ï¿½    ï¿½
    ï¿½      ï¿½ ï¿½LENGTH-CHECK ï¿½                   ï¿½    ï¿½
    ï¿½      ï¿½ GRID                              ï¿½    ï¿½
    ï¿½      ï¿½ ï¿½HIGHLIGHT ï¿½                      ï¿½    ï¿½
    ï¿½      ï¿½ ï¿½LOWLIGHT  ï¿½                      ï¿½    ï¿½

    ï¿½      ï¿½ LEFTLINE                          ï¿½    ï¿½
    ï¿½      ï¿½ OVERLINE                          ï¿½    ï¿½
    ï¿½      ï¿½ PROMPT [CHARACTER IS [literal-1]] ï¿½    ï¿½
    ï¿½      ï¿½ ï¿½REQUIRED    ï¿½                    ï¿½    ï¿½
    ï¿½      ï¿½ ï¿½EMPTY-CHECK ï¿½                    ï¿½    ï¿½

    ï¿½      ï¿½ REVERSE-VIDEO                     ï¿½    ï¿½
    ï¿½      ï¿½ ï¿½SECURE  ï¿½                        ï¿½    ï¿½
    ï¿½ WITH ï¿½ ï¿½NO-ECHO ï¿½                        ï¿½... ï¿½
    ï¿½      ï¿½ SIZE IS ï¿½identifier-6 ï¿½           ï¿½    ï¿½
    ï¿½      ï¿½         ï¿½integer-4    ï¿½           ï¿½    ï¿½

    ï¿½      ï¿½ UNDERLINE                         ï¿½    ï¿½
    ï¿½      ï¿½ ï¿½FOREGROUND-COLOR  ï¿½ IS integer-5 ï¿½    ï¿½
    ï¿½      ï¿½ ï¿½FOREGROUND-COLOUR ï¿½              ï¿½    ï¿½
    ï¿½      ï¿½ ï¿½BACKGROUND-COLOR  ï¿½ IS integer-6 ï¿½    ï¿½
    ï¿½      ï¿½ ï¿½BACKGROUND-COLOUR ï¿½              ï¿½    ï¿½

    ï¿½      ï¿½ CONTROL IS ï¿½identifier-7 ï¿½        ï¿½    ï¿½
    ï¿½      ï¿½            ï¿½literal-2    ï¿½        ï¿½    ï¿½
    ï¿½      ï¿½ ï¿½TIME-OUT ï¿½ AFTER ï¿½integer-7    ï¿½ ï¿½    ï¿½
    ï¿½      ï¿½ ï¿½TIMEOUT  ï¿½       ï¿½identifier-8 ï¿½ ï¿½    ï¿½
    ï¿½      ï¿½ LEFT-JUSTIFY                      ï¿½    ï¿½

    ï¿½      ï¿½ RIGHT-JUSTIFY                     ï¿½    ï¿½
    ï¿½      ï¿½ SPACE-FILL                        ï¿½    ï¿½
    ï¿½      ï¿½ TRAILING-SIGN                     ï¿½    ï¿½
    ï¿½      ï¿½ UPDATE                            ï¿½    ï¿½
    ï¿½      ï¿½ UPPER                             ï¿½    ï¿½
    ï¿½      ï¿½ LOWER                             ï¿½    ï¿½

    ï¿½      ï¿½ ZERO-FILL                         ï¿½    ï¿½

    ï¿½ON ï¿½EXCEPTION ï¿½ imperative-statement-1 ï¿½
    ï¿½   ï¿½ESCAPE    ï¿½                        ï¿½

    ï¿½NOT ON ï¿½EXCEPTION ï¿½ imperative-statement-2 ï¿½
    ï¿½       ï¿½ESCAPE    ï¿½                        ï¿½

    [END-ACCEPT]
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu Down?

- **From:** "Gary Roush" <gkr@adtools.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<K1mL4.44830$y4.1595127@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<8dhstb$5ov$1@ins21.netins.net>`

```
To all COBOL users of Fujitsu COBOL,

The systems people here at Fujitsu are working on this problem. It is
apparently a DNS settings problem that will take them some time to fix.
Thanks for letting us know. Some internet places (ISP routers) are not
working and when that happens, the errors like what you are seeing will
come up where some can see the site and others can't. For myself, I can see
the site from my home site.  Anyways updating the routing numbers is what
has to be done and that takes some time to complete the process as all the
ISP's have to get this updated information to work properly again. Using
the DNS numbers http://192.240.15.199/ instead of the domain name of
www.adtools.com may work for some of you. If the problem persists at end of
today (Wednesday, 4/19), you can let me know at my gkr@adtools.com address.
We apologize for the inconvenience at this time. Thanks.

Regards,
Gary Roush
Fujitsu COBOL Software

to files created from a Fujitsu Cobol.

Tony M. Mina <jets@nbnet.nb.ca> wrote in message
news:38fbeb65.44173627@allnews.nbnet.nb.ca...
> I am looking for a third party software that will allow me to do an SQL
> to files created from a Fujitsu Cobol.
>

the last time I tried it.  Worth a try...tell sales you want the 30 day full
refund guarantee so you can try it out.

Tom Morrison
Liant Software Corporation

driver for Fujitsu files.  Using ODBC you should be able to do the SQL
using a tool such as MYSQL etc.

## Thread: Design Survey — 64 msgs

### Design Survey

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8dkdlo$glo$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```


Hi:

I was wondering again. . .

Anybody so inclined can participate in my survey.

Just post or email to me, your entry.

Question:  If you were to design a BRAND NEW system today,
and you could develop it for ANY hardware or operating
system YOU wanted, what hardware/operating system/stuff
(compiler, thing, method, etc.) would you use? The brand new
system does not use anything else from anywhere else,
it is from scratch. It's not necessary to justify your
choice.

If anybody enters, I will compile the entries and post
the results.

Thanks

Tony Dilworth


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Design Survey

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38FDBDBD.D1205C38@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<8dkdlo$glo$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Foodman wrote:
> 
> Question:  If you were to design a BRAND NEW system today,
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> choice.

That's like asking "If you were to build a new thing today, and you
could use any materials YOU wanted, what materials would you use?"

I might want to check to see whether that thing/system is going to be
used to hit a baseball, to calculate scores, to hold up a roof, to carry
goods across the ocean, to feed astronauts...

If I were designing a new system and had a full choice of what
hardware/operating system to use - then I would determine the system
requirements around my business needs, not the other way around.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Design Survey

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38FE0690.FD226CB6@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<8dkdlo$glo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38fdc335_3@news3.prserv.net> <8dkiuh$mtb$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38FDDE51.E72761AB@cusys.edu> <8dkqsq$fg$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Foodman wrote:
> 
> Hi:
> 
> Other than time and space constraints, what sort of conditions
> would influence your choice of tools?

Some influences off the top of my head (various tools trade off the
following):
How much connectivity is desired - if so what kind of connectivity 
How much security is desired
How much data integrity is desired 
Do I want the tool to have an nice GUI?
Do I need to connect to cash boxes or other hardware?
Will my applications be used simultaneously by lots of people?
Will my applications be used by users using their CPU power on their
PCs?
Do I need my data to talk with some other database?  
Do I need real-time update?  If so, of what? (if someone buys a widget,
putting my inventory down below x units, can I wait until tonight to
order more widgets?)
Who is going to maintain my system?
Will we be adding to the system next year?
Do I need to make sure my programs work on Unix as well as NT?
Do I need the database vendor to hold hands with my users, or can I get
a better performing database with less support?
How many people will be working at creating this project?  What are
their skills?
Will I need multi-lingual support?
What kind of conversions will I be doing?
How should I allocate my budget (between database, language, machine,
labor, etc.)?
Do I want users to access my system via the web?
Do I feel comfortable with water cooled computers?  Windows? 
Do I believe Compaq will still support VAX's?  (plug in your companies
here)

I could go on and on.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Design Survey

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8dmnt1$3k5$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8dkdlo$glo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38fdc335_3@news3.prserv.net> <8dkiuh$mtb$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38FDDE51.E72761AB@cusys.edu> <8dkqsq$fg$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38FE0690.FD226CB6@cusys.edu>`

```
In article <38FE0690.FD226CB6@cusys.edu>,
  Please, do, not, e-mail, replies, to, Howard, Brazee, post, them!!
wrote:
> Foodman wrote:
> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Hi:

Thanks for making such a long list. I responded to each of the points.


> Some influences off the top of my head (various tools trade off the
> following):


> How much connectivity is desired - if so what kind of connectivity
---- As much as you need whatever connectivity is.

> How much security is desired
----How does this affect the choice? All systems should be designed to
be secure.

> How much data integrity is desired
----How does this affect the choice? Data integrity is assumed. WOuld
you design a system without data integrity?

> Do I want the tool to have an nice GUI?
----Of course, why should it be ugly? this does affect your choice.


> Do I need to connect to cash boxes or other hardware?
----In the survey, no, nothing unusual or special like that.

> Will my applications be used simultaneously by lots of people?
----Yes, how does this affect choice (other than hardware, perhaps).
Every system should be multi-user. I mean, all you have to do
is check for locked records and files. What's the big deal?

> Will my applications be used by users using their CPU power on their
> PCs?
----You have a choice of hardware.

> Do I need my data to talk with some other database?
----The survey said it doesn't talk to or use anything foreign.

> Do I need real-time update?  If so, of what? (if someone buys a
widget,
----This is a design issue not a tools issue.

> putting my inventory down below x units, can I wait until tonight to
> order more widgets?)
----This is a design issue not a tools issue.

> Who is going to maintain my system?
----It should NOT require ongoing maintenance -

> Will we be adding to the system next year?
----Perhaps. How does this affect your choice of tools? What is the
difference between 'maintenance' and 'adding to'?

> Do I need to make sure my programs work on Unix as well as NT?
----No, just one platform

> Do I need the database vendor to hold hands with my users, or can I
>get a better performing database with less support?
----I would hope that it would not require that kind of support. If
databases require ongoing support by their vendors, get rid of them.

> How many people will be working at creating this project?  What are
> their skills?
----You are the one.

> Will I need multi-lingual support?
----you mean like English, French, Chinese? no.

> What kind of conversions will I be doing?
----Since this is a new system there are no conversions.

> How should I allocate my budget (between database, language, machine,
> labor, etc.)?
----It's your budget, you allocate.

> Do I want users to access my system via the web?
----For the survey, no.

> Do I feel comfortable with water cooled computers?
---- What the heck does water cooling have to do with design?
Windows?
-Why not?

> Do I believe Compaq will still support VAX's?
- Who cares? you are designing a new system.

> I could go on and on.
----But don't.  Be specific - answer the questions and explain how
they affect your choice and why you would use one tool or thingy
rather than another in each case.

Thanks

Tony Dilworth


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Design Survey

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38FF178B.872A9DDA@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<8dkdlo$glo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38fdc335_3@news3.prserv.net> <8dkiuh$mtb$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38FDDE51.E72761AB@cusys.edu> <8dkqsq$fg$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38FE0690.FD226CB6@cusys.edu> <8dmnt1$3k5$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Foodman wrote:
> 
> > Some influences off the top of my head (various tools trade off the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> ---- As much as you need whatever connectivity is.

By connectivity, I mean the way your application interfaces with other
applications.  Do I need to communicate with a bank, with the web, with
Wal-Mart, with the IRS, using TCP/IP, a LAN, etc.

"As much as you need" isn't sufficient.  Until I analyze the particular
project I have no idea what my needs are.  And it isn't a linear scale -
the best tool for connectivity with my bank may not be the same as the
best tool for connectivity with a supplier.
 
> > How much security is desired
> ----How does this affect the choice? All systems should be designed to
> be secure.

Nothing is 100% secure, but a PC with no connectivity comes close.  Some
tools make it easier to make my internet connection secure, others make
it easier to make sure only the correct employees log on, others have
built in compliance to my bank's standards.  Some are easier to have
off-site backups.  Some are easier to lock up behind guarded doors.  
 
> > How much data integrity is desired
> ----How does this affect the choice? Data integrity is assumed. WOuld
> you design a system without data integrity?

Sure (to some degree).  Every time you download data to a user's PC,
data integrity is risked.  There is no way to guarantee that his copy of
the data stays in sync with the master copy.  If your web application is
cached in a remote site, data integrity is risked.  Shared update of
data is more risky than protected update, but protected update doesn't
allow any other users to use those data.

We warehouse denormalized data - which means a degree of data integrity
is lost.

You can't have everything, but have to pick and choose between
conflicting values.
 
> > Do I want the tool to have an nice GUI?
> ----Of course, why should it be ugly? this does affect your choice.

Thanks, a choice.   But there are other choices between "nice GUI" and
"Ugly GUI".  Most of my applications have "No GUI".
 
> > Do I need to connect to cash boxes or other hardware?
> ----In the survey, no, nothing unusual or special like that.

Most real applications have something unusual or special.  Otherwise, we
would plug in a turnkey application.
 
> > Will my applications be used simultaneously by lots of people?
> ----Yes, how does this affect choice (other than hardware, perhaps).

Certainly hardware.  The bandwidth of a mainframe helps.  You want good
record locking tools.  This may increase the odds of choosing an OO
language, or distributed processing.  Some databases come with better
support for these than others. 

Again, each tool has its strengths and weaknesses.

> Every system should be multi-user. I mean, all you have to do
> is check for locked records and files. What's the big deal?

My Excel spreadsheet isn't multi-user.  It doesn't need to be.

Let's say you pull up your GUI showing all of the seats in an airplane. 
You click on a pair in your travel agency while someone else in Timbuktu
is doing the same thing, but clicking on the other seat first.  There
are much more complicated ways of getting deadlocks.
 
> > Will my applications be used by users using their CPU power on their
> > PCs?
> ----You have a choice of hardware.

My business needs will have a large say in this.  United Airline's
airline reservation company just decided to change from their good
proprietary communication protocol to TCP/IP because it was ubiquitous
and customers understood it.  Let's say this was the project you are
talking about.  Do I design for 20" monitors and light pens if that
works best, or do I design for whatever I think the travel agents will
use?
 
> > Do I need my data to talk with some other database?
> ----The survey said it doesn't talk to or use anything foreign.

That simplifies things.  It is no longer what I would consider an
average business system application.
 
> > Do I need real-time update?  If so, of what? (if someone buys a
> widget,
> ----This is a design issue not a tools issue.

How do you separate these out?  

The answer to this design question will influence my choice of tools.
 
> > putting my inventory down below x units, can I wait until tonight to
> > order more widgets?)
> ----This is a design issue not a tools issue.

The answer to this design question will influence my choice of tools.
 
> > Who is going to maintain my system?
> ----It should NOT require ongoing maintenance -

Very interesting.  I have never come across such a unique system.  I
definitely would need to know more before doing analysis.  
 
> > Will we be adding to the system next year?
> ----Perhaps. How does this affect your choice of tools? What is the
> difference between 'maintenance' and 'adding to'?

example:
There are some design tools which write your code for you, but which are
poor at handling your tweaking of the system.
There are languages which have a reputation of being "write only".  One
strength of COBOL (this is comp.lang.cobol) is that it is easy to
maintain.

There isn't a lot of difference between "maintenance" and "adding to". 
But to get a feel, let's say you have a Billing system.  Maintaining is
something which will enhance how you get your billing done.  Adding to,
will expand it to include accounts receivable, integrated with your
current system.

> > Do I need to make sure my programs work on Unix as well as NT?
> ----No, just one platform
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> databases require ongoing support by their vendors, get rid of them.

That is a large reason why Oracle is as big as it is, customers like the
support they give.  
 
> > How many people will be working at creating this project?  What are
> > their skills?
> ----You are the one.

Ah, a very small system which can be completed in a man-year or so.  I'm
used to working with larger systems.
 
> > Will I need multi-lingual support?
> ----you mean like English, French, Chinese? no.
> 
> > What kind of conversions will I be doing?
> ----Since this is a new system there are no conversions.

No data conversions?  All data will be new?  
 
> > How should I allocate my budget (between database, language, machine,
> > labor, etc.)?
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> ---- What the heck does water cooling have to do with design?

You can't separate implementation from design.  You have to know what
your real-world limitations and preferences are.  This is like designing
a hunting trip and asking if you are comfortable with firearms.

> Windows?
> -Why not?
> 
> > Do I believe Compaq will still support VAX's?
> - Who cares? you are designing a new system.

Am I designing a new computer as well?  If I like tools which are
designed around a VAX, I had better find out whether or not my employer
will be comfortable with me using those tools before I make my design
based upon what those tools can do for me.
 
> > I could go on and on.
> ----But don't.  Be specific - answer the questions and explain how
> they affect your choice and why you would use one tool or thingy
> rather than another in each case.

Done.

Please explain why you are asking these questions.
```

#### ↳ Re: Design Survey

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<8dkdlo$glo$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Like others have said... what requirements does the system have to satisfy.
If the sole requirement to be satisfied were my meglomaniacal cravings then
the answer might be very different than it would be to actually solve a
problem, since I would use the opportunity to get paid to pad my resume with
the latest technology buzzwords, rather than being pragmatic about it.

- Karl

"Foodman" <foodman123@aol.com> wrote in message
news:8dkdlo$glo$1@nnrp1.deja.com...

>
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Design Survey

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8dkmel$quh$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8dkdlo$glo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <TSjL4.214950$Hq3.5434745@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
In article <TSjL4.214950$Hq3.5434745@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>,
  "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com> wrote:
> Like others have said... what requirements does the system have to
satisfy.
> - Karl


Hi:

Remember that I have been 'At Home Alone' for so long, so,
I am surprised that people want to know the requirements
before they choose a solution.

Apart from the obvious volume and time constraints, what
other requirements would influence your choice of tools?

Thanks

Tony Dilworth





Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Design Survey

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<SgnL4.215425$Hq3.5472898@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<8dkdlo$glo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <TSjL4.214950$Hq3.5434745@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <8dkmel$quh$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
How about...

* expected life expectancy of the system. How I'd write a throwaway
conversion utility is different from how I'd write a system that's expected
to last forever.

* stability of requirements which impact ongoing maintenance. If I'm
anticipating lots of changes then I would attempt to isolate those
anticipated changes so as to create a single point of maintenance.

* whether or not the requirements can be met by an existing solution.
There's no way I'm going to write a spreadsheet program when I can buy one
for less than $100.

* need to scale. It's fine to write for the PC but what happens if I get a
bigger sale, or the customer's business grows. Suddenly I need an enterprise
solution with no way to get there. Conversely, developing an enterprise
version for a single user is overkill.

* uptime requirements. Hardware and software choices change if I need
reliability.

* who's likely to be stuck maintaining it and their skill set. I don't want
to be stuck maintaining this beast forever so there had better be a large
pool of talent available to take on the task.

* what does the end user wants to see? There's no point in developing a
state of the art GUI system when the end user isn't a human being.
Similarly, there's no reason to limit the design to a simple GUI if a
virtual reality is required. How's about an arcade-style gaming nterface to
your payroll system? Imagine the fun HR could have 'terminating' employees.

* is the application for business users, consumers, military, internet,
machine-to-machine, real-time, etc. They all impose different requirements
which influence the choice of development tools and hardware.

FWIW, if I were writing a user-interactive business application I would make
sure that the architecture included some kind transaction manager, a
relational database and a clearly defined boundary between presentation and
business logic. That gives me lots of reliability and data integrity. The
user interface can be anything from punch cards and paper tape to virtual
reality engines and voice recognition.

- Karl

…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

In article <SgnL4.215425$Hq3.5472898@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>,
  "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com> wrote:


Hi:

Thanks for taking the time to reply in detail..


> How about...
>
> * expected life expectancy of the system. How I'd write a throwaway
> conversion utility is different from how I'd write a system that's
expected
> to last forever.
--- For the survey, it is a permanent system. Yes, even I use
shortcuts, when justifiable.

> * stability of requirements which impact ongoing maintenance. If I'm
> anticipating lots of changes then I would attempt to isolate those
> anticipated changes so as to create a single point of maintenance.
---If you anticipate lots of changes, make some accomodation in the
files for stuff like that.


>
> * whether or not the requirements can be met by an existing solution.
> There's no way I'm going to write a spreadsheet program when I can
buy one
> for less than $100.
---There is no existing solution.


>
> * need to scale. It's fine to write for the PC but what happens if I
get a
> bigger sale, or the customer's business grows. Suddenly I need an
enterprise
> solution with no way to get there. Conversely, developing an
enterprise
> version for a single user is overkill.

---Sorry, I don't agree. The only consideration should be hardware,
not software. And, the software should be HIGHLY PORTABLE to deal
with that eventuality.


>
> * uptime requirements. Hardware and software choices change if I need
> reliability.
---Uptime should be 100% 24/7, period. Are you suggesting that you
would design a less reliable system depending on something?

>
> * who's likely to be stuck maintaining it and their skill set. I
don't want
> to be stuck maintaining this beast forever so there had better be a
large
> pool of talent available to take on the task.
---Then stick to simple things like COBOL and SP2.

>
> * what does the end user wants to see? There's no point in developing
a
> state of the art GUI system when the end user isn't a human being.
> Similarly, there's no reason to limit the design to a simple GUI if a
> virtual reality is required. How's about an arcade-style gaming
nterface to
> your payroll system? Imagine the fun HR could have 'terminating'
employees.

---don't be silly. Besides why should not your design be the best
you can do regardless?


>
> * is the application for business users, consumers, military,
internet,
> machine-to-machine, real-time, etc. They all impose different
requirements
> which influence the choice of development tools and hardware.
---I really don't think that any system should differ in terms
of ease-of-use, self-documentation, dependability, portability,
maintainability etc., depending on who you design it for. Would your
system be less whatever if you designed it for business or the
consumer and if so, why? Obviously a missile-defense system is
going to be different than payroll.

>
> FWIW, if I were writing a user-interactive business application I
would make
> sure that the architecture included some kind transaction manager, a
> relational database and a clearly defined boundary between
presentation and
> business logic. That gives me lots of reliability and data integrity.
---OK, what would your choices be?


Thanks

Tony Dilworth


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Design Survey

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dFDL4.219924$Hq3.5630117@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<8dkdlo$glo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <TSjL4.214950$Hq3.5434745@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <8dkmel$quh$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <SgnL4.215425$Hq3.5472898@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <8dmor1$4gs$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

"Foodman" <foodman123@aol.com> wrote in message
news:8dmor1$4gs$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> In article <SgnL4.215425$Hq3.5472898@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>,
>   "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com> wrote:
>
>
<<SNIP>>

> >
> > * need to scale. It's fine to write for the PC but what happens if I
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> with that eventuality.

So you're saying I should implement data sharing even though there is never
a requirement for data to be shared. Should I also implement a transaction
server and message queue processor for the system even though it is only
ever going to handle a single user? Nonsense! If I'm building a single user
or groupware application I am going have different infrastructure
requirements and I would use different tools to deliver it in a timely
manner.

>
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> would design a less reliable system depending on something?

An uptime of 24/7 implies a different architecture than one that works in
batch mode. It also implies the need to be able to 'hot swap' when the
primary machine fails. Other considerations are the ability to handle
dynamic backup and reorganization, mirroring, etc. This requirement means
the architecture is more complicated since reliability is now dependent on
more than just my code. It also depends on hardware and the base O/S. The
24/7 requirement just ruled out Windows as my choice of O/S. I'd also be
looking at some alternative hardware like Tandem, Sequent, or clustering
technologies. Are you starting to get a clue as to the kind of issues that
are important to designing enterprise scale systems?

>
> >
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> ---Then stick to simple things like COBOL and SP2.

You're making an arbitrary decision for me. Who says COBOL would be my
choice of language? Who says that it would have a GUI interface? I was kind
of partial to biochip implants and alpha wave recognition.

>
> >
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> you can do regardless?

What the hell does that mean? How can my design be 'the best' if I have no
idea what the user requirements are? In who's opinion is my design 'the
best'. Software design is an optimization excercise. Without criteria to
define the boundaries it is a pointless exercise.

>
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> going to be different than payroll.

Why do you insist on assuming that all applications and users are created
equal? Would you expect your 8 year old nephew to be able to use your COBOL
development tools? They comprise an application but I doubt it is intended
for some joe off the street. Does that mean it's design is flawed? How would
you redesign it so your nephew could use it?

If I'm designing for a consumer then the emphasis will be on making the
system accessible and understandable to anybody off the street. They need to
be able to install it and watch it work. The software should lead them by
the hand to do the right thing. I'd probably design the same way for a
business user if I expected them to be able to use the system without any
training. On the other hand, if I was building an airline reservation system
like SABER then I would give the business user a command line interface and
training to familiarize them with it's use. Why? Because they will thank me
for making it quick and easy for them to get exactly what they want with as
few keystrokes or mouse clicks as possible. They also aren't left
floundering on their own because the person standing next to them can mentor
them through the learning curve so the hand-holding requirements are
different.

Here's a real world example of where business needs differ from consumers...
online help. I was involved in the creation of a large system that tracks
movement of goods across the border. The system has something on the order
of 10,000 users. The powers that be decided they would need extensive online
help so we implemented it. After the first year we took a look at the number
of times online help had been accessed. The number of hits was 43 for a
whole year! That's on the order of 1 access per 200 users per year.

>
> >
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> ---OK, what would your choices be?

That depends, but since you don't agree with much of what I've had to say
previously you won't appreciate why it depends. Maybe if your view of
systems development was a little less myopic I'd enumerate some options, but
they'd just be buzzwords to you. I seem to recall you finding relational
databases to be useless so why would a particular vendor's implementation
possibly be of any interest.

>
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Design Survey

_(thread depth: 6)_

```
In article <dFDL4.219924$Hq3.5630117@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>,
  "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com> wrote:
> > > * need to scale. It's fine to write for the PC but what happens
if I
> > get a
> > > bigger sale, or the customer's business grows. Suddenly I need an
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…

never
