transaction
> server and message queue processor for the system even though it is
only
> ever going to handle a single user? Nonsense! If I'm building a
single user
> or groupware application I am going have different infrastructure
> requirements and I would use different tools to deliver it in a timely
> manner.


---If the system you wrote was in COBOL then file-sharing is
trivial to implement. Maybe things are worse in the mainframe
world than I imagined (I imagined they would be at least as
good as on the PC) but I don't need a transaction server or
a message queue (whatever they are) to accomplish the goal.
All I need is COBOL.

What do I not understand?  Please clarify.

>
> >
> >
> > >
> > > * uptime requirements. Hardware and software choices change if I
need
> > > reliability.
> > ---Uptime should be 100% 24/7, period. Are you suggesting that you
> > would design a less reliable system depending on something?
>
> An uptime of 24/7 implies a different architecture than one that
works in
> batch mode. It also implies the need to be able to 'hot swap' when the
> primary machine fails. Other considerations are the ability to handle
> dynamic backup and reorganization, mirroring, etc. This requirement
means
> the architecture is more complicated since reliability is now
dependent on
> more than just my code.

----I agree that dependability of the whole system is beyond the
scope of our program.  When I talk about dependability 24/7
I am talking about MY program. My program will never break
down.  If you chose an OS which broke down all the time, then
you would have to change OS.  I still don't think that these
issues affect the design of my COBOL program. The best thing
an application programmer can do is write a fool-proof program.
Unfortunately, the other stuff is not as  dependable,
see Windows. Sure, when Windows crashes, my program crashes too,
but there is nothing I can do about that. (Actually, Windows
is stable enough when running a single application that uptime
is pretty much 100% 24/7 - my customers use dedicated PCs and
networks for the POS applications.) My customers can't
afford anything but PCs.






It also depends on hardware and the base O/S. The
> 24/7 requirement just ruled out Windows as my choice of O/S. I'd also
be
> looking at some alternative hardware like Tandem, Sequent, or
clustering
> technologies. Are you starting to get a clue as to the kind of issues
that
> are important to designing enterprise scale systems?


----Yes, but I think these latter issues you raise are more
OS and hardware oriented than application-design oriented. My
COBOL application should run just fine on those Tandem thingies,
no?



>
> >
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > to be stuck maintaining this beast forever so there had better be
a
> > large
> > > pool of talent available to take on the task.

----When every fish in the DP sea has a unique permutation of
skills, I don't think you will ever have a large pool of talent.
Unless, you restrict the skill set to COBOL.


> > ---Then stick to simple things like COBOL and SP2.
>
> You're making an arbitrary decision for me. Who says COBOL would be my
> choice of language?


----This is the COBOL forum, no? Also the need to find people with
a 'skill set' must cost business millions trying to find people
who know the right things. This cannot be good, can it? I would
rather train staff in COBOL and SP2 than all of those other thingies.
Actually, in my last CIO (MIS director) job for a $4B Wall street
company, we never hired experienced programmers - just trainees
in COBOL. They hadn't been poisoned by working in some horrible
environment using umpteen diverse thingies.




Who says that it would have a GUI interface? I was kind
> of partial to biochip implants and alpha wave recognition.
>

---Yes, but they would be GUI implants and GUI alpha waves.
Besides what does GUI mean. All it should be is readable,
easy-to-use, self explanatory reasonably nice to look at.

You're not designing things using a command line are you?


> >
> > >
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Why do you insist on assuming that all applications and users are
created
> equal? Would you expect your 8 year old nephew to be able to use your
COBOL
> development tools?

----No, but I would hope they could operate MY system so long as
they could read and understand the business.


They comprise an application but I doubt it is intended
> for some joe off the street. Does that mean it's design is flawed?
How would
> you redesign it so your nephew could use it?

---Actually, most of the the COBOL development tools are pretty awful.
The thing that comes with the Fujitsu compiler is a bad joke made
by someone who really has no idea.


>
> If I'm designing for a consumer then the emphasis will be on making
the
> system accessible and understandable to anybody off the street. They
need to
> be able to install it and watch it work. The software should lead
them by
> the hand to do the right thing.

----Exactly, lead them by the nose, that's my way. None of this
event-drive nonsense for me.


I'd probably design the same way for a
> business user if I expected them to be able to use the system without
any
> training.



----They should not require training.


On the other hand, if I was building an airline reservation system
> like SABER then I would give the business user a command line
interface and
> training to familiarize them with it's use. Why? Because they will
thank me
> for making it quick and easy for them to get exactly what they want
with as
> few keystrokes or mouse clicks as possible.



----Exactly. Down with the mouse. I never design for the mouse
except in a couple of odd cases (Touchscreens, for example which
my software uses which emulates the mouse).

> They also aren't left floundering on their own because the person
standing next to them can mentor
> them through the learning curve so the hand-holding requirements are
> different.
>
> Here's a real world example of where business needs differ from
consumers...
> online help. I was involved in the creation of a large system that
tracks
> movement of goods across the border. The system has something on the
order
> of 10,000 users. The powers that be decided they would need extensive
online
> help so we implemented it. After the first year we took a look at the
number
> of times online help had been accessed. The number of hits was 43 for
a
> whole year! That's on the order of 1 access per 200 users per year.
>

----I can relate to that. My systems have online help but no-one
hardly ever looks at it. Of course, each program in a system is
self-explanatory, so they don't really need the help.



> >
> > >
> > > FWIW, if I were writing a user-interactive business application I
> > would make
> > > sure that the architecture included some kind transaction
manager, a
> > > relational database and a clearly defined boundary between
> > presentation and
> > > business logic. That gives me lots of reliability and data
integrity.
> > ---OK, what would your choices be?
>
> That depends, but since you don't agree with much of what I've had to
say
> previously you won't appreciate why it depends. Maybe if your view of
> systems development was a little less myopic I'd enumerate some
options, but
> they'd just be buzzwords to you. I seem to recall you finding
relational
> databases to be useless



----Sorry, I don't think I said they were useless (I might
have said useless in a particular context, but I don't think
so.) There are reasons for having them, but I don't think the
reasons outweight the associated headaches and skill sets. -
except in special cases.


Thanks

Tony Dilworth


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.

It would be interesting to pick some specific targets to this question
such as:

What tools would you use to build an ATM machine?

What tools would you use to make a cash box at a BURSAR's office where
the main database is IDMS.

What tools would you use for a SCUD missile defense computer?

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Design Survey

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-20T00:00:00

Foodman wrote:
> 
> ---If the system you wrote was in COBOL then file-sharing is
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> What do I not understand?  Please clarify.

But everything has a cost.  If you have a transaction file which you
want to process at midnight in a batch process, why should you add the
overhead of file sharing or record locking?  You OWN that file and can
zoom through it without checking to see what anybody else is doing by
one simple lock.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Design Survey

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38FF4E55.E09311C1@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<8dkdlo$glo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <TSjL4.214950$Hq3.5434745@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <8dkmel$quh$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <SgnL4.215425$Hq3.5472898@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <8dmor1$4gs$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <dFDL4.219924$Hq3.5630117@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <8dnfpt$uub$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Foodman wrote:
> 
> > An uptime of 24/7 implies a different architecture than one that
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> I am talking about MY program. 

Ahh, but that isn't what the user's talking about when the user talks
dependability 24/7.  The user is talking about the whole system.  He
doesn't care about the components.  It makes no difference to him if he
can't do his job because of a bug in your program or because his hard
drive overheated.  If you are designing critical applications, you have
to design all aspects of that application. 

You don't sell programs, you sell solutions.  Never forget that.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Design Survey

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8dnubf$foc$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8dkdlo$glo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <TSjL4.214950$Hq3.5434745@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <8dkmel$quh$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <SgnL4.215425$Hq3.5472898@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <8dmor1$4gs$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <dFDL4.219924$Hq3.5630117@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <8dnfpt$uub$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38FF4E55.E09311C1@cusys.edu>`

In article <38FF4E55.E09311C1@cusys.edu>,
  Please, do, not, e-mail, replies, to, Howard, Brazee, post, them!!
wrote:
> Ahh, but that isn't what the user's talking about when the user talks
> dependability 24/7.  The user is talking about the whole system.  He
> doesn't care about the components.  It makes no difference to him if
he
> can't do his job because of a bug in your program or because his hard
> drive overheated.
Hi:

Agreed, but, I would think in the mainframe world that programmers
would not be responsible for operations. As it is our responsiblity
to make crash-proof programs, it is operation's responsibility to make
the environment crash-proof.  It would make sense then, to choose
the most dependable OS/hardware imaginable and then design your
application for that if uptime was paramount.


Thanks

Tony Dilworth



Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

Foodman wrote:
> 
> I'd probably design the same way for a
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> ----They should not require training.

It could be.  But if you're saying that in this undefined application,
the user should not require training, I'll take issue.  Training is one
of many costs of doing a business, and as with all costs, you like to
reduce it.  I haven't seen any systems where it has been eliminated.

In many cases, the job works far better if it isn't quite so intuitive
to LEARN, but more intuitive and easy to USE.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Design Survey

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 2000-04-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8dpodr$3ge$3@ssauraaa-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<8dkdlo$glo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <TSjL4.214950$Hq3.5434745@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <8dkmel$quh$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <SgnL4.215425$Hq3.5472898@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <8dmor1$4gs$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <dFDL4.219924$Hq3.5630117@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <8dnfpt$uub$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
<snip>

> ---If the system you wrote was in COBOL then file-sharing is
> trivial to implement. Maybe things are worse in the mainframe
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
    File.record locking may be trivial to implement, but it still costs
something.  If you are assured that you are the ONLY person accessing
these data files (as on a batch mode program), than many things
can be done to speed things along.  If you must allow for record locking,
than throughput tends to go way down.

    As an experiment, try writing a quick and dirty that reads a file
from start to finish.  Make the file long enough to time.   Time it
with access is exclusive and access is manual.

    Note:  some systems implement opportunistic locking.  To disable this,
open the same file in a second process (you need not read).


<big snip>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Design Survey

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38FF1D21.C185C007@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<8dkdlo$glo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <TSjL4.214950$Hq3.5434745@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <8dkmel$quh$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <SgnL4.215425$Hq3.5472898@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <8dmor1$4gs$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Foodman wrote:
> 

> ---Sorry, I don't agree. The only consideration should be hardware,
> not software. And, the software should be HIGHLY PORTABLE to deal
> with that eventuality.

That is the opposite of what you told me.  But hardware is NEVER the
only consideration, because hardware is not what this business is about. 

> > * uptime requirements. Hardware and software choices change if I need
> > reliability.
> ---Uptime should be 100% 24/7, period. Are you suggesting that you
> would design a less reliable system depending on something?

What kind of system are you talking about, a traffic light?  I work with
mainframes, and even there there doesn't exist a "100% 24/7 period"
uptime system.  I design my systems around reality, not fantasy.

> 
> >
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> you can do regardless?

The "best I can do" within what parameters?   Give me 40 years and I
will do better than if you give me 5 days.  Except that "the best I can
do" means that the product is cost effective for my customers.  So I
leave out whistles which fail this criterion.

But in the context of your reply - are you implying that "the best I can
do" is a GUI when the end user isn't a human being?  Now who's being
silly?


> > * is the application for business users, consumers, military,
> internet,
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> going to be different than payroll.

You give up some portability and maintainability if you have very high
efficiency requirements, maybe choosing to have assembly language
subroutines, and secondary databases for update and retrieval.  That
complexity costs but is sometimes necessary.

EVERYTHING is a trade off.  EVERY choice you make has a downside and an
upside.  To design a system, you have to evaluate each downside and
upside against the particular conditions of your needs.  Why do you
think there are so many tools out there?
```

<SNIP>
> > > * uptime requirements. Hardware and software choices change if I need
> > > reliability.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
    In practice, 24/7 means that things like backup and
hardware repair/maintance will need to be done
without disrupting system availability.

    It might reduce capacity, but not eliminate it totally.
Some DB products allow backup and normal operation
at the same time.  I believe that they do this by opening
the normal database in read only mode, and logging changes
to a temporary database.

    Hardware problems can be handled in a number of ways, ranging
from hot swappable disk drives to redundant hosts.


<MAJOR SNIP>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Design Survey

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8dl9bg$j2q$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8dkdlo$glo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <TSjL4.214950$Hq3.5434745@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <8dkmel$quh$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Others have given some replies, but the following are the MINIMUM
"requirements" that I would need to know - before deciding on "tools" (or
even environments and platforms).

1) What types of input will there be?
   (Screen/user input?  Files/databases from other systems? "machine" input
(such as POS, scanner, etc)

2) What types of output are we expecting?
  (screens? reports? updated "masterfile"/databases? commands to other
"machinery")

3) Is the "business" logic heavily mathematical? table-driven? highly
volatile or quite stable? Subject to government requirements?  Subject to
international constraints/demands?

4) What type of "response" or "run" times are expected?
  (sub-second screen response time?  multi-hour "nightly runs"? Weekly,
monthly? quarterly reports/updates?

5) Single user?  Multi-user?  no "real-time" users? (security restrictions?
government audit trails required? government bidding requirements?)

6) What hardware and software is already available for the "users"?  What
type of procedures are we replacing/enhancing/adding?  (Are their "outside"
platform/software requirements?)

   ***

Actually, I would probably ask "generally" what type of application are we
talking about BEFORE even trying to ask any of the above questions.
```

pencil and paper; and would probably be obsolete before finished. <G>

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Design Survey

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8dmpej$55r$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8dkdlo$glo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <TSjL4.214950$Hq3.5434745@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <8dkmel$quh$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8dl9bg$j2q$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
In article <8dl9bg$j2q$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>,
  "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:


Hi:

Thanks for taking the time to respond.



> 1) What types of input will there be?
>    (Screen/user input?  Files/databases from other systems? "machine"
input
> (such as POS, scanner, etc)

---The survey said it would not use anything from anywhere else.
Regarding POS,  all of the POS gadgets I am aware of (I sell POS
software) usually feed their input to the keyboard buffer. For example,
if you have a barcode reader attached to your PC and scan something,
the stuff you scan will be in the keyboard buffer so you can use
ordinary input statements to access it. So, you don't really need
to design for POS hardware.

>
> 2) What types of output are we expecting?
>   (screens? reports? updated "masterfile"/databases? commands to other
> "machinery")

---Just ordinary reports and screen displays. No other 'machinery'.
>
> 3) Is the "business" logic heavily mathematical? table-driven? highly
> volatile or quite stable? Subject to government requirements?
Subject to
> international constraints/demands?

---I don't see how something like government requirements affects
your choice of tools. If it were volatile, the system should reflect
that.

>
> 4) What type of "response" or "run" times are expected?
>   (sub-second screen response time?  multi-hour "nightly runs"?
Weekly,
> monthly? quarterly reports/updates?

---User should be able to run reports whenever they feel like it.
Response time should be immediate.

>
> 5) Single user?  Multi-user?  no "real-time" users? (security
restrictions?
> government audit trails required? government bidding requirements?)
>
---Since the difference between multi-user and single-user is
only a matter of file and record locking, why not design every
system for multi-user?


> 6) What hardware and software is already available for the "users"?
What
> type of procedures are we replacing/enhancing/adding?  (Are
their "outside"
> platform/software requirements?)

---In the survey, you get to choose hardware and tools.
>
>    ***
>
> Actually, I would probably ask "generally" what type of application
are we
> talking about BEFORE even trying to ask any of the above questions.
>
> --

---I'm sorry Bill, but I always seem to get not what I expected.
I guess that I wasn't specific enough.  We could always fall back
on the Time-Clock system in the Great Programming Contest. Perhaps
that is what I should have asked.

Nevertheless, the responses are interesting.


Thanks

TOny Dilworth


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

> >
> > 5) Single user?  Multi-user?  no "real-time" users? (security
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Because not all software supports file-sharing/record-locking BECAUSE not all
operating systems/environments do not support simultaneous multi-user use.  I
think you are too PC/UNIX "biased" to see how much of today's business data
processing works (i.e the VAST quantity of single-threaded batch
applications)
```

Hi:

As you probably remember, I have been 'At Home Alone' for ages.
But, I find it surprising that there are software systems in use
in 2000 which do not provide for multi-user. I am not PC-biased
but COBOL-biased, my COBOL just happens to run on the PC.

Are those single-thread thingies doing batch processing
on flat files?

Thanks

TOny Dilworth

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Design Survey

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8dno9q$nts$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8dkdlo$glo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <TSjL4.214950$Hq3.5434745@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <8dkmel$quh$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8dl9bg$j2q$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <8dmpej$55r$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8dna7g$q9e$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <8dncoh$rd9$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I think you will find that the file-sharing/record-locking syntax in your PC
COBOLs is all carefully marked as EXTENSIONS.  Furthermore, not even all the
PC COBOLs provide them (compare IBM VisualAge COBOL - for example).

Batch thingies may access "flat files", indexed files, databases
(hierarchical and relational) and even "blobs" (I think that is one term for
image files - such as insurance company handwritten accident reports)
```

Hi:

No, I didn't know that they were extensions. I have been using
the Microsoft compiler and the subsequent MF versions since 1983
(on the Tandy 2000). Every version had the file/record locking stuff.

I just took it for granted that nobody would release a compiler
which did not have them - why would they?.

(Although what you say about IBM's Visual Age COBOL does not
surprise me. As I have mentioned before, I firmly believe
that IBM deliberately 'cripples' their software in order
to sell either more hardware or more software.)

Thanks

TOny Dilworth

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Design Survey

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-04-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39007584.5287AEC3@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<8dkdlo$glo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <TSjL4.214950$Hq3.5434745@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <8dkmel$quh$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8dl9bg$j2q$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <8dmpej$55r$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8dna7g$q9e$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <8dncoh$rd9$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8dno9q$nts$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <8dpcj1$vdg$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8dpqej$enj$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> I think that you will find almost (well at least most) of the current
> mainframe COBOL compilers have no "file-sharing/record-locking" included in
> the compiler.  Check out Unisys, HP, Tandem, Dec - among others.

I do lots of record locking via database calls, but that doesn't count. 
I am trying to remember how I have handled this before databases.  

I have used relative files for concurrent data entry.  A transaction
read record #1, updated a pointer, then wrote to the appropriate
record.  Somehow we must have handled what happened when two programs
tried to use record #1.  It was a long time ago.  I don't think I ever
did concurrent updates of a keyed file.
```

news:39007584.5287AEC3@cusys.edu...
> "William M. Klein" wrote:
> >
> > I think that you will find almost (well at least most) of the current
> > mainframe COBOL compilers have no "file-sharing/record-locking" included
in
> > the compiler.  Check out Unisys, HP, Tandem, Dec - among others.
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> did concurrent updates of a keyed file.

Howard,
  As I recall, you are using IBM mainframes.  In those environments you would
normally use JCL (DISP= options) and/or VSAM SHAREOPTIONS to handle this.
However, this wouldn't give you much (if any) control within your COBOL
program to handle "in-use" situations.  (Obviously, CICS and IMS have their
own ways of doing this as well)
```

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>

"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> > I have used relative files for concurrent data entry.  A transaction
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> own ways of doing this as well)

The Relative files I used was on a Univac 9030, although I converted
them to VSAM when the company switched to IBM.  I have worked on IBM
about half of my mainframe life - but never with CICS.  IMS is a
database and mainframe databases handle their own record locking
externally to COBOL.
```

Since this is fantasy, not reality ...

For a small system, MacOS X on iMac.

For a medium system, MacOS X Server with WebObjects
and DB2; iMac as the client.

For a large system, an IBM mainframe server with DB2 as
a backend to multiple MacOS X Servers with WebObjects;
iMac as the client..

For the language, COBOL and C++. OK, COBOL is not
available on Mac; but this is a fantasy!
------------------
Rick Smith

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>

```
Foodman wrote:
> 
> Question:  If you were to design a BRAND NEW system today,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

I design new software on:  Mainframe, Unix and PC, mostly portable
across all.  Once you get the hang of it is not that difficult.

For my perfect user driven application  I would use a mainframe DB2
database server with a Unix Web server with Java script web browser
front end.

For my perfect massive data batch run, definitely mainframe.

For my perfect user front end tool, either xwindows unix or windows. 
(I have never coded xwindows but it has GOT to be better the M$)

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

#### ↳ Re: Design Survey

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 2000-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8dmh6l$hh3$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<8dkdlo$glo$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
My development platform would initially be WinNT, and the runtime platform
would be, where the money was.

As Re-Use is illegal I would look at the application in a business-case
view, and start to produce standards for the branch. Later on I would
modify/add application-specific facilities.

My development tools would be NetExpress for all PC-related facilities, AAI
for access to mainframes or other networks and Mainframe Express for
Mainframe development.

Data access would be SQL-based. DB2 if Mainframe is involved or Oracle if
the application is mainly Internetbased or the runtime platform shows to be
UNIX.

Paper-output will be designed with Flexus FormPrint, and Bob will have to
support whatever runtime platform I choose (think of the money).

The analysis will be made in my very own OO-improved 'Martin Yourkson'. The
improvements are in 2 areas. First improvement is to discover the users
unspoken wishes, and the second is to the real world - my real world is
modified to cover as many customers as possible, by individual
configuration.

BTW: I will have to expand the columns in my invoice where the amounts are.

:-)

Ib



Foodman skrev i meddelelsen <8dkdlo$glo$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>
>
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Design Survey

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 2000-04-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8dpk9t$j4v$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<8dkdlo$glo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8dmh6l$hh3$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <8dmpq4$5nc$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
That's a serious question. And that depends...
OO is not the easiest thing to implement on a mainframe. Well to be honest,
I would like to have all business logic on the PC (network), but as i said -
I'll go for the money.

My intensions are to make a system on one platform, and send requests to
other platforms.

A mainframe system could distribute data to PC- or Unix systems.
Paper-output could easily take place there. Updates from external sources
could easily be executed on the mainframe and distributed to other systems.
In this case 98 % of the programs would be developed for mainframe.

A PC system could easily get company data from the mainframe and/or UNIX
systems. If that is the case, 98 % of the programs would be developed for
PC's

I hope, you se the point. My system will be where the customer  wants/needs
it. I am *not* running on down-/uploaded data. I will get the data from the
platform that holds it.

I am definately *not* producing an application for portation. I'm old, lazy
and clever. I will not support all platforms, and I don't want to maintain
an application in numerous versions and platforms.

Regards
Ib

<a id="t-1490a01426"></a>
## Thread: Connecting to NetExpress' CCITCP — 3 msgs

### Connecting to NetExpress' CCITCP

- **From:** "Notta Chance" <NottaChance@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83lL4.2910$wk.1523797@news1.mia>`

```
I am writing a client in Delphi 5.0 that accesses a server written in the
latest version of NetExress. The server is using some sort of communication
interface called CCI (Common Communication Interface?) over a TCP-IP
connection. I can access the server by using a dll written by a cobol
programmer, but that dll need 8 other dll's from the MF Cobol runtime
library...


What I would like to know is:

Is there any way to talk to a CCITCP server from another platform using a
standard socket connection, and what has to be done to accomplish this. (If
I try to open the IP:Port through winsock I get a message saying the 'server
actively refused the connection'. So I assume there would be something more
than just opening the socket and sending the data.)

Any assistance would be greatly appreciated!
Richard
```

#### ↳ Re: Connecting to NetExpress' CCITCP

- **From:** "Lucas, Todd" <todd.lucas@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38fe15f5_1@news1.prserv.net>`
- **References:** `<83lL4.2910$wk.1523797@news1.mia>`

```
Good luck!  The MF Common Communications Interface (CCI) is a
proprietary interface - I seriously doubt that they (MF) will give you
access to the API needed in order to interface with it.  I never really
cared much for it myself, as I code native TCP calls as well.  Again,
Good luck getting something out of the MF people...

TL

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-20T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<8dnpso$iv5$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

I may be missing something, but the following is the reply that I received
from one of my usually reliable sources,

"Bill, CCI is a published programming interface. should all be listed in the
documentation. upsides of using CCI are a) it's much simpler than sockets
programming, and b) it's protocol independent."
```

## Thread: Sorting...on Unix using MF COBOL 4.1 — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Sorting...on Unix using MF COBOL 4.1

- **From:** Stefan Meyer <meyerst@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8dk715$9gg$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
After reading all the messages about sorting I tried to improve my old
inherited progs because there are many big sorts in.

So I took my MF books and found that CALLSORT"EXTSM" (Chapter 17. PG to
File Handling "Callable Sort Module") will improve sorting.

Ok, I'll try it....reading an ISAM-File with approx. 160 000 records
and sorted all records (key-Length 20 Bytes and 100 Bytes for the rest)
with another order than defined thru primary key (not reverse, more
random)

using:

Sort Sortfile Sort-Key Ascending
 input procedure InSort
 output procedure OutSort

InSort: Read all records and release them
OutSort: Just return all sorted records

The only difference was defining CALLSORT"EXTSM" on the first source
line or not.

I run both testprogs one after another.

Result: Not a single second difference.

Fortunately I'm able to use the Unix machine alone. So that no other
time consuming processes are running.

Please, what did I do wrong?
Is something missing (what's not in my books)?


TIA - Stefan  Meyer


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Sorting...on Unix using MF COBOL 4.1

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8dlsmc06c1@enews3.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<8dk715$9gg$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
It just shows you that a good sort utility takes care of these details.  I
tend not to waste too much time trying to tune a sort.

In OS390 and COBOL II, if I use the USING/GIVING structure instead of the
PROCEDURE structure, the compiler makes the program eligible for the FASTSRT
option.  But I have never tested this against the regular sort, and I don't
know what effect it would have on your UNIX box.  I would check the compiler
messages.

Jeff

<a id="t-8c7bf1a203"></a>
## Thread: Sorting dilema... — 3 msgs

### Re: Sorting dilema...

- **From:** "Eileen Preston" <epreston@lear.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s8fdb665.003@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```


>>> Oscar T. Grouch 04/12 11:00 am >>>
 
> In article <Tb_I4.16808$0o4.113320@iad-read.news.verio.net>,
>   docdwarf@clark.net () wrote:
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.

Perhaps the flat file is shipped from some other system

I once worked on a telephone billing system that handled several hundred
million records per month. The records came from the telephone switchgear,
via some unix boxes, to the mainframe. The records arrived sorted
chronologically (i.e. in the order in which they were created). The result
was your telephone bill.

If you seriously believe that creating indexed files prior to processing is
the correct way to handle that kind of volume then you have a lot to learn
about large scale systems development.

- Karl

I concer with Karl.  The amount of data that I have to work with would preclude the use of VSAM (which are indexed files - ISAM on mainframes died years ago) as well.  And yes Tony - a flat file to a mainframer is a sequential file in no particular order etc aka QSAM to some.

Anyway - to take my data entry files and create a VSAM I'd have to write a program (COBOL of course :D) to write the records to the file.  Since these may not be in key sequence I have to make sure the size of the VSAM file when defined allowed for all the nasty splits such an operation could produce as the file is being written to.  I have no idea if an ISAM file on a PC has such things as CI or CS splits but VSAM on mainframes do and they cause loss of space for the file.  Anyway because of all these splits processing is slowed down.

Now - sorting the flat file into key sequence and using a utility to load the file makes a nice clean file and takes about 20% of the time.  Of course once the file is sorted it may not even be necessary to create a VSAM file because probably all you're doing is a report and now that the file is in order by the control breaks the program for the report is COBOL 101 (for most students - we've seen examples of others here).  

VSAM is great for master files or when it is necessary to access the file randomly - otherwise give me the good ol' sort.

Eileen

 and create a VSAM I'd have to write a program (COBOL of course :D) to

I have to make sure the size of the VSAM file when defined allowed for
all the nasty splits such an operation could produce as the file is
being written to.  I have no idea if an ISAM file on a PC has such
things as CI or CS splits but VSAM on mainframes do and they cause loss
of space for the file.  Anyway because of all these splits processing
is slowed down.


----I have always been convinced that IBM wrote deliberately
inefficient software in order to sell faster hardware/more disks,
etc. E.g., ISAM vis-a-vis CICS


>
> Now - sorting the flat file into key sequence and using a utility to
load the file makes a nice clean file and takes about 20% of the time.
Of course once the file is sorted it may not even be necessary to
create a VSAM file because probably all you're doing is a report and
now that the file is in order by the control breaks the program for the
report is COBOL 101 (for most students - we've seen examples of others
here).

----It does seem that your data-entry files are flat?  Anyway,
for example, would your data-entry method for an Accounts Receivable
system create flat files, or, would the entered data be posted
DIRECTLY to the A/R file thus eliminating any sorting or stuff?

Thanks

Tony Dilworth


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting dilema...

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38FF24A9.DD2BAFBA@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<s8fdb665.003@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM> <8dms42$87s$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Transaction files can be created in time-date sequence by adding a new
record at the end of the file.  This is probably the quickest and
easiest way to get that record stored outside of some database tricks. 

The second quickest is using a relative file, but often that requires
two I-Os unless you are a single user.  If you are a single user, it is
probably the quickest.

Anyway, there is no updating of keys, slowing things down.  On the other
hand, accessing data is slow, as you have to do a linear search for what
you are looking for.  But often you don't care.  That file gets sorted
and fed into another program which processes further.

I have even seen where a nightly job takes an existing keyed file and
copies it to a flat file.  This gets sorted with the daily transactions,
and then the keyed file gets recreated from this sorted combo.  This is
done to make the searches more efficient, at the cost of not having
today's data available.
```

## Thread: New bug reporting tool available — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration)_

### New bug reporting tool available

- **From:** Jens Matschke <Jens.Matschke@novosys.de>
- **Date:** 2000-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38FDCB84.D16772F3@novosys.de>`

```
The Novosys EDV GmbH is introducing a brand new bug reporting tool!

Buggy 1.0 is a multiuser database program designed specifically for
keeping track of bugs in your program(s). Additionally you can keep
track of feature requests (enhancements) and support requests. Instead
of writing a bunch of sticky notes to track which bugs you have and have
not fixed, you can let Buggy keep all of that information organized for
you. You can easily keep track of multiple projects/software packages
and multiple releases so you'll know exactly which changes were made in
each version of the software.
A Security System is included to control who has access to Buggy.
Exporting data to HTML and CSV document files, enhanced filtering and
sorting on all fields and customizable charts/graphs are also included.
A Textsearch engine will assist you to always find the data you want.

Further product information and a fully functional 30-day trial release
can be downloaded from www.novosys.de/Buggy/Buggy.html.

Novosys EDV GmbH

Jens Matschke
```

## Thread: Data Dictionary creation MF ISAM ODBC Crystal Reports — 11 this month / 12 total

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases) · [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### Data Dictionary creation MF ISAM ODBC Crystal Reports

- **From:** "Phil Lee" <plee@orbis.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.accounting
- **Message-ID:** `<sfu36a57l6e180@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Hi, looking for the best way to create data dictionaries with legacy
commercial MF Cobol / ISAM accounting apps (Real World) for ODBC with report
writers such as Crystal Reports.

Have looked at Transoft U/SQL and IQ Reports and Access (CAI / Sterling) and
they both require the source code for the File Definition FD statements.
Looked at Liant Relativity spec sheet and FD are required too.

Has anyone reverse engineered and built data dictionaries from the data
files and indexes by themselves?

Phil, Sys Engr, Minneapolis, Silicon Tundra, plee@orbis.net
```

#### ↳ Re: Data Dictionary creation MF ISAM ODBC Crystal Reports

- **From:** "David" <david@timecalc.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.accounting
- **Message-ID:** `<riML4.30$mO6.2081729545@typhoon.cei.net>`
- **References:** `<sfu36a57l6e180@corp.supernews.com>`

```
How about just getting a source license?  I have a feeling any reverse
engineering would be a violation of the license agreement.

The file structures for RW products used to be quite simple -- all fixed
length with very few items stored as COMP (binary, packed or other) data
types -- if you could figure out the record length (the minimum info you'd
have to have to read the ISAM files), you could almost work out the layout
details by visual inspection of the data.  However, I'm quite certain this
would no longer be the case.

Short of getting the source, I don't have any real suggestion for you..

#### ↳ Re: Data Dictionary creation MF ISAM ODBC Crystal Reports

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.accounting
- **Message-ID:** `<gMEL4.220166$Hq3.5672653@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<sfu36a57l6e180@corp.supernews.com>`

```
It would be extremely difficult to reverse engineer from the data files in
any sort of automated fashion. While it is possible to extract the position
and length of the indices, it is not (usually) possible to determine the
length and position of any other fields other than through intimate
knowledge of the data that is being stored. For all fields, including
indices, the name of the field is not (usually) stored in the data file
itself, nor is the scale of a numeric items (where the decimal point goes).

If you have some knowledge of the data you could reconstruct the copybook
using Micro Focus Data File Editor and build up your own structure using the
associated structure editor.

Since you don't have the source code I will make the assumption that you got
this software from some vendor rather than developing it in-house (assuming
that you lost all your source code would be insulting). If so, you may want
to explain to the vendor what it is you are trying to do and ask them about
supplying the copybooks for the data files. Even if they ask for money it's
probably cheaper and less error-prone than trying to reverse engineer the
data files.

-Karl

Hi, thanks all for your replies. Also see the attached post relating to my
issue. I am going to try using Monarch Imaging Systems', www.monarchis.com ,
Transport ODBC v3. It claims to take any "print to file" report and
transform the data into ODBC data base fields. They have a functional demo
download and then I can use Crystal Reports on the DB in MS Access.

Anybody have further comments on this approach as a solution to the issue?
Any other "scan a report" app vendors that do similar function in
transforming a legacy ISAM DB into an ODBC DB? While not production worthy,
this may work for a few custom monthly reports, especially since it claims
to have automation features..

Phil, Sys Engr, Minneapolis, Silicon Tundra, plee@orbis.net

=======
Re: Recovery of Microfocus Cobol FDs  sal@lasermax.com  wrote on 1998/03/01
in news:alt.cobol

Jan Rooth wrote:
>
> Hello,
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> jan@northfork.net

Jan-

Without a data dictionary, unless you are really really lucky it is
unlikely that you are going to be able to extract the data you want
directly from the COBOL data files.  However, if the data you want to
extract is available on any of the reports available from the software
it is usually very easy to:

1. Print the report(s) to disk files,
2. Use a product such as "Monarch" to extract the data from the report
and store it in a usable disk file.

Sal

#### ↳ Re: Data Dictionary creation MF ISAM ODBC Crystal Reports

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 2000-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.accounting
- **Message-ID:** `<FFFL4.17488$0o4.120823@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<sfu36a57l6e180@corp.supernews.com>`

```
In article <sfu36a57l6e180@corp.supernews.com>,
Phil Lee <plee@orbis.net> wrote:
>Hi, looking for the best way to create data dictionaries with legacy
>commercial MF Cobol / ISAM accounting apps (Real World) for ODBC with report
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>files and indexes by themselves?

I'se jes' a COBOL-codin' fool, but... I cannot see how anything useful can
be generated from files and indices any other way.

Source code (of which an FD is a part) will tell you how a record is
broken up into FIRST-NAME, MIDDLE-INITIAL, LAST-NAME, HOME-PHONE, SSN and
the like; if all one has is a file then all one knows that positions
(mm) through (nn) have alphabetic data (usually), positions (oo) through
(pp) have numeric data (sometimes)... and this gets *really* nasty if one
has multiple record types where the same positions hold different kinds of
data.

In short... the files contain data, the code can contain information about
the data.

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: Data Dictionary creation MF ISAM ODBC Crystal Reports

- **From:** Vadim Maslov <vadik@siber.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.accounting
- **Message-ID:** `<38FFD415.C1C02B28@siber.com>`
- **References:** `<sfu36a57l6e180@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Phil Lee wrote:
> 
> Hi, looking for the best way to create data dictionaries with legacy
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Phil, Sys Engr, Minneapolis, Silicon Tundra, plee@orbis.net

Theoretically it is not possible but we do it
by guessing and gathering some statistics.

Siber Systems has DataGuess program 
http://www.siber.com/sct/datafile/data-guess.html
that makes a fairly good effort to guess FD
just by looking at data file.

It produces FDD and RDD file.
RDD file describes record and you can 
edit it manually to reflect you better 
human understanding of data file.

Feel free to send us you data file samples
so that we can feed them to DataGuess or 
just them to the program yourself.

Vadim Maslov
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Data Dictionary creation MF ISAM ODBC Crystal Reports

- **From:** "Phil Lee" <plee@orbis.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.accounting

```
Hi, just read that Crystal Reports v7 has a built-in Document Import Tool.
This means that it can import and convert reports from a legacy DOS
accounting app like Real World (RW) Classic v8.4  It converts the
print-to-file to a MS Access compatible ODBC file. One can save the report
layout conversion file so that a particular report can be converted over and
over again. Then Crystal Reports can select and group desired data, and
other data files, to your heart's content.

Anybody use this facility? Is this enhanced in Crystal Reports 2000?

This workaround would be a way to convert accounting data without having the
proprietary RW Cobol source FD File Definition statements.

There is a chapter "Importing Legacy Reports with the Document Import Tool,"
p 364-82 in George Peck's "Crystal Reports 7: The Complete Reference,"
Osborne, 99, ISBN 0-07-211978-0, 771ppgs + CD, $50 list. It appears to be a
low end, low-cost solution compared to MonarchIS.com Transform ODBC v3
($400), DataJunction.com $400, Transoft U/SQL $1K++, Liant Relativity $1K+,
and RW / IQ Reports ($400+).

Real World Classic v8 has an MF Cobol ISAM DB, so Crystal Reports is
incompatible because it queries relational ODBC DBs. Real World only offers
the legacy IQ SW Reports and Access product along with limited FD source. RW
also offers an OEM Transoft U/SQL, but I don't know of anybody who has tried
using it.

Phil, Sys Engr, Minneapolis, Silicon Tundra, plee@orbis.net

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Data Dictionary creation MF ISAM ODBC Crystal Reports

- **From:** chris_muller@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-04-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.accounting
- **Message-ID:** `<8e57ap$6vs$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<sfu36a57l6e180@corp.supernews.com> <3900dbba.17168981@news.primenet.com> <sga8o6jccdo9@corp.supernews.com>`

```
If you're looking for a real simple/intuitive (and FREE) way to "hack"
mainframe-style record layouts on a PC. Take a look at GenScript in the
download area of our website. It creates two files: a script file for
actually converting the file contents to ascii, and also a "project"
file that makes it easy to modify the layout. The project file is in a
pretty simple ascii format, and you could (maybe) translate it into
your data dictionary format...

Perhaps worth a look.

Chris Muller
www.mullermedia.com

posted information on this a while ago.

requests that you let him know if you plan on attending any or all of this
meeting.

attend.

    17 � 19 May 2000 - J4
    21 � 23 May 2000 - WG4
    23 � 25 May 2000 - J4

subject had "Newbury" in it) - or send me email and I will try and tell you
more.

## Thread: MVS: GETMAIN / FREEMAIN — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### MVS: GETMAIN / FREEMAIN

- **From:** shine98@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8do0tp$ick$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
How would one do a 'GETMAIN' and 'FREEMAIN' from a MVS batch program.
All the references I see do a 'EXEC CICS'. Code samples appreciated.

~shine


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: GETMAIN / FREEMAIN

- **From:** "Lucas, Todd" <todd.lucas@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38ff8cd3_4@news1.prserv.net>`
- **References:** `<8do0tp$ick$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
You can do this via an assembler program - I like the STORAGE macro,
which allows you to OBTAIN and RELEASE memory.  It also allows you to
specify above / below the line storage, and boundary alignments.

***********************************************************************
* OBTAIN STORAGE FOR THE REQUESTED LENGTH:                            *
***********************************************************************
OBTSTG   MVC   FULLWORD,PARMSLEN       LOAD LENGTH TO FULLWORD
         L     R6,FULLWORD             LOAD REQUESTED STORAGE LENGTH
         MVC   PARMADDR,=F'0'          INIT PARM ADDRESS
         STORAGE OBTAIN,LENGTH=(R6),ADDR=STGADDR,COND=YES,             X
               LOC=BELOW,BNDRY=DBLWD
         LTR   R15,R15                 GOOD STORAGE OBTAIN?
         BZ    OBTGOOD                 YES - BRANCH AROUND ERR PROCESS

and to release storage:

***********************************************************************
* RELEASE STORAGE STARTING AT THE GIVEN ADDRESS FOR REQUESTED LENGTH: *
***********************************************************************
RELSTG   MVC   STGADDR,PARMADDR        LOAD REQUESTED STORAGE ADDRESS
         MVC   FULLWORD,PARMSLEN       LOAD LENGTH TO FULLWORD
         L     R6,FULLWORD             LOAD REQUESTED STORAGE LENGTH
         STORAGE RELEASE,LENGTH=(R6),ADDR=STGADDR,COND=YES
         LTR   R15,R15                 GOOD STORAGE RELEASE?
         BZ    RELGOOD                 YES - BRANCH AROUND ERR PROCESS


Todd Lucas
E-Mail:  Todd.Lucas@C-Cubed.net

- **From:** WOB Consulting <wobconsult@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8do5vs$nvu$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8do0tp$ick$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In article <8do0tp$ick$1@nnrp1.deja.com>,
  shine98@my-deja.com wrote:
> How would one do a 'GETMAIN' and 'FREEMAIN' from a MVS batch program.
> All the references I see do a 'EXEC CICS'. Code samples appreciated.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

        YREGS                           MVS REGISTER-MACRO
        STM	R14,R1,REGSAVE		SAVE R14 THRU R1
        L 	R0,=A(L'STGAREA)	LOAD STORAGE LENGTH
        GETMAIN RU,LV=(0),LOC=ANY	GET STORAGE, ANY LOCATION
        LR	R9,R1		        STORAGE ADDRESSABILITY
        USING	STGDSECT,R9		INFORM ASSEMBLER
        ST      R9,R9SAVE		SAVE STORAGE ADDRESS
        LR      R0,R9			LOAD 'RECVING' ADDRESS
        L       R1,=A(L'STGAREA)	LOAD 'RECVING' LENGTH
        SR      R14,R14			LOAD 'SENDING' ADDRESS
        LA      R15,X'40'               LOAD 'SENDING' LENGTH
        SLL     R15,24                  SHIFT TO HIGH-ORDER
        MVCL    R0,R14			INITIALIZE STORAGE TO SPACES
        LM      R14,R1,REGSAVE		RESTORE R14 THRU R1
        XC      REGSAVE(72),REGSAVE	CLEAR REGISTER SAVEAREA
********
******** SOME PROCESSING IS DONE HERE
********
        STM	R0,R1,REGSAVE 		SAVE R0 AND R1
        L 	R0,=A(L'STGAREA)	LOAD STORAGE LENGTH
        L	R1,R9SAVE		LOAD STORAGE ADDRESS
        FREEMAIN RU,LV=(0),A=(1)	FREE THE STORAGE
        LM	R0,R1,REGSAVE		RESTORE R0 AND R1
        XC	REGSAVE(72),REGSAVE	CLEAR REGISTER SAVEAREA
        SR	R9,R9			CLEAR FORMER DSECT BASE

REGSAVE	DS	18F
R9SAVE  DS      F
STGDSECT DSECT
STGAREA	DS	CL32768

HTH....

WOB


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MVS: GETMAIN / FREEMAIN

- **From:** WOB Consulting <wobconsult@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8dqeim$3h9$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8do0tp$ick$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8do5vs$nvu$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8dpkvq$7t8$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In article <8dpkvq$7t8$1@nnrp1.deja.com>,
  shine98@my-deja.com wrote:
> I really appreciate your response but I was thinking something more in
> the line of: call 'GETMAIN' using this that the-other. Is this
possible?
> Could I assemble your code into a subroutine that I could call? I'll
> have to admit that this is a little out of my usual relm, I just an
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Two things:

01) There is a slight Boo-Boo in my FREEMAIN MACRO coding. Instead of
loading R1 with the R9SAVE fullword, just code A=R9SAVE (no parenthesis
around R9SAVE as it is NOT a register). If you use R1 in my example,
you'll get a S0C4, because the MACRO expansion clears R1 BEFORE issuing
the SVC 120. This is what happens when you go by your memory (what's
that ?) :-)

02) Are you running "LE" (Language Environment)? If so, check out the
Callable Service Routine "CEEGTST" (Get Heap Storage). Refer to Book
Manager at:

http://www.s390.ibm.com/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/library, Book CEEA3020
(SC28-1940-08)

========================================================================

If you just need storage for a STEP, an implicit FREEMAIN is done when
you're STEP completes. It would be mucho overhead to CALL the sub-
program numerous times to Get/Free Storage. I would imagine, you'd call
the sub-program once, get the storage address and return it to your
COBOL program ? Unless I don't understand, COBOL2 (or greater) Working-
Storage should be able to handle large chunks of data, unless this
request has some unique requirements. I trust you're NOT using this
storage for CICS ?

Rather than clutter up C.L.C. with ASSEMBLER stuff, E-Mail me off-line,
explaining exactly what you want to do with this storage. I'll try to
give you an answer sometime next week after Easter.

Regards,

WOB,
Atlanta


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

<a id="t-ff5e72d21c"></a>
## Thread: OS/390 COBOL Programmers — 2 this month / 21 total

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

_Thread started [2000-03](2000-03.md#t-ff5e72d21c)._

#### ↳ Re: OS/390 COBOL Programmers

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38FF8AB8.BB1FACEC@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<20000313095137.22475.00000615@ng-cn1.aol.com>`

```
I suspect that many of the COBOL programmers are doing it a way station
on the corporate ladder, not as a career.  Further the management
attitude is that they should work with what they have and no provision
is made for them to influence the vendor or the standard.  My belief is
that if companies had put minimal effort into defining what they needed
in a business language and working to get it, we would not have the
proliferation of proprietary languages such as DYL280.  

In terms of getting improvements and the business case for those
improvements, my belief is that one of the priorities is anything that
allows us to use existing tools written in other languages.  That means
data types recognized by C/C++ programs along with the calling
conventions for them, object oriented syntax that allows
interoperability with standard class libraries written in other
languages and more generalization of existing structures.  We will have
to be able to handle the double byte character sets, especially Unicode
and the multi-byte superset ISO 10646, XML, and locale sensitive field
comparisons.  This last will be a performance hog even for English let
alone some of the Latin and Germanic languages where there are two
different ways of representing the same accented letter of the alphabet
(see the Unicode standard for mind numbing details).

In addition I would like to see the FD interface enhanced such that the
same select statement and FD can be used to access a true flat file, an
indexed file where the sequence is assumed to be that of the primary
keys, a relational database view where the sequence is implied in the
view or any other data entity where records could be logically read or
written using the information in the SELECT and FD.  This could include
accessing a screen where the glue that connects the file to the screen
also takes care of any data transformations.  The Guide/SHARE Language
Futures Task Force implicitly asked for this in their final report
published in 1984.  

Lastly, I believe that packages are becoming more important because of
the large cost of designing and maintaining systems.  Thus the software
developers will have to make known their needs to the various vendors in
a way that conveys to those vendors the fact that this also affects many
customers of that vendor besides the developer.  

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services Inc.,
cfmtech@istar.ca        

S Comstock wrote:
> 
> Hi,
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
> USA
```

<a id="t-aa2c2d4199"></a>
## Thread: Need Help with Cobol Code — 11 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Need Help with Cobol Code

- **From:** "Ron" <rjn@law.syr.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38ff5850.0@news.syr.edu>`

```
I am having a problem removing - ' from a last name field.
Can someone help me.

Smith-Jones   to SmithJones

I have a statement in my procedure section that says

INSPECT LAST-NAME REPLACING ALL "-" BY " ".
INSPECT LAST-NAME REPLACING ALL "'" BY " ".

I need to get rid of the blanks.

Any suggestions?

Thanks
Ron
```

#### ↳ Re: Need Help with Cobol Code

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<guPL4.492$Dm1.10634@news.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<38ff5850.0@news.syr.edu>`

```
The suggestions you will receive cluster into two basic themes:

1. Move the input to the output a byte at a time, making a decision on
whether to move for each byte, or
2. Use STRING, UNSTRING, FUNCTIONS, REFERENCE MODIFICATIONS, or other
(more advanced) language constructs.

Simpler is usually better.

Ron <rjn@law.syr.edu> wrote in message news:38ff5850.0@news.syr.edu...
> I am having a problem removing - ' from a last name field.
> Can someone help me.
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Need Help with Cobol Code

- **From:** "Lucas, Todd" <todd.lucas@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38ff6117_2@news1.prserv.net>`
- **References:** `<38ff5850.0@news.syr.edu>`

```
I just love being in college again!  <grin>  how about this snippet of
code:

unstring
    last-name delimited by all ' '
    into name-part1
           name-part2
           name-part3
end-unstring.

move spaces to name-no-spaces.
string
   name-part1 delimited by ' '
   name-part2 delimited by ' '
   name-part3 delimited by ' '
    into name-no-spaces
end-string.
```

#### ↳ Re: Need Help with Cobol Code

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38FF609C.DAA87B10@home.com>`
- **References:** `<38ff5850.0@news.syr.edu>`

```


Ron wrote:
> 
> I am having a problem removing - ' from a last name field.
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Ron

You can't get rid of blanks by just inspecting/replacing. To do that you
are going to have to use inspect counts (tallying etc..) and then use
REFERENCE MODIFICATION move myname(p1:p2) to mysecondname(p3:p4).
PS : I've changed the name to give me more characters :-

	Take your Smithson-Jones above:-

	initialize mysecondnanme
	move myname ( 1:8) to mysecondname (1: ) 	*> or (1:8)
	move myname (10:5) to mysecondname (9: ) 	*> or (9:5)

The above is a variation on a theme, I'm sure you will get other
suggestions.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

Ron <rjn@law.syr.edu> wrote in message news:38ff5850.0@news.syr.edu...
> I am having a problem removing - ' from a last name field.
> Can someone help me.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Any suggestions?

Ron, here is a simple portable version that does
not require a modern compiler. If you have a compiler
that allows reference modification you can do it
a lot simpler, but that is for you to decide:

000100 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
000200 PROGRAM-ID.    R.
000300
000400 AUTHOR.        LEIF SVALGAARD.
000500 DATE-WRITTEN.  00/04/20
000600     -REVISED:  00/04/20.
000700
000800 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
000900
001000 CONFIGURATION SECTION.
001100 SOURCE-COMPUTER. PORTABLE.
001200 OBJECT-COMPUTER. PORTABLE.
001300
001400 DATA DIVISION.
001500
001600 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
001700
001800 01  FROM-PTR                    PIC S9(3)  COMP.
001900 01  TO-PTR                      PIC S9(3)  COMP.
002000 01  THE-NAME.
002100     02  THE-NAME-CHAR           PIC X      OCCURS 50 TIMES.
002200
002300 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
002400 BEGIN-PROGRAM.
002500     MOVE "Smith-Jones-And-Sons" TO THE-NAME
002600
002700     MOVE 1 TO TO-PTR
002800     PERFORM SQUEZE-HYPHENS-OUT
002900       VARYING FROM-PTR FROM 1 BY 1
003000         UNTIL FROM-PTR > 50
003100
003200     PERFORM CLEAR-NAME-TAIL
003300       VARYING TO-PTR FROM TO-PTR BY 1
003400         UNTIL TO-PTR > 50
003500
003600     DISPLAY THE-NAME
003700     STOP RUN
003800     .
003900
004000 SQUEZE-HYPHENS-OUT.
004100     IF THE-NAME-CHAR (FROM-PTR) NOT = "-"
004200         MOVE THE-NAME-CHAR (FROM-PTR)
004300           TO THE-NAME-CHAR (TO-PTR)
004400         ADD 1 TO TO-PTR
004500     .
004600
004700 CLEAR-NAME-TAIL.
004800     MOVE SPACE TO THE-NAME-CHAR (TO-PTR)
004900     .
```

#### ↳ Re: Need Help with Cobol Code

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38FF7712.42810528@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<38ff5850.0@news.syr.edu>`

```

Ron wrote:
> 
> I am having a problem removing - ' from a last name field.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> I need to get rid of the blanks.

To produce this output:
LAST-NAME ="JOHN-JACOB-JINGLEHEIMER-SMITH           "
LAST-NAME ="JOHNJACOBJINGLEHEIMERSMITH              "

Run this code:

    DISPLAY 'LAST-NAME ="' LAST-NAME '"'.
    PERFORM VARYING COUNTER FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL COUNTER > 40
       IF LAST-NAME (COUNTER:1) = '-'
           MOVE LAST-NAME(COUNTER + 1:) TO LAST-NAME (COUNTER:)
       END-IF
    END-PERFORM.
    DISPLAY 'LAST-NAME ="' LAST-NAME '"'.
```

#### ↳ Re: Need Help with Cobol Code

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8dntsd$pj8$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<38ff5850.0@news.syr.edu>`

```
You cannot make a byte in the middle of a fixed length field disappear!
Inspecting it and replacing it with anything is going to leave the
"anything" right where it is.  Try unstringing it delimited by the dash,
then restringing it back without.

Ron wrote in message <38ff5850.0@news.syr.edu>...
>I am having a problem removing - ' from a last name field.
>Can someone help me.
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Need Help with Cobol Code

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dTML4.222535$Hq3.5732526@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<38ff5850.0@news.syr.edu> <8dntsd$pj8$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Not to negate your comment about replacement since it is true. However, I
seem to remember that under some obscure circumstances on some platforms you
can make a character disappear by replacing it with low-values. If my
failing memory serves, it involved sending data to a serial terminal on a
VAX (might have been HP3000). The low-value (NULL CHAR) was 'eaten' in
transmission. Certainly no help to Ron but a nice bit of obscure lore and
just the sort of code I'd have nightmares about maintaining.

- Karl

"donald tees" <donald@willmack.com> wrote in message
news:8dntsd$pj8$1@news.igs.net...
> You cannot make a byte in the middle of a fixed length field disappear!
> Inspecting it and replacing it with anything is going to leave the
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need Help with Cobol Code

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8dphu2$nmh$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<38ff5850.0@news.syr.edu> <8dntsd$pj8$1@news.igs.net> <dTML4.222535$Hq3.5732526@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
Actually, I am dealing with the odd "bug" of that nature right now.  In
converting a few hundred programs from MF to Fujitsu, we are discovering all
the old fields that were never initialised.  MF defaults to space on pic X
fields, while Fujitsu defaults to null. In print streams, the null simply
gets stripped out, and can be hard to spot.

Oscar T. Grouch wrote in message ...
>Not to negate your comment about replacement since it is true. However, I
>seem to remember that under some obscure circumstances on some platforms
you
>can make a character disappear by replacing it with low-values. If my
>failing memory serves, it involved sending data to a serial terminal on a
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Print line with fujitsu v3

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8dnpc4$mm1$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<sfuj5kg6l6e120@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Hopefully others will reply with details, but you are going to need to send
"control" characters to your printer BEFORE the first line of "real" data.
What's more these control characters may need to be "tailored" for the
specific printer that you are using.

If you want to get into a "production" situation where you want this to work
with multiple different types of printers, there are some routines and
products available - but I read your question as a "how do I do this for a
specific problem" question.

NOTE to others who may have missed the subject line, it does mention Fujitsu
V3.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Print line with fujitsu v3

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8dpd2n$vvi$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<sfuj5kg6l6e120@corp.supernews.com> <8dnpc4$mm1$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
In article <8dnpc4$mm1$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>,
  "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:
> Hopefully others will reply with details, but you are going to need
to send
> "control" characters to your printer BEFORE the first line of "real"
data.

This is true. I used the HP PCL escape sequences to control the
font and positioning of text via COBOL under DOS for many years
and it worked well.

Unfortunately, Windows screws everything up when you try to do
the same thing.

Windows does not appear to like the escape character (027) and
seems to remove it or something. (Having the 027 as the first
character in a print line also seems not to work. I had to
put leading spaces before the 027 to get it to work.)

Just yesterday, I found that an ASCII 028 in a text string
cannot be moved from one field to another. The 028 apparently
terminates the move.

So, it seems that Windows just doesn't want to cooperate if you
use those other ASCII characters.

Thanks

TOny Dilworth


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

## Thread: accepting record and block sizes — 1 msgs

### RE: accepting record and block sizes

- **From:** "Thompson, William" <wthompson@okc.disa.mil>
- **Date:** 2000-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<D9E8EFCEA4F6D31194C800508B64445E306518@reliant.okc.disa.mil>`

```
Depending on what you want, yes and no.....

In the "olden" days, I'd just alter the DTF generated by COBOL but I would
not do that these days.....

If you define the input file as undefined, you could pretty much do as you
wish, but why not keep the assembler part as a called input routine?  The
best of both worlds - heck if the input was VSAM, you could query VSAM for
that info and adjust the ACB and RPL as needed....

Bill

- **From:** firdaus <callfirdaus@hotmail.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sg1ljg68l6e38@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Hi!
I am new to cobol.I need some help with the PRINT-FILE to print more
than 80 characters on my pc on printer with Fujitsu ver 3.

FD.
PRINT-FILE.
01 PRINT-REC           PIC X(110).    .... (or more)

I actually changed the statement from:

SELECT PRINT-FILE ASSIGN TO PRINTER.   to

SELECT PRINT-FILE ASSIGN TO "PRINTER.DOC".

This gave me an output of printer.doc file, which I later viewed in 
word 97 in LANDSCAPE view and printed it out...BUT I know this is not
the right way to do it and it will not always work..If anybody got
any solution for printing PRINT-REC of PIC X(110) or PIC X(130)..
Any advise on changing the fonts, compiler options etc..please help...

Regards

Firdaus.
```

#### ↳ Re: PRINT-REC of PIC X(110) or more...

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 2000-04-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8dqtn6$3o8$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<sg1ljg68l6e38@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Hey,
Nothing is wrong.

You have learned that a proportional font is not the way to show printed
output.

If you use Word as 'viewer, then change the font in Word. Select everything
and change the font to courier, Terminal or Lineprinter. They are all
bitmapped - all letters including space are exactly same size.

You might like to change the filename to 'Printer.Txt' - the .DOC might
confuse Word, and check that you define the file as line sequential. Some
compilers will default to line sequential when you assign to printer, but
you changed that.

After this you should be able to see your output in Word.

I like Notepad as a viewer. You can turn off word-wrap and choose 'fixed
sys' as font, but keep the extension .TXT on a txt-file.

Hope this helps.

Ib
firdaus skrev i meddelelsen ...
>Hi!
>I am new to cobol.I need some help with the PRINT-FILE to print more
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://www.help.com/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PRINT-REC of PIC X(110) or more...

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 2000-04-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8e9nnb$d5$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<sg1ljg68l6e38@corp.supernews.com> <8dqtn6$3o8$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <3906E595.894BA9D5@zip.com.au> <8e8qia$668$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hey,
The discussion was about Notepad versus Word as a cobol editor, and the
problem here is to get help from someone in the newsgroup. When you use Word
the original format is changed to Word-format, and even if you save it as a
txt-file, the format has changed.

Notepad is one way to do it and it is free. It can be ugly as Ken wrote but
everyone can read it and you have a better chance to get the help you need.

The problem you raise about a 200 char wide output is no problem on the NG
if you use Notepad. Most of us will read your output with a Cobol Editor
with .TXT margins (0-255), and we will be able to help you if something
bothers you in your output.

If you like to have a tool, that enables you to print 200 chars on a line,
look at www.flexus.com

The other solution is to import it into Word or Excell and print from

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PRINT-REC of PIC X(110) or more...

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 2000-04-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39098623.1318924@news.epix.net>`
- **References:** `<sg1ljg68l6e38@corp.supernews.com> <8dqtn6$3o8$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <3906E595.894BA9D5@zip.com.au> <8e8qia$668$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8e9nnb$d5$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <8ebghi$5ad$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
prinzeck@my-deja.com wrote:

>In article <8e9nnb$d5$1@news.inet.tele.dk>,
>  "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk> wrote:
>> Hey,
[snip]
>
>Thanks for Your hints!
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>on WIN NT.

Eckhard:

I just created a FormPrint form in landscape mode with a data field
formatted to X(205), using a 7 point regular weight Courier New font.

I used a Courier New font because in most cases where a single string
of data of that length needs to be printed, it is comprised of many
fields concatenated to form one string.  Printing with a single data
field and a proportionally spaced font would not allow columns of
logical data items to line up properly in a vertical manner.

I set the page up as US Letter size which is 8.5" x 11" (269 mm width
by 207 mm height).  This is close to an A4 format (297 mm width by 210
mm height in landscape mode).

A4 landscape format should actually allow you to print an even longer
text string because the paper is wider than US Letter format.

I initialized it with 205 characters and it printed fine in Windows 98
as well as Windows NT on an HP Laser Jet 4 plus.  Please let me know
if you would like to obtain a copy of the print and I'll be happy to
send it to you.

Please bear in mind that regardless of which tool you use for your
printed output, the specific printing device(s) which will be used is
a very important consideration.  All printing devices which support
the Windows Print Manager are subject to a maximum printable area as
defined by the manufacturer's printer driver.

Ink jet printers almost always have a smaller printable area (larger
minimum margins), therefore it is impossible to fit as many characters
on a printed page rendered by a typical ink jet than one printed on a
typical laser printer.

If you control the type of printers which will be used, a 205
character line is possible, providing that the printable area is large
enough and the font is small enough.  If you don't control the
printing devices which will be used, it will be much more difficult to
fit 205 characters on certain printing devices.  It is possible to use
a smaller point size, but smaller fonts may be too small to be easily
read.  I also printed with a 5 point font and it appears that you can
print an additional 47 characters (252 total) on the page.  It is
readable, but quite small for my aging eyeballs!

This same output could also be printed from a COBOL program running on
a remote server across a TCP/IP network (internet/intranet) using the
Thin Client in conjunction with FormPrint.  This can be accomplished
in a stand-alone Thin Client program or directly from a web browser
with the Thin Client browser plug-in.  It provides much more flexible
output than an HTML based printed page.

I hope that this was helpful.  Send private e-mail if we can be of
assistance in your project.




Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

and are readily proficient in.

TOny Dilworth
