###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Skill Set Survey

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8dshjq$fro$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<8dpgkq$3f6$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <RgZL4.17565$0o4.121606@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8ds42n$qbk$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Foodman wrote in message <8ds42n$qbk$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>
>I also wonder why the Great Programming Contest was such
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I have been programming for thirty-three years now.  I have a full-time
salesman to hand out my resumes and get me work. I have two assistants to
help me write code and make enough money to live by.

You come along, and suggest that I spend a month or two writing a demo
program for you to show you how clever I am, then spend a day or two writing
up thirty-three years of my work to asauge your curiosity, and  wonder why I
am not enamoured of the idea.

Probably the most common phrase I have heard from amateurs over the last
thirty years starts with "You should write ...

My response is canned.  "Good idea!" I exclaim.  Why don't you go out, raise
half a million in capital to cover the first two years and give us some
marketing money.  I'll write the code. That usually cools their jets
somewhat.

I rather suspect that the majority of posters in this NG feel somewhat the
same. We are professionals, not students. I am a lot more concerned about
*what* to write than *how* to write it. Once I know what I want to write,
the rest is just grunt work.  I can find a million people that will give me
a project to write for free, simply by snapping my fingers (or posting one
message on Usenet). If you really want a contest, come up with something
interesting ... don't try to convince me to write you an Isam update
program.  I've done that before.

So.

1.  If you want my resume, contact my salesman/partner.  I doubt that he
will be bothered, as he also does it for a living.  He seldom puts sales
packages together for the joy of it, as he has a private life to satisfy as
well.  However, he does have the data.  I don't even have a copy, as I have
not used it in ten years.

2. If you want me to work for you, tell me how much you pay, or write a
spec, and I will bid the job.  If I want to program for free, I have a
lifetime of projects to play with ... I will choose my own, thank you.
There are dozens of projects that I'd love to write if I had time.  Some of
them, I am busy writing, just for fun. Trying to write the worlds best
customer update module is not one of them, as I have already done it too
many times to count.  I'd rather go out and get laid, or perhaps write
something interesting.

And last but not least:

3. The last contest I entered was by Fujitsu.  In spite of the fact that
they named me a winner (second), and used my name in a press release, they
stiffed my for the prize (a T shirt).  That was fifteen years back.  They
announced a contest last year with a portable PC as the prize.  I did not
bother with a submission for that one. At least on the first they allowed
the entrant to decide what they wanted to program, and I managed to sell a
few thousand copies.  Contests are for beginners to make their bones.

------------****------------

BTW, I ran into a PC application that broke the ISAM methodology this week.
If the application had been queued client/server, there would have been no
problem.

I have an installation that is totally automatic, running on five PC's. The
data base is Isam, sitting on a central server and using file/level locking,
though that could be converted to record level locking quite easily.  The
new extension that is required has to run on the dashboard of a front-end
loader, via packet switched radio/Ethernet. (Front-end loader as in one of
those big construction machines, not as in a program).

Unfortunately, with a mobile platform, the network connection can make and
break constantly as the PC moves around.  Radio fades in and out, sometimes
when the file is locked.  Locking you central database for five minute while
the PC is out of site has rather disturbing consequences. I decided to write
the data needed by the loader out to a small sequential file and have the
loader computer read that file, sort it, and display it.  Transactions back
are handled as sort of a mini-batch run.  By doing that, they can be
deferred until the connection is back.

Thought you might be interested in why Isam does not always work.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Skill Set Survey

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 2000-04-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8dsj6f$6k7$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<8dpgkq$3f6$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <RgZL4.17565$0o4.121606@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8ds42n$qbk$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Wow, this is fun.
Skills are something we should send to an ISO-board. It is very difficult to
compare skills between countries, but even if it's hard it could be fun:

I was born in 1946, obviously as a result of some positive 'vibrations'
after the War :-).

After 12 years in school and 5 years in a company I had the following skils:

Business degree

IBM School training for 077 (Collator), 519 (reproducer), 421/448
(tabulators), 548 (interpreter) and 602 (calculator) - all old punched card
environment.

IBM School training for 360 Assembler, RPG and COBOL

The years in the Army was my basic training in crazy interfaces. We
interfaced most of the NATO systems. Mostly Cobol but a small bite of
370-assembler too.

After the army I had a job for some years with NCR. Purely NEAT/3 (an
assembler)

Then followed some years with IMS, DL/1, MFS, Cobol

5 years thereafter I spent to make and maintain an insurance-application in
CICS DL/1, BMS and Cobol. These years were the birth of the name 'Personal
Computer'. In 1984 I made the first system in Cobol on a PC. All graphics
were made in Basic, called from IBM Cobol 1.0.

From then I became a consultant. In the past couple of years I specialized
my business degree. I took separate degree's in DP, Economics, Oganization,
Auditing and Personnel managing. I did it to be able to face the customers
at their own level, and to make better applications. As a consultant my
primary task was to introduce Micro Focus and to support their products -
that was in 1987. I am still in business with Merant and visited a customer
2 days ago go give my assistance.

My 'spare time' was spent in projects with IMS, CICS, DB2, Oracle, Data
analysis and Database conversions, and of course Cobol all over except for a
small flavour of PL/1 (one of the mistakes from IBM).

Through the years I have been to many training sessions, user conferences
all over the world. I have earned lots of money on this business, so now I
don't need to work. However, I like to work in this business, but now I
insist to have fun.

Attending to CLC is for fun. I have fun when I can help. I have fun when we
have discussions. A long life with DP from the early start has been more
giving than most of you can imagine. It gives a relaxed attitude to 'clever'
people. I'll quote Goldi Hawn 'I vote for the dumb people'.

As long as we can respect newcomers, provoke oldtimers and keep our snotty
nose away from beeng arrogant, I truly think CLC will last.

Have fun
Ib
```

```
Skippy - your little cartoon - did you dream it up or pick it up from

character had a name, (can't remember it) and it would appear with an
appropriate caption, like "Wot no (food) coupons ?", "Wot no ammunition
?" etc...

Anybody in UK old enough to remember ?

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Skippy Cartoon WAS :Re: Skill Set Survey

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3906411E.ABFF55A5@home.com>`
- **References:** `<8dpgkq$3f6$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39033995.560096B1@home.com> <KwHM4.17737$0o4.123290@iad-read.news.verio.net> <000f6f60.f546df59@usw-ex0105-035.remarq.com> <ZI%M4.17808$0o4.124098@iad-read.news.verio.net> <LO%M4.245987$Hq3.6194299@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <3904AF19.9A643195@home.com> <jm2N4.4519$4O2.43532@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```


Leif Svalgaard wrote:
> 
> James J. Gavan <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Lief,

The information you gave was an interesting folllow-up. Now I can't
substantiate this, but only go from memory. I attended a Forecasting
course in late 60's. A topic mentioned was PERT and that it had been
introduced to produce those 200/250+ destroyers to UK on lend-lease. The
point made was that using 'MANUAL' PERT charts the Navy yards were able
to dramatically speed-up the building, (and this would have been prior
to Pearl Harbour). As I say, just from memory. Could be that the
'original' CPM drawings were the seeds for the Polaris computerized
version of PERT.

I don't recall a commercial computerized version of PERT being available
when I attended the course.

Interestingly, if you think about it, the buzz-phrase RAD(Rapid
Application Development) is really PERT with another name.


Jimmy
```

```
I can not point to documentation, but "remember" that at one time on TV an
attempt was made to explain Kilroy to America.  I believe in that show,
they attributed Kilroy to ship builders during WWII, and that G.I.'s picked
it up and spread it all over the world.  That story covers a lot of the
landscape without upsetting to many.

Warren Simmons
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Skippy Cartoon WAS :Re: Skill Set Survey

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3903AF42.FF66F69F@home.com>`
- **References:** `<8dpgkq$3f6$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <u3pM4.17684$0o4.122651@iad-read.news.verio.net> <4EED6C19664F6E3E.0360E945FC1A83B7.CC21A28C38A9538A@lp.airnews.net> <39033995.560096B1@home.com> <KwHM4.17737$0o4.123290@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```


docdwarf@clark.net wrote:
> 
> In article <39033995.560096B1@home.com>,
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
No, I asked my 'Trivia Queen', "She who must be obeyed". It was called
Mr. Chad. (Ask Jeeves didn't help me much - thought I was referring to
the country Chad even though I insisted "Mr. Chad"). Like I said, had
annotations like "Wot No Beer ?" Eileen claims, from memory, that it
started out as an electrical current drawing, the 'squizzy' bits
becoming the hands and the head added as an afterthought with the nose
hanging over the wall.

It was the same era as SNAFU - (used in US & Britain) - now you go work
that one out all by yourselves :-).

Jimmy
```

```
- note: formatting and extraneous characters ('=20') from original -


In article <8e21u9$svk$1@porthos.nl.uu.net>,
The COBOL Frog <H.Klink@IMN.NotThisPart.nl> wrote:
><docdwarf@clark.net> wrote ...
>> James J. Gavan <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>FUBAR?

FUBAR.

>FU like in KUNG FU?

No.
>
>FOO? BAR?
>Foo! Bar!
>
>Foo(); Bar();  // Got it!

Get it cured!

>
>It's a C program! C, see:

Not quite, Mr Klink; it pre-dates C by a few years.  The 'F' in the
acronyms can be expanded into a word which, until a few years ago, was
considered the province of soldiers, dock-workers and other Coarse
Types; as this is a Family Newsgroup I will use the more commonly-used
'Fouled':

SNAFU = Situation Normal, All Fouled Up

TARFU = Things Are Really Fouled Up

FUBAR = Fouled Up Beyond All Relief

DD
```

- **References:** `<8dpgkq$3f6$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8ds42n$qbk$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <rboM4.17680$0o4.122749@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8dt4v8$mge$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <u3pM4.17684$0o4.122651@iad-read.news.verio.net> <4EED6C19664F6E3E.0360E945FC1A83B7.CC21A28C38A9538A@lp.airnews.net> <39033995.560096B1@home.com> <ZGPM4.32112$WF.1448670@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
In article <ZGPM4.32112$WF.1448670@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>,
  "Warren Simmons" <w.g.simmons@worldnet.att.net> wrote:
> >
> > Skippy - your little cartoon - did you dream it up or pick it up
from
> > somewhere. Not quite as detailed as you  show but it does have some
> > historical significance. Goes back to W.W.II in UK. A similar
cartoon
> > character had a name, (can't remember it) and it would appear with
an
> > appropriate caption, like "Wot no (food) coupons ?", "Wot no
ammunition
> > ?" etc...
> >
> > Anybody in UK old enough to remember ?
> >
> > Jimmy

Hi:

Yes, I remember it very well. I was rather fond of it, actually,
and used to draw it in unauthorized places much to the displeasure of
my keepers.

It was "Wot? , no....?"

Whilst on this relatively non-COBOL topic, do any of the English
blokes remember the William books?

Thanks

Tony Dilworth


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<063fa140.f47b0812@usw-ex0105-035.remarq.com>`
- **References:** `<8dpgkq$3f6$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <4EED6C19664F6E3E.0360E945FC1A83B7.CC21A28C38A9538A@lp.airnews.net> <5rHM4.17735$0o4.123520@iad-read.news.verio.net> <0C18D3B992C4C977.3F9A4D2FC432C691.9D7CCD9EEE8E4312@lp.airnews.net> <81_M4.17803$0o4.124291@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
Hey, I told a Massachusetts government agency my skill set
(Burroughs/Unisys COBOL), showed how I met all the specified
qualifications and had met the background check for a Federal
job, got the interview, and was asked "Why did you leave this
job? Why did you leave that job?"  (I stuck with a bad situation
in hopes it would get better.) They filled one of the two
positions and reposted the other one.

Pick and choose, pick and choose, and then complain there aren't
enough "qualified" applicants, that's how the employment market
operates.
```

```
Uh huh.  You are asking people for their resumes.  I'd be supicious if
someone just walked up and asked for mine.  I'm not sure why, either, as
I've had mine posted on Monster.com until just recently, and anybody or
their grandmother's uncle could have seen it there.  Let's just say that
it's not something that's done on this newsgroup.  If we did it for you,
there would be no reason not to do it for recruiters.  I'm fairly sure most
```

<a id="t-32c53b6506"></a>
## Thread: A question for OO people — 8 this month / 11 total

_Topics: [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo)_

### A question for OO people

- **From:** "Costas Giannoulis" <diavasi@otenet.gr>
- **Date:** 2000-04-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8dp62e$jc2$1@newssrv.otenet.gr>`

```
Hi,

About 2 years ago i realized the power of OO Cobol. Since then i use only
object cobol and  NetExpress. The projects we have developed are based on
windows platform. Recently i 've started the conversion of a GUI application
into a WEB based product. I am not an experienced developer on the fields of
web programming and i am still at an experimental stage. A great difference
i think i have undrestood is that a server side program has no state. This
means that the client (browser) sends a request to the server. The server
side program starts, performs the business logic, sends back the results and
'dies'. The problem is, how can i keep my objects 'alive' between the
invocations of the server program ? Do i have to instantiate them all every
time the client sends a request ? Is there any way to save the instances
(persistency) between invocations ?
```

#### ↳ Re: A question for OO people

- **From:** "The COBOL Frog" <H.Klink@IMN.NotThisPart.nl>
- **Date:** 2000-04-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8dpnq6$kbj$1@porthos.nl.uu.net>`
- **References:** `<8dp62e$jc2$1@newssrv.otenet.gr>`

```
Costas,

Indeed, when you write COBOL CGI-exe's via NetExpress, the state of the application is lost after eacht HTML-out. Merant provides some VERY basic help with theit Server-Side-State-file-Routines (the MF_STATE_* calls). But you have to re-instantiate the objects, indeed. I knew there was some (again VERY basic)) persistancy mechanism provided in earlier OO-COBOL-flavours of Micro Focus (Personal COBOL for instance), bu wether this feature is still present under NetExpres ... don't know.

I have written some web-apps. I encapsulated the ServerSideState routines in a more sophisticated set of sub-routines.

The following is based on what I've read and understood, not on experience:

Perhaps you can switch to ISAPI or NSAPI i.s.o. CGI. In that case your programs are compiled as DLL's, which remain loaded in memory. Objects that have to persist, or better sai, that have to stay alive between interactions, could be in the WORKING-STORAGE of some DLL. (Which is static in NetExpress). But you should dive into this yourself to get it working in written&specified behaviour, not on tricks of me ...

The cObOl Frog
```

```

VERY basic)) persistancy mechanism provided in earlier OO-COBOL-flavours of
Micro Focus (Personal COBOL for instance), bu wether this feature is still
present under NetExpres ... don't know.

I have written some web-apps. I encapsulated the ServerSideState routines in

at http://imatix.com/html/xitami/index12.htm
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A question for OO people

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-04-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3905db66.10721928@news.cox-internet.com>`
- **References:** `<8dp62e$jc2$1@newssrv.otenet.gr> <8dpnq6$kbj$1@porthos.nl.uu.net> <IAZL4.292$4O2.9767@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```
On Fri, 21 Apr 2000 14:28:56 GMT, "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
wrote:

>I have written some web-apps. I encapsulated the ServerSideState routines in
>a more sophisticated set of sub-routines.
>
>

This whole "state" issue is again pseudoconversational programming.
Just like CICS.  The lack of persistence of session data with CGI is
why ASP (Active Server Pages) has gained ground.  

Interestingly, the soon to be released HTML interface tool from Flexus
achieves what I like to call "CGI Session Persistence".   It's pretty
neat.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A question for OO people

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** frlsfly@aol.com (FRLSFLY)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000425230242.14802.00002552@ng-fw1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3905db66.10721928@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
Of course, ASPs dirty little secret is that it keeps its' context using
cookies.   How does "Flexus" do it?
Nathan Meyer
Berkeley, CA
"Big Endian" is what I like.
```

```
NOT in fact stateless.  It is more or less
like the normal SP2 package, but instead of having client
software, and a number of data files
on the client side, it is all done with html.  It is intended for
more casual use than the SP2 package,
but is otherwise the same.
```

```
"Costas Giannoulis" <diavasi@otenet.gr> wrote:

? Do i have to instantiate them all every
>time the client sends a request ? Is there any way to save the instances
>(persistency) between invocations ?

i'm not exactly how to explain this, but here goes. on my pc, there is
something called a cache. the cache holds parts of code, ect in ram,
and doesn't get it from the disk. as far as holding variables goes,
you could pass in tables through the linkage section, via reference,
and use entry points, or simply duplicate the linkage section.
```

- **References:** `<8dkdlo$glo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <TSjL4.214950$Hq3.5434745@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <8dkmel$quh$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <SgnL4.215425$Hq3.5472898@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <8dmor1$4gs$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <dFDL4.219924$Hq3.5630117@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <8dnfpt$uub$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38FF4E55.E09311C1@cusys.edu> <8dnubf$foc$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8dnvs7$kv9$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <38FFCDEC.90557231@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" wrote:
> 
> Is there much of that "multi-hundred programmers.....". God how boring
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy

I see where you're coming from.  But really what we have is large
environments which are broken down into manageable tasks - call them
objects.  Sure, when we design a large system, we can't write the whole
system ourselves.  But when we do the coding, we code manageable pieces
the same as you do.  We design our piece, build it, and see it working.

And we have more input into what the guy in the other cubicle is doing
(about some software which interfaces with mine) than you have in what
the guy in Redmond is doing (about some software which interfaces with
yours).
```

```
The project I'm in is a small part of the total activities in-house. We were
146 developers 2 years ago. Then the system went to Prod, but we are still
85 developers to maintain the system, to make further development, to
optimize functions and to educate employee's. Untill the education part is
don, we cannot get the foot chains off.

And yes, Jimmy it is beginning to be boring, but it pays the rent.

Regards
Ib
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<8dkdlo$glo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <TSjL4.214950$Hq3.5434745@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <8dkmel$quh$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <SgnL4.215425$Hq3.5472898@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <8dmor1$4gs$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <dFDL4.219924$Hq3.5630117@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <8dnfpt$uub$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38FF4E55.E09311C1@cusys.edu> <8dnubf$foc$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8dnvs7$kv9$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <38FFCDEC.90557231@home.com>`

```
In article <38FFCDEC.90557231@home.com>,
  "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:
>
>
> "William M. Klein" wrote:
> >
> > You know that thread a few weeks ago about "I want to see a
mainframe".  I
> > really, suggest that you (FOODMAN) take a few weeks to see how
things work in
> > a large mainframe shop (multi-hundred programmers, woking on
applications
> > handling 100,000 to multi-million transactions in night runs,
10,000 users
> > expecting sub-second response time, etc ...)


----But, no-one invited me. I also don't think that programmers
have any responsibility for operational systems which process
100,000 to multi-million transactions, isn't that the
responsibility of operations or is operations now run by
programmers?  Or, are the programmers on night call to fix the
invariable crashes caused by poor design and bug-riddled software?


> >
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> it must be. In such an environment how could you work up enthusiasm
for
> anything, let alone COBOL. No wonder the Personnel Department is now
> called Human Resources, (folks are just like another resource, a slag
> heap of coal).
>
> Jimmy

---I think, Jimmy, That the slag heap was stuff that had no value and
was just dumped outside the mine. Although, maybe programmers
are taken from the slag heaps of the DP world rather than the
rich seams of coal?

----According to something I read in the IEEE magazine about COBOL,
there are 3,000,000 people who know COBOL.

I would guess that only a tiny percentage of that number work in
those 'multi-hundred' shops. The majority work in tiny, small,
medium, size shops, or, like me, work alone and make their
living from the PC. They are still COBOL programmers.

I don't think that volume of data should be reflected in the size of
the staff. Just because you process eight zillion things a day
should not require that you need eight zillion programmers.

The reason these large shops have so many people is because
they have so many applications. Those applications are, I would
guess, based on what I read here, a monstrous mish-mosh of
poorly-design, poorly-programmed, slung-together stuff accumulated over
the years. Another reason is that they are mis-managed.

I also think I read that there are about 900 participants in
CLC. This is a tiny percentage of the 3 million.

It is also apparent that the CLC seems to be a sort of private
club for the 'Dirty Dozen' (you know who you are) who invariably
respond to almost every post.If we could get more people
to participate, then the views might reflect the opinions of
the majority, rather than the minority.

The majority of the things posted to the CLC seems to be about
arcane technical things.  The minority are about COBOL coding
and application programming. This reflects the ridiculous
prevalence of using umpteen things to do something when
one or two would do.

I don't think that the CLC is representative of the whole
community, and, if it is, then things are worse than I thought.
I would hope that participation in the CLC is not restricted
to mainframe people working in the 'multi-hundred' shops.

Thanks

TOny Dilworth


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Design Survey

- **From:** "DennisHarley" <LegacyBlue@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OsK4ABMr$GA.260@cpmsnbbsa04>`
- **References:** `<8dkdlo$glo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <TSjL4.214950$Hq3.5434745@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <8dkmel$quh$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <SgnL4.215425$Hq3.5472898@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <8dmor1$4gs$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <dFDL4.219924$Hq3.5630117@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <8dnfpt$uub$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38FF4E55.E09311C1@cusys.edu> <8dnubf$foc$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8dnvs7$kv9$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <38FFCDEC.90557231@home.com> <8ds3hk$pp7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

Foodman <foodman123@aol.com> wrote in message
news:8ds3hk$pp7$1@nnrp1.deja.com...

>
> ----But, no-one invited me. I also don't think that programmers
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Actually we do have responsibility.
Poor design, or using an inappropriate method for the environment,
means that the transactions are not posted within the allotted time
frame. This causes users to get angry when they cannot use their
system because the nightly batch run hasn't finished.
The 48 hour daily Job is unacceptable in any environment.

It is not Operations responsibility to determine if a solution is
effective, it is their responsibility to run the software we provide.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Design Survey

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 2000-04-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ds9of$o97$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<8dkdlo$glo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <TSjL4.214950$Hq3.5434745@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <8dkmel$quh$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <SgnL4.215425$Hq3.5472898@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <8dmor1$4gs$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <dFDL4.219924$Hq3.5630117@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <8dnfpt$uub$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38FF4E55.E09311C1@cusys.edu> <8dnubf$foc$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8dnvs7$kv9$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <38FFCDEC.90557231@home.com> <8ds3hk$pp7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
You've got a point - look below
Foodman skrev i meddelelsen <8ds3hk$pp7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>In article <38FFCDEC.90557231@home.com>,
>
>----But, no-one invited me.

If you are visiting Scandinavia I surely can make a pass for you, but if
not, mail me your state/country, and we'll see what the 'family' can do.


> I also don't think that programmers
>have any responsibility for operational systems which process
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Many of the figures should be read with one eye closed - Have you ever been
fishing :-)? But yes, initially it is a job for the programmer to take care
of performance in his programs before moving them to production environment.
If your program is heavy on memory usage, elaps-time or I/O, you will hear
from the performance-group(s). The larger installations are running error
statistics, so you will have some explanation to do, if your program fails
from time to time. The programs might be old, but normally they are well
optimized and very stabil.
>
>----According to something I read in the IEEE magazine about COBOL,
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Got it off your chest, ehh? But much of it is so very true.

I don't think the 3,000,000 is the number of active programmers, and I think
that many thousands of programmers are programming from 8 to 5 without
knowing about CLC, and they wouldn't care about what was written here.

But..... 900 is indeed a small number of Cobol-freaks, and I would be very
happy if the number was bigger.

Your argument about the value of programs is to me a real bulls eye. and I
cannot help it, but it was funny to see this thread developing. The
mainframe oriented part of the postings were not the most kreative - sorry,
but I just have to provoke someone. It makes me feel alive.

My view of the program quality is stated above, but I fear that it is not
quite so in all countries. Some of the programs i have seen from 'Gods own
country' have been written without respect to maintenance ('Seek, and you
shall find'), and of course - many old systems have been running for years
without upgrading to new technology.

Please keep up your spirit. It is good for old bones to be exercised, and if
we all agree upon everything, CLC will become a very boring place.

Regards
Ib

>Thanks
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Design Survey

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-04-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39044F8E.65C20734@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<8dkdlo$glo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <TSjL4.214950$Hq3.5434745@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <8dkmel$quh$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <SgnL4.215425$Hq3.5472898@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <8dmor1$4gs$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <dFDL4.219924$Hq3.5630117@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <8dnfpt$uub$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38FF4E55.E09311C1@cusys.edu> <8dnubf$foc$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8dnvs7$kv9$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <38FFCDEC.90557231@home.com> <8ds3hk$pp7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8ds9of$o97$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`

```
Ib Tanding wrote:
> 
> Your argument about the value of programs is to me a real bulls eye. and I
> cannot help it, but it was funny to see this thread developing. The
> mainframe oriented part of the postings were not the most kreative - sorry,
> but I just have to provoke someone. It makes me feel alive.

I would have to agree with you.  PC work can be more innovative and
creative.  

The costs of being inefficient are minor in PC work.  The costs of doing
maintenance are minor.  Even the costs of downtime are minor.

It's like driving across town - we can choose the scenic route without
spending much (maybe we added 15% to a $1 gas bill).  When we have a
trainload of cargo to ship across the country we have to be more careful
in our choices.

So the new techniques come out in PCs.  Some of it doesn't last, some of
it proves to be useful at that level.  Gradually the best of it gets
tried out on mainframes, where the costs are larger, the needs are more
rigorous, and the systems are expected to last.
```

```
below is a survey of an analysis of CLC. You can easily spot
the 'dirty dozen' at the top of the list. The data covers the
time since March 20th, 2000. A total of 273 people have
posted (there are only a few aliases). The number on each
is the number of postings from that person.

To call it a private club is a bit much. The
regulars often give valuable advice to newbies.
Otherwise, I agree that more participation would
be desirable.
```

```
Michael Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message
news:JErM4.866$WE4.196566@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net...
> Leif Svalgaard <leif@ibm.net> wrote in message

> thisis.not.my@real.address

but your real address is nevertheless attached to your
posting, so my survey does not give away anything you
didn't already do.

all I did was to copy the file holding the CLC postings
on my computer to a work file, then write a simple COBOL (!)
program to extract the posters. I think e-mail harvesters
are much more sophisticated than that.
```

```
Yes, I'm sure they [the harvesters] are more sophisticated. But I'm not, and
now I could have a list, too, just like the big boys. Say, you wouldn't
perchance be interested in making $100,000 per year sending e-mail, or
investing in this great little startup firm, or conversing on-line with
naked Catholic high school girls, would you?

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Design Survey

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 2000-04-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<EgXM4.17789$0o4.124062@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<8dkdlo$glo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8e02u7$fu0$1@news.igs.net> <K8NM4.17764$0o4.123747@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8e1c1v$d6b$1@news.igs.net>`

```
In article <8e1c1v$d6b$1@news.igs.net>,
donald tees <donald@willmack.com> wrote:
>$100,000 per year.

Hmmmmm... I wonder, the grammar seems ambiguous.  I was reading it as:

 Say, you wouldn't perchance be interested in making $100,000 per year
('by', or causative connection, understood, linking rate to following
activity) sending e-mail, or investing in this great little startup firm
(different activity, no causative link, no associated rate), or
(different activity, no causative link, no associated rate) conversing
on-line with naked Catholic high school girls, would you?   

... and you seem to be assuming causative links throughout in a fashion
which I cannot quite parse... would you be so kind as to step up to the
board and demonstrate?

DD


>
>docdwarf@clark.net wrote in message ...
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Design Survey

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8e1jcq$gr0$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<8dkdlo$glo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8e02u7$fu0$1@news.igs.net> <K8NM4.17764$0o4.123747@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8e1c1v$d6b$1@news.igs.net> <EgXM4.17789$0o4.124062@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
Sure, I would be pleased to elucidate so that your pedantic mind can grasp
the essentials. Your message says "please quote a rate".  It does not say
"please quote a rate complete with an analysis of the grammar used, all
cause/effect relationships listed, and a complete grammatical parsing of
your message, so that all concerned can successfully interpret your intent".
The writer named a position and a rate.  Just as you asked.  In fact, they
inferred several positions were available, and refrained from expounding on
those as they were probably pornographic in nature.

So there.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Design Survey

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-04-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39044D32.31A20FC9@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<8dkdlo$glo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <TSjL4.214950$Hq3.5434745@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <8dkmel$quh$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <SgnL4.215425$Hq3.5472898@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <8dmor1$4gs$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <dFDL4.219924$Hq3.5630117@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <8dnfpt$uub$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38FF4E55.E09311C1@cusys.edu> <8dnubf$foc$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8dnvs7$kv9$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <38FFCDEC.90557231@home.com> <8ds3hk$pp7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Foodman wrote:
> 
> I would guess that only a tiny percentage of that number work in
> those 'multi-hundred' shops. The majority work in tiny, small,
> medium, size shops, or, like me, work alone and make their
> living from the PC. They are still COBOL programmers.

I bet most COBOL programmers work on mainframes though.  At least
headhunters don't come a calling for PC jobs very often.  One reason for
mainframes is to handle high-volume transactions.  Our design has to
reflect this whether we have 10 programmers or a thousand.
```

## Thread: Frog's Spam Trap : WAS other things....... — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### Frog's Spam Trap : WAS other things.......

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38FFDB60.74729B2F@home.com>`
- **References:** `<CJoG4.3$jA6.206231@news1.i1.net> <8do0ba$3v0$2@porthos.nl.uu.net>`

```


The COBOL Frog wrote:
> 
Mr Frog, 

You people and your spam traps. Just had two messages to you
non-delivered because of 'NotThisPart'. Oh well you'll have to pick them
up from here. I think I'm going to give up on addressing to most
originators.

Jimmy
```

#### ↳ Re: Frog's Spam Trap : WAS other things.......

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-04-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39006211.EAEBE536@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<CJoG4.3$jA6.206231@news1.i1.net> <8do0ba$3v0$2@porthos.nl.uu.net> <38FFDB60.74729B2F@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" wrote:
> 
> The COBOL Frog wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy

I am a big believer that public forums should be public.  Most all
replies should be publicly posted.

I have gotten an e-mail replying to one of my public messages, responded
to it, and then discovered that that e-mail was a duplicate of a public
post - which I have to respond to again to share with other interested
parties.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Frog's Spam Trap : WAS other things.......

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8dpqk4$6vh$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<CJoG4.3$jA6.206231@news1.i1.net> <8do0ba$3v0$2@porthos.nl.uu.net> <38FFDB60.74729B2F@home.com> <39006211.EAEBE536@cusys.edu>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:39006211.EAEBE536@cusys.edu...
> "James J. Gavan" wrote:
> >
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> parties.

Howard didn't mention me (specifically) - but that is my "normal" way of
working.  When I reply to a "specific" issue posted to the NG, I almost
always reply in the NG, but also CC the person who asked the question.  I
thought that this was a "useful" thing to do, but if lots of people object, I
would certainly be willing to stop the private email.
```

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>

```


"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…

Make sure you DON'T stop cc-ing to me :-)

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Frog's Spam Trap : WAS other things.......

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-04-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<390076EC.5B4D52A7@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<CJoG4.3$jA6.206231@news1.i1.net> <8do0ba$3v0$2@porthos.nl.uu.net> <38FFDB60.74729B2F@home.com> <39006211.EAEBE536@cusys.edu> <8dpqk4$6vh$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> > I have gotten an e-mail replying to one of my public messages, responded
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> would certainly be willing to stop the private email.

The irritation is minor - if it is useful for some people, don't stop on
my account.  I probably should put NOSPAM back in my e-mail address
though, I notice I am getting more SPAM than I used to.  Trouble is I
have to remember to remove it when I e-mail my mother, who always
forgets what to do when she replies to one of my messages.   Too bad
Netscape doesn't give settings specific to newsgroup folders (including
my IN folder).
```

#### ↳ Re: Frog's Spam Trap : WAS other things.......

- **From:** "The COBOL Frog" <H.Klink@IMN.NotThisPart.nl>
- **Date:** 2000-04-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8dp896$c6b$1@porthos.nl.uu.net>`
- **References:** `<CJoG4.3$jA6.206231@news1.i1.net> <8do0ba$3v0$2@porthos.nl.uu.net> <38FFDB60.74729B2F@home.com>`

```
James,

It is NOT intended to annoy YOU but THEM SPAMMERS.

Sorry, but it turns out to be necessary. After some 6 onths in c.l.c. without a trap, I now receive about 10 to 20 spam msgs in my mailbox EVERY day.
```

```
We have a valid license. All I want to use CCI for is the server, since it
is written in MF Cobol. For the Client we have chosen a different compiler.
Since CCI(TCP) is really just a tcp server, there should be no reason to
have to use CCI on the client. So whta I did was use standard a socket
interface, however .. after much testing, all I can accomplish is a connect,
and send - no recieve yet. The reason for this post was that I thought there
might be some initialization I might have to send, or some form of header in
my TCP Data... I thought someone might have a clue as to what I am doing
wrong :)

-Richard
```

<a id="t-7b4280c0b2"></a>
## Thread: STD - Current status of "FINALIZER" — 1 msgs

### STD - Current status of "FINALIZER"

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8dqu2d$8o8$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
From the J4 external web page (report from recent meeting)
   (http://merant.microfocus.com/Standards/)

"FINALIZER method

J4 voted unanimously to recommend to WG4 that a subdivision of the COBOL work
item be initiated immediately for a Technical Report (TR) on finalizers and
associated object-oriented language elements.

J4 will begin work on this Technical Report  via an ad hoc group. The TR
should be available in the same timeframe as the standard.

Reasons:

 * There are still major areas of controversy, including side-effects and
syntax.
 * This is a good subject for a Technical Report (TR) because not all details
have been determined. A TR allows J4 to fix the standard later and
implementations to fix problems they find during implementation.
  * Implementations will diverge if nothing is done to provide finalizers.
  * Finalizers require broader consideration of language design than there is
time for in this standard.
  * Java is evolving in the area of finalizers. If Java doesn't have it right
yet, it's premature to think that COBOL does.

This compromise is in everyone's best interests. A specification for
finalizers can be available in the same timeframe as the standard. The
standard will not be delayed. The specification can change if needed. "
```

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### STD - "Flagging" of cases where standard arithmetic is requested but not received

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8dqu3i$fk6$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
From the recent J4 meeting (as reflected in the J4 external web site),

"00-0273, Default native arithmetic flag, was rejected by a 7-2 vote. The
discussion centered around flagging of the new BINARY- ... and FLOAT-...
usages and around operations, including exponentiation to all but a few
exponents, that have implementor-defined results.  There was disagreement as
to which of these items should be flagged. "
```

<a id="t-2566d7cd9c"></a>
## Thread: Public Domain Source - Dates — 14 msgs

### Public Domain Source - Dates

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<390266F3.90EF76E2@home.com>`

```
I know you can direct me to several sites to check on how to handle
dates but I wonder if there isn't a case for CLC people as a group
producing 'open source' covering all date possibilities.

Purely as an exercise, and it isn't finished, I have been producing an
OO class to handle dates, e.g. 2000/01/01 Jun 20 00, Mai 20, Dimanche
23, that is trying to cover as many date permutations by language. Well
your response could well be, 'We already have installation standards to
handle that' - but as you know we do keep getting queries from
newcomers.

I'll make my OO source available, (if required) - and you are not to
laugh out too loudly at my code ! What I would suggest is that we need
three models :-

(a) Structured - no intrinsic functions ( so that includes date routines
    like Judson has produced)
(b) Structured - using intrinsics
(c) OO - using intrinsics (It doesn't make sense to use OO without 
intrinsics - if you got OO you've also got the latter).

To be of any value - as a starting point - it should address the issue
of Western European languages. Perhaps having produced a first model it
should then move on to Slavic, Kanji, you name it....

One part of the exercise would be to see how much consistency we have
between compilers to achieve clean compiles on different machines. Plus,
those 'additions' or 'language extensions' which would make it 'croak'.

If interested, may I suggest that there be three teams of co-operators -
pointless having 101 solutions - let's get groups talking together and
swapping ideas.

If there is any interest in this I'll post my OO source code.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Public Domain Source - Dates

- **From:** Joseph J Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2000-04-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3905A2CF.549DE95A@iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<390266F3.90EF76E2@home.com> <8dtpht$ucr$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3903255C.733CC8DE@home.com>`

```


"James J. Gavan" wrote:
>
> Thanks for the tip Bill - and it appears to be a useful resource.
> However, I wasn't looking for a vendor specific solution, but rather,
> something that we can all use regardless of compiler.
> 
For goodness sake WHY!
I don't know about others, but the first thing I do when coding a
solution is to check out what subroutines etc. the implementation of
COBOL I'm using supports. I mean, the best code is the code that's not
written (at least by me).

It's sort of like IO. The best IO is no IO. Same with coding. Never code
more than you need. Never design a system to do more than has been
requested. The client neither cares or wishes to pay for a design that
will work for 10 years when the life time of the system is only 2 years.

Besides, when the client eventually needs an expansion of the system,
you will automatically be creating work for someone to follow up with.
And remember, this is exactly what the client expects. Otherwise, they
would have paid for the extra work required to make the solution future proof.

I think the days of pandering to clients is over. I for one deliver
exactly what I'm contracted to and no more. This is not a University
project, this is business!
```

```
You did however miss the point - rather than the continuously endless

Japan - but more importantly transportability across compilers. Write
once, recompile for machine.

As regards my own date requirements - dead simple :-

	Reports :	2000 Apr 25 13:34
	Input/Display:	00/01 = Jan 2000

Jimmy
```

```
well, "everybody" already have rolled their own and the NIH-syndrome
reigns supreme. I also have three sets of public source routines:
http://www.etk.com/download/etkpak/etkpak.htm#CALRPK
http://www.etk.com/download/etkpak/etkpak.htm#DATEPK

Date Routine". The problem with such a beast is that it does
EVERYTHING, but EVERYTHING is not needed all the time.

OO could help here, but then portability is out the window (at least
for now).
```

#### ↳ Re: Public Domain Source - Dates

- **From:** Ed Milne <emilne@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8e82cg$bvo$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<390266F3.90EF76E2@home.com>`

```
In article <390266F3.90EF76E2@home.com>,
  "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:
> I know you can direct me to several sites to check on how to handle
> dates but I wonder if there isn't a case for CLC people as a group
> producing 'open source' covering all date possibilities.
<snip>

Perhaps you should clarify whether you are looking for public domain or
open source code.  There are substantial differences between the two
concepts.

Public domain means there are no restrictions on the use of the code.
For example, anyone could take the code and sell it as a commercial
product to those who are not aware it is public domain.

Open source (I presume you mean open source as defined by the OSI
http://www.opensource.org/) is copyrighted and distributed with
restrictions.  It cannot be resold as a commercial product and any
improvements that an individual makes must also be released as open
source.

If you want people to contribute their efforts, then open source is the
way to go.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Public Domain Source - Dates

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-04-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3907b505.1589687@news.cox-internet.com>`
- **References:** `<390266F3.90EF76E2@home.com> <8e82cg$bvo$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
On Thu, 27 Apr 2000 00:41:55 GMT, Ed Milne <emilne@my-deja.com> wrote:

>Perhaps you should clarify whether you are looking for public domain or
>open source code.  There are substantial differences between the two
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Since COBOL is used largely for commercial products, I think the
adoption of restricted open source code would be slow if at all.  I
think it can work, but not with the commercial use rider.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Public Domain Source - Dates

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-04-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39082D16.E4CE6767@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<390266F3.90EF76E2@home.com> <8e82cg$bvo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3907b505.1589687@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> Since COBOL is used largely for commercial products, I think the
> adoption of restricted open source code would be slow if at all.  I
> think it can work, but not with the commercial use rider.

To clarify Thane,  Open source works like this:

Brainiac 1 creates a library of routines that answers some fundamental
programming need.

Joe average picks up the library, makes some small internal changes to
make it work better, these must be released back to open source.  An
then derives a product from the library.  The derived product must
reference the copyright of the library and have a method to get the
library source to the customer (any customer) this is traditionally
through a web page.  The derived product is Joes unless you in turn
choose to release it.

The library becomes stronger and more robust, more people look at it
and debug it than any other application so you have really good code. 
Bad code does not get supported by the community.

Another model of profit is to open source your application and provide
support on contract, look at the MySQL database as an example of this.
The source is open but owned by the vendor who sells support, not the
product.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

---

<a id="t-fdecb25c56"></a>
## Thread: OS/VS to COBOL/390 Conversion Problem — 28 msgs

### OS/VS to COBOL/390 Conversion Problem

- **From:** "Thomas" <tanmoy@singnet.com.sg>
- **Date:** 2000-04-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8dt7qm$oi6$1@mawar.singnet.com.sg>`

```
We are converting an os/vs cobol program to cobol/390.
The program has an expression like this :
A = B * (1 - ((C/12) * D) * (1/1.05) ** (C/12)

The PIC clauses are :
A      PIC      9(7)V9(6).
B      PIC      9(7)V9(6).
C      PIC      9(2).
D      PIC      9V9(5).

For the same values of B,C,D the result obtained under cobol/390 is more
than that in os/vs.
e.g, when  B=125.0525, C=25, D=1.00489

A = 0000123.530454 [ os/vs cobol ]
A = 0000123.530747 [ cobol/390 ]

How can we get the same value under cobol/390? We want to use the cobol/390
compiler
but the result must be same as what was obtained using os/vs cobol compiler.

Is there any workaround?

TIA.
```

```
===> missing parenthesis somewhere

>
> The PIC clauses are :
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> D      PIC      9V9(5).

try to allocate more decimals (use a temp if need be):

(1.0000001.050000) ** (C / 12)
```

```
Thomas <tanmoy@singnet.com.sg> wrote in message
news:8dtqah$t6l$1@clematis.singnet.com.sg...
> > > A = B * (1 - ((C/12) * D) * (1/1.05) ** (C/12)
> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> A = B * (1 - ( (C/12) * D ) ) * (1/1.05) ** (C/12)

If I try with approx. values B = 125, D=1, C/12=2
A= 125 * (1 - 2)  * 1 ** 2 = -125
that is negative, now, the picture of A is unsigned,
so one should take the absolute value, thus A = 125,
approx. This seems to be a ***dangerous*** practice.

Furthermore, if you perform the calculation, the
result is 123.5307467599 so the OS/390 result
is actually correct. Why do you the incorrect OS/VS
value?
```

#### ↳ Re: OS/VS to COBOL/390 Conversion Problem

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8dt9sj$n6c$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8dt7qm$oi6$1@mawar.singnet.com.sg>`

```
I have sent a note to one of my "usually reliable source" to see if this is a
known difference and/or if there is away around the problem.

Two things that you MIGHT try (and I don't know if this will work/help at
all) are:

1) Add some more digits to your numeric literal, e.g. Change "1" to "1.00"

2) Change at least one of your numeric literals to floating-point, e.g.
Change
   1 to 1.0E00 (I think that is a floating-point 1)

I don't know if either of these will change your results or not.  The
"complete rules" for how "intermediate results" are figured appear in both
the OS/VS COBOL and COBOL for OS/390 & VM "Programming Guide".  You could try
studying them to see what has changed, but what you are reporting does NOT
ring a bell with me.
```

```
can't have any impact.  In his example, everything is (implicitly) USAGE
DISPLAY.  TRUNC only impacts USAGE BINARY/COMP items.
```

```
variable names
and represented as A,B,C,D .
Since it is an existing program, I'm not sure why the picture clauses are
not consistent
or why there is no provision for sign. My job is to convert the language &
keep the program
behaviour 'same as before'. Cobol/390 is computing more accurately--- that's
my problem!
User wants the same result as under os/vs, whether it is less accurate or
wrong or whatever.

I have tried all TRUNC options i.e. STD,OPT and BIN.
I have also tried all suggestions in this thread. The results were never
identical.

The basic fact is that the program is running in production. It produces a
report
which has many detail lines per page and there is a Total line. After
converting to
cobol/390, the change in the total value is in the integer portion, which is
not
acceptable to the user.

How would it be if I write a subroutine in os/vs cobol just to evaluate the
expression?
Then I call from the cobol/390 program. Any gotchas?
```

```
Your comment reminds me of a case where a slight inaccuracy had to be
introduced to satisfy the user.  When one sums rounded numbers, the total is
sometimes not 100%.  The user wanted the %-ages to add up to 100, so even
though it was mathematically less accurate, we had to adjust one of the
sub-totals so that the total added up to 100%!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OS/VS to COBOL/390 Conversion Problem

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8e2kdb$n4e$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8dt7qm$oi6$1@mawar.singnet.com.sg> <3903C3C3.C6489BAE@bellsouth.net> <8e080m$9db$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <8e2ghu$t63$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <sM3N4.4535$4O2.44175@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```
"Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net> wrote in message
news:sM3N4.4535$4O2.44175@typhoon.austin.rr.com...
> see comments embedded below:
>
> Thomas <tanmoy@singnet.com.sg> wrote in message
> news:8e2ghu$t63$1@coco.singnet.com.sg...
  <snip>
>
> how can the WRONG result be acceptable? Can you not convince
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
  <snip>

I hate to be the bearer of bad news (which I am certain Thomas is aware of -
but others in the NG may not be).  It is RELATIVELY common in large business
applications to require "identical" - not "more correct" results from "before
and after" maintenance (whether an upgrade of compiler - or simple
application maintenance).  Especially when arithmetic expression end up in
"numbers" that are turned into dollars, it is very, VERY, difficult to
explain that what used to be charged was "wrong" (and we won't give you any
rebate) and what we are charging now is more accurate (and you will need to
start paying the new rate immediately).

The classic example that I ran into time and TIME AGAIN when working at Micro
Focus was in code like the following:

  01 A  Pic 9 Value 7.
  01 B  Pic 9  Value 4
  01 C  Pic 9  Value zero.

   Compute C = (A / B ) * B

Now anyone who has studied arithmetic (not even math) will tell you that C
should end up with a value of 7.  UNFOTUNTATELY for reasons that are well
documented (but a tad strange), the IBM mainframe COBOLs (old and new) will
give an answer of 4, not 7.

It didn't matter how many times that we in Micro Focus explained this to
customers, they STILL wanted the PC COBOL to provide an answer of 7 and not 4
(even for programs that were to be "ported" to the PC for production, not
just those developed there).

Therefore, I have a LOT of sympathy with a shop that is trying to get the
same old "bad" answers in a migration.  I don't know that they are going to
succeed (and I sure hope that the programmers HAVE shown the users how the
new answers are "better").
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OS/VS to COBOL/390 Conversion Problem

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-04-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39059BC2.312A427F@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<8dt7qm$oi6$1@mawar.singnet.com.sg> <3903C3C3.C6489BAE@bellsouth.net> <8e080m$9db$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <8e2ghu$t63$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <sM3N4.4535$4O2.44175@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <8e2kdb$n4e$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> I hate to be the bearer of bad news (which I am certain Thomas is aware of -
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> start paying the new rate immediately).

I still wish people learned to use slide rules.  Once you understand
estimation, significant digits, and proportion, you know all the math
95% of the people would ever need.

I remember a discussion people had about whether the Bible was 100%
accurate.  Someone picked a foolish example - he found where the Bible
mentioned a circular object which was 3 times as far around as it was
across.  Since this implies that pi=3, the Bible was wrong!!!    Putting
aside details about real world measurements etc - what does he think pi
is?   To one significant digit (which was applicable in this case)
pi=3.  That is the way it is.  How many digits does he think is 100%
accurate?

Data processing has many cases where we have to sit down with the
accountant to determine exactly how we should be rounding our figures. 
They have rules which have been established which are very strict (if
not completely logical).  A lot of this was designed to stop fraud. 
Some of it has been developed to stop people from doing currency trading
in a circle ( pounds -> Euros -> dollars -> pounds) to benefit from
today's rounding errors.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<WvhN4.4775$4O2.56331@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```
Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
> "William M. Klein" wrote:
> > I hate to be the bearer of bad news (which I am certain Thomas is aware
of -
> > but others in the NG may not be).  It is RELATIVELY common in large
business
> > applications to require "identical" - not "more correct" results from
"before
> > and after" maintenance
> I remember a discussion people had about whether the Bible was 100%
> accurate.  Someone picked a foolish example - he found where the Bible
> mentioned a circular object which was 3 times as far around as it was
> across.  Since this implies that pi=3, the Bible was wrong

For the die-hard believers of a 100% correct Bible, consider this:

In 1 Kings, 7.23, it is written:
"And he made a molten sea, ten cubits from the one brim to other;
[...] and a line of thirty cubits did compass it round about."

Rabbi Nehemiah (A.D. 150) tells us that clearly the diameter of 10 cubits
was measured from the *inside* ("brim") of the wall while the circumference
was measured on the *outside* of the vessel. This account for the
difference.
Exercise for the reader: how thick was the wall?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OS/VS to COBOL/390 Conversion Problem

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-04-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3905B0DB.A589FCDB@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<8dt7qm$oi6$1@mawar.singnet.com.sg> <3903C3C3.C6489BAE@bellsouth.net> <8e080m$9db$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <8e2ghu$t63$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <sM3N4.4535$4O2.44175@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <8e2kdb$n4e$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <39059BC2.312A427F@cusys.edu> <WvhN4.4775$4O2.56331@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```
Leif Svalgaard wrote:
> 
> Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> Exercise for the reader: how thick was the wall?

How many significant digits do we have in the measurement?  Without
using scientific notation, those 10 and 30 cubits can either be 1 or 2
significant digits (or if you like, assume a precision).  With one
significant digit, the precision of the answer is variable enough to
make such a calculation meaningless.

Neither one of us is talking about the literalness of the Bible - we are
talking about how people get confused with numbers, significance, and
precision.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OS/VS to COBOL/390 Conversion Problem

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>

```
Leif Svalgaard <leif@ibm.net> wrote in message
news:WvhN4.4775$4O2.56331@typhoon.austin.rr.com...
>
> For the die-hard believers of a 100% correct Bible, consider this:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> was measured from the *inside* ("brim") of the wall while the
circumference
> was measured on the *outside* of the vessel. This account for the
> difference.
> Exercise for the reader: how thick was the wall?
>

Well, by modern math, the circumference of the brim (inside) should be 31.4
cubits give or take a tad.

Now if the outside wall is but 30 cubits, clearly what was made violates the
laws of physics, as the circumference of the outer wall must certainly by
greater than the 31.4 cubits of the inner wall.

So the real question is, "What was Rabbi Nehemiah smoking when her did the
measurements?"

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OS/VS to COBOL/390 Conversion Problem

```
Well, maybe he had it backwards, but if the thickness
of the wall were -0.55 cubits, everything comes out right.
This is just like in Thomas's problem where the right-hand
size is actually negative, but since A's picture doesn't
have a sign, the result is positive. Moral: if you want
to fudge, you can always do it.
BTW, Thomas, what did you decide to do about
this whole miserable affair?
For Bill Klein: imagine that Thomas has to do yet
another migration to a third platform a few years
from now, should he still try to emulate the old error?
I mean, where does it end?
```

```
results, do they really want to continue doing so when you CAN provide better
answers).
```

- **References:** `<8dt7qm$oi6$1@mawar.singnet.com.sg> <3903C3C3.C6489BAE@bellsouth.net> <8e080m$9db$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <8e2ghu$t63$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <sM3N4.4535$4O2.44175@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <8e2kdb$n4e$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <39059BC2.312A427F@cusys.edu> <WvhN4.4775$4O2.56331@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <6nrN4.903$mN2.207274@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <6TsN4.27$JH.2595@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```
Leif Svalgaard <leif@ibm.net> wrote in message news:6TsN4.27$JH.2595@typhoon.austin.rr.com...
> BTW, Thomas, what did you decide to do about
> this whole miserable affair?


1. I have made a series of experiments (with various PIC clauses, data-types,
    breaking expression into temp variables, compiler options)
    and prepared a document showing comparative results at different conditions.
    Finally the document was given to the user.

2. I have informed user that it is impossible to keep the results same.
    This discussion thread has been of great help to support my claim.
    When experts (like you all) express their ideas, convincing others is much
     easier task for me :-)

3. Alternate suggestion given (keeping the computational part as vs cobol routine etc..)
    [Thanks Bill Klein!]


Now the matter is with users and top management. Until further decision, old prog will
continue running in production.


Thanks to all of you.
```

```
dunno. but if everything fails, try to find out WHICH
of the factors of the formula cause the difference:

A = B * (1 - ( (C/12) * D ) ) * (1/1.05) ** (C/12)

is it (1-((C/12) * D)) or (1/1.05) ** (C/12) that
are different (with a bit of luck only one is).

my guess would be the exponentiation.
Let's say it is. Then since C can have at
most 100 values (pic 99), compute a
table (tongue beginning to press on inside
of cheek...) with the 100 differences
and simply apply that to falsify the result
to the user's satisfaction.
```

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ijjags0tkefd4sveo0ql961lausvrd80h9@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<8dt7qm$oi6$1@mawar.singnet.com.sg> <3903C3C3.C6489BAE@bellsouth.net> <8e080m$9db$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <8e2ghu$t63$1@coco.singnet.com.sg>`

```
On Tue, 25 Apr 2000 06:13:26 +0800 "Thomas" <tanmoy@singnet.com.sg> wrote:

:>All data items are implicit USAGE DISPLAY. I have cut short only the
:>variable names
:>and represented as A,B,C,D .
:>Since it is an existing program, I'm not sure why the picture clauses are
:>not consistent
:>or why there is no provision for sign. My job is to convert the language &
:>keep the program
:>behaviour 'same as before'. Cobol/390 is computing more accurately--- that's
:>my problem!
:>User wants the same result as under os/vs, whether it is less accurate or
:>wrong or whatever.

:>I have tried all TRUNC options i.e. STD,OPT and BIN.
:>I have also tried all suggestions in this thread. The results were never
:>identical.

:>The basic fact is that the program is running in production. It produces a
:>report
:>which has many detail lines per page and there is a Total line. After
:>converting to
:>cobol/390, the change in the total value is in the integer portion, which is
:>not
:>acceptable to the user.

:>How would it be if I write a subroutine in os/vs cobol just to evaluate the
:>expression?
:>Then I call from the cobol/390 program. Any gotchas?

I would suggest checking out the PMAP from the OS/VS Cobol compilation to
determine the lengths used for the intermediate fields.

Once you know that you can break up the compute statement to several
statements and use the OS/VS Cobol temporary lengths as the lengths of your
temporaries.

From 
  A = B * (1 - ((C/12) * D) * (1/1.05) ** (C/12))

To
  t1 = c / 12
  t2 = t1 * d
  t3 = 1 / 1.05  [1]
  t4 = t3 ** t1
  t5 = t4 * t2
  t6 = 1 - t5
  a = b * t6 

[1] Might be interesting to see if both COBOLs used the same value for this
constant (which cannot be exactly represented in either decimal or binary).
```

```
hearing that
some older mainframe compilers limited the size of temporary
intermedate
variables to match the size of the receiving (or sending for all
I know) field.

    Current compilers use a maximum size temporary variable.  I
think that the
OS390 result is correct.
```

#### ↳ Re: OS/VS to COBOL/390 Conversion Problem

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39025C7B.D1C5B9ED@home.com>`
- **References:** `<8dt7qm$oi6$1@mawar.singnet.com.sg>`

```


Thomas wrote:
> 
> We are converting an os/vs cobol program to cobol/390.
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
I can't answer your specific problem as I work on a PC, but I assume
from what you show above it is a COMPUTE ? As the books warn us there
can be problems with varying field sizes in computes. What I have done
PC-wise is to breakdown a COMPUTE into levels :-

compute result1 = (1/1.05) ** (C/12)
compute result2 = (C/12) * D) * result1
compute result3 = etc.....

Maybe you don't need to go the same extremes, but pick a 'break-up'
which gives you what you 'know' your results should be. Conceivably you
could get the correct result if you made all your fields the same size,
perhaps moving some values to temporary fields with a common size ?

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OS/VS to COBOL/390 Conversion Problem

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8e30q4$3hg$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8dt7qm$oi6$1@mawar.singnet.com.sg> <RgoAOZAJfGB5EwVQ@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`

```
One more time,
  TRUNCATION (and TRUNC compiler options) do NOT impact USAGE DISPLAY numeric
fields.

  ***

I believe (and am still working on verifying this) that as long as your
arithmetic expression includes fractional exponentiation (raising a number to
a fraction), that there is NO way to get identical results in new COBOLs as
were produced in OS/VS COBOL.  The "NewBOL" compilers (VS COBOL II and later)
*all* use a set of mathematical routines that are "shared" among DB2,
Fortran, COBOL, PL/I, C, etc.  These are "highly accurate" routines and just
will NOT (cannot) provide the same results as the older code (which was OS/VS
COBOL specific).

  ***

There is one "possible" solution to this problem (which would be practical if
you only have a few statements that need to get "same results" - but which
would be impractical if there are wide-spread arithmetic expression that need
to get "same results").

If you create a "small" OS/VS COBOL program that JUST does the requested
arithmetic expression and receives all the operands in Linkage Section and
returns the results also thru Linkage section, then you could compile this
program with OS/VS COBOL.

You then take your "major" logic and recompile it with the new COBOL (IBM
COBOL for OS/390 & VM - or similar compiler) and replace the current
arithmetic expression with a call (dynamic or static) to the OS/VS COBOL
program to do the arithmetic.  This would mean that your "main" program would
need to be compiled with DATA(24) - so it could pass the parameters to the
OS/VS COBOL program (where the linkage section needs to be "below the line").

If you aren't aware of it, IBM currently (and for the foreseeable future)
supports OS/VS COBOL compiled programs - as long as they are run with the LE
library.  (Theoretically, you aren't supposed to be compiling these programs
now - but I think the scenario I described above would PROBABLY be
supported.)
```

## Thread: book: Advanced COBOL, Third Edition — 39 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### book: Advanced COBOL, Third Edition

- **From:** infocat@sprynet.com (Frank Swarbrick)
- **Date:** 2000-04-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39012088.23666275@nntp.sprynet.com>`

```
So my boss comes up today, hands me this book, and say to tell him if
it's worth keeping for the shop or if he should take it back to the
store,  He knows me so well!  Anyway, the book is titled "Advanced
COBOL, Third Edition" and subtitled "For Structured and
Object-Oriented Programming."  Author is Gary DeWard Brown.  Publisher
is Wiley Computer Publishing.  Price is $49.99.  (Yikes!)

Has anyone read this book?  Comments?  I just glanced through it so
far.  It seems to touch on a great many things, but doesn't
necessarily go in to a great amount of detail.  Mostly this is in the
"Subroutines" section.  Other sections may be more detailed.  Anyway,
it's not necessarily a bad thing.  Once you know that a certain thing
exists you can use manuals or other books to get more details.

I'm sure I'll read the whole thing.  I can't speak for anyone else at
work, though, as most people seem to get along "fine" without learning
new things.

Anyway, an comments on the book?

Frank Swarbrick
home: infocat@sprynet.com / work: frank.swarbrick@efirstbank.com
"Ignorance and prejudice - and fear walk hand in hand" --Rush "Witch Hunt"
```

#### ↳ Re: book: Advanced COBOL, Third Edition

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39013B8B.23851593@home.com>`
- **References:** `<39012088.23666275@nntp.sprynet.com>`

```


Frank Swarbrick wrote:
> 
> So my boss comes up today, hands me this book, and say to tell him if
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Frank,

I can't guarantee for sure - but from memory this is one of the books I
left on the bookshelf - so many (well there's only about 6 or 8
altogether) generalize about OO COBOL and don't lead you anywhere.
So let me recap on my two "favourites" :-

- SIGS ISBN 1-884842-34-8/Prentic Hall ISBN 0-13-261140-6 - Arranga and
Coyle 'OO COBOL"  $CDN 52

Wilson (Will) Price "Elements of OO COBOL" $?? (can't remember). You can
get this one pronto from objectz.com if you write Ed Arranga - 
74651.2630@compuserve.com. Perhaps Ed has a loft full of the other book
too ;-) . Might be able to get you that. 

They both discuss the topic and then give you examples in 'real' source
code. Arranga/Coyle demonstrate an in-house Library system - ANSI
display and working-storage contains the list of library books. Will
Price's book takes you through it step by step building up source from
structured to OO. Then Will has an addendum dealing with flat file
methods.

Put the two books together, the Arranga/Coyle Library system and
superimpose Will's file handling - and you have a working OO system.

Having got through these two books - then contact me and I'll happily
give you adapted source code based on the Will Price file handling
methodology.

Now a question for Thane and Don - which books have you read that helped
you with OO ? Same goes for any of you other OO people.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
COBOL for VSE (the LE version on VSE) does *not* support OO.

Interestingly enough, even COBOL for MVS & VM (or COBOL for OS/390 & VM) does
NOT even support OO on VM.  (It only works on MVS or OS/390.)
```

#### ↳ Re: book: Advanced COBOL, Third Edition

- **From:** "The COBOL Frog" <H.Klink@IMN.NotThisPart.nl>
- **Date:** 2000-04-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8dt1cm$kn7$1@porthos.nl.uu.net>`
- **References:** `<39012088.23666275@nntp.sprynet.com>`

```
Having learned OO via Analysis and Design courses, with uses of the books "Object Oriented Analasys" and "Object Oriented Design" from Coad/Yourdon, I started programming in Smalltalk and in C++, after courses in those languages too.

Meanwhile (some 4 years) I stayed in the business of programming in COBOL for about 10% of the time. Other days were filled with teaching COBOL/PL/1, C++, OO design, Smalltalk, and some ... about 50% procedural, 50% OO. That's practice, not books!

Than I ran into OO-COBOL by promotion speak of Micro Focus, interest etc. 1997 was in the near future, OO-Cobol and COBOL '97 were synonimous those days. I followed some preliminary version of a COBOL course and later-on developed a definitive version. We used the OO-extension of the MF workbench and later Personal COBOL. Then I started using it (OO-COBOL) in real projects, allbeit small-scaled.

I prefer "Object Oriented software Construction" from Bertrand Meyer as *the* book on OO.

Good programming is good design followed by good coding. Good design is possible either OO or traditional. Coding can be procedural in both cases. But a non-OO design (however well-crafted) is almost impossible to code in OO. 

(I highly prefer to code OO if the design is OO, and to code non-OO if the design is non-OO).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: book: Advanced COBOL, Third Edition

- **From:** infocat@sprynet.com (Frank Swarbrick)
- **Date:** 2000-04-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39026ae7.41395066@nntp.sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<39012088.23666275@nntp.sprynet.com> <k1oM4.17678$0o4.122597@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
On Sat, 22 Apr 2000 20:34:24 GMT, docdwarf@clark.net () wrote:

>In article <39012088.23666275@nntp.sprynet.com>,
>Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com> wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>DD

Please give me a break.  I haven't had homework for over ten years.
What exactly is it about my question that makes it look like homework.

In any case, ignoring your snide reply, I'll make some comments on the
book.  (Looks like no one else has heard of it.)  There are three
parts: I) BASIC COBOL, II) ADVANCED COBOL, and III) BEYOND COBOL.  So
far I've only read part I.  It is, of course, not much use to someone
who already knows COBOL.  But I wanted to see how the writer
approached it.

I honestly don't think it would be much good for just learning COBOL.
Various facts are presented, but it really doesn't go in to *why* you
would want to use a certain feature.  Such as REDEFINES.  I mean, it
says it's to save space by using the same data area, but there's no
real hard examples as to how one would do that in a real life
situation.

He also makes several mistakes.  One is just totally wrong.  He states
that PERFORM WITH TEST AFTER UNTIL ... is the default if you just say
PERFORM UNTIL ... We all know that it defaults to WITH TEST BEFORE,
and in fact WITH TEST AFTER was not even part of the 1974 standard.
(Well, nor was WITH TEST BEFORE, for that matter.)

Then, as an example of "Trust the code and not the external
documentation" he uses the following:
*  Determine if Last-Yr is a LEAP YEAR.  If so, Leap-Flag
*  contains ZERO.
      DIVIDE Last-Yr BY 4 GIVING A-Num REMAINDER Leap-Flag

Now if this were an example of *bad* code that would be fine...
But...

Some of his coding style gets to me, but of course that's just a pet
peave.  He likes to use "THEN" in his IF statements:

IF condition-1
    THEN DISPLAY 'THIS IS TRUE'
                 SET Data-Name TO TRUE
    ELSE DISPLAY 'THIS IS NOT TRUE'
END-IF

I do like his statement not using periods except to end the paragraph,
though.

Anyway, I think I'm going off on a tangent here.  Skimming through the
next section (ADVANCED COBOL) I can see some other problems.  He
mentions the "SELECT OPTIONAL"' clause for files that may not exist.
He says you can test the file status by coding the FILE STATUS clause
as described in Chapter 17.  Chapter 17 then goes on to say that FILE
STATUS is rarely used!  Really?  We use it all the time at our shop,
and I've always heard it's the best way to go.

There is mention of the availability of CALL BY CONTENT and CALL BY
VALUE (as well as the default CALL BY REFERENCE).  Here is his
explaination of CALL BY VALUE:
"BY VALUE is similar to BY CONTENT, except it uses linkage that allows
calls to non-COBOL languages, such as C/C++.  The arguments can be
binary, floating-point, pointer, or a single-byte alphanumeric
character."

Now, knowing C/C++, I realize that this means that the actual value is
passed, rather than a reference (pointer) to a value.  But honestly,
if I didn't already know it I'm not sure that I would have figured it
out from this,  (The COBOL I use does not even offer "BY VALUE".)

As for OO, there's only one chapter on it; about 35 pages.

All in all I'm not terribly impressed.  As I said in a previous
message, there is some mention about most every feature of COBOL.  But
I don't think they're all explained very well.  Another more detailed
reference is definately necessary in many cases.

Frank Swarbrick
home: infocat@sprynet.com / work: frank.swarbrick@efirstbank.com
"Ignorance and prejudice - and fear walk hand in hand" --Rush "Witch Hunt"
```

```
On Sun, 23 Apr 2000 19:02:16 GMT, docdwarf@clark.net () wrote:

>In article <39026ae7.41395066@nntp.sprynet.com>,

Well he gave it to me because he knows that I'm the person at work who
has the most interesting in learning new things.  It's not like it was
```
