That story of a little boy with his finger in a dyk (dijk in Dutch) is just .. a story.

But we do have our own Piet Hein in Holland!!!! He fought against some Spanish Armada, victory followed and he returned home with gold en fame. This has all happened in ... uh, uh, long time ago (that I was in school).

A song was made in still very known in Holland, especially used in schools on queens day (30th of April) and liberation day (end of WWII, 5th of May here).

The song:

Heb je wel gehoord van de Zileren Vloot,
de Zilveren Vloot van Spanje.
Die had er veel Spaanse matten aan boord,
en appeltjes van Oranje.

Piet Hein, Piet Hein,
Piet Hein zijn naam is klein.
Zijn daden bennen groot,
zijn daden bennen groot,
Hij heeft gewonnen de zilvervloot.

Enz.

Oops. Couldn't resist.

<a id="t-020daca63d"></a>
## Thread: other problem — 2 msgs

### Re: other problem

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8dtcrc$hbt$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<39022443.EB66F92@free.fr>`

```
First, you may find that asking your questions in comp.lang.cobol instead of
alt.cobol will get more responses.  That is the "preferred" newsgroup for
COBOL questions.

Concatenation expressions (with "&") is currently an extension to Standard
COBOL - but will be part of the next Standard (due out sometime later this
decade - hopefully in just a few years).  HOWEVER, for those implementations
that do support it (and in the draft Standard) you are correct that you may
only concatenate two literals - not a literal and a data item.

To do what you want, there are a few ways to do it.

1) Use reference modification:
    Move truc to bidule
    Move "machin" to bidule (function Length (truc) + 1:)
       (this last statement figures out how long "truc" is and then moves the
literal after it in the receiving item.

2) Use the STRING statement
   String truc delimited by size
              "machin" delimited by size
        into bidule
```

- **From:** Chow <chow@free.fr>
- **Date:** 2000-04-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3902A99F.6C53BDDB@free.fr>`
- **References:** `<39022443.EB66F92@free.fr> <8dtcrc$hbt$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

<a id="t-63a595af53"></a>
## Thread: Screen Section Question — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Screen Section Question

- **From:** sighkosis@hotmail.com (Ctesias)
- **Date:** 2000-04-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<390242ae.15783935@news.ozemail.com.au>`

```
I'm interested to know whether it's possible to do the following:

In the SCREEN SECTION you define a data input screen of some sort,
such as

01 Data-Input-Screen
  05 LINE 1 COLUMN 1 VALUE "Enter Name: "
  05 Scr-Name	PIC A(9) USING WS-Name
       LINE 1 COLUMN 25.

 then in PROCEDURE DIVISION you say

DISPLAY Data-Input-Screen
ACCEPT Data-Input-Screen

Is it possible instead of getting the data from the console using the
ACCEPT  to instead move the data from working storage?
if so, what's the syntax for it?

				Thanks,
				Miles Gard
				(sighkosis@hotmail.com)
```

#### ↳ Re: Screen Section Question

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
- **Date:** 2000-04-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<BC6CF5F07183E454.2E8ABA60028BD511.C19567024EEF88EF@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<390242ae.15783935@news.ozemail.com.au>`

```
On Sun, 23 Apr 2000 00:34:56 GMT, sighkosis@hotmail.com (Ctesias)
enlightened us:

>I'm interested to know whether it's possible to do the following:
>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>				(sighkosis@hotmail.com)

Maybe someone who is more familiar with PC Cobol will answer you, but
as I understand your coding, the ACCEPT verb will put what was entered
on the screen (or console)  into Working-Storage in data item Ws-Name.
What else might you be looking for?

Regards,
 
          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

And whose cruel idea was it to put an "S" in the word "Lisp"?


Boycott Mitsubishi Industries

Remove nospam to email me.

 Steve
```

It's a while since I did Screen Section - may I suggest you look at the
Screen syntax for DISPLAY ---- using ws-name at line col etc... (i.e.
you either initialize fields for ACCEPTS or you place values in them to
DISPLAY).

Jimmy
```

#### ↳ Re: Screen Section Question

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 2000-04-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8dvk6l$17n$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<390242ae.15783935@news.ozemail.com.au>`

```
Yes, you can accept from command-line, from line xx column yy or you can
accept from a screen, defined in screen section.

If you look in language reference, you will see, that they describe 3
formats, and I can recommend that you use accept format 3 - That's the one
you show in your example. But you should refine it a bit. Try this (most
compilers will allow you to do it)

DISPLAY Data-Input-Screen
ACCEPT Scr-Name

Why? Because it allows you to do field-by-field validations and it is
probably what you want.
Adding some colors to the screen is another thing you can play with. All the
time, all your screen definitions are at one place, and very easy to
maintain.

Regards
Ib

Ctesias skrev i meddelelsen <390242ae.15783935@news.ozemail.com.au>...
>I'm interested to know whether it's possible to do the following:
>
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Screen Section Question

- **From:** sighkosis@hotmail.com (Ctesias)
- **Date:** 2000-04-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39037aad.15622481@news.ozemail.com.au>`
- **References:** `<390242ae.15783935@news.ozemail.com.au> <8dvk6l$17n$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`

```
On Sun, 23 Apr 2000 21:53:16 +0200, "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
wrote:

>Yes, you can accept from command-line, from line xx column yy or you can
>accept from a screen, defined in screen section.
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Nah, what I actually meant was that instead of accepting anything from
the console at all..., actually moving data from working storage to
the screen section and displaying the screen. Is this possible?
I know this probably sounds majorly pointless, cause most people would
just display the structure in working storage, but the screen section
has better layout commands

				Miles
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Screen Section Question

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 2000-04-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8e1f61$o65$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<390242ae.15783935@news.ozemail.com.au> <8dvk6l$17n$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <39037aad.15622481@news.ozemail.com.au>`

```
Yes,
That's lesson 2 :-)
You can accept from fields in screen section, but you can display them as
well, and you don't need to move them. In screen section you say USING
ws-something or FROM ws-a-read-only-field

You can get extreemly high performance if you only display what's needed. so
from your sample, you can easily write:

Display Scr-Name
Accept Scr-Name

Or, whenever your WS-name changes (E.g. after reading from a file) you can
say

Display Scr-Name

Another gimmic is to define a Screen with date and clock (without Blank
Screen). You can display it from time to time. An idea could be to make a
loop with and 'Accept <some field> with timeout 1'. Every second the accept
times out and you can say 'Display time-scr' and return to the the original
accept. Then you have a running clock on the screen.

Hope it helps.
Ib

sorry, it's like my third question about Cobol I ask on different
newsgroup :)

in a school work, i'm doing a program that will use a list of theme in a
file to create as many different HTML file as there is different themes
all works very vell but one thing:
for the different HTML files I use this line:

SELECT f-theme ASSIGN TO sortie (where sortie is a varaible)

then in the DATA DIVISON WORKING-STORAGE SECTION, I wrote:

01 sortie.
  02 file-name PIC X(20).
  02 PIC X(5) VALUE ".html".

and then during the program, I insert the name of the themes in
file-name
but the problem is that I get file like
"cscience-fiction                 .html" or "fantasy
.html" (the name and lots blank space)
I understand that it use all the 20 characters of the variable
definition but is there a way to get ride of them

I try oncatenation but the themes name are store in a data file on 20
characters too

did I do something wrong ? is there a better or easier way ?

thanks for the answers
```

#### ↳ Re: school work

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%AMM4.4228$4O2.35459@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<3903819A.A00B0B8A@free.fr>`

```
01 sortie.
   02 file-name PIC X(20).
   02 extension PIC X(5) VALUE ".html".
01  result pic x(25).
string file-name delimited by space extension delimited by size
into result
select f-theme assign to result

<a id="t-c756358186"></a>
## Thread: Can't create temporary DB table with NetExpress — 5 msgs

### Can't create temporary DB table with NetExpress

- **From:** Dragan Matic <gekko@EUnet.yu>
- **Date:** 2000-04-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3904342B.266E4C2B@EUnet.yu>`

```
The following short program doesn't work. I can create temporary table
(sqlcode = 0), but if I try to access it after that sql server allways
returns error code 'Table doesn't exist'. It looks like after I create a
temporary table program loses connection to database and table is
dropped. Why is this happening? I am using NetExpress 3.0 and MS SQL
server 7.0 on win 2000 server.

-----------------------------------------------

      $set sql(dbman=odbc)
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       exec sql include sqlca end-exec
       procedure division.

           exec sql
               connect to 'database' user 'sa'
           end-exec

           exec sql
               create table #table1 (
                   numb1   int     not null,
                   name1   char(10) not null)
           end-exec
           display sqlcode  *> Everything ok here
           exec sql
               commit
           end-exec

           exec sql
               insert into #table1
               (numb1, name1)
               values
               (1, 'adsdfas')
           end-exec
           display sqlcode  *> Here's the problem: 
           exec sql         *> Table doesn't exist
               commit
           end-exec

           exec sql
               drop table #table1
           end-exec
           display sqlcode  *> Same thing here
           exec sql
               commit
           end-exec

           exec sql
               disconnect current
           end-exec

           stop run
           .
```

#### ↳ Re: Can't create temporary DB table with NetExpress

- **From:** "Randy Zimmerman" <rzmerant@execpc.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3905cd97$0$64482@news.execpc.com>`
- **References:** `<3904342B.266E4C2B@EUnet.yu>`

```
Your problem may have something to do with the table name you picked.  Some
SQL engines may not like the "#" in the table name or will only accept it if
you enclose the table name with the identifier quote character.  You can use
the SQL Assistant tool in Net Express to display that info after connecting
to the data source and right mouse clicking on the data source and selecting
"data source properties".



"Dragan Matic" <gekko@EUnet.yu> wrote in message
news:3904342B.266E4C2B@EUnet.yu...
> The following short program doesn't work. I can create temporary table
> (sqlcode = 0), but if I try to access it after that sql server allways
…[51 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Can't create temporary DB table with NetExpress

- **From:** Dragan Matic <gekko@EUnet.yu>
- **Date:** 2000-04-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39089AE5.C5F1C098@EUnet.yu>`
- **References:** `<3904342B.266E4C2B@EUnet.yu> <3905cd97$0$64482@news.execpc.com>`

```
    The problem is that '#' means: 'Create a temporary table that will be active
only while connection is active'. After that table is automatically dropped.
Now, what happens here is that table is dropped immediately after creating. When
I try to do the same from MS SQL assistant everything works fine.

Randy Zimmerman wrote:

> Your problem may have something to do with the table name you picked.  Some
> SQL engines may not like the "#" in the table name or will only accept it if
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Can't create temporary DB table with NetExpress

- **From:** "Randy Zimmerman" <rzmerant@execpc.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3909a8a8$0$26047@news.execpc.com>`
- **References:** `<3904342B.266E4C2B@EUnet.yu> <3905cd97$0$64482@news.execpc.com> <39089AE5.C5F1C098@EUnet.yu>`

```
I checked with one of our developers who know more about sql server and what
happens under the covers and his reply is:

This is a known featurette of SQLServer 7.0. Essentially, OpenESQL
 prepares and executes most statements. SQL Server creates temp stored
 procs for prepared statements. When you execute you execute a stored proc
 which creates a temp table, at exit SQLServer decides the table is now out
 of scope and drops it. With 6.5 you could tell SQL Server not to create
 temp stored procs, but 7.0 doesn't give you the choice. Hence the upgrade
 from 6.5 to 7.0 has broken some apps.

 The solutions are:

 - use global temp tables (i.e. ##temptable rather than #temptable). This is
  likely to be unsuitable in mutli-user situations, but could be OK for
 batch jobs.

- do the create table via an exec sql execute immediate ... end-exec. In
 this case OpenESQL does SQLExecDirect rather than prepare + execute to
 create the table, so SQL Server doesn't use a temp stored proc and the
 table is available to other statements (whether execute immediate or
 regular 'static' or dynamic SQL).



"Dragan Matic" <gekko@EUnet.yu> wrote in message
news:39089AE5.C5F1C098@EUnet.yu...
>     The problem is that '#' means: 'Create a temporary table that will be
active
> only while connection is active'. After that table is automatically
dropped.
> Now, what happens here is that table is dropped immediately after
creating. When
> I try to do the same from MS SQL assistant everything works fine.
>
> Randy Zimmerman wrote:
>
> > Your problem may have something to do with the table name you picked.
Some
> > SQL engines may not like the "#" in the table name or will only accept
it if
> > you enclose the table name with the identifier quote character.  You can
use
> > the SQL Assistant tool in Net Express to display that info after
connecting
> > to the data source and right mouse clicking on the data source and
selecting
> > "data source properties".
> >
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Can't create temporary DB table with NetExpress

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Dragan Matic <gekko@EUnet.yu>
- **Date:** 2000-04-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<390BF21E.414D4A23@EUnet.yu>`
- **References:** `<3904342B.266E4C2B@EUnet.yu> <3905cd97$0$64482@news.execpc.com> <39089AE5.C5F1C098@EUnet.yu> <3909a8a8$0$26047@news.execpc.com>`

```
Received the following note from <Shawna.Schueller@xinotech.com>: 

-------------------
Dear Mr. Clarke:

I have been following your commentaries on the comp.lang.cobol
bulletin board.  We at www.xinotech.com have COBOL reengineering tools
to generate documentation websites for legacy applications and to
modularize and modernize these applications automatically.  If you
look at our website, I am interested in your opinion regarding how
fitting our technology may be for large applications based on your
experience.  We could also give you a link to an online demonstration
of these tools.  I think we could benefit from your feedback.

Best regards,

Shawna Schueller 
---------------------


Other than responding to a remark about "mainframe opportunities" I
only posted one question many months ago asking an "ultimate novice"
question.   What can they possibly have seen (other than an internet
ID)?     Makes one wonder how carefully they picked the targets of
their e-mail, doesn't it?

<a id="t-a73d135c77"></a>
## Thread: Cost of Software Maintenance — 4 msgs

### Cost of Software Maintenance

- **From:** brian_cromwell@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-04-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8e1uub$t02$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I am currently work in an environment where multiple Cobol/CICS systems
perform basically the same function (from different business units
designing similar systems independently, and as a result of mergers).  I
am looking for some standard numbers that could be applied to the
banking industry for the cost of code maintenance for Cobol/CICS
programs to help estimate the savings that would be realized if
redundant systems were retired and users standardized on a single system
per unique function.  Are there reliable sources (books, websites, etc.)
that could be used.  Internally, these numbers are not available to us.

Thank you,

Brian Cromwell
Octagon Information Services


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cost of Software Maintenance

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 2000-04-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<oT%M4.17809$0o4.124306@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<8e1uub$t02$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In article <8e1uub$t02$1@nnrp1.deja.com>,  <brian_cromwell@my-deja.com> wrote:
>I am currently work in an environment where multiple Cobol/CICS systems
>perform basically the same function (from different business units
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>that could be used.  Internally, these numbers are not available to us.

Hmmmmm... this has an odd 'smell' to it, no offense intended, Mr
Cromwell.  Outside of the usual 'given an item in inventory which requires
a cost of (x) to maintain it then the laws of mathematics mandate the
conclusion that fewer items will require a smaller expenditure'  I wonder
about your last statement: 'Internally, these numbers are not available to
us.'

Does this mean that the institution in question does not use *any* kind of
chargeback or cost-accounting when it comes to data services?  That would
make simple things, like justifying the cost of a new CPU, rather
difficult to do.  Furthermore it has been my experience - and I have lead
a small life, granted! - that banks, for the most part, have the habit of
watching where their pennies go... that's what they are in business to do,
last I looked.

The conclusion at which one can arrive is that the number *are* available,
somewhere, but are being kept from you by Someone With Juice... a Someone
who benefits, directly or indirectly, from the present inefficiencies and
has sufficient authority that they do not need to co-operate with your
requests.  (This is the Corporate equivalent of the schoolyard 'Who's
gonna make me?')  This does not bode well for the success of your efforts.

You have my sympathies, my commiserations and my advice to get *out* of
there and find another contract, fast.

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: Cost of Software Maintenance

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<770N4.4504$4O2.43024@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<8e1uub$t02$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
<brian_cromwell@my-deja.com> wrote in message
news:8e1uub$t02$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> the cost of code maintenance for Cobol/CICS
> programs to help estimate the savings that would be realized if
> redundant systems were retired and users standardized on a single system
> per unique function.  Are there reliable sources (books, websites, etc.)
> that could be used.  Internally, these numbers are not available to us.

These numbers are always hard to come by. Mainly because the
shops are so different and much depends on the staff and the
environment. FWIIW, I show here the statistics for a large system
where we have kept (the process is automatic - thus accurate)
track of the various activities over the last 15 years (since 1985/7/4):

MATERIAL REUSED               LINES:33167
DAYS WITH DEVELOPMENT   360   LINES:80553   COST:  360.80   LINES/DAY: 223
DAYS WITH ENHANCEMENTS  478   LINES:28531   COST:  255.58   LINES/DAY: 111
DAYS WITH MAINTENANCE   907   LINES:10368   COST:  183.83   LINES/DAY:  56
TOTAL EFFORT                  LINES:152619   COST:  800.21   LINES/DAY: 65

the cost is expressed in man-days. The total cost of
enhancements/maintenance
is more than the initial development cost. How you translate that into
dollars
I don't know.

Leif
```

#### ↳ Re: Cost of Software Maintenance

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3904B5B9.9CB764D8@home.com>`
- **References:** `<8e1uub$t02$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```


brian_cromwell@my-deja.com wrote:
> 
> I am currently work in an environment where multiple Cobol/CICS systems
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
 
It's a 1/4 century since I worked with mainframes :-) If I was doing it
now, this is a broad outline of what I would do :-

- New applications - cost them per man-hour - fairly easy because Joe,
Bill etc. work on a new program for x days, weeks or months etc.

- Existing application maintenance - and here I'm assuming you have two
teams, Development and Maintenance - the Maintenance people would need
to keep more rigorous records, time recorded per program
enhanced/altered. Possibly another factor included here would be
Operations re-runs because of foul-ups.

This Maintenance log is going to tell you several things - poorly
programmed, badly (systems) designed, ambiguous operating instructions
etc... You can then start using these statistics to justify
're-inventing' the wheel for new applications.

Of course you do say the numbers aren't available. Well perhaps the
first thing is that a start should be made to ensure they are available
from now on ! Other than a gut feeling about 'replacement' you can't
justify too much without numbers.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

<a id="t-deff0296bb"></a>
## Thread: The Universal Tool — 1 msgs

### The Universal Tool

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-04-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39048D23.46F4D2FD@cusys.edu>`

```
The "Swiss Army Knife" theory of language development has been tried in
the past.  PL/I did pretty well at it.  But I am not sold on the
concept.

But I very much like enhancements to Cobol which are Cobol-like.  The
following is an example which I took out of IEEE Software March/April
2000:

01  birthdate.  *> month, day, year: mmddyyyy
 03 month pic 99 valid values 1 thru 12.
    valid value 1 thru 32 when month =
      1 or 3 or 5 or 7 or 8 or 10 or 12
    valid value 1 thru 29 when month = 2
    valid value 1 thru 30 when month =
      4 or 6 or 9 or 11
 03 year pic 9(4) invalid when year >
    function current-date (1:4)
01  error-message pic x(80)
  error "date is not numeric" on format for month,
     day-of-month, year
  error "month must be <= 12" on content for month
  error "day not valid in that month" on content for day-of-month
  error "invalid birth year" on relation for year.

...
validate birthdate


This is data and business rules centric evolution of the language. 
Furthermore, it is obvious what the above code does - to maintain that
code do you need to pull out a manual to look at those unfamiliar
commands?
```

## Thread: Cobol Assignment - Help!!!! — 1 msgs

### Re: Cobol Assignment - Help!!!!

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 2000-04-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8e1dna$m3j$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<39038dce$1@127.0.0.1>`

```
Hi Terri,
Please do not use Word  for Cobol Source. When I saved your program all
apostrophes were changed to 'smart-quotes' and my compiler couldn't
understand them. The margins were a mess, because tabs were converted to
spaces and so on. Find a Cobol Editor or use Notepad.

Be careful with dots (.), many missing dots.
In working storage you have forgot to put '01  ' in front of datanames.
You must remove some hyphens (-) between '01' and dataname
You perform 300-write. It should be 300-write-headings.

You must define the sort correct.

If you send me a mail, I can return an error list. That will give you
something to work with

Keep it up
Ib


Terri Laquitara skrev i meddelelsen <39038dce$1@127.0.0.1>...
>
>I am in great need of assistance cobol
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>Thanks!!!!!
```

## Thread: Can COBOL MVS be used to access Sybase & DB2? — 11 this month / 13 total

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Can COBOL MVS be used to access Sybase & DB2?

- **From:** "Vic Hariton" <vic.hariton@cpa.state.tx.us>
- **Date:** 2000-04-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8e4t65$iam$1@goblin.tdh.state.tx.us>`

```
I have a need to refresh data stored in Sybase with DB2 v5 data.   We can
extract the data into a flat file and then FTP & load into Sybase, but were
hoping to have a more direct method.
```

#### ↳ Re: Can COBOL MVS be used to access Sybase & DB2?

- **From:** "ChrisOsborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e6ndsfyr$GA.295@cpmsnbbsa04>`
- **References:** `<8e4t65$iam$1@goblin.tdh.state.tx.us>`

```
I thought Sybase offered a product capable of just what you are asking for .
. .

<pause for search through Sybase's website>

Oh yeah, middleware from Sybase called ClearConnect.

Go to this website:
http://www.sybase.com/products/entcon/clear_connect.html

Lead off paragraph of the product description goes like this:

Have a departmental, workgroup, or pilot application that requires access to
IBM DB2 data?
Sybase ClearConnect provides two-tier connectivity from any Windows 3.1,
Windows 95 or Windows NT platform (16- or 32-bit) directly to any IBM DB2
data source. With ClearConnect, applications such as those built with
Powerbuilder, Microsoft Access, Visual Basic, or other ODBC-compliant tools
can access and update DB2 data directly, without a middle-tier access
server.


If your Sybase isn't on a PC, try reading up on DirectConnect or even
OmniConnect (both also from Sybase).

I haven't used any of them, so I can't vouch for their effectiveness.  This
post is just FYI.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Can COBOL MVS be used to access Sybase & DB2?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "ChrisOsborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uPu7G9Xs$GA.237@cpmsnbbsa03>`
- **References:** `<8e4t65$iam$1@goblin.tdh.state.tx.us> <e6ndsfyr$GA.295@cpmsnbbsa04> <8e79b5$gam$1@goblin.tdh.state.tx.us> <#GOPIy#r$GA.303@cpmsnbbsa04> <8ecmh8$7r7$1@goblin.tdh.state.tx.us>`

```
Sorry, I didn't go far enough up the product ladder.

Sybases Open Server Connect is the product you need (in theory, anyway)

Here are it's claims to functionality (note the parts about MVS):


#####################################################
Sybase Open ServerConnect
A Sybase EnterpriseConnect Product

Organizations need to access data from a wide range of heterogeneous systems
to formulate strategic decisions. With Sybase's Open ServerConnect� for CICS
and Open ServerConnect for IMS and MVS, users running their favorite
LAN-based applications on desktops get high-performance read/write access to
data and on-line production applications that reside on CICS, IMS, and MVS
systems. At the same time, these products allow mainframe managers to
maintain control of data integrity and security through existing mainframe
security systems.
Open ServerConnect for CICS and Open ServerConnect for IMS and MVS allow
your mainframe to act as both an application server and a data server in a
distributed environment. These application programming interfaces (APIs)
allow CICS, IMS, and MVS data and application programs to appear as Sybase�
data to a calling program --typically an Open Client� application. These
APIs let you deliver critical CICS, IMS, and MVS data and services to
requesting clients through a single Sybase infrastructure.


DirectConnect for MVS also acts as an OmniSQL Access Module� for enterprise
location transparency with OmniConnect. This allows you to integrate your
mainframe data seamlessly with other LAN-based systems such as joining
Oracle and Adaptive Server data.
###################################

Unfortunately it looks like you need an array of these products to get what
you want.

:(












"Vic Hariton" <vic.hariton@cpa.state.tx.us> wrote in message
news:8ecmh8$7r7$1@goblin.tdh.state.tx.us...
> If I understood correctly, it sounds like these can only be used from the
pc
> side.  I need to do this from the mainframe to  manipulate the data and
then

asking

IBM
> > DB2
> > > > data source. With ClearConnect, applications such as those built
with
> > > > Powerbuilder, Microsoft Access, Visual Basic, or other
ODBC-compliant
> > > tools
> > > > can access and update DB2 data directly, without a middle-tier
access
> > > > server.

> > > >

even
> > > > OmniConnect (both also from Sybase).

effectiveness.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Can COBOL MVS be used to access Sybase & DB2?

- **From:** prinzeck@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-04-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8e9lg2$37t$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8e4t65$iam$1@goblin.tdh.state.tx.us> <e6ndsfyr$GA.295@cpmsnbbsa04> <8e79b5$gam$1@goblin.tdh.state.tx.us>`

```
In article <8e79b5$gam$1@goblin.tdh.state.tx.us>,
  "Vic Hariton" <vic.hariton@cpa.state.tx.us> wrote:
> ... if a product purchase is necessary, I may be out of luck.
>

Mayby You can use FTP-DB2-Connect like this job:

 //* =================================================== *
 //STEP0010 EXEC PGM=IDCAMS
 //SYSPRINT DD  SYSOUT=*
 //SYSIN    DD  *
      DELETE MyTsoUser.SQL.OUTDAR          PURGE
      IF MAXCC = 8 THEN SET MAXCC = 0
 /*
 //* =================================================== *

 //* ==========================================
 //FTPSTEP EXEC PGM=FTP,
 //            PARM='00.0.0.0    (EXIT'
 //STEPLIB  DD  DISP=SHR,DSN=DB2T.DB201.TARG.SDSNLOAD   DSNALI/DSNHLI2..
 //*
 //* MYTSOUSER.SQL.IN
 //*   contains
 //*
 //*     SELECT *
 //*       FROM      Owner.DB2TABLE
 //* ----------------------------------------------
 //*
 //*       FTP-Subcommands:
 //INPUT    DD *
     MyUseerAt00.0.0.0
     MyPasswordAt00.0.0.0
     DIR
     LOCSITE FILETYPE=SQL
     LOCSITE DB2=DB2T
     LOCSITE SQLCOL=NAMES
     LOCSITE SPREAD
     PUT    'MYTSOUSER.SQL.IN'   SQLOUTDAR.TXT
     DIR
     LOCSITE FILETYPE=SEQ
     LOCSITE RECFM=VB
     LOCSITE BLKSIZE=0
     LOCSITE LRECL=32752
     GET     SQLOUTDAR.TXT    SQL.OUTDAR
     DELETE  SQLOUTDAR.TXT
     DIR
     CLOSE
     QUIT
 //*
 //SYSPRINT DD SYSOUT=*
 //OUTPUT   DD SYSOUT=*
 //SYSABEND DD SYSOUT=*
 //*
 //*
 //* ==============
 //


Hope it helps!

Eckhard


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

## Thread: Calling subprogram — 4 this month / 6 total

### Calling subprogram

- **From:** stang93gt@hotmail.com
- **Date:** 2000-04-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8e3g0q$e2i$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I am attempting to call a subprogram from my main program. I keep
getting an error saying the subprogram is not in the current drive or
directory. I have tried putting the subprogram in both the C and A
drives. The PROGRAM-ID of the subprogram is SALESTAB. I am using the
Personal Microfocus. Any help would be appreciated. I have included
partial coding:



ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.
           SELECT CUST-ORDER-FILE  ASSIGN TO 'A:ORDER.DAT'.
           SELECT SALES-REPORT     ASSIGN TO 'A:SALESREP.DAT'.

       DATA DIVISION.

       FILE SECTION.
       FD  CUST-ORDER-FILE     RECORD CONTAINS 11 CHARACTERS
                               BLOCK CONTAINS 10 RECORDS.
       01  CUST-ORDER-RECORD   PIC X(11).

       FD  SALES-REPORT.
       01  REPORT-LINE-1       PIC X(132)  VALUE SPACES.

       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

       01  WS-SWITCH.
           05  EOF                     PIC X   VALUE 'N'.

       01  CUST-ORDER-RECORD-IN.
           05  ORDER-NUM-IN            PIC X(4).
           05  ORDER-WEIGHT-IN         PIC 9(5).
           05  SHIP-DIST-IN            PIC X.
           05  CLASS-IN                PIC X.

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.

       100-MAIN-ROUTINE.
           OPEN INPUT CUST-ORDER-FILE
                OUTPUT SALES-REPORT
           CALL 'SALESTAB'
           READ CUST-ORDER-FILE INTO CUST-ORDER-RECORD-IN
               AT END MOVE 'Y' TO EOF
           END-READ
           PERFORM 200-VALIDITY-CHECK
               UNTIL EOF IS EQUAL TO 'Y'
           CLOSE CUST-ORDER-FILE
                 SALES-REPORT
           GOBACK.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Calling subprogram

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2000-04-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000425100107.09031.00001240@nso-cc.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<8e3g0q$e2i$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In article <8e3g0q$e2i$1@nnrp1.deja.com>, stang93gt@hotmail.com writes:

>
>I am attempting to call a subprogram from my main program. I keep
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Have you checked the placement of the SALESTAB.DLL sub-program?
It must be in a directory that is on your search path.   The easiest placement
is in the same directory as the executable.
```

#### ↳ Re: Calling subprogram

- **From:** "mangogwr" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8w7O4.9023$A75.1865193@news1.mco>`
- **References:** `<8e3g0q$e2i$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Just before you execute the program if in dos batch mode,
execute following
'PATH >\oldpath.bat'
'PATH=%PATH%;(my new path to called module)'
run program
'OLDPATH.BAT'

<stang93gt@hotmail.com> wrote in message news:8e3g0q$e2i$1@nnrp1.deja.com...

#### ↳ Re: Calling subprogram

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 2000-04-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8eb742$ddl$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<8e3g0q$e2i$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Enter ".;" (dot semicolon) in front of your path. Then your current
directory is always "in the path".
Regards
Ib

<a id="t-53116abf68"></a>
## Thread: WindowsNT & XM — 9 msgs

### WindowsNT & XM

- **From:** bojan.horvat@uni-mb.si
- **Date:** 2000-04-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8e3tmi$rn5$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
We have been using Cobol for many years on different platforms (VMS,
DOS, Windows 95/98/NT).
Now we have some problems on Windows NT and our TSR program. In our TSR
program we cover interrupt 1C (system timer tick) so we can display
string "Working..." in two second interval when time consuming
operation is performing.
All our programs use XM. When we use XM, this string is not displaying
and it looks like the interrupt 1C is not working.
We wrote simple test program. When we start this program without XM,
the "Working..." is displaying properly in two second interval.

Our current XM: XM V1.4.4, we use Windows NT with Service Pack 4

Do you have any ideas how to fix this error? We are using this TSR
which covers interrupt 1C for many other purpose so it has to remain.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: WindowsNT & XM

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>

- **References:** `<8e3tmi$rn5$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

>DOS, Windows 95/98/NT).
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…

#### ↳ Re: WindowsNT & XM

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8e4dgi$3u6$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<8e3tmi$rn5$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
As I understand it, you are emulating DOS under a time sliced system and
within that, you  trying to trap the system clock and redirect it. As long
as you ran the program in protected DOS mode, NT was smart enough to trap
your trap, and make it a local trap only so that you did not crash every
other program. Now, you also want to take over memory management, and trap
the NT trap that traps your original trap.

I am not surprised that NT prevents you from doing it.  In fact, I am
surprised that you got as far as you did without converting the programs to
the OS you are running on.

If you cannot convert the programs, then you are going to have to run them
under DOS.  If you convert them, then you are going to have to use a system
call to interrupt at the system clock level. Trying to write hardware
patches at the system clock level for a time-sliced OS is a pretty dangerous
game.

Basically, you are at the point that you have to pee or get off the pot.  Go
to 32 bit, or run in DOS.  The OS is getting too complex for simplistic bare
metal patches.

bojan.horvat@uni-mb.si wrote in message <8e3tmi$rn5$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>We have been using Cobol for many years on different platforms (VMS,
>DOS, Windows 95/98/NT).
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>Before you buy.
```

Jimmy
```

## Thread: Win32API to get screen resolution — 5 msgs

### Win32API to get screen resolution

- **From:** bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536)
- **Date:** 2000-04-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000425141307.17268.00003885@ng-fq1.aol.com>`

```
Good Afternoon,

I am looking for a way to get the current screen resolution (i.e. 640x480).  Is
anyone aware of a Win32API to get this information ?

Thanks,
Bob Hennessey
```

#### ↳ Re: Win32API to get screen resolution

- **From:** khansen@screenio.com (Kevin Hansen)
- **Date:** 2000-04-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3905ebd0.7575823@wingate>`
- **References:** `<20000425141307.17268.00003885@ng-fq1.aol.com>`

```
On 25 Apr 2000 18:13:07 GMT, bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536) wrote:

>Good Afternoon,
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Bob Hennessey

Bob,

Use GetSystemMetrics with the SM_CXSCREEN and SM_CYSCREEN flags, or
you can use GetDeviceCaps(hdcPrimaryMonitor, HORZRES/VERTRES).

Kevin
NORCOM COBOL Programming Tools
GUI ScreenIO; a cool COBOL/Windows Screen Manager
http://www.ScreenIO.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Win32API to get screen resolution

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b7mN4.250752$Hq3.6313070@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<20000425141307.17268.00003885@ng-fq1.aol.com>`

```

"Bob7536" <bob7536@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20000425141307.17268.00003885@ng-fq1.aol.com...
> Good Afternoon,
>
> I am looking for a way to get the current screen resolution (i.e.
640x480).  Is
> anyone aware of a Win32API to get this information ?
>
> Thanks,
> Bob Hennessey

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID.  GETRECT.
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       CONFIGURATION SECTION.
       Special-Names.
           call-convention 66 is APIentry .

       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  hWnd        pic  9(9) comp-5.
       01  bResult     pic  9(9) comp-5.
       01  Rect.
         05  R-left    pic s9(9) comp-5.
         05  R-top     pic s9(9) comp-5.
         05  R-right   pic s9(9) comp-5.
         05  R-bottom  pic s9(9) comp-5.

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       MAIN SECTION.

           Call APIentry "GetDesktopWindow"
               returning        hWnd
           End-Call
           Call APIentry "GetWindowRect" using
               by value         hWnd
               by reference     Rect
               returning        bResult
           End-Call
           If bResult = 0
               Call APIentry "GetLastError"
                   returning       bResult
               End-Call
               Display "GetWindowRect returned (" bResult ")"
           Else
               Display "left  (" R-left ")"
               Display "top   (" R-top ")"
               Display "right (" R-right ")"
               Display "bottom(" R-bottom ")"
           End-If
           STOP RUN.
```

- **From:** bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536)
- **Date:** 2000-04-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000425165139.15257.00003142@ng-co1.aol.com>`

Bob
```

#### ↳ Re: Win32API to get screen resolution

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<DFqN4.111$Qe.2503@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<20000425141307.17268.00003885@ng-fq1.aol.com>`

```
Bob7536 <bob7536@aol.com> wrote in message
> I am looking for a way to get the current screen resolution (i.e.
640x480).  Is
> anyone aware of a Win32API to get this information ?

The GetDeviceCaps API retrieves device-specific information
about a given device:

int GetDeviceCaps (HDC hdc, int nIndex)
```

<a id="t-2a91af9898"></a>
## Thread: Cross reference with third INPUT-FILE while updating MASTER-FILE — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Cross reference with third INPUT-FILE while updating MASTER-FILE

- **From:** firdaus <callfirdaus@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sgbsi15icdo12@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Hi!

I am new to cobol.Please may I ask for some hints if possible.
I am trying to write a program with the following files used:

SELECT TRANSACTION-FILE ASSIGN TO "TF.DAT"      (input file-1) ..SORTED..
       ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.

SELECT OLD-MASTER-FILE ASSIGN TO "OLD-MF.DAT"   (input file-2)
       ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.

SELECT STOCK-PRICE-FILE ASSIGN TO "PRICE.DAT"   (input file-3)
       ORGANIZATION IS INDEXED SEQUENTIAL.

SELECT NEW-MASTER-FILE ASSIGN TO "NEW-MF.DAT"   (output file-1)
       ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.

SELECT PRINT-FILE ASSIGN TO PRINTER.            (output file-2)

       I am ok with reading the files "input file-1" and "input file-2"
at the same time and comparing the customer code number..reading the
file up to the end using HIGH-VALUES clause etc..Now the problem is
how would I get the price from "input file-3" if the customer code 
matches ?
When should I open the "input file-3" and when to read ? Please give me
some basic standard procedure, I hope I will be alright with
rest of the coding..Basically the program is to update the old-cust-master 
file
to a new-generation master file..The transactions that cannot be actioned 
are
to be reported on the printer. 
Waiting for favourable reply.thankyou.

Best Regards
Firdaus.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cross reference with third INPUT-FILE while updating MASTER-FILE

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 2000-04-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8e4vvp$g5m$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<sgbsi15icdo12@corp.supernews.com>`

```
In this case, I would open Old-Master-File, Stock-Price-File,
New-Master-File and Print-File at the same time, and close them after the
last record was written. My decission is based upon the fact that you open
the indexed file for input and the new master and printfile for output. If
something goes wrong, you will have to rerun the job, and then the 2 output
files will be recreated.

When you have a match, you can move the stock no from the transaction file
to the record key and read the Stock-Price-File.

That brings me to the select-statement for the Stock-Price-File. I would
prefer something like this;
SELECT STOCK-PRICE-FILE  ASSIGN TO "PRICE.DAT"
                                            ORGANIZATION IS INDEXED
                                            ACCESS IS DYNAMIC
                                            RECORD KEY IS STOCK-NO
                                            FILE STATUS IS
STOCK-FILE-STATUS.

You will have to define Stock-File-Status and if your compiler is MF (bear
with me I cannot remember it) File Status looks like this:
01 STOCK-FILE-STATUS.
     05 STOCK-KEY-1                    PIC X.
     05 STOCK-KEY-2                    PIC X.
     05 STOCK-KEY-NUM   REDEFINES STOCK-KEY-2    PIC 99 COMP-X.

Well in short. If you read the stock file and Stock-File-Status = '00'
everything is fine, and you found a price. If not, you can handle the
situation according to the file status. That is much better than the
simplified 'invalid key'.

The way to read? move something to Stock-No (the record key) and say
READ STOCK-PRICE-FILE
END-READ

Regards
Ib

firdaus skrev i meddelelsen ...
>Hi!
>
…[39 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://www.help.com/
```

<a id="t-2f71516133"></a>
## Thread: NetExpress Internet Application and HTMP entry fields — 11 msgs

_Topics: [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### NetExpress Internet Application and HTMP entry fields

- **From:** "Aaron Cardon" <aaron@amnutrition.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OPkN4.4751$g7.486189@news-west.usenetserver.com>`

```
I'm using NetExpress 3.0 to create an Internet application.  My problem is
that the entry fields that I create on the HTML form act strange.  For
example, I set the attributes on a field to have a max of 8 characters and
the pic statement is 9(8).  When the form is loaded into the web browser and
you tab to this field, the default is to not type over the character in the
field.  This poses a problem because the field begins with the following
value 00000000.  When the user tabs to this field they cannot type any
additional characters since the max attribute for the field is set to 8 and
there are already eight characters in the field.  If the user presses the
insert key on their keyboard, it types over the values present for that
field.  However, they have to press the insert key on each and every
additional field to get it to type over.

First question: How do you set the default property of the field to act like
the insert button has already been pressed,  in other words, type over what
is initially there?

Second question: How do you prevent your Internet application from
initializing these HTML numeric fields with zeros?  I would rather have
these fields initialized with nothing in them.
```

#### ↳ Re: NetExpress Internet Application and HTMP entry fields

- **From:** Rich Rohde <richNOriSPAM@richware.net.invalid>
- **Date:** 2000-04-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<06b4de99.8110d599@usw-ex0101-005.remarq.com>`
- **References:** `<OPkN4.4751$g7.486189@news-west.usenetserver.com>`

```
In article <OPkN4.4751$g7.486189@news-
west.usenetserver.com>, "Aaron Cardon" <aaron@amnutrition.com>
wrote:
>
>First question: How do you set the default property of the
field to act like
>the insert button has already been pressed,  in other words,
type over what
>is initially there?

Don't set an initial value for the field. You can add validation
to the field to make sure only numeric data can be entered by
right-clicking the field and then select validation from the
content menu. You will have to manually move the digits from the
input field to your pic 9(x) field.

>Second question: How do you prevent your Internet application
from
>initializing these HTML numeric fields with zeros?  I would
rather have
>these fields initialized with nothing in them.

For the field properties, make sure the attributes tab is
selected. Blank out any entry for the Value keyword (bottom of
the list).

Rich



* Sent from RemarQ http://www.remarq.com The Internet's Discussion Network *
The fastest and easiest way to search and participate in Usenet - Free!
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: NetExpress Internet Application and HTMP entry fields

- **Date:** 2000-04-25T00:00:00

- **References:** `<OPkN4.4751$g7.486189@news-west.usenetserver.com> <06b4de99.8110d599@usw-ex0101-005.remarq.com>`

```
Thanks for the quick reply!

I'm still a little confused about how to get rid of the HTML form
initializing with zeros.  Currently, the value attribute for my numeric
fields has nothing in it.  Yet the form still initializes with zeros.  Are
you recommending that I define these fields as alpha-numeric, instead, then
set up a validation to check for numeric?

That would work.  However, I will still be left with the problem of having
an alpha-numeric field that has 8 characters in it, at times, and a max
definition of 8.  When the user tabs to that field and tries to change the
value, it won't let them since there are already 8 characters in it.  Any
ideas there?

Thanks for the help!
Aaron Cardon
aaron@amnutrition.com

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NetExpress Internet Application and HTMP entry fields

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rFmN4.250798$Hq3.6315048@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<OPkN4.4751$g7.486189@news-west.usenetserver.com> <06b4de99.8110d599@usw-ex0101-005.remarq.com> <tgmN4.5293$g7.578277@news-west.usenetserver.com>`

```
The javascript method select() can be used to preselect the text in the text
control. The first keystroke would erase the current contents and replace
them with the keystroke. You can use the onLoad event for the document to
have it preselect all the text in all the fields, or use the text.onFocus
event to select the field when the user navigates to the field.

Beats me how to do it using NetExpress forms though.

- Karl

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>

"Aaron Cardon" <aaron@amnutrition.com> wrote in message
news:RtpN4.6641$g7.806226@news-west.usenetserver.com...
> Thanks for the suggestion!
>
> Any idea on how to get it to automatically type over the existing
> characters, instead of erasing what is there with the first keyboard
stroke?

Yeah, but it's ugly, and not particularly user-friendly. The technique is
similar to subclassing an entry field wherein you capture every keystroke
and manipulate the characters displayed.  The challenge is doing it within
the browser's event model, then making it work for all versions of browsers.
The onKeyPress event is probably what you're looking for. In fairness to
c.l.c this has nothing to do with COBOL so if you plan on pursuing this
further you may want to move to comp.lang.javascript.

>
> Oscar T. Grouch wrote in message ...

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NetExpress Internet Application and HTMP entry fields

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Rich Rohde <richNOriSPAM@richware.net.invalid>
- **Date:** 2000-04-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<12ea2486.d22d0ab3@usw-ex0101-005.remarq.com>`
- **References:** `<OPkN4.4751$g7.486189@news-west.usenetserver.com> <06b4de99.8110d599@usw-ex0101-005.remarq.com> <tgmN4.5293$g7.578277@news-west.usenetserver.com> <rFmN4.250798$Hq3.6315048@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <RtpN4.6641$g7.806226@news-west.usenetserver.com> <yUtN4.253610$Hq3.6387815@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <SADN4.5377$CU6.628734@news-west.usenetserver.com>`

```
Aaron,

You are not off topic. I don't know how many on this list have
written web applications using COBOL, but I have, and you have
valid COBOL questions.

There is no requirements about using JAVA or anything else in
developing a web application, just some common-COBOL-sense.

If you know how to use ACCEPT and DISPLAY, you have the basic
understanding on how to write web applications with COBOL.

IMPORTANT: use only pic x fields when working with HTML form
fields. I even use FrontPage to develop the HTML files and then
bring them into a NetExpress project (be careful, NetExpress
doesn't handle some HTML files correctly).

I tend to back NetExpress in developing web applications, but
Merant needs to apply more resources to make this portion of
NetExpress more stable.

Regards,
Rich

Rich

* Sent from RemarQ http://www.remarq.com The Internet's Discussion Network *
The fastest and easiest way to search and participate in Usenet - Free!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NetExpress Internet Application and HTMP entry fields

- **From:** Rich Rohde <richNOriSPAM@richware.net.invalid>
- **Date:** 2000-04-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<00641fd6.ba5d7744@usw-ex0101-005.remarq.com>`
- **References:** `<OPkN4.4751$g7.486189@news-west.usenetserver.com> <06b4de99.8110d599@usw-ex0101-005.remarq.com> <tgmN4.5293$g7.578277@news-west.usenetserver.com>`

```
In article <tgmN4.5293$g7.578277@news-
west.usenetserver.com>, "Aaron Cardon" <aaron@amnutrition.com>
wrote:
>Thanks for the quick reply!
>
>I'm still a little confused about how to get rid of the HTML
form
>initializing with zeros.  Currently, the value attribute for my
numeric
>fields has nothing in it.  Yet the form still initializes with
zeros.  Are
>you recommending that I define these fields as alpha-numeric,
instead, then
>set up a validation to check for numeric?

What I believe you are saying, is that in the HTML form you have
specified 9(8)? If so, this is incorrect, you must specifify x
(8). Then use the validation routine for the field to make sure
that only whole numbers are entered in the field.

>
>That would work.  However, I will still be left with the
problem of having
>an alpha-numeric field that has 8 characters in it, at times,
and a max
>definition of 8.  When the user tabs to that field and tries to
change the
>value, it won't let them since there are already 8 characters
in it.  Any
>ideas there?

Once the field is identified as alphanumeric, it will be
initialized as spaces. The user can then type what ever they
wish. Remember to set the validation for the field to number. Be
aware, the data will be left justified as oppose to left
justified, which is what you would expect for a numeric field. I
use the following the take care of the alphanumeric to numeric:
(others may have a better suggestion)

       01  misc-data.
           05  sub                     pic 9(04) comp-5.
           05  i-sub                   pic 9(04) comp-5.
           05  login-id                pic 9(07).
.
.
       linkage section.
       01  ls-loginid                  pic x(07).
.
.
               move length login-id to sub
               perform varying i-sub from length ls-loginid by -
1 until
                       i-sub = zeros
                   if ls-loginid (i-sub:1) numeric
                       move ls-loginid (i-sub:1) to login-id
(sub:1)
                       subtract 1      from sub
                   end-if
               end-perform

Regards,
Rich


* Sent from RemarQ http://www.remarq.com The Internet's Discussion Network *
The fastest and easiest way to search and participate in Usenet - Free!
```

#### ↳ Re: NetExpress Internet Application and HTMP entry fields

- **From:** Rich Rohde <richNOriSPAM@richware.net.invalid>
- **Date:** 2000-04-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c310dc.bddcd429@usw-ex0101-005.remarq.com>`
- **References:** `<OPkN4.4751$g7.486189@news-west.usenetserver.com>`

```
One additional thought, you may wish to get a copy of 'Elements
of COBOL Web Programming with Micro Focus NetExpress' by Wilson
Price. Will has done a great job helping those who know nothing
about Web Programming to develop simple web applications with
this book. Go to http://cobolreport.com/books/index.htm for more
information.

Rich

<a id="t-75b086394e"></a>
## Thread: Debugging — 3 msgs

### Debugging

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8e5hll$n2l$1@news.igs.net>`

```
This is an oldie, but I thought a few people might be amused.

What if DR. SEUSS wrote your computer repair manual.. would it go like this?

If a packet hits a pocket on a socket on a port, and the bus is interrupted
as a very last resort, And the address of the memory makes your floppy disk
abort, then the socket packet pocket has an error to report. If your cursor
finds a menu item followed by a dash, and the double-clicking icon puts your
window in the trash, and your data is corrupted 'cause the index doesn't
hash, then your situation's hopeless and the system's gonna crash! If the
label on the cable on the table at your house, says the network is connected
to the button on your mouse, but your packets want to tunnel on another
protocol, that's repeatedly rejected by the printer down the hall, and your
screen is all distorted by the side effects of gauss, so your icons in the
window are as wavy as a souse, then you may as well reboot and go out with a
bang, 'cause as sure as I'm a poet, the sucker's gonna hang! When the copy
of your floppy's getting sloppy on the disk, and the microcode instructions
cause unnecessary risk, then you have to flash your memory and you'll want
to RAM your ROM, quickly turn off the computer and be sure to tell your mom!
```

- **References:** `<8e5hll$n2l$1@news.igs.net>`

```

could go into SYSD and monitor my progress.  Once I got sufficient debug
lines, I could kill my long running job.  A subsequent step won't do it.

SYSOUT=* to SYSOUT=FILE.NAME,DISP=(NEW,CATLG,DELETE),SPACE=...

Interesting, looking at your link I found the following:
        //ddname  DD  SYSOUT=class 

        //name    OUTPUT CLASS=class 
        //ddname  DD     SYSOUT=(,),OUTPUT=*.name 

        //name    OUTPUT DEFAULT=YES,CLASS=class 
        //ddname  DD     SYSOUT=(,) 

What in the world is *.name?

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Split output - BatchPipeWorks ?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8e8g6g$afg$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<39073291.2953296B@cusys.edu> <8e7e4p$j6g$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <39074022.7E99A597@cusys.edu> <8e7grf$hhb$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <39075165.399488BA@cusys.edu> <Pine.GSO.3.96.1000426230122.1779A-100000@eclipse>`

```
I don't think so.  Batch pipes lets you send output from one job to another -
before the 1st job finishes.  I don't think that is what Howard was looking
for.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Split output - BatchPipeWorks ?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-04-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39082DD8.36AD9911@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<39073291.2953296B@cusys.edu> <8e7e4p$j6g$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <39074022.7E99A597@cusys.edu> <8e7grf$hhb$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <39075165.399488BA@cusys.edu> <Pine.GSO.3.96.1000426230122.1779A-100000@eclipse> <8e8g6g$afg$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> I don't think so.  Batch pipes lets you send output from one job to
> another - before the 1st job finishes.  I don't think that is what
> Howard was looking for.

> "Jonathan Nash" <jnash@qis.net> wrote in message
> > Could BatchPipeWorks be used for something like this ?

You could write the equivalent of Unix 'tee' which copies the output
to console and passes it to the next program in the pipe.

program 1  |  tee  |  program 2

tee would dump the output of program 1 and program 2 would also get
the output.  Sledgehammer to crack a nut, but it would work.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Split output

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8e885b$saa$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<39073291.2953296B@cusys.edu> <8e7e4p$j6g$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <39074022.7E99A597@cusys.edu> <y7LN4.18478$0o4.137619@iad-read.news.verio.net> <#5Uct2#r$GA.351@cpmsnbbsa04>`

```
"ChrisOsborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com> wrote in message it.
> >
> > Golly... wasn't the ready availability of COBTEST supposed to make such
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Bits and pieces of "information"

COBTEST was the VS COBOL II debugger - the current "debugger" is called "The
Debug Tool" - and it works for ALL the LE-enabled languages.  (It DOES also
support debugging of VS COBOL II programs in a COBOL for this-and-that
applications.)

The Debug Tool is a "freebie" (unlike earlier IBM "add-ons") - so if your
site has LE, it has the Debug Tool.

Yes, your systems programmers need to "install" it, but it is actually easier
to install it than to avoid it.  Often it is installed in shops - that don't
even know that it is there.

The original question about viewing output as a "debugging" option, is really
independent of COBTEST vs Debug Tool vs Xpediter vs SmarTest vs whatever.
Any one of those debugging programs SHOULD provide you an "option" that (I
would think) would be better than trying to monitor an output file.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Split output

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-04-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39084D30.2B83B434@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<39073291.2953296B@cusys.edu> <8e7e4p$j6g$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <39074022.7E99A597@cusys.edu> <y7LN4.18478$0o4.137619@iad-read.news.verio.net> <#5Uct2#r$GA.351@cpmsnbbsa04>`

```
ChrisOsborne wrote:
> 
> Don't the local sysprogs have to install COBTEST?  I know it wasn't
> installed at all at a former site I worked at; though they had Xpediter to
> make up for it.

To tell the truth, I don't know.  I have never worked at a place where
anybody used debugger tools to follow what a batch program does - at
least past a bit of playing around.  In this case, the volume of data is
such that I simply want to browse through the building output until I
found interesting data.  This might be a hundred thousand records in,
before I killed the process.  I guess we're too lazy to take the time
and effort to use those tools when we can do displays exactly where we
want and get our debugging out of the way quickly and easily.  On the
other hand, it doesn't mean we have less work to do - by finishing up
quickly we just get handed new work.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Split output

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8e9jl1$d55$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<39073291.2953296B@cusys.edu> <8e7e4p$j6g$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <39074022.7E99A597@cusys.edu> <y7LN4.18478$0o4.137619@iad-read.news.verio.net> <#5Uct2#r$GA.351@cpmsnbbsa04> <39084D30.2B83B434@cusys.edu>`

```
Just FYI,
  All of the debuggers have a method to put a "break point" in a debugging
session when a data-item (such as a record description) has a specific value.
You then "run" the program to that break point.  Several of them (I believe)
even have a method of asking (in advance) for "backtrack" capability.  This
would allow you to "run" to the record in question - and "backtrack" to look
at what happened with the previous record.

FYI-2,
  From my days of working for both Micro Focus *and* ADS (the then vendor of
Xpediter), it is truly remarkable how many shops pay for
debugging/development tools but don't provide the time and resources for
their programmers to actually learn how and when to use them.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Split output

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 2000-04-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<u1%N4.18761$0o4.142564@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<39073291.2953296B@cusys.edu> <y7LN4.18478$0o4.137619@iad-read.news.verio.net> <#5Uct2#r$GA.351@cpmsnbbsa04> <39084D30.2B83B434@cusys.edu>`

```
In article <39084D30.2B83B434@cusys.edu>,
Howard Brazee  <Please, do, not, e-mail, replies, to, Howard, Brazee, post,
 	them!!> wrote:

[snippage]

>I guess we're too lazy to take the time
>and effort to use those tools when we can do displays exactly where we
>want and get our debugging out of the way quickly and easily.

BLEARGH!  With all due respect, Mr Brazee, what you describe here is not
what I was taught to call 'laziness'.  To be 'lazy' is to put out the
minimal amount of effort necessary to 'get away' with a task... the task
may or may not actually get done but the lazy one 'gets away' with
it.  You seem to be stating here that you are putting in *more* effort
than the task might otherwise require were your efforts directed
differently... this, as I was taught, is not 'laziness' but more akin to
'stupidity'.

A Story:

A gang of carpenters are working on a house; the Foreman (this story is
old enough to have originated in the days when Foremen were Foremen, not
Forefolk), as is the Forman's wont, is wandering about, keeping an eye on
things.  He notices one particular carpenter laboring *mightily* with his
saw and the following dialogue ensues:

F: 'Hey, Joe... what'cha doin'?'

J: 'Sawin' these planks, Boss, just like ya tol' me to.'

F: 'Yup, that ya are... y'know, I can't really tell from this angle but it
looks, to me, like your saw's kinda dull.'

J: 'You sure got a good eye, Boss... I guess that's why you're the
Boss.  Saw sure is dull, duller'n a butterknife.'

F: 'Oh... ummmm, Joe, why don't you sharpen your saw?'

J: 'Sharpen my saw?  I'm too busy cuttin' these planks to sharpen my saw!'

EOS

Now I have told that tale many times, in many places... and many folks
have laughed and nodded while intoning 'Yup, it's *just* that way here,
too'...

... while *my* response, when I first heard it, was to think 'All
right... and now the Foreman says 'Joe, lissen'a me.  I am your
Boss... Direct Order, stop cuttin' the planks and sharpen your saw, *now*,
please?''

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Split output

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-04-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3908A68F.69875EFE@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<39073291.2953296B@cusys.edu> <39084D30.2B83B434@cusys.edu> <u1%N4.18761$0o4.142564@iad-read.news.verio.net> <39088F27.2483B4DD@cusys.edu> <4S0O4.18780$0o4.143337@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
docdwarf@clark.net wrote:
> 

> >Good story.  I infer then that you believe that if we took the time to
> >really know the debugger well, we would find it to be less work than
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> knowledge of alternatives... but the 'rut you know best', as it were.


I infer then that you believe that the batch debugger tools you are
familiar with are many orders of magnitude better than the online tools
we are familiar with.  

Either that or your batch programs do a lot of calling of subprograms
(possibly OO).  (That kind of complexity requires stronger tools).  Boy
it would be nice if our online tools were half as useful as batch
displays.  Maybe I have just worked in the wrong shops.   

At any rate, I have worked with lots of programmers in lots of shops. 
Maybe a third of us have tried online tools for batch.  None of us have
kept doing so.  The difference between us and that sawyer is that he
believes he has a tool to sharpen his saw.  Maybe you have a better
"sharpener" than what I have seen at my shops, one that actually doesn't
dull the blades.

I do have to admit that in my current job I have no idea whether we have
any such tool at all.  (Our online programs uses ADS/O and ADS-ALIVE for
debugging).  I have asked around, but the systems person who might know
is on vacation.  Since I have worked here for over two years, my
experience may be out of date.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Split output

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-04-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8eaciu$egb$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<39073291.2953296B@cusys.edu> <39084D30.2B83B434@cusys.edu> <u1%N4.18761$0o4.142564@iad-read.news.verio.net> <39088F27.2483B4DD@cusys.edu> <4S0O4.18780$0o4.143337@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3908A68F.69875EFE@cusys.edu>`

```
Again - going back to my Xpediter days (Micro Focus and "porting" testing to
the PC is another story completely), there really should be LESS effort in
invoking an "online debugger" than using DISPLAYs.

Xpediter (and I assume several other online tools) provides a "tool" to
auto-magically convert JCL to TSO CLISTS (or possibly REXX now).  In that
case, all you SHOULD need to do is (one time only) convert your JCL to
CLIST/REXX - and then invoke the debugger (usually under ISPF).

Once the full screen version of your program "comes up" - you can use a
single "line command" (that should look a LOT like and ISPF line command) to
put a break point where you would have put a DISPLAY and then run your
program.  This should then give you a LOT more debugging capabilities than a
"simple display" to resolve your problem.

If this is "difficult," I just don't get it.
   OTOH, there are lots and LOTS more capabilities that online debuggers have
that give you MORE capabilities than "display" statements.  Getting to know
when and how to use those may take some time, but I just don't understand
how/why this is "difficult".

Now, the above DOES assume that your "installer" has installed the debugging
tool "successfully" so that it is easy to reach from your normal ISPF
session.  If that is "missing" then there may be some reasons for never using
it.
```

My apologies for my obscurity, Mr Brazee; I took your statement of 'if we
took the time to really know' as the equivalent of '... but we have not
taken the time and do not really know'.

If my reading is correct then *either* of us might be right... but More
Learning is in order before a conclusion based on more on data and less on
assumption might be arrived at.

[snip]

>At any rate, I have worked with lots of programmers in lots of shops. 
>Maybe a third of us have tried online tools for batch.  None of us have
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>dull the blades.

A sharpening-stone, improperly handled, can dull a blade; first one needs
to learn how to use it.

DD

By now we are pretty much off topic, so I want to jump in with a saw
sharpening comment:

When my project has slow times (which it often does.  Our work load
goes up and down.) I sharpen my saw.  I am sharpening my saw right now
by reading this newsgroup.

Several weeks ago, I got yelled at for sharpening my saw.  My leader
felt that it was not project related work and I should not be doing
it.  I feel that saw sharpening is important, and although it might not
directly support the project, it always indirectly supports the
project, because it supports me.

Unfortunately, many narrow minded people who tell me what to do don't
see it that way, thus my dilemma.  I've noticed several times, DD,
where you justify a given solution you have described by
saying "...Because he who signs my paychecks told me to..."

-Art

- **References:** `<39073291.2953296B@cusys.edu> <3908A68F.69875EFE@cusys.edu> <MP4O4.18869$0o4.145114@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8ecd8a$4qq$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

In article <8ecd8a$4qq$1@nnrp1.deja.com>,
Art Perry  <eowynfuzz@my-deja.com> wrote:
>By now we are pretty much off topic, so I want to jump in with a saw
>sharpening comment:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>saying "...Because he who signs my paychecks told me to..."

For me it is more than 'several times', Mr Perry; I need to remind myself
of this on a daily basis:

'Why am I working on a proc in a public library where anyone, at any time,
can make mods that might screw up what I am doing?  Because the person who
signs my timesheets tells me to.'

... and suchlike.  You mention that a manager hollered on you for
'sharpening your saw'; this is behavior similar to the anecdote and
directly opposed to my msuing over why the Foreman didn't issue a
saw-sharpening order.

I am reminded of A Story! - perhaps apochryphal, granted, but if it isn't
true it should be.

It was told that Someone Important - Kernigan?  Ritchie? - was working at
Bell Labs in the mid '70's, doing some rather... intricate work on data
dictionaries.  He had A Problem in mind and had assumed the Programmer's
Position... cup of coffee in one hand, cigarette in the other, feet up on
the desk, staring at the ceiling and mapping out memory-locations in the
acoustic tiles.

His Boss walks past and bellows out 'What *ARE* you doing?!?'

He shakes himself, recovers from whatever intricacy is now lost... and, in
a calm, measured manner said 'I'm *thinking*, Boss.'

'Oh... well, can't you do that on your own time?'

This brings to mind a bit of a Benny Hill skit I saw, a few decades
back... I think it was a parody of Kojak... anyhow, Benny Hill, as one
character, engages Benny Hill, as another character, in the following
exchange:

'Where were you?

'Getting a haircut.'

'On company time?'

'It grew on company time.'

... and even more interesting, in the case of some companies - the old EDS
or Disney, say - not only does it grow on company time but it is the
company which demands hair of a certain length, too.

DD

>
>In article <MP4O4.18869$0o4.145114@iad-read.news.verio.net>,
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>> to learn how to use it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Split output from a COBOL pgm - BatchPipeWorks

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** Jonathan Nash <jnash@qis.net>
- **Date:** 2000-04-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Pine.GSO.3.96.1000429100821.9404B-100000@eclipse>`
- **References:** `<39073291.2953296B@cusys.edu> <3908A68F.69875EFE@cusys.edu> <MP4O4.18869$0o4.145114@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8ecd8a$4qq$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <N6rO4.19052$0o4.152409@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
Someone on IBM-MAIN said that this 
can be done with BatchPipeWorks:

>Yes, for the price of (a) JCL changes and (b) BatchPipes.  The advantage
>to your management is that the code is maintained (by IBM, compared to
>Paul's suggestion from yesterday.)
>It would be a half-pipe fitting to do the split.
>Tom S.

I do not know enough about BatchPipeWorks to put together the
JCL that would be needed but here is a snippit from a manual
which shows how BatchPipeWorks can send output to more than
one location:

=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=
IBM SmartBatch for OS/390: BatchPipeWorks User's Guide
Document Number GC28-1651-01
� Copyright IBM Corp. 1995, 1998
=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=

5.3.2 The > Stage Function
=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=

The > (rewrite data set) stage function reads records from its input
stream and writes those records to a physical sequential data set or
member of a partitioned data set.  The physical sequential data set must
already exist.  The member of the partitioned data set may or may not
exist.  The physical sequential data set is replaced.  If the member of
the partitioned data set exists, it is replaced.  If the member does not
exist, it is created.  Specify a data set identifier as the operand.  The
data set identifier can be the name of an existing physical sequential
data set, the name of a partitioned data set and the member that you want
to replace or create, or the ddname of an allocated data set.  A > stage
cannot be the first stage of a pipeline.

Like the TERMINAL stage function, the > stage function copies its input
stream to its output stream for use by any following stage.  All output
device drivers work this way.  In fact, both the following pipelines yield
the same results:

pipe tso listcat � > space.data � terminal

pipe tso listcat � terminal � > space.data

In the example, the order of the > and TERMINAL stage functions is
reversed.  TERMINAL displays the LISTCAT response on your terminal, but
also writes the records to its output stream.  The input stream of the >
stage function is connected to the output stream of TERMINAL.  So, the >
stage reads the response records and writes them to the SPACE.DATA data
set, replacing the contents of the data set.

In Figure 154, the < stage function reads the data set DAY.LIST, writing
the records to its output stream.  Then the > stage function reads those
records from its input stream and writes them to the existing data set
NEWDAY.LIST.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------
[snip]
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Split output

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-04-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<390BD473.6B@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<39073291.2953296B@cusys.edu> <39084D30.2B83B434@cusys.edu> <u1%N4.18761$0o4.142564@iad-read.news.verio.net> <39088F27.2483B4DD@cusys.edu> <4S0O4.18780$0o4.143337@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
> 
> I read your reply with sadness, Mr Brazee... see...
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Where, oh where is the Foreman who will tell you to sharpen your saws?

"To a hammer all problems look like nails".

You seem to have no idea of the problem area but you are proposing that
your solution is best regardless of any aspects that you are completely
oblivious to.

While interactive debugging is wonderful for certain sets of problems it
is not always the best solution.  I have a system which would be
extremely difficult to interactively debug and would be almost
unmanageable to maintain in a manner that would facilitate this.  It is
a large Windows API system maintaining a dozen or so main windows and a
multitude of dialog boxes that allows completely unstructured user
interaction.

The system has its own built in 'debugging' trace that can be turned on
or off.  This is equivalent to 'Batch Displays' but uses a dialog list
box and write a log file.  Mostly it is only necessary to turn on the
trace - on the users' site if required, and get the trace results to see
where the problem is occuring.

Different mechanisms may be more appropriate to other needs.
```

- **Date:** 2000-04-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8efeiv$84n$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
